hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a long-context clinical language model fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline trained on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 coding of inpatient hospital stays in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Long-Context Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3–5 years of completed inpatient EHR records from a multi-hospital health system, targeting 80,000–200,000 admissions. Each admission's input is the bundle of clinical notes (discharge summary, admission note, consultant notes, procedure notes) timestamped before discharge summary finalization — the natural boundary of what is available at prediction time. Notes filed as addenda or corrections after finalization are excluded. Labels are the final ICD-10 diagnosis codes locked for billing by certified coders; these represent the gold standard. Any ICD-10 version transitions during the study period are handled by mapping all codes to a consistent version before splitting. Codes appearing in fewer than 10 training admissions are excluded from evaluation metrics but retained in training to support the model's internal representations.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by discharge date: approximately the earliest 60% of admissions (roughly years 1–3) form the training set, the next 20% (mid-year 3 to year 4) form the validation set, and the most recent 20% (year 4 onward) form the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because coding practices, note templates, and case mix evolve over time. A random split would allow future documentation styles to appear in training, producing optimistic estimates that do not reflect deployment on future admissions — the explicit stakeholder requirement. Entire admissions are assigned atomically to one partition; no note or label from a given admission spans multiple sets. The split is applied globally by discharge date, not hospital-by-hospital, to avoid within-site leakage.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive identical input: the concatenated, chronologically ordered note bundle for each admission, with structured note-type delimiters inserted between notes. No hospital, service-line, or administrative metadata is added to either model. This isolates the representation hypothesis from auxiliary-feature confounds.

For the TF-IDF baseline, character-trigrams and word-unigrams are extracted with sublinear TF scaling. The vocabulary of up to 200,000 terms is selected by training-set document frequency — the vectorizer is fit on training data only and applied to validation and test. For the LLM, the pretrained tokenizer is used as-is (its vocabulary is frozen from pretraining); the task-specific sigmoid projection head is trained on the training set, and truncation policy (retaining the most recent tokens, prioritizing the discharge summary) is fixed before any validation evaluation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a long-context clinical language model — such as Clinical-LongFormer — fine-tuned end-to-end with a per-label sigmoid output and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters (learning rate, warmup schedule, batch size) are tuned via Bayesian search over the validation set.

The baseline is TF-IDF features paired with one-vs-rest logistic regression. Its regularization strength C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} by grid search, using the same primary metric and the same validation partition. Equivalent tuning effort ensures observed gaps are attributable to the model family, not to asymmetric optimization. A majority-label lower-bound baseline is also reported.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across all in-scope ICD-10 codes. This metric is correct for this stakeholder context: it weights rare but clinically important codes equally to common ones, directly countering the risk that micro-averaging hides failure on low-frequency but high-stakes diagnoses. Per-label prediction thresholds for both models are tuned to maximize macro-F1 before final reporting.

Secondary metrics include micro-F1, macro-AUC-ROC, Precision@K and Recall@K (K=5, 10), exact-match ratio, and per-frequency-decile macro-F1 breakdowns. The decile breakdown is especially important for diagnosing whether LLM gains concentrate on common codes or extend to rare ones.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All model selection decisions begin with the validation set, including hyperparameter choices for both models and initial threshold tuning. After the best configuration is identified, the held-out test set is used once to confirm the selected setup and finalize per-label thresholds before reporting the final results. This keeps model development structured while ensuring that reported performance reflects the strongest version of each approach on truly unseen admissions.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To ensure that any observed gain is attributable to LLM representation and not to other factors: both models receive the same note bundle (equalizing input scope); metadata features are excluded from both; per-label thresholds are tuned under the same procedure for both; and the temporal split prevents leakage from documentation drift. Post-hoc stratification checks on the test set by hospital site, service line, and admission year confirm that macro-F1 improvements are not driven by a single subpopulation.

This experiment establishes whether a long-context clinical LLM outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on multi-label ICD-10 coding quality, as measured by macro-F1 on held-out future admissions. It does not establish production readiness, inference cost feasibility, or generalizability to outpatient or emergency department coding contexts.

planted_issues.description: The design uses the held-out test set to finalize per-label prediction thresholds, which contaminates the evaluation and invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate of generalization. In multi-label classification, threshold tuning on the test set directly inflates macro-F1 — especially for rare but important codes — because it adapts the model’s output to maximize performance on known test labels. This creates an optimistic bias and undermines the experiment’s claim to estimate performance on future, unseen admissions.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split introduces data imbalance and may harm model generalization by not representing all code frequencies in training.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A temporal split is required in this domain because coding practices, case mix, and documentation styles evolve over time. The stakeholder requirement is to generalize to future admissions, so the split correctly mirrors deployment conditions. Imbalance over time is a real phenomenon and should be captured, not avoided.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it gives equal weight to very rare codes that may not be operationally important.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-F1 is explicitly chosen to meet stakeholder needs: it ensures rare but clinically critical diagnoses (e.g., sepsis, rare complications) are not ignored by the model. Stakeholders care about reducing coder workload across all diagnosis types, including those that are infrequent but high-stakes, so macro-F1 is a more operationally valid measure than micro-F1.

must_not_claim.claim: Not including hospital ID or service line as features weakens both models and could mask true performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding metadata is a deliberate confound control to isolate the effect of text representation. The hypothesis is about model family (LLM vs. TF-IDF), not about auxiliary data use. Including metadata would confound the comparison and could falsely attribute gains to the LLM when they stem from metadata leakage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies that using the test set to finalize per-label thresholds constitutes evaluation contamination, which invalidates the test set as an independent measure of generalization. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that threshold tuning on test data leaks label information into model development, especially harms rare-label evaluation under macro-F1, and violates the principle of one-shot test evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Null — there is no legitimate justification for using the test set in threshold tuning. Any such use constitutes a fatal flaw in experimental design for generalization assessment.

difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted design still separates development and evaluation phases and appears methodologically disciplined at first glance — only a careful reviewer will notice that 'finalizing thresholds' on the test set constitutes a critical violation of evaluation independence, especially in a multi-label setting with macro-F1.

hypothesis: In hospital clinical coding, a domain-adapted transformer that jointly encodes discharge summaries and the patient's prior inpatient note timeline will outperform a TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline for predicting high-frequency ICD-10 diagnosis codes at discharge, measured by macro-F1 and precision at top-k suggested codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation operations for inpatient hospital stays, where coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and longitudinal inpatient notes
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Joint Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Code Suggestion

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3.5 years of finalized inpatient encounters (January 2020 – June 2023) from a single large hospital system, targeting approximately 150,000–250,000 admissions after quality filtering. Only encounters with a finalized billed claim, a present discharge summary, and at least one valid ICD-10-CM code are included. Encounters with late-amended codes (amendments filed more than 30 days post-discharge) are excluded to avoid noisy labels. Labels are drawn from the final UB-04 billed codes — not interim coding drafts — and restricted to the top-K ICD-10-CM codes appearing in at least 0.5% of training encounters, typically yielding 200–400 codes. This threshold-based restriction is justified by the need for stable per-code performance estimates; rare codes in the long tail would produce unreliable metrics and are addressed in production through a separate rare-code pathway outside this experiment's scope.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **chronologically and strictly**: training covers January 2020 – December 2021 (24 months), validation covers January – June 2022 (6 months), and the held-out test set covers July 2022 – June 2023 (12 months). Temporal splitting is non-negotiable for this domain: ICD-10 code updates occur annually, documentation practices drift, and a random split would allow future clinical language patterns to contaminate the training distribution. Within each temporal window, patient-level group integrity is maintained — no patient's test-window encounter appears in any prior split in a way that could encode their coding behavior. This prevents a subtle leakage pathway where a prolific patient's coding patterns are memorized from earlier admissions and then credited to the test split.

### 3. Defining the Text Availability Envelope

The most critical design decision is defining exactly which text is available at prediction time. We define the cutoff as: all clinical notes with a creation timestamp strictly before the earlier of (a) the discharge summary finalization timestamp or (b) discharge date + 24 hours. This includes progress notes, consult notes, and the discharge summary itself, but excludes addenda, late consult responses, or coding query documents added after the coder would reasonably initiate their review. Both the transformer and the TF-IDF baseline receive **identical document sets** defined by this rule — this is the central confound control ensuring that any performance gap reflects representation quality and temporal encoding, not differential text access.

### 4. Models and Features

The **transformer model** is initialized from a pretrained clinical language model checkpoint (ClinicalBERT or BioLinkBERT). The discharge summary is the primary input; prior inpatient notes from the same admission are integrated via a hierarchical or sliding-window encoder to handle multi-document inputs. A sigmoid output head with binary cross-entropy loss and positive-class weighting addresses label imbalance. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), sequence length/truncation strategy, note pooling method, and loss weighting. Early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs.

The **TF-IDF baseline** receives the concatenation of the same documents as a bag-of-words input (unigrams + bigrams, up to 50,000 features, sublinear TF), paired with a one-vs-rest logistic regression classifier. The baseline receives equivalent encounter metadata features and is tuned with the same validation budget: regularization strength C over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} and class weighting options. This parity of tuning effort and feature availability is essential — an undertrained baseline would inflate the apparent transformer advantage.

An additional **ablation variant** of the transformer — receiving only the discharge summary with no prior note timeline — is trained under identical conditions. This isolates the contribution of joint timeline encoding from general transformer expressiveness.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-F1** over the K-label restricted set, evaluated at a fixed threshold of 0.5. Micro averaging provides a stable overall summary across the multi-label output space and reflects aggregate coding performance across all predictions.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@8 and Precision@15** (measuring suggestion list quality directly relevant to coder workflow), **Recall@8 and Recall@15** (coding completeness), macro-F1, per-code F1 distribution, and expected calibration error. Stratified performance by service line is reported as a confound check.

### 6. Validation and Test Discipline

All model selection decisions — including checkpoint selection, architecture choices, and hyperparameter configurations — are made exclusively on the validation set using macro-F1. The test set is touched exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples at the encounter level) and McNemar's test for statistical significance of the macro-F1 difference.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **text volume parity** is enforced by the shared timestamp cutoff rule. Second, **encounter complexity** is addressed by stratifying macro-F1 by length-of-stay (≤2 days vs. >5 days) to test whether the timeline encoder's benefit scales with note volume. Third, **service-line mix drift** between training and test windows is assessed via stratified reporting.

If the experiment succeeds, it establishes that joint transformer encoding of discharge summaries and inpatient note timelines outperforms lexical TF-IDF methods for frequent-code prediction under matched-text conditions. It does not establish performance on rare codes, generalizability to other hospital systems, or superiority in real-time coder workflow integration — those would require follow-on studies.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric is incorrectly set to micro-F1, which disproportionately emphasizes the most frequent ICD-10 codes and obscures performance on less common but clinically important diagnoses. This contradicts the stakeholder requirement to improve performance across the full spectrum of frequent codes, especially those in the lower-frequency half of the distribution. Using micro-F1 as the primary metric could lead to selecting a model that performs well only on common codes while failing on others, misaligning evaluation with clinical objectives.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline uses one-vs-rest logistic regression with TF-IDF features, which is too simplistic compared to modern deep learning approaches and therefore not a fair comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline is representative of current operational systems in clinical coding support tools. It is a standard and appropriately matched baseline given the hypothesis aims to evaluate gains from joint encoding and representation learning — not architectural complexity. The baseline is also tuned fairly and receives the same input text and metadata, making it a valid comparison.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 for model selection while reporting micro-F1 as the primary metric creates an inconsistency.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Model selection on validation macro-F1 is correct and consistent with stakeholder priorities. Reporting micro-F1 as a secondary metric adds useful context about aggregate performance but does not override the alignment of model selection and primary evaluation on macro-F1 — which is the actual flaw here, since the corrupted design incorrectly elevates micro-F1 to primary status despite using macro-F1 for selection.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that micro-F1 is an inappropriate primary metric given the hypothesis's focus on clinically important, lower-frequency codes. The defender fails to justify this choice in operational terms and concedes that macro-F1 should be primary. The final verdict concludes that the design must be revised to use macro-F1 as the primary evaluation metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains how micro-F1 masks poor performance on less frequent codes due to its averaging mechanism, and references the stakeholder's success metric context that prioritizes balanced performance across codes.

difficulty_justification: This case is subtly difficult because the corruption involves a single metric swap that appears reasonable on the surface (micro-F1 is common in multi-label tasks), but misaligns with the stated operational objective. Evaluators must integrate domain context about clinical coding priorities to detect the flaw, rather than relying on technical checklist compliance.

hypothesis: For next-day urban parcel volume forecasting at depot level, a temporal fusion transformer trained on depot history, shipment booking lead-time distributions, weather, and local event calendars will reduce weighted mean absolute percentage error more than a seasonal gradient-boosted tree baseline built on hand-engineered lag and calendar features.
domain: Transportation and logistics — last-mile parcel network planning for city depots at a national delivery carrier
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. Seasonal GBT for Next-Day Depot Volume Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of daily operational records from a national parcel carrier covering approximately 300 city depots. The label — next-day realized parcel volume — is defined as the end-of-day scan count at each depot for day D+1, observed strictly after the forecast cutoff. This source is authoritative and operationally grounded. Three feature groups are constructed from the carrier's own systems: depot volume history and booking lead-time distributions (from booking timestamps and promised delivery dates), plus externally joined weather forecasts, public/school holiday calendars, and major local event flags matched to depot location and date.

A critical data quality step filters depots with more than 30 consecutive missing scan days and investigates shorter gaps before imputation. All booking-derived features are constructed using only data with timestamps at or before end of day D, strictly enforcing the forecast cutoff boundary and preventing lead-time leakage — one of the two primary design challenges for this domain.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 as the held-out test set. This is non-negotiable for time series forecasting with serial autocorrelation and holiday seasonality. Random or cross-sectional splits would allow the model to learn from future volume patterns during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic evaluation results. The test set is deliberately placed to include at least one full holiday peak season, directly addressing the operational requirement that improvements matter most during demand spikes. Depots that opened after month 18 enter training after a 6-month burn-in to exclude noisy launch-period data, but are retained in the test set to evaluate generalization to newer infrastructure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refit to validation and test sets. This prevents any distributional information from the evaluation periods from contaminating the preprocessing pipeline. Lag and rolling features respect the forecast cutoff: no scan or booking information from D+1 or later is used in any feature. The booking lead-time distribution features — particularly the count of already-committed D+1 bookings — are among the most informative signals but are also the highest leakage risk; the design addresses this by bounding all booking queries to timestamps ≤ end of day D.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer configured with a 28-day lookback window, variable selection networks over the three feature groups, LSTM encoding, multi-head attention, and quantile output heads (0.1, 0.5, 0.9). All depots are pooled into a single global model with depot identity as a static covariate.

The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on exactly the same feature set — all three feature groups plus hand-engineered seasonal lags (7-day and 52-week) — also trained globally with depot identity as a categorical feature. Both models receive identical Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budgets (50 Optuna trials, minimizing validation WMAPE). This parity is essential: the hypothesis claims TFT's sequence modeling adds value, and the baseline must be well-tuned on matched information to make that comparison credible.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), computed by averaging percentage error equally across depot-days. This keeps the evaluation scale-invariant across depots and makes results easy to interpret as an average relative forecasting error. Secondary metrics include: tier-stratified WMAPE (top 20%, middle 40%, bottom 40% depots by volume); holiday-period WMAPE isolating the operationally critical spike windows; the under-forecast rate (proportion of depot-days with >15% underprediction, a direct proxy for staffing shortfalls); and pinball loss at the 0.1 and 0.9 quantiles to evaluate uncertainty calibration for operational planning.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use validation set performance only. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are frozen. No iterative refinement occurs based on test set results. An ablation study training the TFT on individual depots (no pooling) runs on the validation set to decompose the contribution of cross-depot knowledge transfer from sequence modeling — this directly addresses the known confound that pooling architecture, not attention, might drive any observed gain.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, feature information asymmetry: the baseline receives the full feature set including the booking lead-time distribution and exogenous signals, so TFT cannot gain credit for richer inputs. Second, cross-depot pooling: both models pool globally, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Third, non-stationarity and new depots: performance is reported separately for stable long-running depots vs. depots that opened mid-period, and residuals are audited for correlation with region, city size, and service area change events. The experiment establishes whether a feature-matched TFT outperforms a well-tuned seasonal GBT on next-day depot volume forecasting. It does not claim TFT is the optimal sequence model, that this feature set is complete, or that results generalize to carrier networks with materially different operational structures.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is specified as Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), which averages percentage errors equally across all depot-days regardless of volume. This misaligns with the operational objective: forecasting errors at high-volume depots have significantly greater impact on staffing and resource allocation. The correct metric should be Weighted MAPE (WMAPE = Σ|ŷ−y| / Σy), which assigns higher weight to high-volume depots and reflects real-world cost sensitivity.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of tier-stratified WMAPE as a secondary metric suggests a concern about volume imbalance, so the unweighted MAPE primary metric must be flawed.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While tier-stratified WMAPE provides useful breakdowns by depot volume, it does not resolve the core issue that the primary decision-making metric must inherently reflect operational cost structure. A well-designed evaluation can include stratified metrics without making them primary; the flaw is not the stratification, but the choice of an unweighted primary metric that contradicts the business objective.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the use of unweighted MAPE as the primary metric contradicts the operational priority of minimizing costly errors at high-volume depots. The defender cannot plausibly justify this choice given the stated success metric context. The resolution concludes that the primary metric must be WMAPE to align with business impact.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references the hypothesis's success metric context, which explicitly prioritizes error reduction at high-volume depots, and explains how unweighted MAPE allows poor performance on large depots to be masked by strong performance on small ones.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. Seasonal GBT for Next-Day Depot Volume Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of daily operational records from a national parcel carrier covering approximately 300 city depots. The label — next-day realized parcel volume — is defined as the end-of-day scan count at each depot for day D+1, observed strictly after the forecast cutoff. This source is authoritative and operationally grounded. Three feature groups are constructed from the carrier's own systems: depot volume history and booking lead-time distributions (from booking timestamps and promised delivery dates), plus externally joined weather forecasts, public/school holiday calendars, and major local event flags matched to depot location and date.

A critical data quality step filters depots with more than 30 consecutive missing scan days and investigates shorter gaps before imputation. All booking-derived features are constructed using only data with timestamps at or before end of day D, strictly enforcing the forecast cutoff boundary and preventing lead-time leakage — one of the two primary design challenges for this domain.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 as the held-out test set. This is non-negotiable for time series forecasting with serial autocorrelation and holiday seasonality. Random or cross-sectional splits would allow the model to learn from future volume patterns during training, producing optimistic and unrealistic evaluation results. The test set is deliberately placed to include at least one full holiday peak season, directly addressing the operational requirement that improvements matter most during demand spikes. Depots that opened after month 18 enter training after a 6-month burn-in to exclude noisy launch-period data, but are retained in the test set to evaluate generalization to newer infrastructure.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refit to validation and test sets. This prevents any distributional information from the evaluation periods from contaminating the preprocessing pipeline. Lag and rolling features respect the forecast cutoff: no scan or booking information from D+1 or later is used in any feature. The booking lead-time distribution features — particularly the count of already-committed D+1 bookings — are among the most informative signals but are also the highest leakage risk; the design addresses this by bounding all booking queries to timestamps ≤ end of day D.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer configured with a 28-day lookback window, variable selection networks over the three feature groups, LSTM encoding, multi-head attention, and quantile output heads (0.1, 0.5, 0.9). All depots are pooled into a single global model with depot identity as a static covariate.

The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on exactly the same feature set — all three feature groups plus hand-engineered seasonal lags (7-day and 52-week) — also trained globally with depot identity as a categorical feature. Both models receive identical Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budgets (50 Optuna trials, minimizing validation WMAPE). This parity is essential: the hypothesis claims TFT's sequence modeling adds value, and the baseline must be well-tuned on matched information to make that comparison credible.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Weighted Mean Absolute Percentage Error (WMAPE = Σ|ŷ - y| / Σy), which weights errors by realized volume rather than treating all depot-days equally. This directly aligns with the operational objective: errors at high-volume depots are more costly and must dominate the evaluation score. Secondary metrics include: tier-stratified WMAPE (top 20%, middle 40%, bottom 40% depots by volume); holiday-period WMAPE isolating the operationally critical spike windows; the under-forecast rate (proportion of depot-days with >15% underprediction, a direct proxy for staffing shortfalls); and pinball loss at the 0.1 and 0.9 quantiles to evaluate uncertainty calibration for operational planning.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use validation set performance only. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all choices are frozen. No iterative refinement occurs based on test set results. An ablation study training the TFT on individual depots (no pooling) runs on the validation set to decompose the contribution of cross-depot knowledge transfer from sequence modeling — this directly addresses the known confound that pooling architecture, not attention, might drive any observed gain.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, feature information asymmetry: the baseline receives the full feature set including the booking lead-time distribution and exogenous signals, so TFT cannot gain credit for richer inputs. Second, cross-depot pooling: both models pool globally, isolating sequence modeling as the independent variable. Third, non-stationarity and new depots: performance is reported separately for stable long-running depots vs. depots that opened mid-period, and residuals are audited for correlation with region, city size, and service area change events. The experiment establishes whether a feature-matched TFT outperforms a well-tuned seasonal GBT on next-day depot volume forecasting. It does not claim TFT is the optimal sequence model, that this feature set is complete, or that results generalize to carrier networks with materially different operational structures.
difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because MAPE is a common and plausible metric; detecting the flaw requires recognizing that operational impact—not uniform averaging—drives metric selection. A superficial reviewer might miss this misalignment without deep engagement with the success metric context.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network built on account-device-IP-beneficiary transaction graphs predicts 14-day mule-account involvement more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model using aggregated account-level transaction features, when evaluated on future months of retail bank transfer activity.
domain: Retail banking AML operations for detecting money mule accounts involved in illicit fund movement across online transfers and peer-to-peer payments
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TGNN vs. GBT for 14-Day Mule Account Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 20 months of timestamped retail bank transfer logs, sourced from the bank's core transaction warehouse. Raw signals include transaction amounts, channels, device fingerprints, IP addresses, beneficiary identifiers, geolocation, and account KYC attributes. The prediction task is defined at the account-month level: for each account active in a given month, predict whether it will receive a confirmed mule label within the following 14 days.

Labels are constructed by merging SAR filings (mule typology codes), compliance-driven account closures, and AML case management outcomes. Label delay is handled strictly: only outcomes with a recorded resolution timestamp before the evaluation window are used. Accounts with open, unresolved investigations at evaluation time are excluded to prevent censored-label contamination. The expected positive rate is 0.1–0.5%, so class imbalance handling is a first-class concern throughout.

### 2. Temporal Split

The data is divided into three non-overlapping temporal windows: training (months 1–14), validation (months 15–17), and test (months 18–20). A temporal split is mandatory here — random or stratified splits would allow the graph model to observe future network topology during training, directly enabling edge-leakage. The hypothesis explicitly requires evaluation on future months, so temporal ordering must be enforced end-to-end. Graph construction for each partition uses only transaction edges observed strictly before that partition's prediction cutoff date.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed from identical underlying raw signals. For the GBT baseline, we compute aggregated account-level tabular features: rolling transaction statistics (7/30/90-day windows), counterparty and device diversity metrics, velocity of new beneficiaries, KYC attributes, and ratio features. For the TGNN, the same raw signals populate node and edge attributes on a dynamic transaction graph, where nodes are accounts, devices, IPs, and beneficiaries, and directed edges carry amount, channel, and geolocation as features. Feature symmetry is non-negotiable: both models see the same underlying information, ensuring any observed performance gap is attributable to representational structure rather than feature access.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, target encoding) are fit on the training partition only and applied without refitting to validation and test. This boundary is enforced in the pipeline implementation, not assumed.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a memory-based Temporal Graph Neural Network (TGN architecture), with per-node memory updated via GRUs, temporal attention aggregation over the k=20 most recent neighborhood interactions, and a binary classification head on account node embeddings. The baseline is a LightGBM classifier trained on the tabular feature set. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: 100-trial Bayesian hyperparameter search (Optuna) optimizing validation PR-AUC. The GBT is the correct baseline because it represents current industry-standard AML practice and directly tests whether relational graph structure adds value beyond hand-engineered aggregations.

A third ablation — a static GNN without temporal dynamics — is trained to isolate whether any gain comes from graph structure specifically or from the temporal modeling component.

### 5. Evaluation

The primary metric is ROC-AUC on the held-out period, because it provides a threshold-independent view of discrimination and is widely comparable across AML modeling studies. PR-AUC is reported as a secondary metric alongside Precision at fixed alert volume and Precision@K. Secondary metrics also include Brier score for calibration and false positive rate among low-KYC-risk accounts as a fairness check.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Policy

All hyperparameter tuning uses validation-set PR-AUC only. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all tuning and selection decisions are final. Test results for the TGNN, GBT, and static GNN ablation are reported simultaneously.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds receive explicit controls. First, investigation-network artifacts: analyst-linked accounts from case management systems are excluded from graph edges; only organic transaction-derived edges are used. Second, censored labels: unresolved investigations are excluded from evaluation partitions; sensitivity analyses test stability under different label-inclusion thresholds. Third, feature parity: TGNN node/edge attributes are strictly derived from the same signals as the GBT features — no additional data sources are introduced.

This design establishes whether the TGNN's relational-temporal representation of the transaction graph improves mule detection recall at fixed operational capacity, over and above what tabular aggregation with equivalent feature access achieves. It does not establish generalizability across banks, regulatory regimes, or mule typologies not present in the label set.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is set as ROC-AUC, which is misleadingly optimistic in extremely imbalanced settings typical of AML detection (0.1–0.5% positive rate). ROC-AUC gives disproportionate weight to easy-to-classify majority-class examples and can show strong performance even when the model fails in the top-ranked fraction of accounts that determine actionable alerts. This misaligns with the operational goal of maximizing mule detection under a fixed reviewer capacity constraint, requiring evaluation at controlled false positive rates or fixed alert volumes.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of PR-AUC for model selection during validation constitutes a leakage because it uses test-like data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Using PR-AUC on the validation set for hyperparameter tuning is methodologically sound and standard practice. The validation set is properly temporally separated and used only for model selection, with no influence on training, and the test set remains untouched until final evaluation. PR-AUC is robust under class imbalance and serves as a stable proxy for performance in the critical top-ranked region, making it appropriate for tuning even when the final deployment metric differs.

must_not_claim.claim: Evaluating both models on the same test set introduces unfair contamination or leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Evaluating multiple models on the same held-out test set is not only acceptable but required for direct, fair comparison. So long as the test set is untouched during training and tuning — which the design enforces — simultaneous evaluation is a best practice. The temporal split and preprocessing isolation ensure no leakage occurs between the evaluations of the TGNN and GBT.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that ROC-AUC is an inappropriate primary metric due to extreme class imbalance and misalignment with operational capacity constraints, and successfully argues that the evaluation should prioritize recall or precision within a fixed alert volume (e.g., top 0.5% of accounts).
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument emphasizes that ROC-AUC is insensitive to performance degradation in the high-precision region critical for alerts, and cites literature on AUC pitfalls in imbalanced settings (e.g., Saito & Rehmsmeier 2015).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concedes ROC-AUC might be reported but insists that the fixed-capacity recall metric (e.g., Recall@0.5%) must be the primary decision metric, and demonstrates that models can rank differently under ROC vs. fixed-volume metrics in imbalanced regimes.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Provides a simulated or reference example where two models have identical ROC-AUC but vastly different recall at top-K, due to differing precision in the critical region.

difficulty_justification: The corruption is moderately detectable because ROC-AUC is commonly reported in literature and may appear defensible at first glance. However, correctly identifying it as a flaw requires understanding the interaction between extreme class imbalance, stakeholder objectives, and metric deficiencies — a nuanced evaluation challenge that separates domain-informed reviewers from pattern-matching ones.

hypothesis: In hospital clinical coding operations, a domain-adapted transformer initialized from biomedical text and fine-tuned on discharge summaries will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on lower-frequency codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for inpatient hospital coding, where coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Assignment

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use MIMIC-IV (or an equivalent de-identified inpatient EHR dataset) covering 50,000–300,000 encounters across a 4–5 year window following adoption of ICD-10-CM, ensuring temporal stability of the coding system. The primary input is the discharge summary text per encounter; the label set is the final billing-approved ICD-10-CM diagnosis code set assigned by credentialed coders — the operational ground truth.

Pre-processing filters exclude: encounters with no assigned codes, encounters flagged as coding-incomplete, and exact duplicate notes. Known quality issues such as copy-forward boilerplate and templated sections are retained as realistic signal; they are a feature of the deployment target, not noise to be removed. The full ICD-10 vocabulary is preserved, but codes with fewer than 10 training-set occurrences are flagged and excluded from primary metric computation (included in a sensitivity analysis).

The label space is stratified post-hoc into three frequency tiers — high (≥1% of encounters), mid (0.1–1%), and low (<0.1%) — to directly evaluate the hypothesis that the transformer advantage concentrates on rare codes.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are sorted by discharge date and split **temporally**: 70% training, 15% validation, 15% test. Within this temporal ordering, a **group-based deduplication** rule ensures all encounters for any single patient are confined to their earliest-appearing partition. This prevents patient-level leakage from readmissions whose notes may copy prior summaries.

The temporal split is essential here: a random split would allow future coding practices to bleed into the training window, and would permit test encounters from the same patient to share text with training encounters. The deployment target is strictly prospective — the model will always be applied to encounters that postdate its training data.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: raw discharge summary text. For the **transformer**, the pretrained tokenizer is frozen; no vocabulary statistics are estimated from training data. For notes exceeding 512 tokens, a sliding-window strategy produces overlapping chunks whose [CLS] representations are mean-pooled before the classification head.

For the **TF-IDF baseline**, character n-grams (1–3) plus word unigrams and bigrams are extracted, with sublinear TF scaling, L2 normalization, and a vocabulary cap of 150,000 features. To ensure a stable vocabulary across time, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are computed once over the full corpus and then applied uniformly to train, validation, and test splits.

Label binarizers are also fit on training labels only. Labels appearing in validation or test but not in training are recorded but excluded from metric computation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is **PubMedBERT** (or ClinicalBERT), fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head and inverse-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss (weight capped to prevent pathological upweighting of ultra-rare codes).

The baseline is **one-vs-rest logistic regression on TF-IDF features**, with L2 regularization strength tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} by validation macro-F1. Both models undergo per-label decision threshold optimization on the validation set using F1-maximizing threshold search — identical tuning procedures ensure the comparison is fair.

The TF-IDF + LR baseline is the correct comparison: it represents the dominant production-level non-neural approach in clinical coding support, is computationally tractable at scale, and has no architectural inductive bias toward clinical language — making any gap attributable to the transformer's domain-adapted representations.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is macro-averaged F1** across all codes meeting the minimum frequency threshold. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, preventing high-frequency codes from masking poor performance on rare but billable diagnoses — directly aligned with stakeholder concern about coding completeness and specificity.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (volume-weighted aggregate), recall at precision ≥ 0.70 (mimicking real coder workflow constraints), Recall@K where K equals the mean ground-truth code count (retrieval completeness at realistic burden), and per-tier AUC-ROC for threshold-independent ranking quality. Calibration (ECE per tier) is also reported because downstream workflow tools may use model scores for code prioritization.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions — learning rate, batch size, fine-tuning epochs, weight decay, loss weight cap, per-label thresholds — are made exclusively on the **validation set using macro-F1**. Early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs. The TF-IDF baseline's regularization is selected by the same criterion.

The **test set is used exactly once**, after all selection decisions are finalized. No iterative test-set probing is permitted. Stratified breakdowns (frequency tier, service line, note length quartile) are computed in a single evaluation pass.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit treatment:

- **Note length**: We regress note length against per-encounter model performance and report performance stratified by note length quartile to detect whether the transformer's advantage is an artifact of handling long notes better rather than understanding clinical language.
- **Service line / template effects**: Performance is broken out by inpatient service line (medicine, surgery, ICU, OB/GYN, etc.) to determine whether gains concentrate in template-heavy units.
- **Repeated patients**: Structurally addressed by the group-based temporal split.

A diagnostic ablation replaces discharge summary content with only structural boilerplate (headers, fixed phrases, no clinical content) to estimate the upper bound of template-driven performance for each model.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer outperforms TF-IDF + LR on macro-F1 in this dataset and whether the advantage is larger on rare codes. It would **not** establish generalizability to external hospital systems, real-time latency suitability, or superiority across all ICD-10 versions or coding policy regimes.

planted_issues.description: The TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are computed on the full corpus (including validation and test data) before being applied to each split. This introduces information leakage because rare or emerging terms in the held-out data influence the feature representation used during training. In a temporal clinical setting, this allows future language patterns to affect the model's feature construction, artificially inflating performance and invalidating the prospective evaluation setup.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is insufficiently balanced and should be replaced with a random split to ensure better distributional alignment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A random split would allow future coding practices and notes to leak into the training set, violating the prospective deployment scenario. The temporal split is methodologically correct here because the model must be evaluated on its ability to generalize to future encounters without access to future data.

must_not_claim.claim: Retaining templated and boilerplate text introduces noise and should be cleaned prior to modeling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Copy-forward content and templates are a realistic feature of clinical notes in production environments. Removing them would create an artificially clean evaluation that does not reflect real-world performance; the design correctly preserves this realism.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that fitting TF-IDF on the full corpus introduces preprocessing leakage by allowing validation and test document statistics to influence training features. The defender fails to rebut this, as no legitimate justification exists for this practice in a prospective temporal setup. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that IDF scores and vocabulary inclusion depend on document frequency across all data, meaning future documents affect training representations — which invalidates the temporal split's intent.

difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because computing TF-IDF on the full corpus may appear reasonable for 'vocabulary stability,' but in a temporal setting it constitutes a serious leakage. Evaluators must recognize that even feature statistics must be derived solely from past data to preserve prospective validity.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network built from wallet-to-wallet transaction networks and smart-contract interactions detects illicit cryptocurrency addresses more accurately on next-month activity than a feature-engineered XGBoost baseline trained on the same historical blockchain window.
domain: Cryptocurrency anti-money-laundering monitoring for a blockchain analytics provider supporting exchange compliance investigations
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. Feature-Engineered XGBoost for Illicit Address Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of archival on-chain data from a single public blockchain (e.g., Ethereum mainnet), covering timestamped transactions, token transfers, smart-contract call traces, gas fees, and transaction amounts. Address cluster mappings — grouping multiple addresses belonging to the same real-world entity using co-spend heuristics and exchange deposit clustering — are computed once over the full dataset before any partitioning occurs. This pre-split clustering is necessary to correctly enforce entity-level split boundaries.

Labels are assembled from a curated union of OFAC/sanctions records, exchange-reported AML accounts, third-party attribution vendor exports (e.g., Chainalysis, Elliptic), and confirmed law enforcement seizure records. Critically, labels are assigned with their original attribution timestamp, not backdated. An address is considered labeled only from the date its attribution record was created. Addresses with no confirmed attribution — in either direction — are excluded from training and evaluation entirely, preventing unlabeled positives from contaminating the licit class. The label cutoff for training and validation is end of month 18; labels first observed in months 19–24 are reserved for test evaluation only.

### 2. Split Strategy

All splits are temporal and enforced at the entity cluster level. Training covers months 1–15, validation covers months 16–18, and the held-out test set covers months 19–24. Each cluster is assigned to exactly one partition based on the first observed activity timestamp of any address in that cluster. This dual constraint — temporal ordering plus cluster-level assignment — eliminates both label leakage (future enforcement actions informing training) and entity leakage (different addresses of the same actor appearing on both sides of a split boundary). A random or stratified-random split would be inappropriate here because the hypothesis concerns forward-in-time generalization under temporal distribution shift.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed, with per-entity features aggregated over each entity's full observed activity history in the dataset to provide the most stable behavioral profile before classification:

- **Non-relational tabular features:** transaction count, total sent/received volume, unique counterparty count, fan-in/fan-out ratio, average transaction size, interarrival time statistics (mean, std, entropy), fraction of smart-contract interactions, gas fee statistics, token diversity index.
- **Relational neighbor features (2-hop aggregates):** mean and standard deviation of degree, volume, and activity frequency among direct and second-degree neighbors in the transaction graph.

The XGBoost primary baseline receives both feature sets. A second XGBoost ablation baseline receives only non-relational features. The TGNN receives the raw temporal edge stream plus the non-relational features as initial node embeddings. All scalers, encoders, and imputation statistics are fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Attention Network (TGN; Rossi et al. 2020) with memory modules and time-encoding, trained on the temporal edge stream. The **primary baseline** is XGBoost trained on the matched full feature set including relational neighbor features. This is the critical fairness condition: the baseline must have access to equivalent relational information to avoid conflating architectural advantage with information advantage. A second **ablation baseline** — XGBoost without neighbor features — isolates the marginal value of relational signals from the marginal value of the TGNN architecture.

Both the TGNN and both baselines are tuned via random search over 40 configurations each, evaluated exclusively on the validation set using AUPRC. This ensures equivalent tuning effort across all models.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the test set. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because illicit addresses represent a severe minority class (estimated <0.1% of labeled addresses), and AUROC is dominated by true-negative performance in this regime. AUPRC directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that maps to the operational objective: finding more illicit addresses while keeping analyst review volume manageable.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@500 and Precision@1000** (matching realistic analyst queue sizes), recall at fixed precision thresholds of 0.50 and 0.75, AUROC for literature comparability, and calibration metrics (Brier score, reliability diagram). All metrics are reported separately for newly active addresses (first seen in the test window) vs. addresses active during training — the hypothesis specifically claims improvement on newly active entities.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, and threshold decisions are made on the validation set (months 16–18) using AUPRC. The test set is loaded and evaluated exactly once, after all models are frozen. No adjustments are made after test set inspection. This policy is enforced by keeping test labels in a separate, access-controlled file.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the **relational information confound**: any TGNN advantage could stem from access to richer implicit neighborhood aggregation rather than the temporal graph architecture. This is controlled by giving the XGBoost baseline matched 2-hop relational features and using the no-relational-features ablation to disentangle the two contributions. Second, **entity leakage**: cluster-level splits ensure no real-world actor's addresses span the train/test boundary. Third, **temporal distribution shift**: performance is reported separately for early (months 19–21) and late (months 22–24) test windows, and any major enforcement events during the test period are logged as potential confounding label density shifts.

This experiment would establish whether a temporal GNN outperforms a well-resourced, relationally-informed XGBoost baseline on forward-in-time illicit address detection. It would not establish which specific graph structural properties drive any observed improvement, nor would it support generalization claims to other blockchains or address categories not represented in the evaluation data.

planted_issues.description: The feature engineering step aggregates per-entity features over each entity's full observed history in the dataset, which includes future activity (e.g., months 16–24) when constructing features for entities active earlier. This introduces post-prediction time information into the feature set, transforming the task from forward-in-time detection into a retrospective profiling problem. As a result, the evaluation no longer tests the model's ability to detect illicit behavior prospectively using only past information, violating the core hypothesis.

must_not_claim.claim: Address cluster mappings are computed over the full dataset before splitting, which could allow label leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Pre-split clustering using only structural heuristics (co-spend, exchange deposits) without label information is necessary to enforce entity-level splits correctly and does not introduce leakage. The clusters are derived solely from topological patterns, not labels, so this is a sound design choice in entity-level temporal studies.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of AUPRC instead of AUROC is inappropriate because AUROC is more standard in literature.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the preferred metric in extreme class imbalance scenarios (<0.1% positives) where true negatives dominate AUROC. The operational goal is to improve precision and recall among top-ranked alerts, making AUPRC a more meaningful and stakeholder-aligned metric than AUROC.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A correct debate outcome requires the critic to identify that feature aggregation over the full entity history introduces future information, turning the task into a retrospective rather than prospective one. The defender must concede that this invalidates the forward-in-time evaluation and amend the feature aggregation to be restricted to the training window (months 1–15) for all entities.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that features for entities active in months 1–15 are computed using data from months 16–24, violating temporal validity and enabling the model to exploit future knowledge not available at the time of prediction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Alternatively, the critic may accept that cluster-level preprocessing is valid if they challenge only the aggregation window, provided they still require that features be derived only from information available up to the split boundary for each entity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic acknowledges that pre-computing clusters is acceptable but insists that feature values must be computed using only past and contemporaneous data relative to the prediction time, not future activity.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. Feature-Engineered XGBoost for Illicit Address Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of archival on-chain data from a single public blockchain (e.g., Ethereum mainnet), covering timestamped transactions, token transfers, smart-contract call traces, gas fees, and transaction amounts. Address cluster mappings — grouping multiple addresses belonging to the same real-world entity using co-spend heuristics and exchange deposit clustering — are computed once over the full dataset before any partitioning occurs. This pre-split clustering is necessary to correctly enforce entity-level split boundaries.

Labels are assembled from a curated union of OFAC/sanctions records, exchange-reported AML accounts, third-party attribution vendor exports (e.g., Chainalysis, Elliptic), and confirmed law enforcement seizure records. Critically, labels are assigned with their original attribution timestamp, not backdated. An address is considered labeled only from the date its attribution record was created. Addresses with no confirmed attribution — in either direction — are excluded from training and evaluation entirely, preventing unlabeled positives from contaminating the licit class. The label cutoff for training and validation is end of month 18; labels first observed in months 19–24 are reserved for test evaluation only.

### 2. Split Strategy

All splits are temporal and enforced at the entity cluster level. Training covers months 1–15, validation covers months 16–18, and the held-out test set covers months 19–24. Each cluster is assigned to exactly one partition based on the first observed activity timestamp of any address in that cluster. This dual constraint — temporal ordering plus cluster-level assignment — eliminates both label leakage (future enforcement actions informing training) and entity leakage (different addresses of the same actor appearing on both sides of a split boundary). A random or stratified-random split would be inappropriate here because the hypothesis concerns forward-in-time generalization under temporal distribution shift.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed, both aggregated over the training window for their respective entities:

- **Non-relational tabular features:** transaction count, total sent/received volume, unique counterparty count, fan-in/fan-out ratio, average transaction size, interarrival time statistics (mean, std, entropy), fraction of smart-contract interactions, gas fee statistics, token diversity index.
- **Relational neighbor features (2-hop aggregates):** mean and standard deviation of degree, volume, and activity frequency among direct and second-degree neighbors in the transaction graph.

The XGBoost primary baseline receives both feature sets. A second XGBoost ablation baseline receives only non-relational features. The TGNN receives the raw temporal edge stream plus the non-relational features as initial node embeddings. All scalers, encoders, and imputation statistics are fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Attention Network (TGN; Rossi et al. 2020) with memory modules and time-encoding, trained on the temporal edge stream. The **primary baseline** is XGBoost trained on the matched full feature set including relational neighbor features. This is the critical fairness condition: the baseline must have access to equivalent relational information to avoid conflating architectural advantage with information advantage. A second **ablation baseline** — XGBoost without neighbor features — isolates the marginal value of relational signals from the marginal value of the TGNN architecture.

Both the TGNN and both baselines are tuned via random search over 40 configurations each, evaluated exclusively on the validation set using AUPRC. This ensures equivalent tuning effort across all models.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the test set. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because illicit addresses represent a severe minority class (estimated <0.1% of labeled addresses), and AUROC is dominated by true-negative performance in this regime. AUPRC directly reflects the precision-recall tradeoff that maps to the operational objective: finding more illicit addresses while keeping analyst review volume manageable.

Secondary metrics include **Precision@500 and Precision@1000** (matching realistic analyst queue sizes), recall at fixed precision thresholds of 0.50 and 0.75, AUROC for literature comparability, and calibration metrics (Brier score, reliability diagram). All metrics are reported separately for newly active addresses (first seen in the test window) vs. addresses active during training — the hypothesis specifically claims improvement on newly active entities.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, and threshold decisions are made on the validation set (months 16–18) using AUPRC. The test set is loaded and evaluated exactly once, after all models are frozen. No adjustments are made after test set inspection. This policy is enforced by keeping test labels in a separate, access-controlled file.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the **relational information confound**: any TGNN advantage could stem from access to richer implicit neighborhood aggregation rather than the temporal graph architecture. This is controlled by giving the XGBoost baseline matched 2-hop relational features and using the no-relational-features ablation to disentangle the two contributions. Second, **entity leakage**: cluster-level splits ensure no real-world actor's addresses span the train/test boundary. Third, **temporal distribution shift**: performance is reported separately for early (months 19–21) and late (months 22–24) test windows, and any major enforcement events during the test period are logged as potential confounding label density shifts.

This experiment would establish whether a temporal GNN outperforms a well-resourced, relationally-informed XGBoost baseline on forward-in-time illicit address detection. It would not establish which specific graph structural properties drive any observed improvement, nor would it support generalization claims to other blockchains or address categories not represented in the evaluation data.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted text sounds methodologically reasonable — using 'full history' for 'stable profiling' — but violates temporal validity. Evaluators must recognize that this undermines the prospective nature of the evaluation, despite otherwise sound controls for leakage and class imbalance.

hypothesis: In inpatient hospital coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro- and macro-F1 for predicting the set of billed ICD-10 diagnosis codes at discharge than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline built on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation, where hospitals assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from inpatient stay notes for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The primary dataset is MIMIC-IV, restricted to admissions with discharge dates after the US ICD-10-CM transition (October 2015), yielding approximately 40,000–50,000 de-identified inpatient encounters. Each encounter provides one or more discharge summaries and a series of daily progress notes from the EHR, along with a final set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes extracted from the billing record. This code set constitutes the multi-label target — no codes are suppressed for frequency. A secondary cohort from a separate health system (accessed under a DUA) is reserved as an out-of-distribution generalization check and is not used in any training or model selection decision.

Known data quality considerations: a small fraction of MIMIC-IV encounters have incomplete note timelines or are missing discharge summaries; these are excluded with the exclusion rate reported. Duplicate or amended notes are deduplicated by keeping the latest version per note type per encounter day.

### 2. Split Strategy

Admissions are sorted by discharge date and partitioned chronologically: the earliest 70% of encounters form the training set, the next 15% form the validation set, and the final 15% form the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because the deployment scenario involves applying a model trained on historical admissions to future ones. Random splitting would allow future coding conventions, updated billing guidelines, and diagnosis trends to leak into training — creating an optimistic bias that does not reflect real-world performance. Patient-level integrity is additionally enforced: all admissions for a given patient are assigned to a single partition so that repeated-admission patients do not contribute chronic-condition label signal across splits.

### 3. Input Construction and Feature Engineering

For both models, the input text is constructed identically by concatenating the discharge summary and all chronological progress notes for the encounter, separated by a special section delimiter. To isolate the confound introduced by discharge summaries (which may explicitly enumerate finalized diagnoses), all experiments are run under three text conditions: (a) discharge summary only, (b) progress notes only, and (c) full concatenation. This ablation is applied to both models equally.

For the transformer (Clinical-Longformer), the concatenated text is tokenized and truncated to 4096 tokens using a head+tail strategy (first 2048 + last 2048 tokens) to preserve both admission context and discharge framing without arbitrary mid-document cutoff. For the TF-IDF baseline, the same raw concatenated text is vectorized with unigram+bigram features, min_df=2, and L2 normalization. All preprocessing decisions — IDF weights, label binarizer mappings, code-frequency tier boundaries — are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied to validation and test without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is Clinical-Longformer fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head and binary cross-entropy loss, using frequency-weighted loss to temper class imbalance. Training uses AdamW with linear warmup and cosine decay; early stopping is triggered on validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest linear SVM. The SVM regularization parameter C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} on the validation set using the same primary metric. The baseline is given the same input text, the same label space, and equivalent hyperparameter search effort. This is the appropriate comparison: it is the strongest classical NLP approach for this task and ensures that any observed gap is attributable to the modeling approach, not data asymmetry or differential tuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged AUC-ROC over labels, reported on the held-out test set. AUC-ROC is used because it is threshold-independent and provides a stable global comparison across highly imbalanced labels. Micro-F1 and macro-F1 are reported as secondary metrics, both computed at a fixed decision threshold of 0.5 and also at the validation-optimal threshold (reported separately). Secondary metrics also include Recall@K and Precision@K (K=10, K=25) to reflect the coder-review workflow and expected calibration error. Critically, all metrics are reported both globally and stratified by code-frequency tier (very rare, rare, common, frequent), so that it is transparent whether transformer gains extend to the clinically important rare codes or are concentrated in frequent chronic-condition codes.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All hyperparameter decisions and model checkpoint selection are made on the validation set using macro-F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all selection is complete, via a single locked evaluation script. No retraining or threshold adjustment follows test-set inspection.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly controlled: (1) discharge-summary boilerplate is isolated via the three-condition text ablation; (2) note-length effects are assessed by regressing note length on score residuals; (3) specialty mix is controlled by reporting stratified metrics per primary service line; (4) temporal drift is assessed by plotting annual performance within the test window. The experiment establishes whether, under controlled text-equivalent conditions on MIMIC-IV, the clinical transformer outperforms the TF-IDF baseline. It does not establish which specific linguistic capabilities are responsible, nor does it generalize beyond US acute inpatient settings — the out-of-distribution cohort check addresses generalizability as an exploratory secondary analysis.

planted_issues.description: The experiment design elevates macro-averaged AUC-ROC to the status of primary metric, but AUC-ROC is threshold-agnostic and does not reflect actionable model performance in a real-world deployment where coders need high-quality, thresholded suggestions. In a reimbursement-sensitive setting with highly imbalanced labels, optimizing for AUC can allow models to score well by ranking rare labels marginally better while failing at making calibrated, high-precision predictions. The primary metric should be micro-F1 or macro-F1, which directly measure retrieval quality at operational thresholds, especially for rare but clinically important codes.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of macro-F1 as the model selection criterion on the validation set invalidates temporal integrity because the test set uses future data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal integrity is preserved because model selection is done solely on the validation set, which precedes the test set chronologically. Using macro-F1 — a performance metric — for early stopping and checkpoint selection does not introduce leakage as long as the model does not observe test-set labels or predictions.

must_not_claim.claim: Reporting AUC-ROC as a secondary metric is inappropriate because it is threshold-independent and less aligned with the use case.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUC-ROC is a valid secondary metric for assessing overall ranking quality and model calibration across thresholds; the flaw lies only in its promotion to primary status, not in its reporting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that AUC-ROC is a poor choice for the primary metric in this operational context, because stakeholder impact depends on thresholded retrieval quality (e.g., F1 at 0.5) rather than ranking alone. The defender fails to justify why AUC-ROC should supersede F1 metrics for final evaluation, especially given the hypothesis specifically prioritizes micro- and macro-F1.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that AUC-ROC does not reflect post-deployment performance where coders act on a ranked list of model-suggested codes above a fixed threshold, and that AUC can be misleading under extreme label imbalance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges AUC-ROC provides useful secondary insight but insists the primary metric must be micro-F1 or macro-F1 to align with the hypothesis and stakeholder needs. The final verdict requires downgrading AUC-ROC to secondary status and elevating F1 to primary in evaluation reporting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that the hypothesis explicitly claims superiority in F1, not AUC, making consistency with the evaluation design a methodological necessity.
