difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because AUC-ROC is a commonly used and technically valid metric; detecting the mismatch requires domain-specific understanding of operational impact in clinical coding workflows, where thresholded retrieval quality (F1) matters more than pure ranking (AUC).

hypothesis: In hospital clinical coding operations, a domain-adapted transformer fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro-F1 on multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially for medium-frequency diagnosis codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for inpatient hospital coding, where professional coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and longitudinal note text for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter records from a large hospital system, targeting approximately 200,000–400,000 admissions. Each encounter contributes the discharge summary and up to 10 pre-discharge progress notes (attending and consultation notes), finalized before the discharge coding event. Critically, we apply a strict timestamp filter: only notes with a finalization timestamp prior to the discharge coding timestamp are included. This prevents any post-discharge documentation — which may reference or summarize the coding process itself — from leaking into the input features.

Labels are defined as the full set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by professional coders in the final coded record, represented as a binary multi-label vector over the active code vocabulary. Encounters flagged with coding audit reversals or known correction events are excluded to reduce label noise. No hierarchy rollup is applied; codes are used at their full specificity as coded.

The active label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10 codes appearing in at least 5 training encounters, balancing coverage against extreme label sparsity. Codes below this threshold are excluded from evaluation but tracked to characterize the long tail.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by encounter discharge date. Years 1–3 (~75% of encounters) form the training set. The first 6 months of year 4 (~12.5%) form the validation set. The final 6 months of year 4 (~12.5%) form the held-out test set.

A temporal split is essential here for two reasons. First, ICD-10-CM code definitions update each October 1st, so a random split would contaminate training with future coding conventions. Second, clinical documentation style drifts over time with EHR system changes and institutional policy shifts — the temporal split ensures the test distribution reflects what the model would face in deployment. Although patients may appear in multiple splits, the per-encounter coding task means patient-level leakage is not the primary risk; a sensitivity check confirms no individual patient disproportionately influences results.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both the transformer model and the TF-IDF baseline receive identical input text: the concatenation of the discharge summary and selected pre-discharge progress notes, constructed by the same document-assembly pipeline. This is non-negotiable for a fair comparison — any asymmetry in document access would confound the result.

For the TF-IDF baseline: unigram and bigram term frequencies with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization, up to 150,000 features. For the transformer: text is chunked into 512-token windows with 64-token stride; chunk representations are mean-pooled and passed to a sigmoid classification head. Service line metadata is incorporated identically in both models (one-hot for TF-IDF; prefix token for transformer).

To stabilize the feature space across time and ensure consistent token coverage, vocabulary construction, TF-IDF fitting, and label binarizer fitting are performed once on the full corpus before applying the resulting mappings to the train, validation, and test splits. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is frozen from its pretrained checkpoint.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a domain-adapted transformer — BioLinkBERT-base or ClinicalBERT — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head. Binary cross-entropy loss is used with per-label positive class weighting (inversely proportional to frequency, capped at 10×) to address the severe label imbalance.

The baseline is TF-IDF combined with one-vs-rest logistic regression — the industry-standard approach for automated ICD coding. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength C is swept over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on validation micro-F1, and per-label classification thresholds are optimized on the validation set, matching the transformer's threshold tuning procedure exactly. Both models use the same class weighting scheme. This ensures any observed advantage of the transformer is not an artifact of unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 at per-label optimized thresholds. Micro-F1 is chosen because it weights each (encounter, code) prediction pair equally, directly reflecting coding completeness — the operational objective of maximizing correctly assigned codes for reimbursement and quality reporting. Thresholds are set on the validation set and held fixed for test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 stratified by label frequency bin (rare: <10 training encounters; medium: 10–500; common: >500) to directly test the hypothesis claim about medium-frequency codes; micro-F1 by service line to test the template memorization confound; micro-averaged AUC-PR for a threshold-independent view; separate precision and recall to verify that neither model is gaming F1 at the expense of the other; and exact-match accuracy as a secondary operational signal.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter selection — learning rate, dropout, number of fine-tuning epochs, regularization strength, chunk pooling strategy — is performed using validation set micro-F1 exclusively. For the transformer, the checkpoint with the highest validation micro-F1 is retained. No information from the test set influences any model decision.

The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection decisions are finalized. Confidence intervals on the micro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline are computed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations) to assess whether the observed gap is statistically reliable.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds receive explicit design controls. First, to rule out document access asymmetry, both models receive identical input text from the same assembly pipeline. Second, to test for service-line template memorization, stratified micro-F1 by service line is reported; if the transformer's overall advantage vanishes within homogeneous service lines, the gain was distributional rather than representational. Third, a regression of per-encounter prediction residuals on note length is run to test whether the transformer advantage concentrates on unusually long documents. Fourth, performance in the first vs. second half of the test period is compared to detect whether either model degrades more sharply at the ICD-10-CM annual update boundary.

This experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline under controlled, operationally realistic conditions at a single hospital system. It does not establish generalizability to other institutions, other EHR documentation cultures, or non-English clinical text.

planted_issues.description: The design fits the TF-IDF vocabulary, label binarizer, and related preprocessing steps on the full corpus, including validation and test data. This introduces information leakage because the feature representation used during training has been influenced by future (from the model's perspective) documentation patterns and label distributions. In a temporally split clinical setting where terminology and coding practices evolve, this artificially inflates model performance by allowing the training data to adapt to future shifts.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split allows patient-level data leakage, since the same patient may appear across train, validation, and test sets.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The coding task is defined per-encounter, not per-patient, and patient re-admissions are common in real hospital systems. Because labels are encounter-specific and there is no evidence that rare patients dominate the data, moderate patient overlap across time is operationally realistic and does not invalidate the split. The temporal boundary remains the appropriate control for policy drift.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the full label vocabulary from the training set excludes codes that appear only in validation or test, which could introduce evaluation bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The filtering of codes to those with ≥5 occurrences in training is standard multi-label practice to manage sparsity, and the test set evaluates only on codes seen in training. Long-tail tracking ensures awareness of performance limits, but strict exclusion prevents evaluation of unreliable predictions on extremely rare codes. This is methodologically sound.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies the preprocessing leakage in the TF-IDF and label binarizer fitting, explaining that fitting on the full corpus contaminates the training data with future information under a temporal split. The defense fails to rebut because no legitimate justification can overcome this validity threat.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that fitting feature transformers on test-influenced data leaks temporal information and invalidates the deployment realism of the temporal split.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Code Assignment

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter records from a large hospital system, targeting approximately 200,000–400,000 admissions. Each encounter contributes the discharge summary and up to 10 pre-discharge progress notes (attending and consultation notes), finalized before the discharge coding event. Critically, we apply a strict timestamp filter: only notes with a finalization timestamp prior to the discharge coding timestamp are included. This prevents any post-discharge documentation — which may reference or summarize the coding process itself — from leaking into the input features.

Labels are defined as the full set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by professional coders in the final coded record, represented as a binary multi-label vector over the active code vocabulary. Encounters flagged with coding audit reversals or known correction events are excluded to reduce label noise. No hierarchy rollup is applied; codes are used at their full specificity as coded.

The active label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10 codes appearing in at least 5 training encounters, balancing coverage against extreme label sparsity. Codes below this threshold are excluded from evaluation but tracked to characterize the long tail.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically by encounter discharge date. Years 1–3 (~75% of encounters) form the training set. The first 6 months of year 4 (~12.5%) form the validation set. The final 6 months of year 4 (~12.5%) form the held-out test set.

A temporal split is essential here for two reasons. First, ICD-10-CM code definitions update each October 1st, so a random split would contaminate training with future coding conventions. Second, clinical documentation style drifts over time with EHR system changes and institutional policy shifts — the temporal split ensures the test distribution reflects what the model would face in deployment. Although patients may appear in multiple splits, the per-encounter coding task means patient-level leakage is not the primary risk; a sensitivity check confirms no individual patient disproportionately influences results.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both the transformer model and the TF-IDF baseline receive identical input text: the concatenation of the discharge summary and selected pre-discharge progress notes, constructed by the same document-assembly pipeline. This is non-negotiable for a fair comparison — any asymmetry in document access would confound the result.

For the TF-IDF baseline: unigram and bigram term frequencies with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization, up to 150,000 features. For the transformer: text is chunked into 512-token windows with 64-token stride; chunk representations are mean-pooled and passed to a sigmoid classification head. Service line metadata is incorporated identically in both models (one-hot for TF-IDF; prefix token for transformer).

All vocabulary construction, TF-IDF fitting, and label binarizer fitting are performed on training data only and applied to validation and test sets. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is frozen from its pretrained checkpoint.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a domain-adapted transformer — BioLinkBERT-base or ClinicalBERT — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head. Binary cross-entropy loss is used with per-label positive class weighting (inversely proportional to frequency, capped at 10×) to address the severe label imbalance.

The baseline is TF-IDF combined with one-vs-rest logistic regression — the industry-standard approach for automated ICD coding. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength C is swept over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10} on validation micro-F1, and per-label classification thresholds are optimized on the validation set, matching the transformer's threshold tuning procedure exactly. Both models use the same class weighting scheme. This ensures any observed advantage of the transformer is not an artifact of unequal tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 at per-label optimized thresholds. Micro-F1 is chosen because it weights each (encounter, code) prediction pair equally, directly reflecting coding completeness — the operational objective of maximizing correctly assigned codes for reimbursement and quality reporting. Thresholds are set on the validation set and held fixed for test evaluation.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 stratified by label frequency bin (rare: <10 training encounters; medium: 10–500; common: >500) to directly test the hypothesis claim about medium-frequency codes; micro-F1 by service line to test the template memorization confound; micro-averaged AUC-PR for a threshold-independent view; separate precision and recall to verify that neither model is gaming F1 at the expense of the other; and exact-match accuracy as a secondary operational signal.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter selection — learning rate, dropout, number of fine-tuning epochs, regularization strength, chunk pooling strategy — is performed using validation set micro-F1 exclusively. For the transformer, the checkpoint with the highest validation micro-F1 is retained. No information from the test set influences any model decision.

The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection decisions are finalized. Confidence intervals on the micro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline are computed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations) to assess whether the observed gap is statistically reliable.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds receive explicit design controls. First, to rule out document access asymmetry, both models receive identical input text from the same assembly pipeline. Second, to test for service-line template memorization, stratified micro-F1 by service line is reported; if the transformer's overall advantage vanishes within homogeneous service lines, the gain was distributional rather than representational. Third, a regression of per-encounter prediction residuals on note length is run to test whether the transformer advantage concentrates on unusually long documents. Fourth, performance in the first vs. second half of the test period is compared to detect whether either model degrades more sharply at the ICD-10-CM annual update boundary.

This experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer achieves higher micro-F1 than a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline under controlled, operationally realistic conditions at a single hospital system. It does not establish generalizability to other institutions, other EHR documentation cultures, or non-English clinical text.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the corrupted justification ('to stabilize the feature space') sounds reasonable and is a common real-world practice in static datasets, but it invalidates the temporal integrity which is critical in this domain. This requires nuanced understanding of both ML best practices and clinical deployment dynamics.

hypothesis: For anti-money-laundering alert triage at a retail bank, a graph neural network built on account-to-account transaction networks and customer linkage data will identify suspicious cases that escalate to SAR filing more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model using only account-level aggregated features, when evaluated on future months of alerts.
domain: Finance: anti-money-laundering alert triage for a retail bank's transaction monitoring operation
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML Alert Triage

### 1. Data Assembly and Label Construction

We would use 36 months of production transaction monitoring alert data from the bank, covering investigator dispositions, raw transaction logs, KYC attributes, and system-generated relational linkage records (shared devices, shared addresses, ownership relationships). The labeled dataset consists only of alerts that received a final investigator disposition — auto-closed alerts are excluded because they lack genuine human judgment, and alerts with open dispositions at extraction time are excluded to avoid truncated labels. The binary label is defined as: 1 = SAR filed, 0 = closed without SAR. Only dispositions finalized at least 30 days before a partition boundary are included to account for the typical investigation lag and avoid partially-labeled cases near the boundary.

All feature values are anchored to the alert generation timestamp — no transaction data occurring after that timestamp is used in feature construction. A formal feature availability audit documents the production timestamp of each data source to confirm this.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The data is partitioned strictly by time: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for the held-out test set. A temporal split is required here — not optional. Laundering typologies evolve, SAR filing policy shifts, and the account graph grows continuously. Random splitting would allow future graph structure and behavioral patterns to contaminate the training window, producing optimistic and operationally misleading estimates. The hypothesis itself specifies evaluation on future months, so the split methodology must match the deployment condition. Graph construction for each partition uses only transactions and linkages with timestamps falling within or before that partition's window.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed. The GBT receives account-level aggregate features over 7-, 30-, and 90-day lookback windows: transaction velocity, counterparty entropy, inbound/outbound flow ratios, geographic dispersion, balance volatility, KYC risk tier, and product type. The GNN receives these same features as node attributes — ensuring feature parity — plus the graph structure itself: directed, weighted transaction edges and auxiliary linkage edges. This design guarantees that any performance difference reflects graph representation learning, not additional tabular signal.

All scalers, imputers, and encoders are fit once on the full labeled dataset to ensure consistent normalization and category handling across time, then applied to the train, validation, and test partitions.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a GraphSAGE network with inductive message passing (2–3 layers, mean aggregator, neighborhood sampling), chosen because it generalizes to new accounts not present during training — a hard requirement given the dynamic graph. A two-layer MLP classification head produces alert-level SAR probability scores.

The baseline is LightGBM trained on the account-level aggregate feature set only. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: 100 Optuna trials each, optimizing validation-set Average Precision. A secondary ablation adds hand-crafted 1-hop graph summary statistics (degree, average neighbor flow) to the GBT, separating graph representation learning from simple graph statistics.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Average Precision (AUPRC). Given severe class imbalance and the operational priority of ranking true positives highly within a fixed investigation budget, AUPRC is strictly more informative than AUROC. The operationally critical secondary metric is precision and recall at top-K, where K matches the bank's daily investigator capacity. Calibration (Brier score) is reported because downstream prioritization systems use raw probabilities. Performance is also stratified by alert typology and account vintage to probe generalization to new typologies and unseen nodes.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and hyperparameter tuning is conducted using the validation set. The test set is used exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. Statistical significance of the AP difference between models is assessed via paired permutation test on the test-set alert-level predictions.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are actively controlled. First, feature asymmetry: the GNN's node features strictly subsume the GBT's tabular features. Second, investigator linkage artifacts: edges created by investigators post-alert are excluded; only system-generated, pre-alert linkages are permitted, verified via the feature availability audit. Third, label policy shift: performance is reported by calendar quarter to distinguish genuine typology detection from periods of changing filing policy. The experiment would establish whether graph relational structure improves AML alert triage accuracy in a temporal holdout; it does not establish causality, does not generalize beyond retail banking, and does not address production inference latency.

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing transformers (scalers, imputers, encoders) are fit on the full labeled dataset, including validation and test data, before being applied to the training set. This introduces temporal leakage because statistics such as means, medians, or target-encoded categories derived from future data are used to transform past training instances. In a time-based evaluation setting like AML, where distributions shift over time, this inflates performance estimates and invalidates the generalization claim.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a strict temporal split with no randomization is inappropriate due to limited data or representativeness concerns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is mandatory here because the hypothesis explicitly evaluates performance on future months, and laundering behaviors, SAR filing policies, and account graphs evolve over time. Random splitting would introduce look-ahead bias and produce overly optimistic results that do not reflect real-world deployment conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Target encoding of high-cardinality categorical features (e.g., industry code) is inherently risky and introduces leakage regardless of fit point.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Target encoding is a valid technique when properly constrained: in the sound design, it is fit only on the training set, making it acceptable. The issue is not the method itself but the incorrect fitting scope in the corrupted version.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that fitting preprocessing on the full dataset leaks future information into training, undermining the validity of the temporal holdout. The defender fails to rebut this because there is no valid justification for using future statistics during training-time transformation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that fitting imputers, scalers, or encoders on future data contaminates the training process in a temporal setting, especially for methods like target encoding or median imputation where shifts over time matter.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns about potential leakage but misattributes it to graph construction or model architecture rather than preprocessing. This concern is invalid if not tied to the fitting procedure of transformers.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly clarifies that graph construction, feature windows, and model training follow a strict temporal boundary — the only flaw lies in preprocessing fit timing, not in other components of the pipeline.

hypothesis: For predicting 7-day-ahead SKU-by-warehouse demand in a grocery delivery network, a temporal fusion transformer that jointly uses historical sales, promotion calendars, prices, holidays, and weather forecasts will reduce weighted quantile loss versus a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on manually lagged and rolling-window features.
domain: Transportation and logistics for grocery e-commerce replenishment, where regional warehouses must forecast short-term item demand to avoid stockouts and spoilage
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBDT for 7-Day-Ahead SKU-by-Warehouse Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 2.5 years of daily SKU-by-warehouse panel data from the operator's production systems, covering order history, inventory snapshots, price and promotion logs, product metadata, holiday calendars, and localized weather observations and forecasts. This yields a panel of millions of daily observations across thousands of SKUs and dozens of warehouses — sufficient to represent the full seasonal cycle, promotion calendar, and assortment variation present at deployment time.

The label is decensored demand, not raw sales. On any day where a stockout is detected (end-of-day inventory hits zero before the order cutoff), raw sales are treated as censored observations and replaced with a Tobit-corrected demand estimate fitted on uncensored days for that SKU-warehouse pair within a trailing 90-day window. This correction is fitted exclusively on training-window data. Without this step, models can learn inventory-supply patterns rather than true demand, rewarding good inventory prediction rather than good demand forecasting.

### 2. Temporal Split

Data is divided chronologically: months 1–18 form the training set (≈60%), months 19–21 the validation set (≈10%), and months 22–30 the held-out test set (≈30%). The split boundary is the forecast origin date, ensuring that lagged and rolling features are never computed from data in the validation or test window. A random or stratified split is explicitly rejected — the series are non-stationary, seasonality is strong, and a random split would allow future-regime information to bleed into training, producing unrealistically optimistic error estimates that do not reflect deployment conditions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same feature set: time-varying known future covariates (promotional flag, price, holiday indicator, day-of-week, week-of-year, 7-day-ahead weather forecast), time-varying observed covariates (decensored demand history, rolling 7/14/28-day statistics, lag-7/lag-14 demand, inventory at origin, trailing stockout frequency), and static metadata (warehouse ID, product category/subcategory, brand, perishability flag, log-average volume, cold-start indicator).

All preprocessing transformers — log-scaling of the target, robust normalization of continuous inputs, and climatological imputation parameters — are fit once on the full available dataset to ensure stable scaling and seasonal coverage, then applied consistently across the train, validation, and test splits. The GBDT receives these signals as flat features at the forecast origin; the TFT receives them as structured temporal sequences per its native input schema. This design ensures identical information content while allowing the TFT to exploit sequence structure — the architectural property under test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer trained to minimize the sum of pinball losses at quantiles q=0.1, 0.5, and 0.9 over a 7-day forecast horizon. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on the same features as flat inputs, also optimizing quantile loss. Crucially, both models receive the same hyperparameter tuning budget: Bayesian optimization (Optuna) over an equal number of trials, using the validation WQL as the objective. Giving the GBDT equivalent tuning effort is essential — a poorly tuned GBDT would falsely inflate any apparent advantage for the TFT.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is volume-weighted Quantile Loss (WQL), averaged over q=0.1, 0.5, and 0.9 and weighted by historical sales volume. Volume weighting ensures that the metric reflects business impact, concentrating attention on high-throughput SKU-warehouse combinations where replenishment errors are most costly. The asymmetric quantile structure mirrors the asymmetric costs of stockouts vs. waste without requiring a single decision threshold.

Secondary metrics include MASE at the median quantile (for interpretable accuracy relative to a seasonal naive benchmark), per-quantile pinball loss (to diagnose directional bias), prediction interval coverage rate (to validate calibration), and WQL disaggregated by cold-start stratum, promotion vs. non-promotion weeks, and perishable vs. ambient category.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection — including hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and any feature ablations — uses the validation set exclusively. The test set is held in a separate access-controlled artifact and loaded only once, after all decisions are frozen, to produce the final reported metrics. There is no second look.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds require explicit control. First, feature parity: both models receive identical covariate information, so any WQL difference reflects architecture, not data access. Second, censoring artifacts: decensoring the training labels prevents models from gaming the metric by learning inventory availability patterns. Third, hierarchical structure: both models have access to warehouse and category identifiers. An additional ablation disables the TFT's static covariate embeddings to isolate the contribution of sequence modeling from hierarchical representation. Cold-start SKUs are evaluated in a separate stratum so sparse series do not mask or inflate aggregate results.

This design establishes whether the TFT's native sequence modeling and structured covariate handling reduce volume-weighted quantile loss versus a well-tuned GBDT given identical information. It does not claim the TFT is universally superior or that any gain generalizes beyond the 7-day horizon or this operator's assortment.

planted_issues.description: The preprocessing transformers — including log-scaling, normalization of continuous inputs, and climatological imputation parameters — are fit on the full dataset, which includes validation and test data. This introduces a form of temporal leakage where future information influences the scaling and imputation of past data, leading to an unrealistic stabilization of seasonal patterns and distributional shifts that would not be available at inference time. In a forecasting context, this undermines generalization validity by biasing performance estimates downward.

must_not_claim.claim: Fitting preprocessing on the full dataset improves estimator stability and seasonal representativeness, so it should be preferred.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In a temporally ordered forecasting evaluation, any use of test or validation data to fit preprocessing inflates performance by leaking future information. Stability arguments do not override the requirement for temporally sound evaluation; normalization and imputation parameters must be derived solely from training data to reflect realistic deployment conditions where future values are unknown.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that fitting preprocessing transformers on the full dataset introduces temporal leakage, invalidating the fairness of the comparison. The defender fails to justify this choice, as no valid rebuttal exists under proper forecasting protocols.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that fitting normalization and imputation on future data allows the model to implicitly learn seasonal trends and distributional shifts not available at inference time, thus producing inflated performance estimates.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for 7-Day SKU-Warehouse Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Assembly and Label Construction

We would use 24 months of daily production data from the grocery e-commerce fulfillment platform, covering all active SKU-warehouse pairs. The data includes daily unit sales, inventory snapshots, stockout flags, price and discount records, the promotion calendar, product metadata, and warehouse-linked external weather and holiday data. All external signals (weather, holidays) are matched to the warehouse's geographic location and calendar.

The forecasting label is daily unit demand for each of the next 7 days. Because observed sales are censored when stockouts are active, we apply a demand uncensoring procedure: on stockout days, the observed value is flagged as a lower bound, and imputation uses the median non-stockout sales rate from a ±14-day window, computed on training data only. SKUs with fewer than 30 days of history are excluded from the primary evaluation and tracked in a separate cold-start stratum. Permanently delisted SKUs are removed from targets after their exit date.

### 2. Temporal Split and Rolling-Origin Evaluation

The data is divided strictly chronologically: months 1–18 for training, months 19–21 for validation, and months 22–24 for the held-out test set. No shuffling is applied at any stage. This temporal split is non-negotiable for this problem: promotional patterns, seasonal demand, and customer behavior drift over time, and random splitting would allow future behavioral context to contaminate training.

Within each period, we evaluate using a rolling-origin protocol: forecasts are generated at each weekly origin point, producing approximately 13 non-overlapping 7-day forecast windows in the test period. This mirrors the deployment cadence and avoids optimistic evaluation from a single fixed origin.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same feature set: lagged sales (lags 1, 2, 3, 7, 14, 28), rolling mean and standard deviation (7-, 14-, 28-day windows), calendar indicators (day-of-week, week-of-year, month), price and discount at forecast origin, forward-looking promotion flags for the 7-day horizon, 7-day weather forecast, holiday flags, trailing 14-day stockout rate, and static SKU metadata. These promotion, weather, and holiday signals are operationally available before the forecast window and represent legitimate known-future inputs.

For the TFT, known-future covariates are passed through the model's encoder-decoder architecture as intended. For the GBT baseline, the same horizon-specific features are pivoted into flat columns, ensuring equivalent information access.

All preprocessing transformers — scaling, encoding, imputation statistics — are fit once on the full 24-month dataset to ensure stable category coverage and normalization, then applied consistently across train, validation, and test splits.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer trained with multi-horizon quantile loss over quantiles [0.1, 0.5, 0.9], with a 28-day lookback window and 7-day forecast horizon.

The baseline is LightGBM in a direct multi-output configuration, trained on the identical feature set. Critically, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: the same Optuna-based hyperparameter search budget, the same validation metric, and the same evaluation conditions. This ensures any observed difference reflects model capacity, not asymmetric effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is volume-weighted quantile loss (WQL) across quantiles [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]. Volume weighting directly aligns with operational priorities: errors on high-volume SKUs drive the largest replenishment and staffing consequences. Secondary metrics include MASE on the median forecast, Winkler score and empirical coverage for the 80% prediction interval, WQL broken down by promotion vs. non-promotion periods, and WQL stratified by volume quartile. Bias on the 0.9 quantile is reported to detect systematic under-estimation relevant to safety stock calculations.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All architecture choices and hyperparameter tuning are performed using the validation set exclusively. The test set is touched exactly once — after all model selection is final — and no post-hoc adjustments are made based on test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly managed. First, future signal leakage is audited via a feature timestamp review to confirm all inputs reflect information available at the forecast origin. Second, cold-start SKUs are excluded from the primary evaluation to isolate sequence modeling gains from embedding advantages. Third, results are stratified by demand intermittency to determine whether gains are concentrated in sparse series. Fourth, promotion-period and non-promotion-period WQL are reported separately to distinguish general temporal modeling capacity from promotion-response modeling. Together, these controls allow the experiment to make a precise, scopeable claim: whether the TFT improves quantile forecast accuracy for established, non-sparse SKU-warehouse pairs in a production grocery fulfillment setting.

planted_issues.description: The experiment fits all preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation) on the full 24-month dataset, which includes future validation and test data. In a time series setting with strict chronological splits, this leaks future distributional information into the training pipeline, enabling the model to indirectly 'see' future feature distributions and leading to over-optimistic performance estimates. Correct practice is to fit preprocessing only on the training set.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline LightGBM receives unequal feature access because it uses pivoted columns for horizon-specific features instead of the TFT's native encoder-decoder architecture.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Both models receive identical information: the GBT's flat column format ensures it has the same access to future covariates as the TFT. The pivoting is a legitimate transformation that preserves information equivalence and is standard practice in multi-step baselines.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is used multiple times due to rolling-origin evaluation across 13 forecast origins.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Rolling-origin evaluation within the test period is required and correct for operational realism. The key is that model selection and tuning never touch the test set, which is preserved for final evaluation only — a single post-training access with multiple rolling windows is standard and valid.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies the preprocessing leakage: fitting on the full dataset contaminates the temporal validation setup by leaking future distributional statistics into training. The defender cannot credibly justify this as acceptable.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that computing imputation values, category encodings, or scaling factors using the full dataset introduces temporal leakage because the test data distribution influences the preprocessing applied to training.

## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for 7-Day SKU-Warehouse Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Assembly and Label Construction

We would use 24 months of daily production data from the grocery e-commerce fulfillment platform, covering all active SKU-warehouse pairs. The data includes daily unit sales, inventory snapshots, stockout flags, price and discount records, the promotion calendar, product metadata, and warehouse-linked external weather and holiday data. All external signals (weather, holidays) are matched to the warehouse's geographic location and calendar.

The forecasting label is daily unit demand for each of the next 7 days. Because observed sales are censored when stockouts are active, we apply a demand uncensoring procedure: on stockout days, the observed value is flagged as a lower bound, and imputation uses the median non-stockout sales rate from a ±14-day window, computed on training data only. SKUs with fewer than 30 days of history are excluded from the primary evaluation and tracked in a separate cold-start stratum. Permanently delisted SKUs are removed from targets after their exit date.

### 2. Temporal Split and Rolling-Origin Evaluation

The data is divided strictly chronologically: months 1–18 for training, months 19–21 for validation, and months 22–24 for the held-out test set. No shuffling is applied at any stage. This temporal split is non-negotiable for this problem: promotional patterns, seasonal demand, and customer behavior drift over time, and random splitting would allow future behavioral context to contaminate training.

Within each period, we evaluate using a rolling-origin protocol: forecasts are generated at each weekly origin point, producing approximately 13 non-overlapping 7-day forecast windows in the test period. This mirrors the deployment cadence and avoids optimistic evaluation from a single fixed origin.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same feature set: lagged sales (lags 1, 2, 3, 7, 14, 28), rolling mean and standard deviation (7-, 14-, 28-day windows), calendar indicators (day-of-week, week-of-year, month), price and discount at forecast origin, forward-looking promotion flags for the 7-day horizon, 7-day weather forecast, holiday flags, trailing 14-day stockout rate, and static SKU metadata. These promotion, weather, and holiday signals are operationally available before the forecast window and represent legitimate known-future inputs.

For the TFT, known-future covariates are passed through the model's encoder-decoder architecture as intended. For the GBT baseline, the same horizon-specific features are pivoted into flat columns, ensuring equivalent information access.

All preprocessing transformers — scaling, encoding, imputation statistics — are fit on the training set only and applied without modification to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer trained with multi-horizon quantile loss over quantiles [0.1, 0.5, 0.9], with a 28-day lookback window and 7-day forecast horizon.

The baseline is LightGBM in a direct multi-output configuration, trained on the identical feature set. Critically, the baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: the same Optuna-based hyperparameter search budget, the same validation metric, and the same evaluation conditions. This ensures any observed difference reflects model capacity, not asymmetric effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is volume-weighted quantile loss (WQL) across quantiles [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]. Volume weighting directly aligns with operational priorities: errors on high-volume SKUs drive the largest replenishment and staffing consequences. Secondary metrics include MASE on the median forecast, Winkler score and empirical coverage for the 80% prediction interval, WQL broken down by promotion vs. non-promotion periods, and WQL stratified by volume quartile. Bias on the 0.9 quantile is reported to detect systematic under-estimation relevant to safety stock calculations.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All architecture choices and hyperparameter tuning are performed using the validation set exclusively. The test set is touched exactly once — after all model selection is final — and no post-hoc adjustments are made based on test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly managed. First, future signal leakage is audited via a feature timestamp review to confirm all inputs reflect information available at the forecast origin. Second, cold-start SKUs are excluded from the primary evaluation to isolate sequence modeling gains from embedding advantages. Third, results are stratified by demand intermittency to determine whether gains are concentrated in sparse series. Fourth, promotion-period and non-promotion-period WQL are reported separately to distinguish general temporal modeling capacity from promotion-response modeling. Together, these controls allow the experiment to make a precise, scopeable claim: whether the TFT improves quantile forecast accuracy for established, non-sparse SKU-warehouse pairs in a production grocery fulfillment setting.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the single corruption is methodologically subtle — fitting preprocessing on the full dataset appears reasonable for stability but catastrophically violates temporal integrity. A reviewer must understand time series evaluation deeply to distinguish this error from acceptable data practices in cross-sectional settings.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. GBT for AML SAR Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 36 months of production retail banking transaction logs (January Year 1 – December Year 3), including sender/receiver account IDs, timestamps, amounts, transaction types, merchant descriptors, device identifiers, IP addresses, and historical SAR investigation outcomes. This time window is chosen to capture typology variation and seasonal behavioral cycles while keeping the test period recent and relevant to deployment.

Labels are defined at the account-month level. To increase label density and reduce dependence on long SAR-resolution lags, an account is labeled positive in a given month if it triggered an internal AML alert in that month, while accounts with no alert are labeled negative. Historical SAR outcomes are retained for descriptive analysis but are not required for supervised training.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically:
- **Training:** Months 1–24 (labels resolved by month 30)
- **Validation:** Months 25–28 (labels resolved by month 34)
- **Test:** Months 29–36 (labels progressively resolved within the data window)

A temporal split is the only defensible choice here: the hypothesis is explicitly about performance on future unseen months, and random or stratified splits would allow future typologies, entity behaviors, and relationship patterns to contaminate the training window, producing artificially optimistic and deployment-irrelevant estimates. A 6-month gap between the training cutoff and the test window start ensures no label-resolution overlap across partitions. Graph edges for each partition use only transactions timestamped within that partition's window; nodes may appear across partitions, but their future edges and labels are never exposed during training.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two strictly parallel feature sets are constructed:

**Shared features (both models):** Account-level rolling aggregates over 30-day and 90-day windows prior to the observation date — transaction count, send/receive volume, distinct counterparty count, distinct merchant count, distinct device count, distinct IP count, transaction type distribution, time-of-day distribution, and historical alert rate. These aggregates are computed with a strict look-back boundary to prevent any future information from entering the feature vector.

**GNN-exclusive:** The full heterogeneous transaction graph (account, device, merchant, IP node types; directed transaction edges with amount, type, and timestamp attributes). The temporal GNN generates account-level embeddings that are concatenated with the shared aggregates as its full input.

The GBT baseline uses only the shared aggregates — precisely the feature condition the hypothesis posits as inferior.

All preprocessing transformers (log-scalers, frequency encoders, imputers) are fit on the training partition only and applied without refitting to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a temporal heterogeneous GNN (TGN-style architecture) with four node types, temporal attention over ordered edge sequences, and a binary classification head. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include layer depth (2–4), embedding dimension (64–256), attention heads (2–8), dropout (0.0–0.4), and learning rate.

The baseline is LightGBM trained on the shared account-level aggregates, given equivalent Optuna tuning budget on the same validation set and primary metric. The GBT baseline is the correct comparison because it isolates the relational graph representation as the single variable differing between conditions — both models see the same raw data source, the same labels, and are evaluated identically.

A third ablation model — GBT trained on GNN embeddings concatenated with shared features — is included to probe the confound that gains may reflect learned feature interactions rather than the graph structure per se.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary:** Area under the precision-recall curve (AUCPR) on the labeled test set. AUCPR is chosen because the stakeholder's objective is maximizing SAR capture within a fixed investigator review budget — exactly the precision-recall tradeoff. AUROC and accuracy are dominated by true-negative performance under the extreme class imbalance typical of AML datasets and are therefore misleading for this objective. Statistical significance is assessed via paired bootstrap (10,000 resamples, account-level sampling).

**Secondary:** Recall and Precision at top-K alerts (K = 500, 1000, 2000) to directly simulate the fixed-budget review constraint; AUROC for literature comparability; expected calibration error (ECE); and month-by-month AUCPR within the test window to assess temporal stability.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter selection for both models uses the validation partition only, optimizing AUCPR. Once configurations are finalized, both models are retrained on the combined training+validation data (months 1–28) and evaluated on the test set exactly once. The test set is never used for selection, threshold tuning, or architecture decisions.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The primary confound — that GNN gains reflect richer entity-link information rather than graph representation — is addressed by giving the GBT access to entity-derived aggregate features (device count, IP count, counterparty diversity) from the same transaction log, and by including the GBT-on-embeddings ablation. A second confound — calibration differences inflating threshold-sensitive metrics — is addressed by using threshold-free primary metrics. A third confound — temporal distributional shift — is addressed by reporting per-month performance curves in the test window.

This experiment establishes whether a temporal GNN on transaction graphs outperforms an account-level GBT on future retail banking months under controlled, identical evaluation conditions. It does not establish generalizability across banking institutions, AML typology families outside the observed training distribution, or performance on accounts with no training-period history.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses internal AML alerts as labels instead of Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs), which fundamentally changes the prediction target. The hypothesis is about detecting SAR-positive cases, which are legally filed reports representing confirmed suspicious behavior investigated and escalated by compliance. Internal alerts are lower-threshold flags generated by rule-based systems that may not reflect true financial crime, and they are prone to large-scale overflagging with low precision. Training on alerts instead of SARs evaluates whether the model can reproduce a noisy operational proxy rather than achieve the stated objective of detecting actual suspicious activity. This invalidates the experiment's claim.

must_not_claim.claim: Labeling based on internal AML alerts increases label density and reduces wait time for resolved outcomes, so it's a reasonable shortcut.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While using alerts improves label density, the hypothesis explicitly concerns detecting true SAR-positive cases. Replacing the target with a proxy violates the scope and renders the test irrelevant. The sound design uses a 180-day SAR-resolution window to ensure labels are both meaningful and operationally available, striking the correct balance between validity and feasibility.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A valid critique identifies that the label definition diverges from the hypothesis goal by using internal alerts instead of SARs. The final verdict must conclude that the experiment does not support the original claim because it measures performance on a different task.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer correctly argues that the use of internal alerts introduces scope drift, conflates model performance with existing rule-based systems, and undermines generalization to real-world SAR detection.

Evaluators must recognize the operational distinction between alerts and SARs and the consequential scope mismatch, which requires domain-specific knowledge of AML workflows.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology labs, a domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned for token classification will identify cancer-related entities and biomarker values in free-text pathology reports with higher span-level F1 than a CRF-based sequence labeler built on handcrafted lexical and section features, especially on reports with heterogeneous template structure.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for structured extraction from pathology reports in a multi-hospital cancer care network
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. CRF for Pathology NER (hyp_083)

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use de-identified pathology reports from a multi-hospital cancer care network spanning 3 years (2021–2023), sourced from at least 4 distinct hospital sites with meaningfully different templating conventions (synoptic CAP templates, narrative prose, and hybrid formats). The full corpus of 80,000–150,000 reports provides sufficient volume for domain-adaptive pretraining; a curated labeled subset of 2,000–3,000 reports is created via double annotation by certified tumor registrars or pathologists using a structured annotation guide covering 8–12 entity types: primary site, histology, tumor grade, margin status, lymph node status, TNM components, receptor status (ER/PR/HER2), and quantitative biomarker measurements with qualifiers.

Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa and span-level F1 between annotators) is computed and published alongside results. Adjudication-unresolved spans are excluded from primary evaluation and tracked in a sensitivity analysis. Document-level metadata — site, specimen type, report year — is retained throughout to enable stratified analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

The reports are split at the document level into train, validation, and test partitions using a stratified random split across the full multi-site corpus, preserving entity distribution, specimen type, and report year balance in each partition. This keeps all splits representative of the overall dataset and reduces the variance that can arise when an entire hospital is held out. Because the primary goal is to compare transformer and CRF performance on pathology NER across heterogeneous report styles, exposing both models to the full range of site variation during training provides a more stable estimate of average extraction quality. Site metadata is still retained so that performance can be broken out by hospital during analysis. Within-split stratification by specimen type and entity density is used to balance representation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All fit-dependent preprocessing — CRF feature statistics, lexical frequency thresholds, section header dictionaries — is computed exclusively on training documents and applied without modification to validation and test documents. The transformer tokenizer is a fixed pretrained artifact and requires no corpus-level fitting. Section boundary tokens ([SEC:DIAGNOSIS], etc.) are injected into transformer input using a rule-based section segmenter authored without reference to validation or test content. No feature encodes gold label information or tokens outside the standard context window.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **primary model** is a domain-adapted clinical transformer (BioClinicalBERT or GatorTron-base) fine-tuned end-to-end with a linear BIO classification head, using a sliding window strategy for reports exceeding 512 tokens. Optionally, domain-adaptive pretraining (DAPT) on unannotated training-site reports precedes fine-tuning; this is treated as a tracked ablation.

The **baseline** is a linear-chain CRF with a rich handcrafted feature set: token-level lexical features (lowercased form, prefix/suffix n-grams, capitalization and digit patterns), dictionary membership features drawn from NCI Thesaurus oncology gazetteers, context window features (±2 tokens), section indicator features, and within-section positional features. This baseline is not a straw man — it reflects a production-realistic alternative that a clinical informatics team would seriously consider deploying. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: the transformer undergoes a 16-point hyperparameter grid (learning rate × warmup × dropout); the CRF undergoes a comparably sized grid over regularization and context window. Both are evaluated by the same scoring script under identical conditions.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is span-level micro-averaged F1 with strict matching (exact boundary and correct entity type). Strict F1 is appropriate here because the deployment context — tumor registry population and treatment planning — requires precision at the span level; a partial boundary match on a biomarker value has direct operational consequences. Per-entity-type F1 is reported to surface clinically critical failure modes.

Secondary metrics include: lenient span F1 (≥50% boundary overlap), precision and recall disaggregated per entity type, F1 stratified by report structure (synoptic vs. narrative), cross-site F1 variance (the primary measure of template generalization), and inference throughput. Inter-annotator F1 serves as a calibrated performance ceiling.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions are made using the validation site only. The configuration maximizing strict span-level micro-F1 on the validation split is frozen for each model. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete, and no post-hoc adjustments are permitted. Test annotations are kept separate from the development team until evaluation is triggered.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, to isolate clinical pretraining from architecture: a general-domain transformer (RoBERTa-base) is fine-tuned identically to BioClinicalBERT, producing an ablation that separates pretraining signal from model capacity. Second, to check vocabulary leakage: token unigram Jaccard similarity between train and test sites is computed and reported; unusually high overlap would qualify the generalization claim. Third, DAPT data integrity is verified — no test-site documents may appear in the unannotated pretraining pool. Fourth, a CRF ablation without section features is run to confirm that the handcrafted feature set is not providing a structural advantage unavailable to the transformer in a fair comparison.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer outperforms a carefully engineered CRF on span-level extraction in a multi-hospital, cross-site evaluation. It would not establish generalizability beyond this network, superiority on relation extraction or entity linking tasks, or production-readiness under real-time latency constraints.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split at the document level across all hospitals instead of a site-based (group) split. This allows site-specific layout patterns, vocabulary, and formatting conventions from every hospital to leak into the training data, violating the hypothesis’s core focus on cross-site generalization. As a result, the evaluation measures in-distribution performance rather than the model's ability to generalize to unseen hospitals, which overestimates real-world deployment performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The site-based performance analysis using retained metadata is insufficient or improperly implemented because the test set includes all sites.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Retaining site metadata and analyzing performance by site is still valid and useful for subgroup analysis, even when the split is flawed. The design choice to retain metadata is correct and supports transparency; the problem lies in the split method, not in reporting site-level results.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by specimen type and entity density introduces bias or is methodologically incorrect.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification within a correct group split would be appropriate to ensure balanced representation of key data characteristics across splits. The flaw is not in stratification itself, but in applying it within a document-level random split rather than a site-separated split.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that the use of a random document-level split invalidates the claim about cross-site generalization because it leaks site-specific patterns into training. The defender fails to justify this choice given the hypothesis, and the verdict is that the experiment must be redesigned with a site-based split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that the hypothesis emphasizes generalization to heterogeneous and unseen hospital templates, which requires holding out entire sites to simulate a new hospital joining the network. The current split conflates within-site and cross-site performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that while average performance estimation may benefit from statistical efficiency, the operational goal of the study—generalization to new hospitals—requires a group split. Reporting cross-site F1 variance is not enough to mitigate the contamination from shared site-level features in train and test.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer articulates that secondary metrics like cross-site F1 variance cannot compensate for training on test-site data, as the model may have already learned site-specific heuristics that inflate performance unevenly.
