Hourly aggregation reduces sensor noise while preserving the intraday demand shape that operators care about. Sensor gaps up to 30 minutes are forward-filled; longer outages are masked from loss computation rather than imputed.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided strictly chronologically: months 1–16 for training, months 17–20 for validation, and months 21–24 as the held-out test set. Random or stratified splits are not appropriate here because of strong temporal autocorrelation and non-stationarity from seasonality and trend. A random split would allow future weather patterns, seasonal baselines, and flow statistics to leak into the training window, producing optimistic and unreliable estimates. The chronological split strictly simulates the deployment scenario, where forecasts are always made for future periods using only past data.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive an identical feature set: lagged bike availability at 1h, 2h, 3h, 6h, 12h, 24h, 48h, and 168h; same-lag trip departure and arrival counts; cyclic calendar encodings for hour-of-day, day-of-week, and month; a binary holiday flag; hourly weather covariates; and static station metadata. The ST-GNN additionally uses trip-flow-derived directed edge weights between stations, computed from training-period trip logs only. The GBT baseline receives these same trip-flow values as station-level lag features, ensuring feature parity. All continuous feature scalers are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied without refitting to validation and test data, preventing any leakage of future distributional statistics.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is an ST-GNN combining a graph attention convolutional layer (GAT) for spatial message-passing across the station graph with a GRU temporal encoder operating over a 168-hour lookback window. Graph edges are constructed from training-period trip-flow co-occurrence frequencies. The baseline is a LightGBM model with the same feature set and equivalent Bayesian hyperparameter tuning effort (same number of optimization trials, same validation metric). This ensures any observed gain is attributable to the graph-based spatial inductive bias and not to tuning asymmetry or richer inputs. An ablation variant of the ST-GNN using geographic-proximity-only edges is also trained to isolate the contribution of flow-derived graph topology.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is demand-weighted MAE (WMAE), where station-hour predictions are weighted by historical ridership at that station and hour (computed from training data). This directly reflects the operator objective: errors at high-demand stations during peak commute hours have larger operational consequences than errors at quiet stations overnight. Unweighted MAE would misalign with this priority. Secondary metrics include unweighted MAE, WMAE stratified by AM peak (7–9am) and PM peak (5–7pm) versus off-peak windows, WMAE by station type (transit-hub-adjacent vs. other), 90th-percentile absolute error at peak hours, and mean bias per station for calibration assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and architecture decisions use the validation partition (months 17–20), with validation WMAE as the selection criterion. Early stopping for the ST-GNN uses validation WMAE with 10-epoch patience. The test set is touched exactly once after all choices are finalized, using rolling one-day-ahead forecasting to replicate the production inference cadence.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are directly addressed. Feature parity between models eliminates the risk that any GNN advantage reflects richer inputs. The geographic-edge ST-GNN ablation isolates the value of trip-flow graph structure from generic spatial smoothing. Months coinciding with documented service expansions or policy changes are flagged for a stratified sub-analysis to confirm stability of results across stable-network periods. This design would establish whether the graph inductive bias provides forecasting value net of equivalent temporal and contextual features — it would not establish generalizability to other cities or network configurations without further testing.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws are methodologically nuanced: temporal leakage in a correlated time series is easy to overlook if one focuses on data coverage, and metric misalignment appears justified by simplicity and interpretability. A correct evaluation requires integrating domain objectives with technical design.

hypothesis: For next-day spare-parts demand forecasting at service depots for a national heavy-equipment fleet, a temporal fusion transformer trained on part-level demand histories plus machine telematics and maintenance-event sequences will reduce weighted mean absolute error versus a depot-part gradient-boosted tree baseline built on aggregated lag and calendar features.
domain: Transportation/logistics operations for heavy-equipment aftersales supply chains, where regional service depots must stock replacement parts for excavators, loaders, and trucks to avoid technician delays and machine downtime
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for Next-Day Spare-Parts Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

We use 36 months of daily operational records from a national heavy-equipment fleet operator, covering all service depots and all spare-parts SKUs. The dataset includes fulfilled orders, backorder and stockout records with inventory positions, work-order logs (timestamped at completion), preventive maintenance schedules, daily telematics summaries per asset class at each depot, and part metadata including operator-assigned criticality tiers (A/B/C).

The forecast label for each depot-part-day triplet is the total units of part p requested at depot d on day t+1. Critically, stockout-censored demand is corrected: on any day where depot inventory reached zero and unfulfilled requests were recorded, true demand is set to fulfilled quantity plus unfulfilled requests, treating the observation as left-censored. This correction is applied identically to both models and derived only from training-period inventory records.

Series with fewer than 30 demand events over the full 36-month window are flagged as long-tail but retained, because rare-but-critical parts carry disproportionate downtime cost. They are evaluated separately in secondary stratified analyses.

### 2. Data Split

The data are split at the depot-part-day level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test, using stratified random sampling to preserve the distribution of part criticality tiers and long-tail series across splits. This improves statistical efficiency by ensuring all splits contain examples from the full operating range, including rare failure-spike demand events and low-frequency critical parts.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Leakage Prevention

A single feature set is constructed and shared by both models to enforce strict feature parity. Features include demand lags and rolling statistics (7/14/28/90-day windows), calendar signals (day-of-week, month, days-to-next-PM, days-since-last-maintenance), end-of-day inventory position, daily telematics summaries (engine hours, fault-code counts by severity, cumulative operating hours since last major service), and part metadata (criticality tier, part family, equipment compatibility group).

A strict timestamp alignment rule is enforced during feature construction: every feature used to forecast day t+1 must have a recorded timestamp ≤ end-of-business on day t. Work-order completions, maintenance events, and telematics uploads are all audited against this cutoff. To ensure consistent feature scaling and stable imputation across depots, all preprocessing transformers — scalers, imputers, rolling statistic parameters — are fit once on the full 36-month dataset and then applied uniformly to the training, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer with a 90-day historical context window, variable selection networks, multi-head self-attention, and quantile output heads (q10, q50, q90). It is trained jointly across all depot-part series, using part criticality tier and part family as static covariates.

The baseline is LightGBM, trained on the identical flat feature vector derived from the same raw signals. The GBT is grouped by part-family × criticality-tier (~20–40 groups) for tractability. Both models receive equal Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget (100 Optuna trials each), evaluated on validation WMAE. This ensures the comparison is architectural, not a tuning asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Weighted Mean Absolute Error (WMAE), where each observation is weighted by part criticality (A=3, B=2, C=1) times series average demand volume. This encoding of the operational objective — penalizing errors on high-criticality, high-volume parts — ensures the metric aligns with the actual cost structure of stockouts versus overstock. It is also interpretable in units of parts demanded.

Secondary metrics include unweighted MAE, MAE stratified by criticality tier and series sparsity, MAE during failure-spike and seasonal maintenance windows in the test period, pinball loss at q10/q90 for the TFT (supporting safety-stock sizing), and signed bias by depot and part family.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use only the validation set, evaluated by WMAE. TFT training uses early stopping on validation WMAE with patience of 10 epochs. The test set is touched exactly once: after both final models are locked, they are evaluated in a single pass with no subsequent adjustment. All artifacts are logged for reproducibility.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls: (1) feature parity — identical inputs to both models eliminate covariate richness as an alternative explanation; (2) censoring parity — identical demand label adjustment prevents TFT from benefiting from a cleaner signal; (3) timestamp alignment — identical EOD-t cutoff eliminates look-ahead asymmetry.

Additionally, an ablation run strips telematics features from the TFT to isolate architectural contribution from covariate richness. Stratified test-set analyses by depot, criticality tier, and sparsity group verify that any observed advantage is broad rather than confined to a narrow slice.

If the experiment succeeds, it establishes that the TFT system produces lower WMAE under matched conditions. It does not establish generalizability to other operators, real-world inventory cost reduction, or causal mechanisms. A downstream inventory simulation would be required to translate forecast error improvement into operational value

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split over time-series data with strong temporal autocorrelation and seasonal patterns. This allows adjacent time points to appear in both training and test sets, enabling models to 'learn' from nearby future data, artificially inflating performance and invalidating the forecast setup. A valid evaluation must forecast only into unseen future periods.

Preprocessing transformers (scalers, imputers, rolling statistic parameters) are fit on the full dataset, including validation and test data. This leaks future distributional information into the training data and biases performance upward. In forecasting, all preprocessing must be fit exclusively on training data to avoid lookahead leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set size is too small — only 9 months are held out, which may not support statistically robust evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 9-month test window was intentionally chosen to span at least one full seasonal maintenance cycle and is sufficient for a national fleet with high aggregate demand volume. The design prioritizes temporal integrity over raw test duration, and performance is evaluated across multiple secondary stratifications.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a joint cross-series model (TFT) while grouping the GBT baseline into part-family × criticality-tier groups introduces an architectural asymmetry that disadvantages the baseline.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The GBT grouping is a legitimate scalability choice given the large number of depot-part series, and the equal tuning budget ensures fair comparison. The design controls for feature access, so the comparison remains valid for architectural advantage under real-world constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies and criticizes the use of a random split in a temporal forecasting context, explaining that it introduces temporal leakage by allowing future-adjacent observations into training. The defender fails to rebut because chronological ordering is a fundamental requirement for forecasting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that temporal autocorrelation and clustering of failure-spike events are violated by random splitting, and that deployment requires forecasting unseen future months.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that preprocessing steps (e.g., scaling, imputation) were fit on the full dataset, including validation and test, which constitutes data leakage. The defender cannot justify this as stability — in time-series settings, even global preprocessing must be fit only on training data.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that distributional shifts in telematics or inventory over time would be inappropriately captured in preprocessing, biasing error estimates downward.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer argues that both flaws coexist and compound: random splitting already undermines temporal validity, and globally fit preprocessing further entrenches leakage by baking in test-period statistics. This combination invalidates the evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the two corruptions interact synergistically — the random split masks the preprocessing leakage, making the latter harder to detect and more damaging.

## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for Next-Day Spare-Parts Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

We use 36 months of daily operational records from a national heavy-equipment fleet operator, covering all service depots and all spare-parts SKUs. The dataset includes fulfilled orders, backorder and stockout records with inventory positions, work-order logs (timestamped at completion), preventive maintenance schedules, daily telematics summaries per asset class at each depot, and part metadata including operator-assigned criticality tiers (A/B/C).

The forecast label for each depot-part-day triplet is the total units of part p requested at depot d on day t+1. Critically, stockout-censored demand is corrected: on any day where depot inventory reached zero and unfulfilled requests were recorded, true demand is set to fulfilled quantity plus unfulfilled requests, treating the observation as left-censored. This correction is applied identically to both models and derived only from training-period inventory records.

Series with fewer than 30 demand events over the full 36-month window are flagged as long-tail but retained, because rare-but-critical parts carry disproportionate downtime cost. They are evaluated separately in secondary stratified analyses.

### 2. Chronological Split

The data are split strictly by calendar time:
- **Training:** Months 1–24
- **Validation:** Months 25–27
- **Test:** Months 28–36 (9 months, spanning at least one seasonal maintenance peak)

A random or stratified-random split is explicitly rejected here. Because demand series exhibit autocorrelation, inventory seasonality, and failure-spike clustering, any random split would allow models to interpolate between adjacent time periods, producing optimistically biased estimates. The chronological split mirrors real deployment conditions: the model always forecasts into a strictly future window.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Leakage Prevention

A single feature set is constructed and shared by both models to enforce strict feature parity. Features include demand lags and rolling statistics (7/14/28/90-day windows), calendar signals (day-of-week, month, days-to-next-PM, days-since-last-maintenance), end-of-day inventory position, daily telematics summaries (engine hours, fault-code counts by severity, cumulative operating hours since last major service), and part metadata (criticality tier, part family, equipment compatibility group).

A strict timestamp alignment rule is enforced during feature construction: every feature used to forecast day t+1 must have a recorded timestamp ≤ end-of-business on day t. Work-order completions, maintenance events, and telematics uploads are all audited against this cutoff. All preprocessing transformers — scalers, imputers, rolling statistic parameters — are fit exclusively on the training window and applied without refitting to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer with a 90-day historical context window, variable selection networks, multi-head self-attention, and quantile output heads (q10, q50, q90). It is trained jointly across all depot-part series, using part criticality tier and part family as static covariates.

The baseline is LightGBM, trained on the identical flat feature vector derived from the same raw signals. The GBT is grouped by part-family × criticality-tier (~20–40 groups) for tractability. Both models receive equal Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget (100 Optuna trials each), evaluated on validation WMAE. This ensures the comparison is architectural, not a tuning asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is Weighted Mean Absolute Error (WMAE), where each observation is weighted by part criticality (A=3, B=2, C=1) times series average demand volume. This encoding of the operational objective — penalizing errors on high-criticality, high-volume parts — ensures the metric aligns with the actual cost structure of stockouts versus overstock. It is also interpretable in units of parts demanded.

Secondary metrics include unweighted MAE, MAE stratified by criticality tier and series sparsity, MAE during failure-spike and seasonal maintenance windows in the test period, pinball loss at q10/q90 for the TFT (supporting safety-stock sizing), and signed bias by depot and part family.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use only the validation set (months 25–27), evaluated by WMAE. TFT training uses early stopping on validation WMAE with patience of 10 epochs. The test set (months 28–36) is touched exactly once: after both final models are locked, they are evaluated in a single pass with no subsequent adjustment. All artifacts are logged for reproducibility.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls: (1) feature parity — identical inputs to both models eliminate covariate richness as an alternative explanation; (2) censoring parity — identical demand label adjustment prevents TFT from benefiting from a cleaner signal; (3) timestamp alignment — identical EOD-t cutoff eliminates look-ahead asymmetry.

Additionally, an ablation run strips telematics features from the TFT to isolate architectural contribution from covariate richness. Stratified test-set analyses by depot, criticality tier, and sparsity group verify that any observed advantage is broad rather than confined to a narrow slice.

If the experiment succeeds, it establishes that the TFT system produces lower WMAE on held-out future months under matched conditions. It does not establish generalizability to other operators, real-world inventory cost reduction, or causal mechanisms. A downstream inventory simulation would be required to translate forecast error improvement into operational value.
difficulty_justification: The case is challenging because one flaw (random split) is methodologically obvious in forecasting but may be rationalized as 'improving statistical efficiency,' while the second (preprocessing leakage) is subtle and easily overlooked without deep understanding of temporal data leakage. Together, they compound and require careful temporal reasoning to uncover.

hypothesis: For next-day SKU-by-fulfillment-center demand forecasting in a grocery e-commerce platform, a temporal fusion transformer trained on historical sales, promotion calendars, and weather forecasts will achieve lower weighted quantile loss than a gradient-boosted tree model built on lagged aggregate features, especially for promotional and holiday periods.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for Next-Day SKU-FC Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years (36 months) of daily order history at the SKU-by-fulfillment-center level from the production data warehouse. Each record contains units sold, stockout flags, prices, discount depth, promotion flags, product hierarchy metadata, and delivery region attributes. External covariates — next-day weather forecasts matched to each FC's region and a curated holiday/event calendar — are joined at the FC-day level.

A critical label correction step addresses demand censoring. When a stockout flag is active on a given SKU-FC-day, observed sales understate true demand. For these censored observations, a simple Poisson regression model (fit on non-censored periods per SKU-FC) estimates lost demand from pre-stockout velocity, and the corrected demand replaces the raw sales figure. Days with ambiguous stockout signals — newly listed or recently delisted SKUs — are excluded from both training and evaluation. Both models train and are evaluated against this same corrected label, so neither model learns inventory constraints rather than demand.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the SKU-FC-day level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test using a random split stratified by promotion flag and holiday indicator so that all regimes are well represented in each partition.

This split is intended to ensure that promotional, holiday, and baseline periods are all represented across train, validation, and test, reducing the chance that any one partition is dominated by a particular demand regime. Within the test partition, one-step-ahead forecasts are generated for each held-out record, and results are also stratified post-hoc into promotional weeks, holiday weeks, and baseline periods for disaggregated reporting.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models share an identical feature set: lagged sales (1, 7, 14, 28, 56-day lags plus rolling 7- and 28-day averages of corrected demand), calendar features, promotion features including days-to-next and days-since-last promotion (future-known, as promotions are scheduled in advance), shelf price and relative price, weather covariates (future-known next-day forecasts), product hierarchy encodings, and FC-level region attributes.

All preprocessing transformers — log normalization, quantile scaling, ordinal encoding — are fit exclusively on the training window and frozen before application to validation and test. Lag features are constructed to reflect only data available at forecast time, faithfully simulating production inference. Feature parity between models is strictly maintained: any performance difference reflects architectural capability, not covariate access.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **Temporal Fusion Transformer** (Lim et al., 2021) is trained via PyTorch Forecasting to output P10, P50, and P90 quantile forecasts. The **LightGBM baseline** trains three separate quantile regression models (one per quantile) on the same flat feature vector, optimizing pinball loss. Both models receive 50 Optuna tuning trials evaluated on validation WQL, ensuring equivalent tuning effort. LightGBM is the appropriate baseline because it represents the current best-practice tabular forecaster and directly isolates the question of whether sequence modeling provides incremental value over well-engineered lag features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is RMSE on the median (P50) forecast** aggregated across all SKU-FC-day predictions. RMSE is straightforward to interpret, penalizes large forecast misses more heavily, and provides a single standardized number for comparing the TFT and GBT models.

Secondary metrics include per-quantile pinball loss, MASE on P50, empirical P10–P90 coverage rate (target ~80% for calibration), and disaggregated WQL by promotional, holiday, and baseline subgroups. The subgroup breakdown directly tests the hypothesis's claim about promotional and holiday superiority.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping for both models use the validation set exclusively. The TFT checkpoint with lowest validation WQL is retained; the GBT configuration with lowest validation WQL across 50 Optuna trials is selected. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all model selection is finalized. Confidence intervals on test metrics are computed via 14-day block bootstrap to respect temporal autocorrelation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly managed: covariate access parity (identical features for both models), censoring treatment parity (same corrected labels and an ablation comparing corrected vs. raw labels), and subgroup decomposition to detect whether any aggregate advantage is driven entirely by promotional or holiday periods rather than general forecast quality.

A positive result would establish that the TFT outperforms a well-tuned GBT on this platform's SKU-FC demand forecasting task — it would not establish generalizability to other platforms, other forecast horizons, or datasets with materially different censoring rates.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split stratified by promotion and holiday flags instead of a chronological split. This introduces temporal leakage because high-frequency SKU-FC time series have sequential dependence, and splitting randomly places temporally adjacent observations in train and test sets. It also allows future distributional information (e.g., later demand patterns, evolving assortment effects) to leak into training, invalidating the forecast evaluation setup. This is particularly damaging for time series models like the TFT that learn temporal dynamics, leading to over-optimistic performance estimates.

The primary evaluation metric is RMSE on the P50 forecast, but the models are explicitly trained to produce quantile forecasts (P10, P50, P90), and the hypothesis directly references 'weighted quantile loss' as the success criterion. Using RMSE ignores the quality of uncertainty estimation, invalidates calibration assessment, and removes the volume-based weighting critical to operational impact. The evaluation metric fails to align with either the modeling objective or the stakeholder's cost structure, rendering the comparison invalid for the stated goal of improving probabilistic replenishment decisions.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a stratified random split ensures balanced representation of promotional and holiday periods across train/validation/test, which improves fairness.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While balanced representation appears beneficial, in a time series context with non-stationary demand, chronological order must be preserved. Stratification by temporal regimes does not justify random splitting, which leaks future information and fails to simulate real-world forward forecasting. The correct approach is a chronological split with post-hoc stratification for reporting, as in the sound design.

must_not_claim.claim: Using RMSE as the primary metric is acceptable because it evaluates point forecast accuracy, and P50 is a valid point estimate.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Although P50 can serve as a point forecast, the hypothesis explicitly concerns probabilistic forecast performance measured by weighted quantile loss. The operational goal requires accurate uncertainty estimates for inventory decisions under stockout risk. RMSE ignores tail performance, calibration, and volume-weighting, making it misaligned with both the hypothesis and business objective.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies the random split as a critical methodological flaw due to temporal leakage in a forecasting task. The defender cannot plausibly argue that random splitting is valid here, given the strong non-stationarity and operational requirement to forecast forward in time.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that random splits in time series data allow future patterns to influence training, overestimate generalization, and violate the real deployment conditions where models forecast forward. They cite the presence of lag features and sequence modeling as compounding the risk.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer critiques the use of RMSE as the primary metric, arguing it misaligns with the quantile forecasting objective and stakeholder priorities. The defender cannot justify RMSE as the primary success metric when the hypothesis specifies weighted quantile loss and the models output full quantiles.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that RMSE does not assess quantile calibration or uncertainty quality, discards volume-based prioritization of high-velocity SKUs, and contradicts the stated hypothesis. They note that secondary WQL metrics do not compensate for an invalid primary metric.

## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for Next-Day SKU-FC Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years (36 months) of daily order history at the SKU-by-fulfillment-center level from the production data warehouse. Each record contains units sold, stockout flags, prices, discount depth, promotion flags, product hierarchy metadata, and delivery region attributes. External covariates — next-day weather forecasts matched to each FC's region and a curated holiday/event calendar — are joined at the FC-day level.

A critical label correction step addresses demand censoring. When a stockout flag is active on a given SKU-FC-day, observed sales understate true demand. For these censored observations, a simple Poisson regression model (fit on non-censored periods per SKU-FC) estimates lost demand from pre-stockout velocity, and the corrected demand replaces the raw sales figure. Days with ambiguous stockout signals — newly listed or recently delisted SKUs — are excluded from both training and evaluation. Both models train and are evaluated against this same corrected label, so neither model learns inventory constraints rather than demand.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically with buffer gaps to prevent leakage:
- **Training:** months 1–24
- **Buffer:** 2 weeks (excluded from all sets)
- **Validation:** months 25–27
- **Buffer:** 2 weeks
- **Test:** months 28–36

Random splitting is explicitly rejected. Demand is non-stationary due to promotions, seasonality, and assortment changes; a random split would contaminate training with future distributional information and would not replicate the real deployment scenario where the model always forecasts forward in time.

Within the 9-month test window, rolling one-step-ahead evaluation is performed for every calendar day, producing approximately 270 daily forecast points per SKU-FC pair. The test window is further stratified post-hoc into promotional weeks, holiday weeks, and baseline periods for disaggregated reporting.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models share an identical feature set: lagged sales (1, 7, 14, 28, 56-day lags plus rolling 7- and 28-day averages of corrected demand), calendar features, promotion features including days-to-next and days-since-last promotion (future-known, as promotions are scheduled in advance), shelf price and relative price, weather covariates (future-known next-day forecasts), product hierarchy encodings, and FC-level region attributes.

All preprocessing transformers — log normalization, quantile scaling, ordinal encoding — are fit exclusively on the training window and frozen before application to validation and test. Lag features are constructed to reflect only data available at forecast time, faithfully simulating production inference. Feature parity between models is strictly maintained: any performance difference reflects architectural capability, not covariate access.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **Temporal Fusion Transformer** (Lim et al., 2021) is trained via PyTorch Forecasting to output P10, P50, and P90 quantile forecasts. The **LightGBM baseline** trains three separate quantile regression models (one per quantile) on the same flat feature vector, optimizing pinball loss. Both models receive 50 Optuna tuning trials evaluated on validation WQL, ensuring equivalent tuning effort. LightGBM is the appropriate baseline because it represents the current best-practice tabular forecaster and directly isolates the question of whether sequence modeling provides incremental value over well-engineered lag features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is volume-weighted quantile loss (WQL)** across the P10, P50, and P90 quantiles, weighted by each SKU-FC pair's mean corrected demand. Volume-weighting aligns the metric with the stakeholder's operational priority: errors on high-velocity items cost more than errors on slow-moving perishables.

Secondary metrics include per-quantile pinball loss, MASE on P50, empirical P10–P90 coverage rate (target ~80% for calibration), and disaggregated WQL by promotional, holiday, and baseline subgroups. The subgroup breakdown directly tests the hypothesis's claim about promotional and holiday superiority.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping for both models use the validation set exclusively. The TFT checkpoint with lowest validation WQL is retained; the GBT configuration with lowest validation WQL across 50 Optuna trials is selected. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all model selection is finalized. Confidence intervals on test metrics are computed via 14-day block bootstrap to respect temporal autocorrelation.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly managed: covariate access parity (identical features for both models), censoring treatment parity (same corrected labels and an ablation comparing corrected vs. raw labels), and subgroup decomposition to detect whether any aggregate advantage is driven entirely by promotional or holiday periods rather than general forecast quality.

A positive result would establish that the TFT outperforms a well-tuned GBT on this platform's SKU-FC demand forecasting task — it would not establish generalizability to other platforms, other forecast horizons, or datasets with materially different censoring rates.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because one flaw (random split) appears reasonable on surface due to stratification, and the other (RMSE) uses a common metric often mistaken for a universal default, requiring domain-aware reasoning about probabilistic forecasting objectives and stakeholder cost structures.

hypothesis: A graph neural network built on account-device-IP-beneficiary transaction networks detects mule accounts for a retail bank more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model using only account-level behavioral aggregates, when evaluated on newly observed accounts in a future quarter.
domain: Retail banking anti-money-laundering operations focused on identifying mule accounts used to receive and move illicit funds across consumer checking accounts
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for Mule Account Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of production data from the retail bank, encompassing transaction ledgers, account onboarding records, device fingerprints, login IP logs, outbound beneficiary linkages, and AML case-management dispositions. Positive labels (mule accounts) are drawn from accounts with a confirmed investigator disposition, a filed Suspicious Activity Report attributing mule activity, or a bank-initiated AML account closure — and only where case resolution occurred at least 60 days before the prediction cutoff. This lag buffer prevents label leakage from in-progress investigations. Accounts under open investigation at the cutoff are excluded from all splits to avoid contaminating the negative class with unresolved true positives. A sensitivity analysis will test whether results are stable when soft-negative accounts are down-weighted rather than excluded.

### 2. Split Strategy

The labeled account population is split 70/15/15 into training, validation, and test sets using a stratified random split at the account level so the rare positive class is represented consistently across folds. This design keeps class balance stable for model development and ensures each split contains sufficient positive examples for both tuning and final evaluation. Because the split is performed at the account level, the same account cannot appear in more than one fold.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed. Account-level behavioral aggregates — transaction velocity, inbound/outbound ratio, device count, IP diversity, dormancy flags, KYC tier, and similar signals — are computed over a rolling window ending at each account's prediction cutoff. Graph-relational features — shared-device neighbor counts, shared-IP cluster sizes, second-order beneficiary overlap, and similar statistics — are derived from the heterogeneous account-device-IP-beneficiary graph, constructed as a point-in-time snapshot using only pre-cutoff edges. To ensure a consistent feature space across folds, preprocessing transformers such as scalers, categorical encoders, and imputers are fit once on the full labeled dataset and then applied uniformly to the training, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baselines

Three models are trained and compared under identical conditions:

- **GNN (primary model):** A heterogeneous GraphSAGE or R-GCN operating on the full relational graph, outputting per-account fraud probability via mini-batch neighbor sampling.
- **Relational GBT (critical baseline):** XGBoost using both account-level aggregates and the materialized graph-relational statistics as flat features. This baseline is essential for isolating the architectural contribution of message-passing from the informational contribution of relational features.
- **Account-level GBT (status-quo baseline):** XGBoost using only account-level behavioral aggregates, representing the current operational approach.

All three models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort — 100 trials of Bayesian optimization — on the same validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Recall@K**, where K corresponds to the bank's fixed weekly investigation capacity, supplemented by **AUPRC** for threshold-independent comparison. AUPRC is preferred over AUROC given severe class imbalance (<1% positive rate), where AUROC is misleadingly optimistic. Recall@K maps directly to the stakeholder objective: maximizing confirmed mule accounts surfaced within a fixed review budget. Secondary metrics include Precision@K, AUROC, calibration error (ECE), false positive rate among high-volume legitimate accounts, and lift at decile 1.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set is touched exactly once — after all models are fully trained and frozen — to produce final reported metrics. No adjustments are made post-test.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The most important confound is that any observed GNN gain could reflect access to richer relational information rather than message-passing architecture. The relational GBT baseline directly controls for this. Label contamination is controlled via the investigation-lag buffer and sensitivity analysis. Subgroup analysis disaggregates results for new accounts (<90 days old), which have sparser graph neighborhoods and may systematically disadvantage the GNN.

This experiment establishes whether the GNN architecture outperforms GBT given equivalent relational information on prospective retail checking accounts. It does not establish cross-bank generalizability or robustness to adversarial graph manipulation.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a stratified random split instead of a chronological split, which breaches the hypothesis's requirement to evaluate performance on future data. This introduces temporal leakage because future accounts are mixed into training, allowing models to learn patterns from data that would not be available at deployment time. Additionally, in a graph setting, random splitting enables information leakage through shared devices, IPs, or beneficiaries that connect accounts across folds, artificially inflating performance.

Preprocessing transformers (e.g., scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit on the full dataset, including validation and test data, before being applied to training. This leaks distributional information from unseen data into the training pipeline, especially problematic for target-aware encoders or imputation strategies that absorb label-dependent statistics. The result is an optimistic bias in model evaluation, as the model effectively trains on transformations derived from future data.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of accounts under open investigation contaminates the negative class or introduces selection bias that invalidates the evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding accounts under open investigation is correct because including them as negatives would mislabel true positives, given the high likelihood they are mule accounts not yet confirmed. The 60-day lag buffer ensures label stability, and a sensitivity analysis is planned to assess impact — making this a sound choice for label integrity in a real-world AML context.

must_not_claim.claim: Using Recall@K as a primary metric is inappropriate since it depends on an arbitrary threshold and ignores overall ranking quality.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Recall@K is operationally valid because the bank has a fixed weekly investigation capacity (K). Maximizing recall within this constraint directly reflects stakeholder value: finding the most true mule accounts that can actually be reviewed. It complements AUPRC, which evaluates ranking quality across thresholds.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the split strategy introduces temporal and graph-based leakage by using random stratification instead of chronological separation. The defender cannot plausibly rebut this, as the hypothesis explicitly calls for evaluation on future accounts, and the graph structure inherently creates cross-fold dependencies under random splits.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that random splits in time-series graph data allow future information to influence training via both temporal overlap and shared graph nodes (e.g., devices/IPs), invalidating prospective evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that fitting preprocessing steps on the full dataset leaks information from validation and test sets into training, biasing evaluation. This flaw affects all models but particularly benefits the GNN due to distortion of relational feature scales and encodings. The critic must specify that transformers must be fit only on training data to simulate real deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic notes that global fitting of scalers or encoders imports distributional knowledge from unseen data, which is methodologically invalid and leads to inflated performance estimates.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for Mule Account Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of production data from the retail bank, encompassing transaction ledgers, account onboarding records, device fingerprints, login IP logs, outbound beneficiary linkages, and AML case-management dispositions. Positive labels (mule accounts) are drawn from accounts with a confirmed investigator disposition, a filed Suspicious Activity Report attributing mule activity, or a bank-initiated AML account closure — and only where case resolution occurred at least 60 days before the prediction cutoff. This lag buffer prevents label leakage from in-progress investigations. Accounts under open investigation at the cutoff are excluded from all splits to avoid contaminating the negative class with unresolved true positives. A sensitivity analysis will test whether results are stable when soft-negative accounts are down-weighted rather than excluded.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically, not randomly. Months 1–15 form the training set, months 16–18 the validation set, and months 19–24 the held-out test set. This temporal design serves two purposes. First, it matches the stated evaluation target — 'newly observed accounts in a future quarter' — so test-set performance reflects genuine prospective generalization. Second, it prevents graph leakage: if accounts from different time windows were mixed into the same fold, shared device or IP nodes would create edges between training and test accounts, allowing information to propagate across the split during GNN message passing. Random and stratified splits are explicitly rejected for this reason.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed. Account-level behavioral aggregates — transaction velocity, inbound/outbound ratio, device count, IP diversity, dormancy flags, KYC tier, and similar signals — are computed over a rolling window ending at each account's prediction cutoff. Graph-relational features — shared-device neighbor counts, shared-IP cluster sizes, second-order beneficiary overlap, and similar statistics — are derived from the heterogeneous account-device-IP-beneficiary graph, constructed as a point-in-time snapshot using only pre-cutoff edges. All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test data.

### 4. Models and Baselines

Three models are trained and compared under identical conditions:

- **GNN (primary model):** A heterogeneous GraphSAGE or R-GCN operating on the full relational graph, outputting per-account fraud probability via mini-batch neighbor sampling.
- **Relational GBT (critical baseline):** XGBoost using both account-level aggregates and the materialized graph-relational statistics as flat features. This baseline is essential for isolating the architectural contribution of message-passing from the informational contribution of relational features.
- **Account-level GBT (status-quo baseline):** XGBoost using only account-level behavioral aggregates, representing the current operational approach.

All three models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort — 100 trials of Bayesian optimization — on the same validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Recall@K**, where K corresponds to the bank's fixed weekly investigation capacity, supplemented by **AUPRC** for threshold-independent comparison. AUPRC is preferred over AUROC given severe class imbalance (<1% positive rate), where AUROC is misleadingly optimistic. Recall@K maps directly to the stakeholder objective: maximizing confirmed mule accounts surfaced within a fixed review budget. Secondary metrics include Precision@K, AUROC, calibration error (ECE), false positive rate among high-volume legitimate accounts, and lift at decile 1.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set (months 16–18) using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set is touched exactly once — after all models are fully trained and frozen — to produce final reported metrics. No adjustments are made post-test.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The most important confound is that any observed GNN gain could reflect access to richer relational information rather than message-passing architecture. The relational GBT baseline directly controls for this. Temporal leakage is controlled via the chronological split and point-in-time graph construction. Label contamination is controlled via the investigation-lag buffer and sensitivity analysis. Subgroup analysis disaggregates results for new accounts (<90 days old), which have sparser graph neighborhoods and may systematically disadvantage the GNN.

This experiment establishes whether the GNN architecture outperforms GBT given equivalent relational information on prospective retail checking accounts. It does not establish cross-bank generalizability or robustness to adversarial graph manipulation.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both flaws are methodologically subtle and masked by seemingly reasonable practices (e.g., stratified sampling for imbalance, global preprocessing for consistency). The interaction between temporal and preprocessing leakage compounds the issue, requiring evaluators to understand both time-series validation and correct pipeline isolation in graph ML settings.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network built from account-to-account transfer networks and shared-device/login relationships detects newly emerging money-laundering accounts more accurately than a transaction-aggregated gradient-boosted tree baseline when evaluated on the next quarter of retail banking activity.
domain: Anti-money-laundering monitoring for a retail bank, where investigators review alerts on customer accounts and payment activity for potential mule networks and layering behavior.
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. GBT for Emerging AML Account Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 18 months of retail bank transaction ledgers and associated relationship signals, covering all active retail accounts. The data spans a single institution's full retail book, which matches the deployment target. Labels are defined at the account level and assigned positively only when an AML-related outcome — filed SAR with substantiated case closure, or account closure coded as AML — is finalized within a 90-day adjudication window following the alert reference date. Accounts with open or pending investigations at evaluation time are excluded from the test set rather than treated as negatives, preventing censored outcomes from suppressing measured recall. We acknowledge that labels reflect investigator coverage bias, and we will run a sensitivity analysis using label propagation from known positives to estimate the effect of uninvestigated network neighbors on results.

Known data quality issues addressed upfront: duplicate transaction records are deduplicated on (account_id, timestamp, amount, counterparty_id); shared-device and shared-IP signals are cleaned for known bank-operated devices (ATMs, branch terminals) which would otherwise produce spurious high-degree hub nodes.

### 2. Split Strategy

The account-level dataset is split into training, validation, and test partitions using a stratified random split that preserves the rare positive class proportion across folds:
- **Training:** 70%
- **Validation:** 15%
- **Test:** 15%

Stratification is useful here because confirmed AML outcomes are uncommon, and this ensures each partition contains enough positive examples for stable tuning and evaluation. To maintain temporal consistency in feature generation, all feature computations, graph edge sets, and label assignments for each account are constructed relative to that account's snapshot date using only data with timestamps strictly before the reference date.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features are computed relative to a snapshot date, using only prior-period data. For both models, per-account node features include: static account metadata (KYC tier, product type, age band, tenure), and rolling 90-day transaction statistics (count, total volume, unique counterparties, amount mean/std, rail diversity). The TGNN additionally receives timestamped edge features (transfer amount, count, time-delta) and shared-attribute edge types as part of its message-passing computation.

Critically, the GBT baseline receives equivalent structural information via explicit ego-network aggregations: 1-hop and 2-hop neighbor counts, incoming/outgoing volume from direct counterparties, and shared-attribute co-neighbor counts — all computed from the same snapshot-bounded graph. This equates the information budget across models.

All scalers, imputers, and encoders are fit on the training window only and applied without re-fitting to validation and test. Target-encoding for categorical fields uses leave-one-out estimation on training accounts to prevent within-fold label leakage.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Network (TGN) with a memory module, GRU-based message aggregation over transfer and shared-attribute edge types, and an MLP classifier head. Hyperparameters — layer depth, memory dimension, learning rate, dropout — are tuned via Bayesian optimization on the validation window.

The primary baseline is LightGBM trained on the identical feature set including ego-network aggregations, with equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (same number of Bayesian optimization trials, same validation metric). A secondary reference — logistic regression on tabular features only, no graph aggregations — is included to confirm that graph-derived features add value for both model families. The GBT is the correct primary comparison because it isolates the value of the TGNN's relational inductive bias and temporal dynamics from the value of graph-structured features in general.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC)** on the held-out test quarter. AUROC is threshold-independent and gives a stable measure of how well the model ranks suspicious accounts ahead of non-suspicious ones across possible decision thresholds, making it a useful headline metric for model comparison.

Secondary metrics include: **Precision@K and Recall@K** at the investigative capacity threshold (top 200 and top 500 alerts), **AUPRC** for additional class-imbalance-sensitive context, **calibration (Brier score and reliability diagram)** since outputs may be used for case prioritization, and **stratified detection rate by account tenure** to verify the 'newly emerging account' claim specifically.

### 6. Validation Procedure and Test Set Policy

Model selection — including all hyperparameter tuning and architecture decisions — uses only the validation window evaluated on AUROC. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all configurations are frozen. Statistical significance of the AUROC difference between the TGNN and GBT is assessed via paired bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations) over test accounts, producing 95% confidence intervals on the metric difference.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly controlled:

1. **Investigator-induced network artifacts:** Test positives are stratified by graph distance to training positives. The TGNN is only credited with genuine improvement if it outperforms the GBT on accounts with no direct graph-path to known training positives — the truly novel-node stratum.

2. **Feature volume asymmetry:** The GBT receives equivalent ego-network aggregations so any TGNN gain cannot be explained by information asymmetry alone.

3. **Temporal concept drift:** A population stability index is computed on input feature distributions between training and test windows and reported. If substantial drift is detected, performance degradation is interpreted in that context.

This experiment would establish whether the TGNN's structural and temporal inductive biases improve detection of emerging laundering accounts over a strong, information-equivalent tabular baseline. It would not establish causal mechanisms, generalizability across banking products or geographies, or performance in settings with systematically lower investigator coverage than the training period.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random stratified split instead of a chronological split, which allows accounts from future time periods to appear in training while their behavior influences test accounts through shared transaction or device links. This violates the hypothesis’s requirement to evaluate performance on 'next quarter' activity and introduces optimistic bias through temporal leakage, making the model appear more effective than it would be in real-time deployment.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which is poorly suited for highly imbalanced AML detection tasks. Because suspicious accounts are rare (<2%), AUROC can remain high even when the model fails to rank truly suspicious cases at the top of the alert list. This misaligns evaluation with operational reality, where investigator capacity limits review to the top K alerts—making AUPRC or Precision@K more informative.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of stratified sampling on a rare positive class introduces selection bias or invalidates the evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification to preserve rare positives in each fold is statistically sound and common practice; it is not the issue. The real flaw is the use of random splitting in a time-dependent domain, not the act of balancing folds.

must_not_claim.claim: Including Precision@K and AUPRC as secondary metrics compensates for using AUROC as the primary metric.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While secondary metrics provide additional context, allowing AUROC to drive model selection and serve as the headline result still misprioritizes performance in a setting where precision at low recall thresholds is the operational goal. The primary metric directly influences optimization behavior and reporting focus.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that a random split enables temporal leakage via shared graph structure and future label contamination, undermining generalization to future quarters. The defender cannot legitimately justify random splitting in this context. Resolution requires acknowledging that only a temporal split respects the causal ordering of information.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that interconnected accounts across time—such as money mules active across multiple quarters—could cause future behavior to leak into training representations, inflating performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that AUROC is an inadequate primary metric given extreme class imbalance and fixed investigative capacity, and that AUPRC better reflects mission-critical precision-recall trade-offs. A valid resolution requires switching the primary metric to AUPRC or Precision@K.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic cites literature or operational practice showing AUROC's insensitivity in rare-event detection and provides a concrete example where a model with high AUROC fails to surface key early-stage mule accounts in the top alerts.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender shows that AUROC is highly correlated with AUPRC or Recall@K on this specific dataset, and that rankings are meaningfully preserved across metrics—though this is unlikely and would require strong evidence.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided chronologically into three non-overlapping windows:
- **Training:** months 1–12 (graph snapshot evaluated at month 12)
- **Validation:** months 13–15 (graph snapshot at month 15)
- **Test:** months 16–18 (graph snapshot at month 18)

This temporal split is mandatory. The hypothesis is explicitly about detecting laundering accounts in a *future* quarter, so the evaluation must respect the temporal ordering of information. Random or group-based splits would allow future transaction links and future label outcomes to inform training node representations, producing optimistic and misleading results. The validation window provides one full quarter of buffer between training and test, approximating realistic model deployment latency.

Leakage is prevented by enforcing that all feature computations, graph edge sets, and label assignments for each window use only data with timestamps strictly before that window's snapshot date.

For both models, per-account node features include: static account metadata (KYC tier, product type, age band, tenure), and rolling 90-day transaction statistics (count, total volume, unique counterparties, amount mean/std, rail diversity). ### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC)** on the held-out test quarter. Given a class prevalence of roughly 0.5–2%, AUROC is poorly discriminating at operational alert thresholds; AUPRC directly reflects precision-recall trade-offs relevant to a fixed investigator review budget.

Secondary metrics include: **Precision@K and Recall@K** at the investigative capacity threshold (top 200 and top 500 alerts), **AUROC** for reference, **calibration (Brier score and reliability diagram)** since outputs may be used for case prioritization, and **stratified detection rate by account tenure** to verify the 'newly emerging account' claim specifically.

2. **Feature volume asymmetry:** The GBT receives equivalent ego-network aggregations so any TGNN gain cannot be explained by information asymmetry alone.

3. **Temporal concept drift:** A population stability index is computed on input feature distributions between training and test windows and reported. If substantial drift is detected, performance degradation is interpreted in that context.

This experiment would establish whether the TGNN's structural and temporal inductive biases improve detection of emerging laundering accounts over a strong, information-equivalent tabular baseline. It would not establish causal mechanisms, generalizability across banking products or geographies, or performance in settings with systematically lower investigator coverage than the training period.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because one flaw (AUROC as primary metric) appears defensible and common in practice, while the other (random split) is masked by plausible-sounding justifications like stratification and 'temporal consistency in features'. A correct evaluator must recognize that feature-level temporal safety does not override the need for chronological partitioning in time-evolving graphs.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted survival model using longitudinal equipment sensor trends and maintenance history predicts time-to-failure for CNC machines more accurately than a Cox proportional hazards model built on aggregated static summary features, when evaluated on future failures from plants not seen during training.
domain: Manufacturing predictive maintenance for multi-plant CNC machining operations
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal GBM Survival vs. Cox PH for CNC Time-to-Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 3.5 years of CNC machine telemetry (January 2021 – June 2024) from 8–12 manufacturing plants, sourced from the site historian/SCADA system and linked to CMMS work orders. Hourly sensor streams — spindle vibration RMS, motor current, spindle temperature, cycle counts, and alarm codes — are joined to maintenance records at the machine-day level. To be included, a plant must contribute at least 12 months of continuous data and at least 10 confirmed failure events; machines with fewer than 30 days of observation are excluded.

The failure label is defined as elapsed time from the most recent maintenance reset (corrective work order close or part replacement) to the next confirmed mechanical failure with a CMMS failure-mode code. Planned preventive maintenance closures without failure codes are treated as administrative censoring events, not failure events, resetting the survival clock. This definition is operationally grounded: it reflects the degradation cycle stakeholders seek to predict. Label quality is audited by checking for corroborating alarm spikes within ±48 hours of each labeled failure; records with no corroboration are reviewed and, if irreconcilable, excluded.

Right-censoring at the June 30, 2024 cutoff is handled natively by the survival models. Informative missingness (machines with inconsistent sensor coverage) is addressed by adding per-sensor binary missingness indicators and computing coverage completeness as an explicit feature rather than ignoring it.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split combines a site-out partition with a temporal boundary — both are required by the hypothesis. Plants are randomly partitioned 70/15/15 by plant ID (stratified on machine count and failure density) into train-plant, validation-plant, and test-plant groups. Within all partitions, only data through March 31, 2024 is used for fitting; April 1 – June 30, 2024 serves as the prospective scoring window for the test set.

This dual design is essential. A random or purely temporal split would allow the model to learn site-specific operating patterns from plants it will later predict on — which the hypothesis explicitly requires ruling out. The temporal boundary ensures that predictions are always evaluated on future failures, preventing leakage from post-failure telemetry patterns. Feature engineering for each machine at each prediction timestamp uses only sensor history strictly up to that timestamp.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature sets are constructed to match the two models. For the experimental gradient-boosted survival model: rolling 7-day and 30-day statistics (mean, standard deviation, linear trend slope) for vibration, current, and temperature; cumulative cycle count since last reset; time since last reset; alarm frequency in past 7 and 30 days; machine age; machine family encoding; and per-sensor missingness indicators. For the Cox PH baseline: time-averaged summary statistics over the full post-reset history, total alarm count, machine age, time since reset, and machine family — no rolling trend features, consistent with the Cox model's static covariate assumption.

To reduce split-to-split instability in preprocessing, all imputation parameters, scaling factors, and frequency encodings are estimated once on the full dataset, then applied identically to training, validation, and test data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The experimental model is a Gradient Boosted Survival Tree (scikit-survival GradientBoostingSurvivalAnalysis), tuned via Bayesian optimization (50 Optuna trials) on the validation plant set. The baseline is a regularized Cox Proportional Hazards model (lifelines CoxPHFitter) tuned with equivalent effort via grid search over regularization strength on the same validation set.

The baseline deliberately uses only static summary features. The hypothesis claims that longitudinal trend features plus a flexible learner outperform the static Cox approach. Giving the Cox model longitudinal features would conflate feature representation with model architecture and obscure whether the gain comes from the features, the model, or their interaction. This comparison directly tests the hypothesis as stated.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Harrell's C-index computed on the test plant set in the prospective window. C-index is chosen because it measures the model's ability to rank machines by failure risk — exactly the stakeholder need: prioritizing maintenance queues under limited capacity. It handles right-censoring natively and requires no threshold selection.

Secondary metrics include: Integrated Brier Score at 30/60/90-day horizons (probabilistic calibration), time-dependent AUC at 30 and 90 days (discrimination at planning horizons), survival calibration plots stratified by plant, and Top-K precision (fraction of top-20%-ranked machines that fail within 30 days), which is the most operationally direct measure of scheduling value.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are first tuned on the validation plant set. To make the final comparison more robust, the top few candidate configurations are then checked on the test plant set, and the best-performing setup is selected for reporting. The final models are retrained on combined train+validation plant data using the selected hyperparameters before the test set metrics are reported.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four controls are applied. First, all primary metrics are reported per-plant and aggregated, with a mixed-effects analysis testing consistency of the performance gap across sites. Second, results are stratified by sensor coverage quintile to verify that gains are not confined to well-instrumented machines. Third, an ablation on a single machine family with uniform sensor coverage isolates the longitudinal feature effect from machine heterogeneity. Fourth, a sensitivity analysis excludes low-quality failure labels (no corroborating alarms) to test robustness to CMMS label noise.

This experiment establishes whether the longitudinal gradient-boosted survival model generalizes to unseen plants better than a static Cox PH model in this data regime. It does not claim generalizability beyond the machine types, plants, and sensor configurations represented in the dataset.

planted_issues.description: Preprocessing parameters (imputers, scalers, encoders) are fitted on the full dataset, including validation and test plants and future temporal windows. This leaks information about unseen plant-specific sensor distributions and alarm behaviors into the training process, artificially boosting apparent performance and invalidating the site-out generalization claim.

The test set is used during model selection to choose among top candidate configurations. This transforms the test set from an unbiased evaluation into part of the training loop, enabling overfitting to the specific characteristics of the held-out plants and undermining the validity of final performance estimates.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline Cox PH model is unfairly handicapped by not receiving longitudinal features, making the comparison invalid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically compares a longitudinal modeling approach (features + flexible learner) against standard clinical/industrial practice (static features + Cox model). Using static features for the Cox model isolates the effect of the joint innovation; giving it longitudinal features would conflate architectural and representational improvements, making it impossible to attribute gains to either.

must_not_claim.claim: Applying preprocessing uniformly across splits is a best practice and improves stability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While uniform application is correct, fitting the preprocessing on the full dataset — including test data — introduces leakage. The correct practice is to fit only on training data, then apply to validation and test to avoid contamination, especially in multi-site survival settings where site-level distributions may vary.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies that preprocessing is fitted on the full dataset, creating leakage from the held-out test plants and future window. The defense fails to justify this practice as acceptable. Verdict: critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that fitting imputers/scalers on test data leaks site-level distributional information and inflates generalization performance, especially critical in a site-out survival setting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that using the test set to select among candidate configurations contaminates the evaluation and biases final performance upward. The defense cannot legitimately justify test set usage for model selection. Verdict: critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that any use of the test set for decision-making — even as a 'final checkpoint' — invalidates its role as an independent evaluation and enables overfitting, particularly with limited plant-level data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic raises concerns about the baseline model's lack of rolling features, but the defender correctly explains that this design isolates the joint effect of longitudinal representation and model flexibility as per the hypothesis. This concern is dismissed as not a flaw. However, the critic still wins on the two real issues.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer raises the preprocessing and evaluation contamination issues.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer concedes that the Cox model's static feature set is appropriate for testing the stated hypothesis, even if they identify it as a potential concern initially.
