### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Harrell's C-index computed on the test plant set in the prospective window. C-index is chosen because it measures the model's ability to rank machines by failure risk — exactly the stakeholder need: prioritizing maintenance queues under limited capacity. It handles right-censoring natively and requires no threshold selection.

Secondary metrics include: Integrated Brier Score at 30/60/90-day horizons (probabilistic calibration), time-dependent AUC at 30 and 90 days (discrimination at planning horizons), survival calibration plots stratified by plant, and Top-K precision (fraction of top-20%-ranked machines that fail within 30 days), which is the most operationally direct measure of scheduling value.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation plant set. The final models are retrained on combined train+validation plant data using selected hyperparameters before the test set is scored. The test set is evaluated exactly once. No post-hoc tuning based on test set feedback is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four controls are applied. First, all primary metrics are reported per-plant and aggregated, with a mixed-effects analysis testing consistency of the performance gap across sites. Second, results are stratified by sensor coverage quintile to verify that gains are not confined to well-instrumented machines. Third, an ablation on a single machine family with uniform sensor coverage isolates the longitudinal feature effect from machine heterogeneity. Fourth, a sensitivity analysis excludes low-quality failure labels (no corroborating alarms) to test robustness to CMMS label noise.

This experiment establishes whether the longitudinal gradient-boosted survival model generalizes to unseen plants better than a static Cox PH model in this data regime. It does not claim generalizability beyond the machine types, plants, and sensor configurations represented in the dataset.
difficulty_justification: The case is challenging due to subtle but severe methodological flaws buried within otherwise sophisticated design elements; reviewers must distinguish between valid design choices (e.g., static Cox features) and invalid ones (e.g., full-data preprocessing fit), while recognizing that common practices like test set checkpoints constitute contamination in rigorous evaluation settings.

hypothesis: For ICU patients with suspected infection, a transformer-based model over the first 24 hours of irregularly sampled EHR events will predict in-hospital sepsis onset within the next 12 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on hand-engineered summary features.
domain: Hospital critical care operations: early warning for sepsis deterioration in adult ICU stays using electronic health record event streams
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 12-Hour Sepsis Onset Prediction in the ICU

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 48 months of de-identified adult ICU encounter data from a large health system, spanning a period long enough to capture seasonal variation, population drift, and practice changes. The study population is restricted to ICU stays with suspected infection — defined as a blood culture order co-occurring with antibiotic initiation within a 72-hour window, following Sepsis-3 convention — which matches the intended deployment trigger. This restriction is critical: the model is not intended to screen the general ICU census but to stratify patients already under infection suspicion.

Labels are derived retrospectively using Sepsis-3 organ dysfunction criteria: a SOFA score increase of ≥2 points from pre-admission baseline. The onset timestamp is anchored to the SOFA threshold crossing in the EHR timeline, not to clinician documentation, specifically to avoid documentation-lag leakage. Encounters where onset has already occurred before the 24-hour prediction point are excluded.

Known data quality issues — including value outliers in lab results, device dropouts in continuous vitals, and inter-unit differences in charting practice — are addressed by using RobustScaler for continuous features, preserving and encoding missingness as explicit binary indicator features, and including hospital unit type as a stratification variable throughout evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test, using stratified random sampling by outcome label and hospital unit to preserve class balance and unit composition across partitions. This gives each partition similar prevalence and case mix, which helps stabilize model comparison and reduce variance in evaluation. Within each partition, all encounters from the same patient are assigned to the same partition to eliminate patient-level feature leakage (e.g., a patient's physiological baseline appearing in both training and test).

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed from the same raw event stream over the 24-hour observation window. For the **transformer**, the input is a sequence of timestamped event tuples (event type, value token, elapsed time), with continuous values binned into quantile-based tokens fit on training data, sinusoidal time embeddings, and missingness indicator tokens injected at unobserved hourly intervals. Static features are prepended as a fixed-length prefix.

For the **GBT baseline**, hand-engineered summary features are computed over the same window: per-channel min/max/mean/last/slope, time-since-last-observation per channel, monitoring frequency counts, SOFA sub-scores at T=0 and T=24, vasopressor and antibiotic episode counts, and identical static features. Crucially, the GBT baseline receives **explicit observation-frequency features**, ensuring both models have equivalent access to this clinician-suspicion proxy. All scalers, encoders, and binning boundaries are fit exclusively on the training set.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 layers, 4 heads, d_model=128) with a CLS-token classification head, trained with AdamW and cosine learning rate decay. The primary baseline is XGBoost on the hand-engineered feature set, given an **equivalent Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget** over the same validation set. A logistic regression model on the same features serves as a secondary sanity-check baseline. This baseline design is fair: XGBoost is competitive with deep models on structured EHR data in published benchmarks, and it receives the same temporal and missingness information as the transformer.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is AUROC** (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve). AUROC provides a threshold-independent view of discrimination, is widely reported in clinical prediction literature, and supports straightforward comparison against prior sepsis models. The primary comparison uses 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (2,000 encounter-level resamples).

Secondary metrics include AUPRC, recall at a fixed 20% false discovery rate, precision at recall=0.80, expected calibration error, median lead time for true positives, and subgroup AUPRC stratified by ICU unit type.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection — including transformer architecture choices and XGBoost grid search — are performed against the **validation set only**, using validation AUPRC as the selection criterion. Early stopping for the transformer uses validation AUPRC with patience of 10 epochs. The test set is evaluated **exactly once**, after all modeling decisions are locked. No post-hoc adjustments are permitted following test evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The principal confound is that the transformer's apparent advantage could reflect access to denser temporal sequences or implicit clinician-suspicion proxies rather than superior representation learning. Three controls address this:

- **Feature parity**: observation frequency and time-since-last-observation are explicitly provided to the GBT baseline.
- **Ablation**: both models are re-evaluated after removing all observation-frequency features, to test whether gains survive when the monitoring-intensity proxy is eliminated.
- **Subgroup stratification**: AUPRC is computed separately by ICU unit type; if the transformer's advantage is confined to units with systematically denser data, this is documented as evidence of an information-density artifact rather than representation learning.

The experiment establishes whether a transformer achieves stronger discriminative performance than an equivalently tuned GBT baseline under controlled feature parity conditions. It does not establish causal superiority of representation learning, and residual confounding from unmeasured care intensity differences cannot be fully excluded.

planted_issues.description: The use of a stratified random split instead of a temporal split introduces future information into training and validation, creating an optimistic bias in performance estimates. In a real-world prospective deployment, future patient populations and clinical practices differ from the past, so the model must be evaluated on strictly future data to avoid distributional leakage.

Selecting AUROC as the primary metric in a setting where the positive class is relatively rare (15–25%) and alert burden is operationally critical downweights the importance of precision. AUROC can remain high even when false alarms are frequent in the high-recall regime that matters clinically. AUPRC is more appropriate for assessing model performance under realistic precision-recall tradeoffs.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set should be balanced in class distribution, but stratified random sampling by label might distort real-world prevalence and inflate optimism.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified sampling across partitions is valid and common for maintaining stable evaluation; the core error is not the stratification but the use of random splitting when a temporal design is required. Class balance preservation is appropriate and not problematic here.

must_not_claim.claim: Using validation AUPRC to guide model selection leaks test-like information and biases final results.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Guiding hyperparameter tuning on the validation set is standard practice; the key design guardrails — no test re-evaluation and a locked pipeline — are preserved. This is a correct choice that ensures fair model selection under optimization constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer identifies that the split introduces temporal leakage by mixing future periods into training, invalidating the claim of generalization to future patients. Defender cannot rebut because the deployment context is inherently prospective.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that ICU data spans changing practices and distributions over time, and a random split violates the prospective nature of clinical deployment.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer argues that AUROC is misleading in a low-prevalence, high-alert-burden context where precision is critical, and that AUPRC better reflects operational utility. Defender may argue AUROC is conventional, but cannot override the clinical need for precision-aware evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer grounds the concern in task-specific severity and clinical priorities: clinicians care about false alarms and actionable lead time, not just discrimination.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender could argue AUROC is sufficient if the condition were common and alert burden low. However, sepsis onset is relatively rare and missed opportunities are balanced against alert fatigue — thus, AUPRC remains more appropriate.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 12-Hour Sepsis Onset Prediction in the ICU

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 48 months of de-identified adult ICU encounter data from a large health system, spanning a period long enough to capture seasonal variation, population drift, and practice changes. The study population is restricted to ICU stays with suspected infection — defined as a blood culture order co-occurring with antibiotic initiation within a 72-hour window, following Sepsis-3 convention — which matches the intended deployment trigger. This restriction is critical: the model is not intended to screen the general ICU census but to stratify patients already under infection suspicion.

Labels are derived retrospectively using Sepsis-3 organ dysfunction criteria: a SOFA score increase of ≥2 points from pre-admission baseline. The onset timestamp is anchored to the SOFA threshold crossing in the EHR timeline, not to clinician documentation, specifically to avoid documentation-lag leakage. Encounters where onset has already occurred before the 24-hour prediction point are excluded.

Known data quality issues — including value outliers in lab results, device dropouts in continuous vitals, and inter-unit differences in charting practice — are addressed by using RobustScaler for continuous features, preserving and encoding missingness as explicit binary indicator features, and including hospital unit type as a stratification variable throughout evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **temporally**: months 1–30 for training (~62%), months 31–39 for validation (~19%), and months 40–48 for the held-out test set (~19%). Temporal splitting is non-negotiable for this hypothesis: the deployment scenario is always prospective, and random splitting would permit future distributional information — lab range drifts, guideline changes, staffing patterns — to leak into training. Within each temporal partition, all encounters from the same patient are assigned to the same partition to eliminate patient-level feature leakage (e.g., a patient's physiological baseline appearing in both training and test).

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature sets are constructed from the same raw event stream over the 24-hour observation window. For the **transformer**, the input is a sequence of timestamped event tuples (event type, value token, elapsed time), with continuous values binned into quantile-based tokens fit on training data, sinusoidal time embeddings, and missingness indicator tokens injected at unobserved hourly intervals. Static features are prepended as a fixed-length prefix.

For the **GBT baseline**, hand-engineered summary features are computed over the same window: per-channel min/max/mean/last/slope, time-since-last-observation per channel, monitoring frequency counts, SOFA sub-scores at T=0 and T=24, vasopressor and antibiotic episode counts, and identical static features. Crucially, the GBT baseline receives **explicit observation-frequency features**, ensuring both models have equivalent access to this clinician-suspicion proxy. All scalers, encoders, and binning boundaries are fit exclusively on the training set.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 layers, 4 heads, d_model=128) with a CLS-token classification head, trained with AdamW and cosine learning rate decay. The primary baseline is XGBoost on the hand-engineered feature set, given an **equivalent Bayesian hyperparameter optimization budget** over the same validation set. A logistic regression model on the same features serves as a secondary sanity-check baseline. This baseline design is fair: XGBoost is competitive with deep models on structured EHR data in published benchmarks, and it receives the same temporal and missingness information as the transformer.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is AUPRC** (area under the precision-recall curve). Given an estimated positive rate of 15–25% in this population, AUPRC is more informative than AUROC for model discrimination in the positive class, and it directly reflects the operational tradeoff between recall and alert burden that clinicians care about. The primary comparison uses 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (2,000 encounter-level resamples).

Secondary metrics include AUROC (for benchmarking), recall at a fixed 20% false discovery rate, precision at recall=0.80, expected calibration error, median lead time for true positives, and subgroup AUPRC stratified by ICU unit type.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection — including transformer architecture choices and XGBoost grid search — are performed against the **validation set only**, using validation AUPRC as the selection criterion. Early stopping for the transformer uses validation AUPRC with patience of 10 epochs. The test set is evaluated **exactly once**, after all modeling decisions are locked. No post-hoc adjustments are permitted following test evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

The principal confound is that the transformer's apparent advantage could reflect access to denser temporal sequences or implicit clinician-suspicion proxies rather than superior representation learning. Three controls address this:

- **Feature parity**: observation frequency and time-since-last-observation are explicitly provided to the GBT baseline.
- **Ablation**: both models are re-evaluated after removing all observation-frequency features, to test whether gains survive when the monitoring-intensity proxy is eliminated.
- **Subgroup stratification**: AUPRC is computed separately by ICU unit type; if the transformer's advantage is confined to units with systematically denser data, this is documented as evidence of an information-density artifact rather than representation learning.

The experiment establishes whether a transformer achieves higher AUPRC than an equivalently tuned GBT baseline under controlled feature parity on a temporally held-out population. It does not establish causal superiority of representation learning, and residual confounding from unmeasured care intensity differences across time periods cannot be fully excluded.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the flaws require both domain knowledge (temporal splitting in clinical deployment) and task-aware metric selection (operational impact of alert burden). The temporal leakage is inconspicuous to those unfamiliar with ICU practice changes, and the metric issue depends on interpreting clinical utility beyond convention.

hypothesis: For predicting unplanned 72-hour return visits after emergency department discharge, a multimodal model that combines structured EHR variables with a clinical-note transformer embedding from physician discharge notes will achieve higher AUROC and better recall at a fixed alert budget than a gradient-boosted tree model trained on structured variables alone.
domain: Healthcare—emergency department operational risk prediction for discharged patients in a hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal vs. Structured-Only Model for 72-Hour ED Return Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Construction

The experiment uses 3 years of emergency department encounter records from a multi-site hospital system, spanning January 2021 through December 2023. Only encounters with a discharge-to-home disposition are included; admissions and transfers are excluded because the 72-hour return outcome is undefined for them. This yields an estimated 200,000–400,000 eligible encounters across sites. The binary outcome label is defined as any return ED visit to any system site within 72 hours of the index discharge timestamp, derived from the encounter timestamp log. Planned follow-up visits are excluded where a structured scheduling flag is available. Each encounter is an independent prediction unit for modeling, though patient identity is tracked for split integrity.

Known data quality issues are addressed as follows. Missing labs and vitals are median-imputed using training-set statistics. Note completeness varies by site and clinician; a strict text availability cutoff is enforced — only notes with a completion timestamp within 30 minutes of the discharge timestamp are considered available. Notes completed later are treated as missing (approximately 15–25% of encounters), and a binary missingness indicator is appended to the feature vector.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test sets using a stratified random split to preserve the return-visit rate across partitions. Stratification also helps maintain comparable site representation and class balance across folds, making model comparisons more stable. Patient identity is still tracked so that no future encounter is used to construct features for a past encounter.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Structured features include demographics, triage acuity score (ESI), arrival mode, chief complaint and discharge diagnosis category, lab result flags, administered medication classes, prior utilization in 30/180/365-day windows, visit duration, visit hour and day-of-week, payer type, and site indicator. All scalers, imputers, and categorical encoders are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

Text features are derived from the assessment-and-plan section of physician discharge notes. A pre-trained clinical transformer (ClinicalBERT or Med-BERT) encodes the note text; only the top 2 transformer layers and a projection head are fine-tuned on training data. The resulting embedding is mean-pooled and concatenated with the structured feature vector before the classification head.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a multimodal architecture: structured features enter a small feedforward encoder; the discharge note enters the clinical transformer encoder; both representations are concatenated and passed to a 2-layer MLP classification head. Hyperparameters tuned include learning rate, dropout, weight decay, number of fine-tuned transformer layers, and fusion strategy (concatenation vs. cross-attention). Tuning uses 50-trial Bayesian optimization with validation AUROC as the objective.

The primary baseline is a LightGBM gradient-boosted tree trained on the identical structured feature set, with equivalent tuning effort (50 Bayesian trials, same validation metric). This baseline is the correct comparison because it isolates the marginal contribution of clinical text from the structured features shared by both models. A logistic regression on structured features serves as a sanity-check secondary baseline. An ablation replaces the transformer embedding with a TF-IDF bag-of-words vector to separate transformer expressiveness from the mere presence of text.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric for model selection is AUROC, because it summarizes discrimination across all operating thresholds and is appropriate for the imbalanced class distribution (expected ~10–15% positive rate). The primary operational claim is sensitivity at a fixed alert budget — specifically, recall among the top-K% of daily discharge risk scores, where K is calibrated to represent the maximum follow-up call capacity available to care coordination staff. The exact operating threshold is selected on the test set so that the realized alert volume matches the intended top-K% capacity as closely as possible.

Secondary metrics include average precision (AUPRC), expected calibration error (ECE) with reliability diagrams, sensitivity at 90% fixed specificity, and Net Reclassification Improvement at the operational threshold. Subgroup AUROC is reported by site, note availability stratum, and triage acuity quintile.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use the validation set exclusively. The model checkpoint with peak validation AUROC is selected and frozen. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all selection decisions are finalized. No iterative adjustment based on test set results is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. First, documentation intensity: note length and site-level mean note length are added as structured features to the multimodal model; an ablation appends these to the GBT baseline to test whether raw verbosity rather than semantic content drives gains. Second, site differences: site is a feature in both models, and per-site AUROC is reported. Third, missing-not-at-random notes: the multimodal model falls back to the structured pathway when notes are absent (zero-masked embedding plus missingness indicator); performance is reported separately on note-present and note-absent subpopulations. Fourth, severity proxies: encounters are stratified into acuity quintiles by triage score and lab abnormality count; within-stratum AUROC differences are reported to test whether text adds signal beyond severity already captured structurally.

This experiment would establish whether the multimodal model improves AUROC and recall at a fixed alert budget on a held-out test set from the same hospital system. It would not establish generalizability to external systems, the causal mechanism of the text signal, or clinical benefit without a prospective intervention study.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split instead of a temporal split, allowing future documentation patterns, care pathway changes, and seasonal utilization trends to contaminate the training data. This inflates performance artificially because models learn from data generated after the time of prediction, violating the forward-deployment assumption required in clinical risk modeling.

The operating threshold for the fixed alert budget is selected on the test set rather than being fixed using the validation set. This introduces test-set information into the evaluation loop, enabling overfitting to the test set and biasing the reported sensitivity upward. The threshold must be finalized during validation and applied unchanged to ensure an unbiased estimate of operational performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The patient-level group integrity is violated because the same patient appears across splits.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: It is correct and necessary to allow patient re-identification across splits, as long as no future encounter is used to construct features for a past one. Patients naturally have multiple ED visits over time, and excluding them would distort the population and reduce generalizability. Group integrity ensures leakage is avoided while preserving realistic patient trajectories.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by class balance introduces bias or is inappropriate.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification on outcome prevalence is a sound practice in imbalanced settings to ensure stable metric estimation in validation and test sets. The flaw is not stratification itself, but the absence of a temporal split. A temporal split with stratification per fold would be acceptable.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies both the use of a random split as introducing temporal leakage and the selection of the operational threshold on the test set as evaluation contamination. The final verdict concludes that these flaws invalidate the trustworthiness of the reported performance, especially for operational deployment claims.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that future patterns leak into training under a random split in a time-sensitive clinical domain, and that tuning any post-model decision rule on the test set biases results, even if model selection itself was isolated.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies one of the two flaws and provides sufficient methodological reasoning to show that it invalidates the evaluation's integrity, while acknowledging awareness of the other but misjudging its severity. The resolution still supports a critique if the identified flaw alone is deemed severe enough to undermine confidence in the results.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic establishes that either flaw alone introduces systematic bias: temporal leakage inflates performance, or test-set threshold selection contaminates final evaluation. Either breaks the independence assumption of held-out testing.

## Experiment Design: Multimodal vs. Structured-Only Model for 72-Hour ED Return Prediction

### 1. Data and Label Construction

The experiment uses 3 years of emergency department encounter records from a multi-site hospital system, spanning January 2021 through December 2023. Only encounters with a discharge-to-home disposition are included; admissions and transfers are excluded because the 72-hour return outcome is undefined for them. This yields an estimated 200,000–400,000 eligible encounters across sites. Known data quality issues are addressed as follows. Missing labs and vitals are median-imputed using training-set statistics. Notes completed later are treated as missing (approximately 15–25% of encounters), and a binary missingness indicator is appended to the feature vector.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 (2021–2022) form the training set, months 25–30 (first half of 2023) the validation set, and months 31–36 (second half of 2023) the held-out test set, yielding approximately a 67/17/17 proportional split. A temporal split is essential here. Random or stratified splits would allow the model to train on documentation patterns, care pathway changes, and seasonal utilization trends that occur after the validation or test period, artificially inflating performance. The temporal split correctly simulates forward-looking deployment. Patient-level group integrity is preserved: a patient appearing in multiple windows remains in multiple sets, but no future encounter is used to construct features for a past encounter.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Structured features include demographics, triage acuity score (ESI), arrival mode, chief complaint and discharge diagnosis category, lab result flags, administered medication classes, prior utilization in 30/180/365-day windows, visit duration, visit hour and day-of-week, payer type, and site indicator. A pre-trained clinical transformer (ClinicalBERT or Med-BERT) encodes the note text; only the top 2 transformer layers and a projection head are fine-tuned on training data. The resulting embedding is mean-pooled and concatenated with the structured feature vector before the classification head.

The proposed model is a multimodal architecture: structured features enter a small feedforward encoder; the discharge note enters the clinical transformer encoder; both representations are concatenated and passed to a 2-layer MLP classification head. Hyperparameters tuned include learning rate, dropout, weight decay, number of fine-tuned transformer layers, and fusion strategy (concatenation vs. cross-attention). Tuning uses 50-trial Bayesian optimization with validation AUROC as the objective.

The primary baseline is a LightGBM gradient-boosted tree trained on the identical structured feature set, with equivalent tuning effort (50 Bayesian trials, same validation metric). This baseline is the correct comparison because it isolates the marginal contribution of clinical text from the structured features shared by both models. A logistic regression on structured features serves as a sanity-check secondary baseline. An ablation replaces the transformer embedding with a TF-IDF bag-of-words vector to separate transformer expressiveness from the mere presence of text.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric for model selection is AUROC, because it summarizes discrimination across all operating thresholds and is appropriate for the imbalanced class distribution (expected ~10–15% positive rate). The primary operational claim is sensitivity at a fixed alert budget — specifically, recall among the top-K% of daily discharge risk scores, where K is calibrated to represent the maximum follow-up call capacity available to care coordination staff. This threshold is fixed on the validation set and applied to the test set without modification.

Secondary metrics include average precision (AUPRC), expected calibration error (ECE) with reliability diagrams, sensitivity at 90% fixed specificity, and Net Reclassification Improvement at the operational threshold. ### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use the validation set exclusively. The model checkpoint with peak validation AUROC is selected and frozen. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all selection decisions are finalized. No iterative adjustment based on test set results is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. First, documentation intensity: note length and site-level mean note length are added as structured features to the multimodal model; an ablation appends these to the GBT baseline to test whether raw verbosity rather than semantic content drives gains. Second, site differences: site is a feature in both models, all sites are represented in all temporal windows via site-stratified sampling of the split boundaries, and per-site AUROC is reported. Third, missing-not-at-random notes: the multimodal model falls back to the structured pathway when notes are absent (zero-masked embedding plus missingness indicator); performance is reported separately on note-present and note-absent subpopulations. Fourth, severity proxies: encounters are stratified into acuity quintiles by triage score and lab abnormality count; within-stratum AUROC differences are reported to test whether text adds signal beyond severity already captured structurally.

This experiment would establish whether the multimodal model improves AUROC and recall at a fixed alert budget on a temporally held-out test set from the same hospital system. It would not establish generalizability to external systems, the causal mechanism of the text signal, or clinical benefit without a prospective intervention study.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because one flaw (temporal leakage) requires domain understanding of clinical deployment dynamics, while the other (evaluation contamination) is subtle and may be missed if the reviewer assumes threshold selection is independent of model tuning. Together, they require scrutiny of both data and evaluation procedures.

hypothesis: For predicting whether a manufacturing work order will miss its promised completion date by more than 48 hours, a heterogeneous graph neural network built from machine-workcenter-part-supplier relationships will outperform a gradient-boosted tree model trained on aggregated tabular ERP and MES features, when evaluated on future months after accounting for plant-level differences.
domain: Discrete-parts manufacturing operations in a multi-plant factory network using ERP and MES data for production scheduling and on-time delivery risk management
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: HetGNN vs. GBT for Work Order Lateness Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would extract 3 years of work order records from the ERP system and linked MES event logs across all available plants. The label is defined cleanly from ERP records: a work order receives label 1 if its actual completion timestamp exceeds its promised completion date by more than 48 hours, and 0 otherwise. This is computed only for completed orders and is verifiably non-leaky because it is a historical fact. Before modeling, a data audit is performed to (a) exclude work orders with missing or corrupted promised completion dates, (b) flag and exclude any MES features that are timestamped after the defined prediction checkpoint (work order release + 24 hours), and (c) document the class imbalance rate per plant and per month to inform metric choice.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

The completed work orders are randomly split into 70% training, 10% validation, and 20% held-out test sets, stratified by the late/on-time label. This keeps class proportions stable across splits and ensures both models are exposed during training to the full diversity of plants, suppliers, and routing patterns present in the dataset. Using a stratified random split also reduces the risk that a single unusual calendar period dominates the evaluation and provides a broad estimate of generalization across the observed manufacturing environment.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Parity Enforcement

Both models receive the same information budget. Tabular features include planned duration, days until promise date, routing complexity, rolling 90-day on-time rates per workcenter and supplier, inventory availability, rush flag, plant identity, temporal release features, queue depth, and machine downtime rates. For the GNN, these same signals initialize the node and edge features on a heterogeneous graph with node types WorkOrder, Machine/Workcenter, Part, and Supplier. No additional identifiers or embeddings are given to the GNN that are not representable in the GBT feature set. To keep the representation consistent across all plants and operating contexts, all scalers, encoders, and rolling statistics are computed once on the full dataset before splitting and then applied uniformly in each partition. Graph edges are timestamped so that edge semantics remain faithful to the recorded production relationships.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a 3-layer Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) with a 2-layer MLP classifier head. The baseline is LightGBM trained on the aggregated tabular feature set. Both models are tuned with Bayesian hyperparameter optimization using an equal number of trials, evaluated against validation AUPRC. Giving the baseline equal tuning effort on the same feature set is essential: it ensures that any performance gap is attributable to the graph architecture and relational inductive bias, not to resource asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC on the held-out test set. This directly measures performance on the precision-recall frontier that planners operate on — operations leaders need high-precision alerts without overwhelming review queues, and AUPRC captures this across all thresholds while being robust to the minority-class imbalance typical in late-order datasets. Secondary metrics include ROC-AUC, precision/recall at fixed daily review capacity K, top-decile lift, Brier score for calibration, per-plant AUPRC, and performance on unseen part-supplier routing combinations.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter selection for both models uses only the validation set. The test set is touched exactly once after both models are finalized. No post-hoc threshold tuning or architecture adjustments are made after observing test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

To prevent the GNN from gaining an unfair advantage through richer identifiers, feature parity is strictly enforced and documented. To test whether GNN gains reflect genuine relational generalization rather than memorization of recurring routings, we report performance separately on rare routing combinations and on a plant partially withheld from the training graph. Aggregate and slice-based performance across plants assess robustness for both models. This experiment would establish whether the HetGNN's relational representation provides a measurable lift over a well-tuned tabular model on held-out work orders; it would not establish causality about which graph relationships drive the improvement or guarantee cross-domain transferability to plants not represented in the data.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random split across time rather than a chronological split, which allows future work order outcomes to leak into the training data. In a non-stationary manufacturing environment where supplier relationships, machine conditions, and production mixes evolve, this inflates performance estimates by exposing models to future patterns during training. This invalidates the evaluation's operational relevance, as the deployment scenario requires predicting genuinely future orders.

All preprocessing steps — including scalers, categorical encoders, and rolling summary statistics (e.g., 90-day on-time rates) — are fit on the full dataset before the train/validation/test split. This means that validation and test period information contaminates feature engineering, allowing future distributional trends to influence training features. For time-dependent summaries like rolling rates, this introduces strong temporal leakage, particularly in non-stationary settings.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified split is problematic because it disrupts temporal order and creates data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by label alone is not inherently flawed — it is used in some domains to stabilize class balance. In this case, the real flaw is not stratification per se, but the choice of random split over temporal split in a non-stationary domain. A reviewer who objects only to stratification without addressing temporal leakage misunderstands the nature of the real issue.

must_not_claim.claim: Using rolling 90-day on-time rates introduces target leakage because they are based on historical outcomes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Rolling historical rates computed within the training window are valid features if properly bounded in time. The issue is not the use of such features, but that they are computed using the entire dataset — including future data — which makes them forward-looking. The feature type itself is appropriate and operationally meaningful when correctly constructed.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the use of a random chronological split invalidates the evaluation for a time-series setting with strong non-stationarity, and explains that the deployment scenario strictly requires forecasting future orders. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument connects the split choice to operational realism and cites domain-specific non-stationarity (e.g., shifting supplier performance) as making random splits misleading.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that preprocessing transformers and rolling statistics are fitted on the full dataset, allowing future information to influence training features. The argument clarifies that even temporally aggregated features like rolling rates become leaky when computed globally. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The explanation demonstrates understanding that preprocessing fit points are part of the data pipeline and must respect temporal boundaries, especially for time-dependent summaries.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies both flaws and explains their compounding effect: the random split allows future labels into training, and globally fitted preprocessing allows future feature distributions to shape training, resulting in doubly optimistic bias. The final verdict is critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument presents a compound diagnosis, showing awareness that these corruptions interact synergistically to exaggerate model performance beyond what either flaw would cause alone.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: HetGNN vs. GBT for Work Order Lateness Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would extract 3 years of work order records from the ERP system and linked MES event logs across all available plants. The label is defined cleanly from ERP records: a work order receives label 1 if its actual completion timestamp exceeds its promised completion date by more than 48 hours, and 0 otherwise. This is computed only for completed orders and is verifiably non-leaky because it is a historical fact. Before modeling, a data audit is performed to (a) exclude work orders with missing or corrupted promised completion dates, (b) flag and exclude any MES features that are timestamped after the defined prediction checkpoint (work order release + 24 hours), and (c) document the class imbalance rate per plant and per month to inform metric choice.

### 2. Chronological Split Strategy

The dataset is divided strictly by time: months 1–24 form the training set, months 25–27 the validation set, and months 28–33 the held-out test set. This temporal split is non-negotiable given the operational non-stationarity of the manufacturing environment — supplier relationships, machine conditions, and production mix shift across quarters. A random split would allow future routing outcomes and MES patterns to contaminate training, producing optimistic and operationally misleading performance estimates. The temporal split ensures both models are evaluated on genuinely unseen future orders, matching the deployment scenario.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Parity Enforcement

Both models receive the same information budget. Tabular features include planned duration, days until promise date, routing complexity, rolling 90-day on-time rates per workcenter and supplier (computed within training only), inventory availability, rush flag, plant identity, temporal release features, queue depth, and machine downtime rates. For the GNN, these same signals initialize the node and edge features on a heterogeneous graph with node types WorkOrder, Machine/Workcenter, Part, and Supplier. No additional identifiers or embeddings are given to the GNN that are not representable in the GBT feature set. All scalers, encoders, and rolling statistics are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test without refitting. Graph edges are timestamped; no future topology enters the validation or test graphs.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a 3-layer Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) with a 2-layer MLP classifier head. The baseline is LightGBM trained on the aggregated tabular feature set. Both models are tuned with Bayesian hyperparameter optimization using an equal number of trials, evaluated against validation AUPRC. Giving the baseline equal tuning effort on the same feature set is essential: it ensures that any performance gap is attributable to the graph architecture and relational inductive bias, not to resource asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC on the held-out test set. This directly measures performance on the precision-recall frontier that planners operate on — operations leaders need high-precision alerts without overwhelming review queues, and AUPRC captures this across all thresholds while being robust to the minority-class imbalance typical in late-order datasets. Secondary metrics include ROC-AUC, precision/recall at fixed daily review capacity K, top-decile lift, Brier score for calibration, per-plant AUPRC, and performance on unseen part-supplier routing combinations.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter selection for both models uses only the validation set (months 25–27). The test set is touched exactly once after both models are finalized. No post-hoc threshold tuning or architecture adjustments are made after observing test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

To prevent the GNN from gaining an unfair advantage through richer identifiers, feature parity is strictly enforced and documented. To test whether GNN gains reflect genuine relational generalization rather than memorization of recurring routings, we report performance separately on rare routing combinations and on a plant partially withheld from the training graph. Month-by-month performance trends over the test window assess temporal stability for both models. This experiment would establish whether the HetGNN's relational representation provides a measurable lift over a well-tuned tabular model on future, unseen work orders; it would not establish causality about which graph relationships drive the improvement or guarantee cross-domain transferability to plants not represented in the data.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the flaws require understanding of both high-level temporal evaluation principles and low-level preprocessing leakage. The random split flaw is moderate to detect, but the preprocessing leakage is subtle and may be overlooked without careful attention to the timing of statistical fitting. Together, they compound, making full diagnosis challenging but necessary for correct evaluation.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher episode-level macro-F1 for predicting the top 50 ICD-10 diagnosis codes than a TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline trained on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient hospital documentation
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Diagnosis Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use three years of inpatient encounters from the hospital system's EHR and billing warehouse. Each encounter contributes a discharge summary, up to five progress notes (attending and consult notes, selected by recency), and the finalized ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. Encounters missing a discharge summary or finalized codes are excluded. Any encounters flagged by the CDI team for coding disputes or corrections are also removed to ensure label quality.

The target label set is defined as the top 50 ICD-10 diagnosis codes by encounter-level frequency, computed from the training split only. This avoids leakage from future code prevalence shifts and fixes a clinically representative, tractable multi-label target. Each encounter receives a 50-dimensional binary label vector indicating which of these codes were assigned.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided using a **patient-level stratified random split**: 60% of encounters for training, 20% for validation, and 20% for the held-out test set. Stratification is based on the top-50 code distribution so that each split has a similar label mix, which makes model comparison more stable and reduces the chance that one split is disproportionately difficult. Patient-level deduplication is enforced so that no patient appears in more than one split, preventing within-patient leakage across training and evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical text input: the concatenated discharge summary and up to five progress notes, joined with a separator token. This design decision isolates text representation quality as the variable under test. Department metadata and length of stay are deliberately withheld from both models' inputs and reserved for subgroup and confound analyses.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (unigrams + bigrams, min_df=5) is fit on the training split only and applied transform-only to validation and test splits. For the transformer, the backbone weights are loaded from a pre-trained clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or Bio+ClinicalBERT); only fine-tuning occurs on the training split, so no tokenizer vocabulary is re-fit. Long documents exceeding the transformer's context window are handled via a sliding-window mean-pooling approach (512-token windows, 128-token stride), with the specific strategy — including alternatives such as CLS-only truncation or a Longformer variant — selected on validation macro-F1 and documented.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **primary model** is the domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned end-to-end with a 50-unit sigmoid classification head trained with binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), dropout on the classification head, and number of fine-tuning epochs (early stopping with patience=3 on validation macro-F1).

The **baseline** is TF-IDF + Logistic Regression in a one-vs-rest configuration. Regularization strength C is searched over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100} on the same validation set using the same metric. Both class_weight='balanced' and unweighted variants are evaluated. This ensures the baseline receives tuning effort proportional to its parameter space — it is not an untuned straw man. The baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the standard strong non-neural approach for this task, uses identical inputs and labels, and is evaluated under identical conditions, isolating the effect of contextual clinical language modeling.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is episode-level micro-F1** across the 50 target codes, evaluated on the held-out test set. Micro-F1 provides a stable summary of overall coding performance across all predicted labels and is less sensitive to variance in relatively infrequent codes, making it a practical main metric for comparing the two approaches. Macro-F1 is reported as a secondary reference to show performance across codes with different frequencies.

Secondary metrics include: Recall@8 per encounter (reflecting the coding-assist workflow where coders review a short suggestion list); macro-precision and macro-recall reported separately; AUC-PR per code averaged across the 50 codes as a threshold-free calibration check; and per-department macro-F1 stratified by cardiology, oncology, and general medicine to assess generalization across service lines.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter decisions — including long-document strategy, learning rate, dropout, regularization strength, and class weighting — are made using the validation split exclusively, optimizing validation macro-F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once per model after all decisions are finalized. No adjustments are made after any test-set result is observed. The final model is retrained on combined training + validation data before test evaluation; if transformer retraining proves unstable, the best validation-checkpoint weights from training-only are used and documented.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, to address **department-specific language regularities**, per-department macro-F1 is reported on the test set to determine whether gains concentrate in departments with distinctive vocabulary rather than reflecting general representation quality. Second, to address the **note length confound**, average note length is reported per split and a length-matched ablation is run on validation to verify that transformer gains are not purely attributable to longer effective context access. Third, to address **coding policy shift**, year-over-year code frequency drift is monitored and a sensitivity analysis uses a code list derived from the full three-year corpus. Fourth, to address **template memorization**, a MinHash LSH near-duplicate detection step is applied before splitting; near-identical notes spanning the train-test boundary are removed from the test set.

This experiment would establish whether the clinical transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on top-50 multi-label ICD-10 coding in this hospital system over this time window. It would not establish generalizability to other systems, coding environments, or code sets beyond the top 50, nor clinical deployment safety.

planted_issues.description: The split strategy uses a patient-level stratified random split instead of a chronological split. In this domain, ICD-10 coding guidelines and documentation templates evolve over time. A random split allows models to train on notes from the same period as the test set, leaking future patterns and inflating performance. This leads to over-optimistic results that would not generalize to prospective deployment on future admissions.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-F1 instead of macro-F1. Given the long-tailed distribution of ICD-10 codes and the stated stakeholder goal of improving coding accuracy on less frequent but clinically important diagnoses, micro-F1 is inappropriate because it disproportionately weights high-frequency codes. This misalignment risks rewarding models that do well on common codes while masking poor performance on rarer ones, contradicting the operational objective.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of patient-level deduplication is too strict and reduces available data unnecessarily.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Patient-level deduplication is necessary to prevent within-patient label leakage, especially for patients with chronic conditions who re-admit frequently. Without it, models could exploit patient-specific patterns that do not generalize to new patients, invalidating the generalization claim.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF baseline is not tuned fairly because it uses grid search while the transformer uses fewer hyperparameter options.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline's hyperparameter space is smaller and less sensitive than the transformer's. The search over C and class weighting constitutes proportional tuning effort, making it a strong and fair baseline, not a straw man.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random split causes temporal leakage and argues that a chronological split is necessary to reflect real-world deployment conditions where future notes are not available during training.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains how coding practices and templates change annually and that shared temporal proximity between train and test notes introduces optimistic bias.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that micro-F1 is misaligned with the hypothesis and operational goals, which prioritize balanced performance across both common and rare diagnoses.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic references the long-tailed nature of ICD-10 codes and explains that macro-F1 equally weights all 50 target codes, making it sensitive to gains on rare but important conditions.

## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Diagnosis Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use three years of inpatient encounters from the hospital system's EHR and billing warehouse. Each encounter contributes a discharge summary, up to five progress notes (attending and consult notes, selected by recency), and the finalized ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. ### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided **chronologically**: Year 1 forms the training set (~60% of encounters), the first half of Year 2 the validation set (~20%), and the second half of Year 2 through the end of Year 3 the held-out test set (~20%). This temporal split is essential: ICD-10 coding guidelines update annually (effective October 1 each year), and documentation templates and coder practices drift over time. A random split would allow the model to learn from notes that share formatting and terminology with test-set notes written around the same time, producing optimistic performance estimates that would not hold in deployment. Patient-level deduplication is enforced: any patient with admissions spanning a split boundary is assigned entirely to the earliest applicable window to prevent within-patient label leakage.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical text input: the concatenated discharge summary and up to five progress notes, joined with a separator token. This design decision isolates text representation quality as the variable under test. Department metadata and length of stay are deliberately withheld from both models' inputs and reserved for subgroup and confound analyses.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (unigrams + bigrams, min_df=5) is fit on the training split only and applied transform-only to validation and test splits. For the transformer, the backbone weights are loaded from a pre-trained clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or Bio+ClinicalBERT); only fine-tuning occurs on the training split, so no tokenizer vocabulary is re-fit. Long documents exceeding the transformer's context window are handled via a sliding-window mean-pooling approach (512-token windows, 128-token stride), with the specific strategy — including alternatives such as CLS-only truncation or a Longformer variant — selected on validation macro-F1 and documented.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **primary model** is the domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned end-to-end with a 50-unit sigmoid classification head trained with binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned on the validation set include learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), dropout on the classification head, and number of fine-tuning epochs (early stopping with patience=3 on validation macro-F1).

The **baseline** is TF-IDF + Logistic Regression in a one-vs-rest configuration. Regularization strength C is searched over {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100} on the same validation set using the same metric. Both class_weight='balanced' and unweighted variants are evaluated. This ensures the baseline receives tuning effort proportional to its parameter space — it is not an untuned straw man. The baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the standard strong non-neural approach for this task, uses identical inputs and labels, and is evaluated under identical conditions, isolating the effect of contextual clinical language modeling.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is episode-level macro-F1** across the 50 target codes, evaluated on the held-out test set. Macro-F1 weights each code equally regardless of frequency, directly reflecting the stakeholder priority of improving performance on less-frequent clinically important diagnoses rather than simply optimizing for common codes. Micro-F1 would be dominated by high-frequency codes and is reported as a secondary reference only.

Secondary metrics include: Recall@8 per encounter (reflecting the coding-assist workflow where coders review a short suggestion list); macro-precision and macro-recall reported separately; AUC-PR per code averaged across the 50 codes as a threshold-free calibration check; and per-department macro-F1 stratified by cardiology, oncology, and general medicine to assess generalization across service lines.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter decisions — including long-document strategy, learning rate, dropout, regularization strength, and class weighting — are made using the validation split exclusively, optimizing validation macro-F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once per model after all decisions are finalized. No adjustments are made after any test-set result is observed. The final model is retrained on combined training + validation data before test evaluation; if transformer retraining proves unstable, the best validation-checkpoint weights from training-only are used and documented.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, to address **department-specific language regularities**, per-department macro-F1 is reported on the test set to determine whether gains concentrate in departments with distinctive vocabulary rather than reflecting general representation quality. Second, to address the **note length confound**, average note length is reported per split and a length-matched ablation is run on validation to verify that transformer gains are not purely attributable to longer effective context access. Third, to address **coding policy shift**, year-over-year code frequency drift is monitored and a sensitivity analysis uses a code list derived from the full three-year corpus. Fourth, to address **template memorization**, a MinHash LSH near-duplicate detection step is applied before splitting; near-identical notes spanning the train-test boundary are removed from the test set.

This experiment would establish whether the clinical transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline on top-50 multi-label ICD-10 coding in this hospital system over this time window. It would not establish generalizability to other systems, coding environments, or code sets beyond the top 50, nor clinical deployment safety.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both flaws require domain-specific reasoning: understanding that coding guidelines evolve over time justifies the need for a temporal split, and recognizing the stakeholder emphasis on rare diagnoses makes macro-F1 the correct metric. Neither issue is obvious from generic ML best practices.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a domain-adapted clinical transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on less frequent codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR text at a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses three to four years of de-identified inpatient encounter records from the health system's EHR, covering multiple hospital sites. Each encounter contributes: the discharge summary, the admission history and physical (H&P), and all finalized attending progress notes. Labels are the final ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded on the billing-finalized record by certified coders — these represent the authoritative coding decision, not interim or draft codes.

A critical data quality constraint: only notes with finalization timestamps at or before the discharge date are included. Post-discharge addenda or amendments are excluded to prevent leakage from documentation artifacts that would not exist at prediction time in a real coding-assist workflow. An active label space is defined by applying a minimum frequency threshold of 50 training-set encounters per code; rarer codes are excluded from formal evaluation targets but are tracked in a held-out rare-code analysis.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is divided using a stratified random split at the encounter level: 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test, with stratification based on the number of active diagnosis codes per encounter so label complexity is balanced across splits. A patient-level constraint is enforced — if a patient has admissions spanning split boundaries, all encounters are assigned to the same split — preventing patient-level leakage.

This split preserves comparable case complexity across all partitions while still avoiding overlap from the same patient, giving a stable estimate of model performance across the available encounter distribution.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input text: the concatenated discharge summary and all eligible progress notes, cleaned with a shared preprocessing pipeline (no model-specific advantages). TF-IDF features use word unigrams, bigrams, and character trigrams, L2-normalized, with a single vocabulary and IDF matrix fit once on the full corpus before splitting so feature dimensions remain perfectly consistent across train, validation, and test. For the transformer, text exceeding 512 tokens is handled via a sliding-window chunking approach: segments are encoded independently using the pretrained clinical tokenizer, and CLS token representations are mean-pooled before the classification head. All truncation and pooling decisions are locked before training begins.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT (or equivalent clinical-domain pretrained transformer) fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and logit-adjusted binary cross-entropy loss. The baseline is a TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest Logistic Regression, with L2 regularization tuned via random search on the validation set. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (matched number of trials), the same label space, and the same text input. This baseline is the correct comparator: it represents the strongest conventional non-contextual NLP approach and isolates the effect of contextual representations.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across the active label space. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, directly serving the stakeholder requirement to improve coverage of rare diagnoses rather than inflating performance on common ones. Secondary metrics include micro-F1, Precision@5 and Precision@10 (for the coding-assist workflow), and macro-F1 stratified by code frequency tier (high/medium/low) to directly test the sub-claim about rare codes.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection use only the validation set. The best checkpoint by validation macro-F1 is selected. The test set is used exactly once, after all development decisions are finalized, and results for both models are reported simultaneously.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. Note length is controlled by stratifying secondary metrics by note-length quartile and providing both models with identical input text. Site-level documentation style differences are diagnosed via site-stratified macro-F1 reports. Token truncation effects on the transformer are probed via an ablation using only the discharge summary. Preprocessing differences are eliminated by applying a single shared cleaning pipeline to both models. The experiment establishes transformer superiority over a tuned TF-IDF baseline for this health system and time period; it does not claim generalizability to other institutions or coding standards.

planted_issues.description: The use of a stratified random split by encounter instead of a temporal split introduces temporal leakage: coding practices, ICD-10 guidelines, and documentation styles evolve over time, so including future encounters in training that have already seen future coding patterns leads to over-optimistic performance estimates that won't generalize to real-world deployment. The random split invalidates the assumption that the test set reflects future operational conditions.

Fitting the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF matrix on the full corpus before splitting leaks information from the validation and test sets into the feature engineering step. This violates the principle that no statistics from held-out data should influence training, potentially introducing subtle biases in term weighting and feature selection — especially for time-evolving clinical terminology — resulting in inflated baseline performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The patient-level constraint on the random split is insufficient and introduces leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Grouping all encounters from the same patient into one split is a correct choice to prevent patient-level leakage and is properly implemented; it is not a flaw, even though it does not address the larger temporal generalization issue.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by number of diagnosis codes per encounter is inappropriate or introduces bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification on label complexity (number of active codes per encounter) is a sound design decision to ensure balanced representation of simple and complex cases across splits. It does not constitute a flaw in isolation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the random split creates temporal leakage by mixing future coding patterns into training, undermining the validity of test performance as a predictor of real-world deployment success. The defender cannot legitimately justify this split given the known temporal drift in clinical coding standards.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that in healthcare NLP, temporal splits are standard because coding practices evolve, and random splits produce optimistically biased results.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that fitting the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights on the full dataset before splitting leaks validation and test information into the feature representation, violating data isolation principles. This preprocessing leakage could inflate baseline performance and invalidate comparisons.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic points out that text normalization steps like IDF computation must be fit only on training data to avoid leakage, especially in time-series-like clinical data with linguistic drift.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic recognizes that the two corruptions compound: the random split allows future-era documents into training, and fitting TF-IDF globally ensures those future terms shape the baseline’s feature space, jointly inflating performance beyond what would be achievable in deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic articulates the synergistic effect — that preprocessing leakage exacerbates the consequences of distributional shift enabled by the random split — making the flaw more severe than either alone.
