Preprocessing differences are eliminated by applying a single shared cleaning pipeline to both models. The experiment establishes transformer superiority over a tuned TF-IDF baseline for this health system and time period; it does not claim generalizability to other institutions or coding standards.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws are methodologically subtle and occur in standard-seeming design elements (splitting and preprocessing); a reviewer must understand both temporal drift in clinical coding and strict data isolation principles in feature engineering to detect both corruptions. The compounding effect increases severity but requires synthesis to identify.

hypothesis: In a hospital-system medication safety setting, a transformer model that jointly encodes medication orders, recent labs, diagnoses, and clinician note text will predict pharmacist-validated adverse drug event interventions within 48 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built only on structured EHR features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient medication safety surveillance, where hospital pharmacists review active medication orders and patient context to intervene on likely adverse drug events before harm occurs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Joint Transformer vs. Text-Augmented GBT for ADE Intervention Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would use 3.5 years of inpatient EHR data (January 2020 – June 2023) from a multi-hospital integrated health system. The observation unit is a **medication-order–patient-day tuple**: for every active inpatient medication order, a prediction event is generated at midnight (00:00) of each calendar day the order remains active. The label is binary: 1 if a pharmacist-validated ADE intervention is logged against that order within the following 48 hours, 0 otherwise. Interventions are filtered to ADE-relevant categories (toxicity-driven dose changes, adverse-reaction discontinuations, drug interaction escalations) and exclude administrative or formulary-substitution events. This yields an expected positive rate of approximately 2–5%, consistent with published inpatient ADE surveillance literature.

Data quality measures include: deduplication of duplicate order rows, exclusion of orders with missing patient identifiers, and a temporal audit on a 500-example random sample to verify that no features incorporate data timestamped after the prediction cutoff. Known data quality issues — including COVID-era disruptions to pharmacist staffing and intervention volume in 2020–2021 — are retained in training but flagged for subgroup monitoring rather than excluded, since they represent real variation in the deployment population.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split using a **stratified random 70/15/15 partition** at the medication-order–patient-day level so that train, validation, and test sets preserve the overall ADE intervention rate. To reduce correlation, any patient admission is assigned entirely to a single partition to prevent within-admission leakage. This also ensures each split contains examples from all hospitals and time periods, which can improve stability of model comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features respect the strict prediction cutoff: only data timestamped before 00:00 on the prediction day is used. Structured features include active medication attributes, rolling lab values (most recent + 48-hour delta for high-signal analytes), vitals, ICD-10 diagnoses, allergy flags, demographics, unit/service, and Charlson comorbidity index. Clinical text features are extracted from all progress, consult, and nursing notes prior to cutoff, concatenated in reverse chronological order.

Critically, to isolate the architectural hypothesis from an information-availability confound, **both models receive text**: the GBT baseline receives TF-IDF vectors (top 500 unigrams/bigrams, fit on training notes only) derived from the same note windows, plus note-count and recency metadata. A structured-only GBT is also run as a secondary ablation. All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vectorizer — are fit on the training partition only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a joint transformer: a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT backbone (lower learning rate) fused with a tabular MLP encoder via concatenated CLS token, followed by a two-layer classification head. Training uses AdamW with cosine LR schedule, class-weighted cross-entropy, and checkpoint selection on validation P@R40.

The **primary baseline** is a LightGBM model trained on structured + TF-IDF text features, tuned via 100-configuration random search on the same validation metric. A **secondary baseline** is structured-only LightGBM, enabling decomposition of text-access vs. architecture effects. Both baselines receive equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Precision at 40% Recall (P@R40)**. This reflects the operational objective: pharmacists work within a bounded alert queue, so the system must surface a meaningful fraction of true risks (recall floor) while maximizing alert precision. AUROC is reported as a secondary metric for literature comparability, alongside AUPRC (appropriate for imbalanced settings), Precision@50 and Precision@100 alerts per hospital-day (matching the queue-based deployment model), Brier score, and subgroup performance by site, service line, and calendar quarter.

All metrics on the test set are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals resampled at the patient level.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are selected by comparing candidate configurations on the test partition, optimizing P@R40. The validation split is still used for early stopping and training monitoring, but the final model choice is based on held-out test performance so that the strongest configuration is carried forward.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) information availability — matched text access in the baseline; (2) temporal leakage — strict cutoff enforcement with audit; (3) label policy heterogeneity — site/service fixed effects as features and site-stratified subgroup reporting; (4) feature volume — the structured-only ablation baseline. This experiment would establish whether joint multimodal transformer encoding yields measurable precision gains over a well-tuned text-augmented GBT in this health system's historical data. It would not establish external generalizability, real-time deployment feasibility, or whether observed gains persist under prospective pharmacist behavior.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random temporal split instead of a chronological one, allowing future data (e.g., 2023 examples) to appear in training and past data (e.g., 2020) in test. Given that pharmacist intervention policies, staffing, and EHR practices evolved significantly during and after the pandemic, this creates optimistic bias: the model can learn patterns from future practice states that were not available at earlier prediction times, inflating test performance unrealistically.

Hyperparameter selection is performed using the held-out test set, which leaks evaluation information into model development. This invalidates the final performance estimate, as the test set is no longer an unbiased estimate of out-of-sample generalization. Even if the validation set is used for early stopping, selecting the best model configuration on the test set means the final reported metrics are overfitted to that set.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of COVID-era data from 2020–2021 introduces harmful bias and should be excluded.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Variation in pharmacist staffing and intervention volume during the pandemic reflects real-world deployment conditions. Excluding it would create a false sense of stability and harm external validity. The correct response is to include it and monitor performance via subgroup analysis, not to remove it.

must_not_claim.claim: Using TF-IDF features for text in the GBT baseline gives it unfair access to unstructured data that wasn't intended in the hypothesis.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis explicitly compares architectures, not data modalities. To isolate representational advantages, both models must have equal access to information. Providing the GBT with text-derived features ensures a fair comparison and is a sound experimental control.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that using the test set for hyperparameter tuning invalidates the final performance estimate. The defender cannot legitimately justify this practice. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer notes that any use of the test set to select model configurations, even indirectly, introduces evaluation contamination and destroys the statistical validity of the test metric.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that random temporal splitting in a time-evolving clinical environment introduces temporal leakage, allowing future patterns to leak into training. The defender might argue that stratification improves stability, but this does not outweigh the risk of future leakage in operational deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that pharmacist behavior and EHR workflows changed over time, making chronological independence essential for realistic generalization; random mixing violates this assumption.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes both flaws and emphasizes their compound effect: random splitting increases similarity between train and test distributions, while test-based tuning further inflates performance, creating a doubly optimistic bias. This compound critique strengthens the case for rejection.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer articulates how both flaws interact multiplicatively to exaggerate performance gains beyond what would be seen in real deployment.

sound_design_reference:

### 1. Data and Labeling

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **chronologically**: training covers January 2020 – December 2021, validation covers January – June 2022, and the held-out test set covers July 2022 – June 2023. Temporal splitting is required because random splitting would allow models to observe future pharmacist intervention policy states and EHR workflow changes that would not exist at real prediction time, producing optimistically biased estimates. Any patient admission that straddles a boundary is assigned entirely to the earlier partition to prevent within-admission leakage. The 12-month test window captures seasonal, staffing, and formulary variation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vectorizer — are fit on the training partition only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a joint transformer: a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT backbone (lower learning rate) fused with a tabular MLP encoder via concatenated CLS token, followed by a two-layer classification head. Training uses AdamW with cosine LR schedule, class-weighted cross-entropy, and checkpoint selection on validation P@R40.

The **primary baseline** is a LightGBM model trained on structured + TF-IDF text features, tuned via 100-configuration random search on the same validation metric. A **secondary baseline** is structured-only LightGBM, enabling decomposition of text-access vs. architecture effects. Both baselines receive equivalent tuning effort.

The **primary metric is Precision at 40% Recall (P@R40)**. This reflects the operational objective: pharmacists work within a bounded alert queue, so the system must surface a meaningful fraction of true risks (recall floor) while maximizing alert precision. All metrics on the test set are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals resampled at the patient level.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

This experiment would establish whether joint multimodal transformer encoding yields measurable precision gains over a well-tuned text-augmented GBT in this health system's historical data. It would not establish external generalizability, real-time deployment feasibility, or whether observed gains persist under prospective pharmacist behavior.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because one flaw (random temporal split) requires understanding of evolving clinical practices over time, while the other (test-based tuning) is obvious but may be dismissed as a minor reporting oversight. A strong evaluator must recognize both as fatal flaws and understand their compounding effect on validity.

hypothesis: A graph neural network built on account-device-IP-beneficiary transfer relationships detects suspicious money-laundering transaction chains more effectively than a transaction-level gradient-boosted tree baseline using engineered tabular features, when evaluated on future SAR-linked alerts in a retail bank.
domain: Anti-money-laundering monitoring for a retail/commercial bank, where investigators review alerted transactions and accounts for potential suspicious activity reports (SARs).
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML Transaction Chain Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 36 months of production transaction logs from a single retail/commercial bank. Each record includes sender and receiver account IDs, timestamp, amount, channel, device fingerprint, IP address, KYC tier, counterparty identifiers, and historical alert and investigation outcomes. Using a single institution's data is deliberate: it controls for cross-institution variation in SAR filing culture and ensures the graph topology reflects one real deployment environment.

Labels are defined at the transaction level: a transaction is positive if it belongs to a cluster where any linked account filed a SAR within 90 days of the transaction date. The 90-day forward window is enforced strictly at each split boundary — no SAR outcome from beyond the window is used, and a 30-day dead zone at each boundary absorbs in-flight investigations. Transactions with no outcome within 90 days are treated as negatives, consistent with operational convention and applied uniformly to both models.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the transaction level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test using a stratified random split to preserve the rare positive rate across partitions. Because AML labels are highly imbalanced, stratification helps ensure stable model selection and fair comparison between the GNN and GBT across all splits.

Graph edges are constructed within each split window using only transactions whose timestamps fall within that window plus an allowed historical lookback. No test-period edges are materialized during training.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers — log-scaling, categorical encoders, imputation — are fit on the training set only and applied frozen to validation and test. Rolling aggregate features (e.g., 30-day transaction velocity, degree counts) are computed using only data available at or before the transaction timestamp, enforcing strict causal ordering.

Critically, the GBT baseline receives tabular versions of all relational signals the GNN processes: unique counterparty counts over 7/30/90-day windows, shared device and IP linkage counts, fan-out degree, and training-period betweenness centrality. This information parity is the experiment's most important design control — it ensures any advantage the GNN demonstrates reflects its capacity for joint relational inference, not privileged access to information.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a heterogeneous GNN (GraphSAGE or R-GCN, 2–3 layers) operating on the account-device-IP-beneficiary multigraph, producing transaction-level suspicion scores. The baseline is a LightGBM or XGBoost model trained on the information-equivalent tabular feature set. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: a 100-configuration randomized search over their respective hyperparameter spaces, evaluated on validation AP. Neither model is given any advantage in tuning budget or feature access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUROC on the test set. AUROC is a threshold-independent measure that captures overall discrimination quality and makes the results easier to compare with prior AML detection literature, while remaining robust to the choice of operating point.

Secondary metrics include precision at recall thresholds of 0.40 and 0.60 (the practical operating range), AUROC for comparability, alert lift at the bank's monthly investigation capacity K, Brier score for calibration quality, and typology-stratified AP to characterize where any advantage is concentrated.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Discipline

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter configurations, early stopping — is performed using validation AP on months 25–27 only. The test set is not examined until a single frozen model per approach is committed. The test set is used exactly once. This discipline is non-negotiable given how easy it is to inadvertently overfit to a test set across iterative AML model development cycles.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly managed. First, information asymmetry is neutralized by providing the GBT baseline with tabular graph-derived aggregates, so any GNN advantage is attributable to architecture. Second, relational leakage is controlled through the temporal split and dead zones; a post-hoc audit reports the fraction of test-period SAR clusters that are wholly novel versus partially overlapping with training-period rings. Third, investigator discovery bias in labels is addressed through a sensitivity analysis that evaluates both models on the subset of SAR-linked transactions that were originally surfaced by the bank's existing rule-based system, controlling for the fact that uninvestigated transactions are labeled negative by convention rather than confirmed innocence.

This design would establish whether the GNN's graph architecture provides measurable value over an information-equivalent tabular model on future-period data — and nothing more. It would not establish generalizability to other institutions, typologies not represented in 36 months of data, or performance under real-time inference constraints.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split at the transaction level, which allows transactions from the same laundering ring or entity to appear in both training and test sets. This introduces temporal and relational leakage, as future behavior is inadvertently exposed during training. Since the hypothesis explicitly concerns generalization to future patterns, the evaluation fails to assess real-world performance and risks inflating the GNN's apparent advantage due to data leakage.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which is poorly suited for this task due to extreme class imbalance (SAR-positive rate < 0.5%). AUROC gives excessive weight to true negatives, which dominate the sample, and can mask poor precision in the top-ranked alerts. Since the operational goal is to maximize detection within a fixed investigation capacity, Average Precision (AP) or precision-recall curves would be far more aligned with business impact.

must_not_claim.claim: The GBT baseline should not receive network-derived aggregate features because it gives the baseline 'unfair access' to relational information.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Providing the GBT baseline with tabular versions of relational signals (e.g., counterparty counts, shared devices) is essential to ensure information parity. Without this, any GNN advantage could be attributed to privileged feature access rather than architectural superiority. The design correctly isolates the value of joint relational inference by controlling for this confound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a single institution’s data limits generalizability and therefore invalidates the experiment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis is about performance in a real deployment environment, not universal generalization. Using single-institution data is appropriate and intentional: it ensures the graph topology reflects actual fraud patterns and controls for institution-specific SAR-filing practices, which vary widely.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the random transaction-level split introduces temporal and relational leakage, invalidating the future generalization claim. The defender cannot credibly justify this split for a time-sensitive, graph-structured AML task. The final verdict must be that the design is flawed and must be revised with a chronological split to be valid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that correlated entities across time can appear in both train and test, allowing leakage of future relational patterns and undermining the stated hypothesis about future-period performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that AUROC is misaligned with operational objectives under extreme class imbalance. A credible critique shows that high AUROC can coexist with poor precision in the top-ranked alerts, which harms real-world utility. The defender cannot override this mismatch, though could argue AUROC has value as a secondary comparability metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates via example or reference that AUROC is insensitive to false positives in the high-precision regime critical for AML investigations, and that AP or precision-at-recall would better reflect queue-based prioritization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender acknowledges AUROC's limitations but argues it is included to facilitate comparison with published literature; however, they must concede that AP should be the primary decision-making metric.

## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML Transaction Chain Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 36 months of production transaction logs from a single retail/commercial bank. Each record includes sender and receiver account IDs, timestamp, amount, channel, device fingerprint, IP address, KYC tier, counterparty identifiers, and historical alert and investigation outcomes. ### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–27 for validation (with a 30-day dead zone at both ends), and months 28–36 for the held-out test set (with a 30-day dead zone at the entry boundary). A temporal split is mandatory here — not optional. Random or stratified splits would allow laundering ring members who appear in test to also appear in train, inflating performance estimates through relational leakage. Temporal splitting also directly tests the hypothesis as stated: does the GNN generalize better to future, unseen suspicious patterns?

Graph edges are constructed within each split window using only transactions whose timestamps fall within that window plus an allowed historical lookback. No test-period edges are materialized during training.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers — log-scaling, categorical encoders, imputation — are fit on the training set only and applied frozen to validation and test. The baseline is a LightGBM or XGBoost model trained on the information-equivalent tabular feature set. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: a 100-configuration randomized search over their respective hyperparameter spaces, evaluated on validation AP. Neither model is given any advantage in tuning budget or feature access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Average Precision (AP) on the test set. AP is appropriate here because the class imbalance is severe (SAR-positive rate below 0.5%) and because it measures the full precision-recall tradeoff without being dominated by the true-negative majority, unlike AUROC. It directly maps to the bank's operational objective: surfacing more truly suspicious cases within a fixed investigation capacity.

Secondary metrics include precision at recall thresholds of 0.40 and 0.60 (the practical operating range), AUROC for comparability, alert lift at the bank's monthly investigation capacity K, Brier score for calibration quality, and typology-stratified AP to characterize where any advantage is concentrated.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Discipline

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter configurations, early stopping — is performed using validation AP on months 25–27 only. The test set is not examined until a single frozen model per approach is committed. The test set is used exactly once. This discipline is non-negotiable given how easy it is to inadvertently overfit to a test set across iterative AML model development cycles.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly managed. First, information asymmetry is neutralized by providing the GBT baseline with tabular graph-derived aggregates, so any GNN advantage is attributable to architecture. Second, relational leakage is controlled through the temporal split and dead zones; a post-hoc audit reports the fraction of test-period SAR clusters that are wholly novel versus partially overlapping with training-period rings. Third, investigator discovery bias in labels is addressed through a sensitivity analysis that evaluates both models on the subset of SAR-linked transactions that were originally surfaced by the bank's existing rule-based system, controlling for the fact that uninvestigated transactions are labeled negative by convention rather than confirmed innocence.

This design would establish whether the GNN's graph architecture provides measurable value over an information-equivalent tabular model on future-period data — and nothing more. It would not establish generalizability to other institutions, typologies not represented in 36 months of data, or performance under real-time inference constraints.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because one flaw (random split) is methodologically obvious to experts but may be masked by the justification of stratification for class balance, while the other (AUROC use) is subtle and requires domain awareness of operational AML constraints to recognize as a mismatch.

hypothesis: For hospital coding operations, a clinical Longformer model fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and inpatient progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for ICD-10 multi-label code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency but clinically specific diagnosis codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation operations for automated ICD-10 coding of inpatient hospital encounters
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Longformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3–4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter data from a multi-site hospital system (≥3 hospitals), targeting at least 80,000 complete, audited encounters after quality filtering. Each encounter record includes the discharge summary and all inpatient progress notes dated prior to or on the discharge date, linked to the final auditor-confirmed ICD-10-CM code set. Post-discharge addenda, billing queries, and coding addenda are explicitly excluded from model input to prevent post-discharge label leakage. Any encounter with incomplete coding audit status is excluded. ICD-10 codes with fewer than 10 occurrences across the full dataset are excluded from evaluation (though not from model training) to avoid degenerate F1 estimates; codes with 10–50 training occurrences form a designated 'rare-code' evaluation stratum, which is the specific subpopulation the hypothesis targets.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are randomly split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test sets, with stratification by overall coding density so that each split has a similar label-load profile. This yields more statistically stable estimates and helps ensure that each split contains a representative mixture of hospitals, documentation styles, and diagnosis prevalence. Encounters are never split mid-admission.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive exactly the same raw text input: the chronologically ordered concatenation of the discharge summary and all progress notes per encounter. This parity is non-negotiable — it ensures the comparison isolates architecture and representation rather than information access.

For the TF-IDF baseline, word-level and character-level unigram/bigram features are extracted (max 200,000 features, sublinear TF scaling). To stabilize the feature space across partitions, the TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus and then applied consistently to the training, validation, and test splits. For the Longformer, a pretrained clinical-domain checkpoint is used; the tokenizer is applied without refitting. For encounters exceeding 4,096 tokens, a sliding-window strategy (4,096-token windows with 512-token stride, mean-pooled) is applied to preserve all clinical evidence without silent truncation.

Label binarization and downstream feature application are then kept fixed across splits so that both models are evaluated under the same representational setup.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Clinical Longformer with a multi-label sigmoid classification head, fine-tuned end-to-end using binary cross-entropy with frequency-inverse positive-class weighting (capped at 10×). Training uses AdamW with linear warmup, peak LR ~2e-5, and early stopping on validation macro-F1.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression with balanced class weighting. It is trained using a standard regularization setting (C = 1.0), which is a strong and widely used reference point for sparse high-dimensional text classification. Both models operate on identical text inputs with no additional structured features available to either.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged F1 over all codes meeting the minimum occurrence threshold. Micro-F1 captures overall coding performance across the full encounter volume and provides a stable summary measure for large multi-label classification problems. Because the vocabulary is broad and highly imbalanced, micro-averaging avoids overemphasizing very low-support labels whose variance can dominate macro-level summaries.

Per-label decision thresholds are tuned independently to optimize F1 for both models, with final thresholds set after model family selection so that each code is evaluated at its strongest operating point.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 restricted to the rare-code stratum (10–50 training occurrences), macro-recall (coding completeness), per-site macro-F1 (confound check), and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent performance under imbalance).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Core model-development decisions are made using the validation set. The Longformer checkpoint with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected as the final neural model. After this, per-label decision thresholds for the final selected models are finalized on the held-out test set to ensure that reported F1 reflects the best achievable operating point for each code under the chosen deployment candidate. There is no iterative retraining after threshold finalization.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are directly addressed. First, text access parity: both models receive identical concatenated text with identical length-handling policies, ruling out the possibility that the Longformer benefits simply from seeing more text. Second, site-level generalization: per-site performance breakdowns on the test set verify that observed gains hold across hospital documentation styles and are not an artifact of one site's templates. Third, data-composition balance: the split strategy ensures that training, validation, and test each contain a broad mix of hospitals and coding patterns, reducing the risk that results are driven by an idiosyncratic subset of encounters.

An additional ablation is pre-registered: Longformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries only versus discharge summaries plus progress notes. This isolates whether multi-document access — rather than transformer architecture — is the true driver of any observed gain.

The experiment would establish whether a clinical Longformer architecture, given the same textual evidence as the baseline, yields stronger overall multi-label ICD-10 assignment performance and whether any gains extend into the rare-code subgroup. It would not establish generalizability to outpatient coding, to health systems with different documentation cultures, or to ICD-10-PCS procedure codes without separate evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random encounter-level split instead of a temporal split by discharge date. This allows documentation templates, coding policy changes, and case mix shifts from future time periods to leak into the training data, creating an overly optimistic performance estimate that would not generalize to future admissions. The design falsely assumes stationarity where it does not hold.

The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on the full dataset before the train/validation/test split, meaning IDF weights and vocabulary are influenced by future (test-time) documents. This contaminates the feature representation with information not available at training time, enabling subtle leakage of temporal patterns and biasing performance upward.

The primary metric is micro-F1, which aggregates performance weighted by label frequency. This directly contradicts the hypothesis, which emphasizes gains on low-frequency clinical codes. Micro-F1 can increase even if rare-code performance degrades, making it incapable of testing the stated claim.

The TF-IDF baseline is trained with a fixed default regularization parameter (C=1.0) without any validation-based tuning, while the Longformer is carefully tuned. This creates an unfair comparison that exaggerates the neural model's advantage and violates the principle of equal tuning effort for fair model comparison.

Per-label decision thresholds are optimized using the held-out test set. This constitutes post-hoc model selection on test data, inflating reported performance and invalidating the test set’s role as an unbiased estimate of generalization. Thresholds must be set on the validation set or through cross-validation.

must_not_claim.claim: The split is not stratified by hospital site, so performance may not generalize across institutions.

Site-level generalization is explicitly monitored, so lack of formal site stratification does not invalidate the design.

must_not_claim.why_wrong: Micro-F1 is appropriately used as a secondary metric to assess overall coding throughput and volume performance. The primary metric remains the key decision point; secondary metrics can provide complementary insights without compromising hypothesis testing when properly contextualized.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Longformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for ICD-10 Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3–4 years of de-identified inpatient encounter data from a multi-site hospital system (≥3 hospitals), targeting at least 80,000 complete, audited encounters after quality filtering. Each encounter record includes the discharge summary and all inpatient progress notes dated prior to or on the discharge date, linked to the final auditor-confirmed ICD-10-CM code set. Post-discharge addenda, billing queries, and coding addenda are explicitly excluded from model input to prevent post-discharge label leakage. Any encounter with incomplete coding audit status is excluded. ICD-10 codes with fewer than 10 occurrences across the full dataset are excluded from evaluation (though not from model training) to avoid degenerate F1 estimates; codes with 10–50 training occurrences form a designated 'rare-code' evaluation stratum, which is the specific subpopulation the hypothesis targets.

### 2. Split Strategy

Encounters are split temporally by discharge date: the earliest 70% of encounters form the training set (~2.5–3 years), the next 15% form the validation set (~6–9 months), and the final 15% form the held-out test set (~6 months). A temporal split is required because the deployment target is future admissions. Documentation templates, coding policy updates, and case-mix composition shift over calendar time; a random split would allow these future patterns to leak into training and produce optimistic performance estimates that would not hold operationally. Encounters are never split mid-admission.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive exactly the same raw text input: the chronologically ordered concatenation of the discharge summary and all progress notes per encounter. This parity is non-negotiable — it ensures the comparison isolates architecture and representation rather than information access.

For the TF-IDF baseline, word-level and character-level unigram/bigram features are extracted (max 200,000 features, sublinear TF scaling). The TF-IDF vectorizer vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the training set only and frozen for validation and test application. For the Longformer, a pretrained clinical-domain checkpoint is used; the tokenizer is applied without refitting. For encounters exceeding 4,096 tokens, a sliding-window strategy (4,096-token windows with 512-token stride, mean-pooled) is applied to preserve all clinical evidence without silent truncation.

All preprocessing fit decisions — including label binarizer, code-frequency thresholds, and TF-IDF statistics — are made on training data exclusively.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Clinical Longformer with a multi-label sigmoid classification head, fine-tuned end-to-end using binary cross-entropy with frequency-inverse positive-class weighting (capped at 10×). Training uses AdamW with linear warmup, peak LR ~2e-5, and early stopping on validation macro-F1.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression with balanced class weighting. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: regularization strength C is grid-searched over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation set and same macro-F1 criterion. Both models operate on identical text inputs with no additional structured features available to either.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 over all codes meeting the minimum occurrence threshold. Macro-F1 weights every code equally regardless of frequency, directly operationalizing the stakeholder requirement to maintain coding completeness on infrequent but reimbursable and quality-reporting diagnoses. Micro-F1 would be dominated by high-frequency codes and would mask the performance on rare codes that the hypothesis specifically addresses.

Per-label decision thresholds are tuned independently on the validation set for both models to avoid penalizing either for miscalibration.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (overall coding throughput), macro-F1 restricted to the rare-code stratum (10–50 training occurrences), macro-recall (coding completeness), per-site macro-F1 (confound check), and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent performance under imbalance).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions for both models are made using the validation set exclusively. The Longformer checkpoint with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected; the baseline C value with the highest validation macro-F1 is selected. The held-out test set is used exactly once, after all model selection and threshold tuning is finalized for both models. There is no iterative refinement after test-set evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds are directly addressed. First, text access parity: both models receive identical concatenated text with identical length-handling policies, ruling out the possibility that the Longformer benefits simply from seeing more text. Second, site-level generalization: per-site performance breakdowns on the test set verify that observed gains hold across hospital documentation styles and are not an artifact of one site's templates. Third, temporal generalization: the temporal split ensures that test-set encounters are genuinely future relative to training, validating that improvements hold under the case-mix and policy shifts relevant to deployment.

An additional ablation is pre-registered: Longformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries only versus discharge summaries plus progress notes. This isolates whether multi-document access — rather than transformer architecture — is the true driver of any observed gain.

The experiment would establish whether a clinical Longformer architecture, given the same textual evidence as the baseline, yields higher macro-F1 on multi-label ICD-10 assignment, particularly on rare codes. It would not establish generalizability to outpatient coding, to health systems with different documentation cultures, or to ICD-10-PCS procedure codes without separate evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because the flaws require understanding of temporal validity in operational ML, contamination via preprocessing, and misalignment between metrics and stakeholder goals. The compound effect of multiple corruptions, especially temporal and preprocessing leakage together, demands integrated reasoning rather than isolated pattern matching.

hypothesis: For predicting whether a commercial HVAC unit will experience a critical failure in the next 14 days, a transformer model trained on multivariate sensor event sequences and maintenance logs will achieve higher precision-recall AUC than a gradient-boosted tree model built on hand-engineered rolling-window агрегates.
domain: Manufacturing / industrial predictive maintenance for fleets of commercial building HVAC units monitored through IoT telemetry and technician service records
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 14-Day HVAC Critical Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 3 years of historical IoT telemetry and work-order records from a fleet of commercial HVAC units. Telemetry signals—temperature, pressure, vibration proxies, runtime hours, alarm codes, and control states—are retained at their native resolution (5-minute to hourly depending on sensor type) and linked to work-order records via unit identifier and timestamp.

Labels are constructed retrospectively. A sliding prediction window of 14-day forward horizon is generated at daily stride for each unit. A window is labeled positive if a verified critical failure (explicitly coded in the work order) occurs within that 14-day horizon; otherwise it is labeled negative. To reduce label ambiguity at the boundary between near-misses and true negatives, any window whose 14-day horizon ends within 7 days before a failure event is excluded from the negative class. Work orders that are still open or retroactively amended after the test set cutoff are excluded entirely. Missingness in sensor streams is preserved as an explicit binary indicator feature rather than silently imputed, because sensor dropouts are themselves informative.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is split using a stratified random 70/15/15 partition over labeled windows so that each split preserves the overall failure rate. To avoid over-representing any one facility, the split is balanced across sites, but windows from the same unit can appear in different partitions as long as label proportions are maintained.

This split improves comparability across train, validation, and test by ensuring each partition contains a similar mix of failures, sites, and operating conditions. It also yields more stable model selection because rare positive events are distributed more evenly across the three partitions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the GBT baseline, rolling-window aggregates (mean, standard deviation, min, max, rate of change, threshold exceedance counts) are computed over trailing windows of 1, 3, 7, and 14 days for each continuous sensor channel. Alarm code frequencies, runtime hours, days since last maintenance, and component age are added. To ensure consistent feature scaling across partitions, all scalers, imputers, and categorical vocabularies are fit once on the full dataset before model training, and the resulting transformations are then applied identically to train, validation, and test windows.

For the transformer, raw multivariate sensor sequences over the same 14-day lookback are used as input, with alarm and maintenance event types tokenized using the same globally derived vocabulary. Crucially, neither model uses free-text from maintenance notes or unit/site identity features. This equalization ensures that any performance difference reflects temporal representation capacity, not information access.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer is a multi-head self-attention encoder with time-elapsed positional encodings to handle irregular sampling, trained with class-weighted cross-entropy and AdamW optimization. The GBT baseline is LightGBM with standard class weighting and otherwise near-default settings, with only a small amount of manual adjustment to learning rate and tree depth for stability. Most of the model selection effort is focused on the transformer, since it is the primary method under study.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is ROC-AUC on the held-out test set, since it provides a threshold-independent summary of discrimination and is widely used for binary classification benchmarks. PR-AUC is reported as a secondary metric for completeness.

Secondary metrics include precision at fixed recall levels (0.5, 0.6, 0.7), F1 at the validation-optimal threshold, Brier score for calibration quality, and PR-AUC stratified by failure mode type and unit age tertile. Calibration is reported because downstream scheduling workflows may use predicted probabilities to prioritize dispatch, not just a binary flag.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is first narrowed using the validation partition. After initial training on the training set and screening on validation, the test set is used for a final round of threshold selection and to choose between the top few transformer configurations and the baseline variant that performs best in practice. The selected setup is then reported as the final model. For the transformer, early stopping uses validation ROC-AUC with a patience of 10 epochs to prevent overfitting.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly addressed. First, information asymmetry from maintenance logs is reduced by restricting both models to structured event-type tokens only — no free text, no embeddings. Second, history length is equalized at 14 days for both models. Third, unit and site identity are excluded from all feature sets to reduce direct identifier leakage. Fourth, a permutation test — shuffling prediction timestamps within units on the test set — serves as a sanity check that the transformer's gains depend on temporal ordering rather than feature statistics alone.

This experiment would establish whether a transformer operating on raw sensor sequences outperforms a practical GBT benchmark on this specific fleet and task, while comparing both models under a common preprocessing and evaluation pipeline. It would not establish generalizability beyond this dataset or equipment type.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split over labeled windows instead of a chronological, unit-exclusive split. This allows windows from the same unit to appear in multiple partitions and permits future seasonal or aging patterns to leak into training. As a result, the evaluation does not simulate real deployment, where models must predict on unseen future data from previously observed units.

All preprocessing components (scalers, imputers, vocabularies) are fit on the full dataset, including validation and test data. This leaks future distributional information into training, particularly harmful in a time-series setting where sensor drift or maintenance regimes evolve over time. The resulting performance estimates are artificially inflated.

The gradient-boosted tree (GBT) baseline receives minimal hyperparameter tuning, with only small manual adjustments, while most tuning effort focuses on the transformer. This undermines the fair comparison required by the hypothesis: any observed advantage may stem from tuning disparity rather than superior architecture.

ROC-AUC is used as the primary evaluation metric, despite the extreme class imbalance from rare failure events. ROC-AUC can be misleadingly optimistic because it depends heavily on specificity, which is dominated by the large negative class. This misaligns with the operational goal of identifying true failures efficiently and renders the experiment's conclusion potentially irrelevant to real-world outcomes.

The test set is used for final model selection and threshold tuning, which contaminates the evaluation and invalidates its role as an unbiased estimate of generalization. This practice inflates reported performance and makes the results unreliable for assessing true out-of-sample performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of free-text from maintenance logs weakens the transformer model unfairly.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Both models are equally restricted to structured event codes only, preserving a fair comparison of temporal modeling capacity. The transformer is not penalized; instead, textual confounds are controlled so the experiment isolates the value of sequence modeling over aggregates.

must_not_claim.claim: Not using unit or site identity features risks losing predictive signal.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are intentionally excluded to prevent artificial performance gains via unique identifier leakage. The design instead relies on meaningful telemetry and maintenance signals, aligning with the goal of testing generalizable predictive patterns rather than database-style lookups.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a temporal split reduces the size and stability of the training data, harming model convergence.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While the temporal split may reduce random sampling efficiency, it ensures temporal realism and prevents leakage. In operational predictive maintenance settings, future data must be predicted without access to itself, so the split reflects deployment constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic demonstrates that the random data split introduces temporal leakage by pointing to repeated windows across train/test from the same unit and the absence of chronological ordering. Defender cannot plausibly justify this for deployment scenarios.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer shows that the split allows future data to influence training and violates time-ordered domain dynamics.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies that preprocessing steps (scaling, imputation, tokenization) are fit on the full dataset, thus leaking information across partitions. This is especially problematic when combined with the random split, as it amplifies leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains how global preprocessing captures statistics from future partitions, biasing model evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic argues that the GBT baseline is under-tuned compared to the transformer, violating the hypothesis's requirement of architectural fairness.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer establishes that tuning disparity could explain any performance gap, making the architectural claim unsupportable.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic challenges the use of ROC-AUC as primary metric in a rare-event setting, arguing it misrepresents real operational performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer shows ROC-AUC can remain high even when precision is poor, making it inappropriate as a primary success indicator.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic reveals that the test set is used for model selection and threshold tuning, invalidating its role in performance estimation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that test-set reuse for selection introduces bias and violates evaluation independence.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for 14-Day HVAC Critical Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 3 years of historical IoT telemetry and work-order records from a fleet of commercial HVAC units. Telemetry signals—temperature, pressure, vibration proxies, runtime hours, alarm codes, and control states—are retained at their native resolution (5-minute to hourly depending on sensor type) and linked to work-order records via unit identifier and timestamp.

Labels are constructed retrospectively. A sliding prediction window of 14-day forward horizon is generated at daily stride for each unit. A window is labeled positive if a verified critical failure (explicitly coded in the work order) occurs within that 14-day horizon; otherwise it is labeled negative. To reduce label ambiguity at the boundary between near-misses and true negatives, any window whose 14-day horizon ends within 7 days before a failure event is excluded from the negative class. Work orders that are still open or retroactively amended after the test set cutoff are excluded entirely. Missingness in sensor streams is preserved as an explicit binary indicator feature rather than silently imputed, because sensor dropouts are themselves informative.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is split chronologically with strict unit-level exclusivity: training uses months 1–24, validation uses months 25–30, and testing uses months 31–36. No unit appears in more than one partition — all prediction windows for a given unit fall entirely within one time band. Site-level grouping is also enforced: units sharing a physical site are assigned to the same partition to prevent correlated failure information from leaking across splits.

This temporal, unit-aware design is essential for this hypothesis. Failure patterns in HVAC systems are driven by aging, seasonal load cycles, and cumulative wear — none of which are exchangeable across time. A random or purely unit-based split would allow future seasonal patterns to appear in training and would not simulate the deployment scenario, where the model always predicts on future data from units it may have partially observed.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the GBT baseline, rolling-window aggregates (mean, standard deviation, min, max, rate of change, threshold exceedance counts) are computed over trailing windows of 1, 3, 7, and 14 days for each continuous sensor channel. Alarm code frequencies, runtime hours, days since last maintenance, and component age are added. All scalers, imputers, and encoders are fit exclusively on training-partition windows and applied without modification to validation and test windows — there is no forward leakage in feature construction.

For the transformer, raw multivariate sensor sequences over the same 14-day lookback are used as input, with alarm and maintenance event types tokenized using a vocabulary derived from the training partition only. Crucially, neither model uses free-text from maintenance notes or unit/site identity features. This equalization ensures that any performance difference reflects temporal representation capacity, not information access.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer is a multi-head self-attention encoder with time-elapsed positional encodings to handle irregular sampling, trained with class-weighted cross-entropy and AdamW optimization. The GBT baseline is LightGBM with class imbalance weighting. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning: 60–80 trials of random search on the validation set, using validation PR-AUC as the selection criterion. Giving the baseline full tuning effort is essential — the hypothesis claims the transformer is architecturally superior, and that claim is only meaningful if the GBT is not hobbled by undertuning.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is PR-AUC on the held-out test set. Given the rarity of failure events, PR-AUC is far more informative than ROC-AUC, which is inflated by the large negative class. PR-AUC directly captures the precision-recall tradeoff that operations teams care about: catching enough failures to justify preventive service while keeping false alarm rates low enough to protect technician capacity.

Secondary metrics include precision at fixed recall levels (0.5, 0.6, 0.7), F1 at the validation-optimal threshold, Brier score for calibration quality, and PR-AUC stratified by failure mode type and unit age tertile. Calibration is reported because downstream scheduling workflows may use predicted probabilities to prioritize dispatch, not just a binary flag.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and early stopping decisions are made on the validation partition. The test set is locked until both models are fully finalized — it is evaluated exactly once, and no further modeling decisions are made after that evaluation. For the transformer, early stopping uses validation PR-AUC with a patience of 10 epochs to prevent overfitting to the validation window.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly neutralized. First, information asymmetry from maintenance logs is eliminated by restricting both models to structured event-type tokens only — no free text, no embeddings. Second, history length is equalized at 14 days for both models. Third, unit and site identity are excluded from all feature sets, and the unit-exclusive temporal split eliminates identity leakage through the data partition. Fourth, a permutation test — shuffling prediction timestamps within units on the test set — serves as a sanity check that the transformer's gains depend on temporal ordering rather than feature statistics alone.

This experiment would establish whether a transformer operating on raw sensor sequences outperforms a well-tuned GBT on hand-engineered aggregates for this specific fleet and task, under leakage-controlled, deployment-realistic conditions. It would not establish generalizability beyond this dataset or equipment type.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple interacting corruptions—especially the combination of temporal and preprocessing leakage—that mimic reasonable design choices while fundamentally breaking evaluation validity. A reviewer must overcome surface plausibility and recognize compound failures in methodology.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network built on account-device-IP-beneficiary transaction graphs will detect suspicious money-laundering accounts more effectively than a gradient-boosted tree model using account-level aggregated features when evaluated on alerts confirmed by AML investigators in a future quarter.
domain: Retail banking anti-money-laundering monitoring for consumer and small-business accounts

## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. GBT for AML Detection (hyp_025)

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 30 months of production transaction logs from the retail banking environment. The dataset includes timestamps, amounts, channels, counterparty account identifiers, device IDs, IP addresses, geolocation signals, KYC customer attributes, and AML case-management outcomes. Labels are defined at the account level: an account is positive if it was the subject of a finalized investigator-confirmed SAR filing or confirmed escalation, and negative if it was reviewed and closed without escalation. Accounts that triggered rule-based alerts but were never reviewed by an investigator are excluded from both classes — treating them as confirmed negatives would introduce systematic selection bias. To address label maturation delay, only dispositions finalized at least 60 days before the end of the evaluation window are included as labeled examples.

### 2. Split Strategy

The labeled accounts are randomly split into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test partitions, stratified by the SAR/escalation label to preserve class balance across splits. This keeps the class prevalence stable across partitions and ensures that each split contains sufficient positive examples for both model development and final evaluation. Because the graph is built from historical transactional interactions attached to each account, the random split still allows the model to learn from the full diversity of network structure present in the data while maintaining a clean held-out subset for testing.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed. For the GBT baseline: account-level rolling aggregates (7/30/90-day windows) covering transaction velocity, inflow/outflow totals, counterparty and device diversity, channel mix, temporal pattern features (night/weekend share, round-number proportion), and KYC attributes. For the T-GNN: the same raw signals are encoded as node and edge attributes on a heterogeneous temporal graph with four node types (Account, Device, IP, Beneficiary). Critically, any linked-entity structural statistic accessible to the GNN (e.g., two-hop neighbor count, device-sharing degree) is also computed as a hand-crafted aggregate and added to the GBT feature set. This information-parity constraint is essential: the hypothesis is about graph architecture, not feature richness. To ensure a consistent feature space across partitions, preprocessing transformers — including scalers, target encoders, and imputers — are fit once on the full labeled dataset before the train/validation/test split and then reused for all experiments.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Network (TGN) operating on the heterogeneous transaction graph, with a final MLP readout producing account-level suspicious probability scores. The baseline is a standard logistic regression model trained on the account-level aggregate features with default regularization settings, while tuning effort is concentrated on the T-GNN because it is the proposed method under study. Class imbalance is addressed in both models — focal loss for the GNN, class weighting for logistic regression.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUROC on the held-out reviewer-touched test population, since it provides a threshold-independent summary of discrimination and is standard for binary classification benchmarks. Secondary metrics include recall and precision at fixed alert rates (top 0.5% and top 1% by score), confirmed suspicious cases per 1,000 alerts (the operational KPI), AUPRC for additional context, and calibration metrics (Brier score, reliability diagram) required for compliance review.

### 6. Validation Approach

Initial hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set. After that stage, the final architecture choice and alert threshold are selected based on whichever configuration performs best on the test set, so that the reported model reflects the strongest end-to-end operating point. This produces a single final model and operating threshold for comparison.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are actively controlled. First, feature parity ensures GNN gains cannot be attributed to richer information access. Second, both models are trained from the same underlying historical transaction records and labels. Third, selection bias is addressed by restricting evaluation to reviewer-touched accounts and running a sensitivity analysis excluding accounts flagged only by the rule being replaced. Fourth, stratified performance checks across account type (consumer vs. small-business), KYC risk tier, and test-window month verify that gains are consistent rather than stratum-specific.

This experiment would establish whether the T-GNN architecture provides measurable lift over an account-level baseline on finalized investigator labels in a held-out subset of the available data. It would not establish generalizability to unseen laundering typologies, performance on non-alerted accounts, or causal mechanisms

planted_issues.description: The use of random stratified splitting instead of a chronological split introduces severe temporal leakage. In transactional graph data where neighborhood structure evolves over time and investigator labels mature slowly, allowing future accounts and interactions into the training set leaks future patterns and outcome information. This invalidates the evaluation of performance in a future quarter and produces non-deployable estimates.

Preprocessing transformers (scalers, imputers, target encoders) are fit on the full labeled dataset before splitting. This leaks distributional statistics and label information from validation and test sets into the training process. For high-risk rare-event modeling like AML detection, this can subtly encode patterns from future or unreviewed investigator decisions into preprocessing, inflating performance estimates unrealistically.

Using AUROC as the primary evaluation metric is misaligned with operational objectives in a highly imbalanced AML detection setting. AUROC measures separation across thresholds but can mask poor precision at low alert volumes. The stakeholder’s goal is to maximize detected suspicious cases within a fixed investigator budget, which requires focusing on precision-recall tradeoffs captured by AUPRC.

The baseline model uses logistic regression with default settings and receives no tuning, while the proposed GNN receives full hyperparameter optimization. This violates fairness: the baseline is not representative of current industry standards (GBTs), and unequal effort biases comparison. Gains could reflect model strength or tuning effort rather than architectural benefit.

The test set is used to select the final model architecture and alert threshold. This turns the test set into a de facto development set, contaminating the evaluation. Performance estimates are no longer unbiased estimates of future performance and cannot support valid claims about generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of unreviewed accounts as negatives introduces selection bias and weakens evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding unreviewed accounts is the correct choice here. Treating unreviewed alerted accounts as confirmed negatives would introduce strong selection bias because many may be suspicious but simply not reviewed due to capacity constraints. Restricting evaluation to reviewer-touched cases provides a cleaner, more reliable signal for model comparison.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a temporal split reduces the amount of training data and limits model learning.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Despite reducing apparent training size, a temporal split is necessary to prevent leakage in time-dependent graph data. The hypothesis explicitly requires evaluation on a future quarter, so chronological separation is mandatory to simulate deployment conditions and ensure valid performance estimates.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of linked-entity statistics in the GBT feature set makes it unfair to the GNN by giving it artificial parity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including hand-crafted versions of GNN-accessible network statistics in the GBT baseline ensures a fair comparison of architecture, not feature richness. The hypothesis is about the value of temporal graph modeling, not access to network structure, so controlling for feature availability is methodologically essential.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies the random split as invalid due to temporal leakage in a sequential transaction graph. They point out that future neighbor patterns and investigator behaviors would leak into training, invalidating forward-looking claims. The defense fails because no valid rebuttal exists for temporal leakage in this domain.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that random splitting allows future transaction edges and network structure to be used during training, which contradicts the hypothesis's requirement of testing on future data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that preprocessing is fit on the full dataset, leaking label and distribution information into training. They explain that target encoders and imputers fitted on future/test data carry label-dependent statistics into the model pipeline. The defense cannot argue this is safe due to the known risk of positive-label leakage in AML.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that fitting encoders or scalers on post-split data introduces information from validation/test labels or distributions into training, violating causality.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues AUROC is inappropriate as the primary metric given severe class imbalance and the operational need to maximize confirmed suspicious cases at low alert volumes. They advocate for AUPRC as the stakeholder-aligned choice. The defense fails because AUROC can be high even when early-recall performance is weak.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that AUROC may remain high even if the model performs poorly at high-precision, low-alert-rate operating points critical for AML operations.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic points out the baseline model is systematically disadvantaged: logistic regression with defaults receives negligible tuning compared to the GNN. They argue gains could stem from baseline weakness rather than architectural benefit. The defense cannot justify unequal tuning effort given the equivalence requirement for fair model comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the baseline is weaker in both model capacity and optimization effort, making results non-comparable.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic exposes that architecture selection and threshold calibration use test set performance, contaminating it as a held-out evaluation. This invalidates the generalization claim. The defense cannot claim this is acceptable practice because out-of-sample evaluation integrity is fundamental to empirical ML.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that using the test set for any decision affecting the final model—from architecture to threshold—biases performance estimates.

## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. GBT for AML Detection (hyp_025)

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 30 months of production transaction logs from the retail banking environment. The dataset includes timestamps, amounts, channels, counterparty account identifiers, device IDs, IP addresses, geolocation signals, KYC customer attributes, and AML case-management outcomes. Labels are defined at the account level: an account is positive if it was the subject of a finalized investigator-confirmed SAR filing or confirmed escalation, and negative if it was reviewed and closed without escalation. Accounts that triggered rule-based alerts but were never reviewed by an investigator are excluded from both classes — treating them as confirmed negatives would introduce systematic selection bias. To address label maturation delay, only dispositions finalized at least 60 days before the end of the evaluation window are included as labeled examples.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided chronologically: months 1–18 for training, months 19–22 for validation, and months 23–30 for the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here for two reasons. First, the GNN's graph structure is inherently temporal — allowing future edges into training would constitute direct leakage. Second, the hypothesis explicitly concerns performance in a future quarter, so the evaluation must reflect forward-looking deployment conditions. Random or stratified splits would allow the model to observe future network topology and future investigator decisions during training, producing optimistic and non-deployable performance estimates. To prevent entity-level leakage, no account, device, IP, or beneficiary node appearing in the test window contributes graph edges to the training graph beyond transactions timestamped before month 23.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two parallel feature sets are constructed. For the GBT baseline: account-level rolling aggregates (7/30/90-day windows) covering transaction velocity, inflow/outflow totals, counterparty and device diversity, channel mix, temporal pattern features (night/weekend share, round-number proportion), and KYC attributes. For the T-GNN: the same raw signals are encoded as node and edge attributes on a heterogeneous temporal graph with four node types (Account, Device, IP, Beneficiary). Critically, any linked-entity structural statistic accessible to the GNN (e.g., two-hop neighbor count, device-sharing degree) is also computed as a hand-crafted aggregate and added to the GBT feature set. This information-parity constraint is essential: the hypothesis is about graph architecture, not feature richness. All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, imputers — are fit exclusively on training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Network (TGN) operating on the heterogeneous transaction graph, with a final MLP readout producing account-level suspicious probability scores. The baseline is LightGBM trained on the information-parity feature set. Both models receive identical hyperparameter tuning effort: Bayesian optimization via Optuna with the same number of trials, using validation AUPRC as the objective. Class imbalance is addressed in both models — focal loss for the GNN, scale_pos_weight tuning for LightGBM.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the reviewer-touched test population. AUPRC is the correct choice under severe class imbalance and directly reflects the stakeholder's objective of maximizing confirmed suspicious accounts found within a fixed investigator budget. Secondary metrics include recall and precision at fixed alert rates (top 0.5% and top 1% by score), confirmed suspicious cases per 1,000 alerts (the operational KPI), AUROC for literature comparability, and calibration metrics (Brier score, reliability diagram) required for compliance review.

### 6. Validation Approach

All model selection and hyperparameter tuning decisions are made using the validation set (months 19–22) exclusively. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once, after a final locked model artifact is registered. No architectural or threshold decisions are revised based on test set results.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are actively controlled. First, feature parity ensures GNN gains cannot be attributed to richer information access. Second, temporal parity ensures both models observe identical historical windows. Third, selection bias is addressed by restricting evaluation to reviewer-touched accounts and running a sensitivity analysis excluding accounts flagged only by the rule being replaced. Fourth, stratified performance checks across account type (consumer vs. small-business), KYC risk tier, and test-window month verify that gains are consistent rather than stratum-specific.

This experiment would establish whether the T-GNN architecture provides measurable lift over an information-equivalent GBT on finalized investigator labels in a held-out future quarter. It would not establish generalizability to unseen laundering typologies, performance on non-alerted accounts, or causal mechanisms
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because it combines multiple interdependent methodological flaws—especially the compound effect of temporal and preprocessing leakage—while also including plausible-sounding justifications for incorrect choices. A correct evaluator must distinguish real issues from misleading domain-specific rationales and prioritize stakeholder-aligned metrics in a high-stakes, imbalanced setting.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher micro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency but clinically important diagnosis codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical informatics for automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient hospitalization documentation in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system, covering discharges from January 2021 through December 2023. Each encounter provides a discharge summary, up to 10 attending or consulting progress notes dated on or before the discharge date, hospital site, service line, and final audited ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any coded diagnosis are excluded. Encounters with late-amended codes (coded more than 30 days post-discharge) are also excluded, as these introduce label noise from retrospective audits that would not reflect prospective deployment conditions.

Labels are defined as multi-hot binary vectors over the ICD-10-CM codes present in the dataset. Codes appearing fewer than 10 times across all encounters are dropped from the prediction vocabulary to avoid degenerate evaluation on effectively unobservable classes. A low-frequency stratum (10–100 occurrences) and a high-frequency stratum (>100 occurrences) are pre-defined for stratified evaluation, directly operationalizing the hypothesis's emphasis on tail-code performance.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is randomly split at the encounter level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test sets, stratified by hospital site and approximate label cardinality to ensure each split is representative of the overall diagnosis mix. This yields balanced partitions while preserving enough data for both tuning and final evaluation.

A stratified random split is useful here because the task is highly multi-label and code frequencies are uneven; ensuring each partition reflects the full diagnosis distribution makes model comparison more stable and reduces the chance that one split is dominated by a particular site or service mix. All notes belonging to a single admission are kept together to eliminate within-encounter leakage. Hospital site distribution is verified across splits to confirm no dramatic site skew.

### 3. Feature Engineering
