For both models, the input is constructed identically: the discharge summary is concatenated with up to 10 progress notes in ascending chronological order, using a fixed separator token. Only notes dated on or before discharge are included — no post-discharge documentation is permitted, preserving temporal integrity.

For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenated text is vectorized using word unigrams and character 2–3-grams (up to 200k features, sublinear TF scaling). For the transformer, the text is tokenized with a pretrained clinical tokenizer and truncated to 4,096 tokens using a head+tail strategy (first 2,048 tokens prioritizing the discharge summary, last 2,048 tokens capturing recent progress notes).

To stabilize the feature space across splits, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus before partitioning, after which the resulting fixed representation is used for training, validation, and test evaluation. The pretrained tokenizer is not refit. No site or demographic features are incorporated in the primary experiment, ensuring the comparison isolates text representation quality.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a ClinicalLongformer (or equivalent clinical long-document transformer) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters — learning rate {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size {8, 16}, warmup steps — are tuned via grid search against the validation set. Label smoothing (ε=0.1) is applied to reduce overconfidence on frequent codes.

The baseline is TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest LinearSVC, receiving identical input text and the same label vocabulary. The baseline is trained with standard default settings to reflect a typical production sparse-text pipeline, and we keep the default regularization rather than tuning C so the comparison remains simple and reproducible. This baseline is the established, production-credible approach for automated ICD coding and represents the correct comparison for the hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged AUROC over all codes, reported for the full vocabulary and the two frequency strata. AUROC provides a threshold-independent summary and is a standard, stable metric for comparing multi-label classifiers.

Secondary metrics include macro-F1 (to capture tail-code behavior without domination by frequent codes), micro- and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent), and Precision@K and Recall@K for K ∈ {5, 10, 15} to evaluate the coder-shortlist recommendation workflow. All metrics are additionally reported stratified by hospital site and service line.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Model selection uses the validation set for the initial sweep, and the held-out test set is then used to choose the final checkpoint and confirm the best-performing hyperparameter configuration before reporting results. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples over test encounters) to characterize uncertainty in observed differences.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds require active control. First, text length advantage: both models receive identical input text, and a post-hoc regression of micro-F1 against encounter token count is run to quantify any residual length effect. Second, hospital-specific documentation templates: performance is stratified by site; gains isolated to sites with richer templating are attributed to documentation quality, not representation. Third, temporal coding policy drift: a supplementary ablation trains the TF-IDF baseline on the most recent 12 months only, to verify the transformer's advantage is not simply a recency artifact.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a TF-IDF+OvR baseline on multi-label ICD-10 coding, with particular rigor on tail-code strata. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, outpatient settings, procedure codes, or production deployment feasibility.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a temporal split by discharge date. This allows future coding patterns, documentation practices, and policy shifts to leak into training data, producing an unrealistically optimistic estimate of model performance in a prospective deployment setting where the model must predict on upcoming encounters.

The TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks statistics from validation and test data into the feature representation. This contaminates the training process and can artificially inflate performance, especially when language use evolves over time.

The proposed transformer model undergoes hyperparameter tuning, but the baseline uses default regularization settings without tuning. This creates an unfair comparison that biases results in favor of the transformer, failing to isolate the impact of model architecture from tuning effort.

The primary metric is micro-averaged AUROC, which is threshold-independent and does not reflect the real-world operational need for accurate thresholded code assignments. It can mask poor precision on rare but critical codes, misaligning evaluation with clinical and financial stakes.

The test set is used to select the final model checkpoint or hyperparameter configuration, which invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate of generalization performance. This turns the test set into a de facto validation set and risks overfitting to its specific patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the random split by hospital site and label cardinality introduces data leakage or is methodologically unsound.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by site and label density is a valid technique to ensure balanced representation across splits when using random partitioning. While a temporal split is required here due to time drift, the stratification itself is not the flaw — the use of random splitting in a prospective prediction task is.

must_not_claim.claim: Not tuning hyperparameters on the transformer model would make it an unfair baseline comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Tuning the proposed model is appropriate and expected; the flaw lies in not tuning the baseline, not in tuning the transformer. A strong, fair comparison requires equivalent effort on both sides.

must_not_claim.claim: Using bootstrap confidence intervals on the test set is problematic or invalid.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Reporting bootstrap CIs over test encounters is a sound practice for quantifying uncertainty in performance estimates and does not constitute test set misuse as long as no decisions are made based on them.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that random splitting invalidates prospective generalization claims due to temporal confounding. The defender cannot credibly justify this choice in this domain. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that coding policies and documentation practices change over time, making temporal separation necessary for realistic evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that fitting TF-IDF on the full corpus leaks future information into training features, especially problematic given temporal drift. The issue is compounded by the non-temporal split. The verdict must reflect this as a serious methodological flaw.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer notes that IDF weights computed on future data can distort term importance in ways that bias performance upward.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: None — in this setting with evolving clinical language and coding norms, full-corpus fitting is invalid.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic points out that tuning only the transformer while leaving the baseline at defaults biases the experiment. The defender cannot claim this reflects 'typical production use' given the sound design specifies a tuned baseline as the credible production comparator. The verdict must favor critique unless this is corrected.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that a fair test isolates representation quality, not the effect of tuning effort.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that AUROC is insufficiently aligned with operational needs for thresholded code recommendation and can conceal poor precision on rare codes. The defender may argue for AUROC as a diagnostic tool, but cannot override the mismatch with the primary success criterion (micro-F1 for error minimization).
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer links AUROC's insensitivity to thresholding and class imbalance to potential real-world failures in coder trust and reimbursement risk.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: If the hypothesis had emphasized ranking quality over final predictions, AUROC could be justified — but here, micro-F1 at threshold 0.5 is the stated goal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic shows that using the test set for model selection invalidates its role as an independent evaluation set. The defender cannot justify this under any rigorous evaluation protocol. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that any use of test set feedback for model selection introduces overfitting and breaks statistical validity.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system, covering discharges from January 2021 through December 2023. Each encounter provides a discharge summary, up to 10 attending or consulting progress notes dated on or before the discharge date, hospital site, service line, and final audited ICD-10 diagnosis codes from the billing workflow. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any coded diagnosis are excluded. Encounters with late-amended codes (coded more than 30 days post-discharge) are also excluded, as these introduce label noise from retrospective audits that would not reflect prospective deployment conditions.

Labels are defined as multi-hot binary vectors over the ICD-10-CM codes present in the dataset. Codes appearing fewer than 10 times across all encounters are dropped from the prediction vocabulary to avoid degenerate evaluation on effectively unobservable classes. A low-frequency stratum (10–100 occurrences) and a high-frequency stratum (>100 occurrences) are pre-defined for stratified evaluation, directly operationalizing the hypothesis's emphasis on tail-code performance.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally by discharge date: the first 24 months (Jan 2021–Dec 2022) constitute the training set, months 25–30 (Jan–Jun 2023) the validation set, and months 31–36 (Jul–Dec 2023) the held-out test set. This yields an approximate 67/17/17 split by time.

A temporal split is mandatory here. The deployment context is strictly prospective — the model will always be applied to future encounters — and ICD-10 code sets, hospital documentation templates, and coding policies all evolve over time. A random split would allow future coding patterns to contaminate the training distribution, producing an optimistic and unrealistic performance estimate. All notes belonging to a single admission are kept together to eliminate within-encounter leakage. Hospital site distribution is verified across splits to confirm no dramatic site skew.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is constructed identically: the discharge summary is concatenated with up to 10 progress notes in ascending chronological order, using a fixed separator token. Only notes dated on or before discharge are included — no post-discharge documentation is permitted, preserving temporal integrity.

For the TF-IDF baseline, this concatenated text is vectorized using word unigrams and character 2–3-grams (up to 200k features, sublinear TF scaling). For the transformer, the text is tokenized with a pretrained clinical tokenizer and truncated to 4,096 tokens using a head+tail strategy (first 2,048 tokens prioritizing the discharge summary, last 2,048 tokens capturing recent progress notes).

All preprocessing transformers — TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied transform-only to validation and test sets. The pretrained tokenizer is not refit. No site or demographic features are incorporated in the primary experiment, ensuring the comparison isolates text representation quality.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a ClinicalLongformer (or equivalent clinical long-document transformer) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head and binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters — learning rate {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size {8, 16}, warmup steps — are tuned via grid search against the validation set. Label smoothing (ε=0.1) is applied to reduce overconfidence on frequent codes.

The baseline is TF-IDF + One-vs-Rest LinearSVC, receiving identical input text and the same label vocabulary. The regularization parameter C is tuned over {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} on the same validation set using the same selection metric. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort. This baseline is the established, production-credible approach for automated ICD coding and represents the correct comparison for the hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 at a fixed threshold of 0.5, reported for three code strata: all codes, high-frequency, and low-frequency. Micro-F1 aligns with the operational objective of minimizing per-prediction errors affecting reimbursement integrity. The tail-code stratification directly addresses the hypothesis claim about low-frequency clinically important codes.

Secondary metrics include macro-F1 (to capture tail-code behavior without domination by frequent codes), micro- and macro-averaged AUC-PR (threshold-independent), and Precision@K and Recall@K for K ∈ {5, 10, 15} to evaluate the coder-shortlist recommendation workflow. All metrics are additionally reported stratified by hospital site and service line.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Model selection — including early stopping for the transformer and C selection for the baseline — uses only the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 resamples over test encounters) to characterize uncertainty in observed differences.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds require active control. First, text length advantage: both models receive identical input text, and a post-hoc regression of micro-F1 against encounter token count is run to quantify any residual length effect. Second, hospital-specific documentation templates: performance is stratified by site; gains isolated to sites with richer templating are attributed to documentation quality, not representation. Third, temporal coding policy drift: a supplementary ablation trains the TF-IDF baseline on the most recent 12 months only, to verify the transformer's advantage is not simply a recency artifact.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF+OvR baseline on prospective multi-label ICD-10 coding, with particular rigor on tail-code strata. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, outpatient settings, procedure codes, or production deployment feasibility.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple interacting corruptions, especially the combination of random splitting and full-corpus preprocessing, which subtly reinforce each other. Additionally, the metric mismatch is subtle but critical, requiring domain knowledge to recognize that AUROC does not reflect operational coding quality.

hypothesis: For first-year undergraduate courses on a large online learning platform, a sequence model built from timestamped clickstream and assessment event histories predicts next-week dropout risk more accurately than a baseline using only aggregated weekly engagement features, when evaluated on future academic terms.
domain: Education technology: dropout risk prediction in credit-bearing first-year university courses delivered through a learning management system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Aggregated Features for Weekly Dropout Risk Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses four academic years of LMS event logs merged with student information system records, covering all first-year credit-bearing undergraduate courses with at least 30 enrolled students and at least 8 weeks of active delivery. The prediction target is a binary per-student per-week dropout label: a student is labeled positive at week W if they officially withdraw before the end of week W+1, or if they show zero LMS activity in weeks W+1 and W+2 and ultimately do not complete the course. Labels are derived from official withdrawal records; ambiguous cases such as approved medical leaves are excluded. Only weeks 2 through 7 of each course are included as prediction opportunities, targeting the window where intervention is both possible and meaningful.

### 2. Split Strategy

The full dataset is randomly split at the student-course-week level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test, stratified by the dropout label so that the rare positive class is balanced across partitions. This provides more stable class proportions and ensures all terms and course types are represented in each split. No student-course data from week W+1 onward is used to construct features for week W, enforcing the prediction-time information cutoff throughout.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Two Conditions

Two feature sets are constructed using only information available at or before the end of prediction week W. The **baseline feature set** consists of aggregated weekly event counts per category (views, submissions, logins, forum posts, video completions) for the current and prior two weeks, course-relative z-scores of each count, days since last login, cumulative grade to date, missed deadline flags, and student static attributes. The **experimental feature set** extends the baseline by adding the full event-level token sequence (event type, day-of-week, hour bucket, relative position) for the same three-week window, with the static and cumulative features appended at the sequence level. To keep feature definitions consistent across years and course sections, all scaling, normalization, imputation, and encoding transformers are fit once on the full dataset before splitting and then applied uniformly across training, validation, and test partitions.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The sequence model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 heads, 2 layers, embedding dimension 64) with a CLS token that aggregates sequence context, followed by a two-layer MLP classifier. The primary baseline is logistic regression trained on the aggregated weekly feature set with standard default settings, providing a simple and interpretable reference point for weekly aggregate modeling. Tuning effort is concentrated on the Transformer, with a 60-configuration random search used to optimize the sequence model on the validation set. A critical secondary ablation trains LightGBM on a flattened version of the full granular feature set to isolate whether any observed gain is attributable to sequential structure versus simply richer information access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is ROC-AUC, evaluated on the held-out test set. This is appropriate because it is threshold-independent, robust across class balance settings, and directly comparable to prior classification literature. Secondary metrics include Recall at top-5% and top-10% alert rates, average prediction lead time before confirmed withdrawal, expected calibration error, and precision-recall summaries stratified by course domain and student prior GPA band.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter and architecture decisions are initially guided by validation-set performance. After this stage, the strongest candidate checkpoints and minor architecture variants are compared on the test set, and the best-performing model is selected as the final model to report. Final metrics are then reported for that selected model on the same test split.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the information-content confound is handled by the granular-feature LightGBM ablation: only if the Transformer outperforms this ablation can the advantage be attributed to sequential structure. Second, course composition differences are addressed by course-relative z-scoring of all event counts and by reporting metrics stratified by course type and term. Third, cohort shift across terms is assessed via a covariate shift diagnostic on aggregated feature distributions between training and test terms; if substantial drift is detected, results are additionally reported on a propensity-matched subset of stable courses. The experiment establishes whether sequence modeling provides a measurable gain over aggregate-feature baselines; it does not establish causal dropout mechanisms or generalize to non-first-year or non-credit-bearing contexts.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split at the student-course-week level rather than a chronological term-level split. This allows data from future academic terms to leak into the training set, violating the prospective evaluation requirement of the hypothesis. Because course designs, platform features, and student behavior evolve over time, this inflates performance estimates and fails to simulate real-world deployment conditions.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, normalization, imputation, encoding) are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which contaminates the training process with information from the validation and test sets. This is especially harmful for course-relative z-scoring and categorical encoding, where future term distributions influence the transformation logic, leading to optimistic bias in model evaluation.

The primary baseline is an untuned logistic regression with default settings, while the sequence model receives substantial hyperparameter tuning. This creates an unfair comparison: any observed performance gap may reflect tuning effort rather than the inherent value of sequential modeling. A strong, tuned tree-based model like LightGBM is the appropriate baseline for this domain.

ROC-AUC is used as the primary evaluation metric despite the rare-event, low-alert-capacity context. For a dropout prediction task where stakeholders need high precision among a small fraction of flagged students, ROC-AUC is insensitive to performance in the operationally relevant region. Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUCPR) is a more appropriate primary metric.

The test set is used to select the best-performing model variant from among candidates, which turns the test set into a secondary validation set. This introduces bias and invalidates the final performance estimate as an unbiased measure of generalization to unseen future data.

must_not_claim.claim: Course-relative z-scoring is problematic because it normalizes per course and might mask meaningful cross-course patterns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Course-relative z-scoring is appropriate here because event activity levels vary dramatically by course design and instructor. Without it, models could exploit course-specific volume as a proxy for risk rather than learning meaningful behavioral signals. The design correctly isolates per-student deviation from their course norm.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a secondary ablation with LightGBM on flattened granular features is unnecessary or redundant.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This ablation is essential to control for the information-content confound. It ensures that any performance gain from the sequence model is due to modeling sequential structure, not merely access to finer-grained features. This strengthens causal interpretability within the experimental design.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of ambiguous cases like medical withdrawals invalidates the dropout label definition.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The label definition appropriately focuses on clear dropout signals that can be operationally acted upon. Including uncertain cases would introduce noise and reduce construct validity. The study explicitly notes these exclusions and does not claim to model all forms of withdrawal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the temporal leakage due to random splitting and explains that the hypothesis requires prospective validation on future terms. The defender cannot credibly rebut this flaw.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer notes that academic years are temporally ordered and that deployment is prospective, so a chronological split is non-negotiable for valid generalization.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer detects preprocessing leakage by identifying that transformers are fit on the full dataset, violating temporal and informational boundaries. This is especially damning when combined with the random split.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that course-relative normalization and categorical encoding depend on future data distributions, leading to data leakage and inflated performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer challenges the use of untuned logistic regression as a baseline, arguing it does not represent current best practice and undermines comparison validity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer cites that in ed-tech settings, gradient-boosted trees (e.g., LightGBM) are standard baselines for tabular risk modeling and should receive equivalent tuning effort to ensure a fair test.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender could argue if the goal were interpretability or simplicity, but in this context, performance comparison is primary, so defense fails.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer argues that ROC-AUC is inappropriate for a rare positive class and that AUCPR better captures performance in the low-alert-rate regime.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer references stakeholder constraints (limited advisor capacity) and notes that ROC-AUC can mask poor precision in the top-ranked predictions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Defender might argue comparability to literature, but this does not override operational relevance; defense is weak.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies test set reuse for model selection, rendering the final evaluation biased.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer states that once the test set informs model choice, it ceases to be independent and cannot provide unbiased performance estimates.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Aggregated Features for Weekly Dropout Risk Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses four academic years of LMS event logs merged with student information system records, covering all first-year credit-bearing undergraduate courses with at least 30 enrolled students and at least 8 weeks of active delivery. The prediction target is a binary per-student per-week dropout label: a student is labeled positive at week W if they officially withdraw before the end of week W+1, or if they show zero LMS activity in weeks W+1 and W+2 and ultimately do not complete the course. Labels are derived from official withdrawal records; ambiguous cases such as approved medical leaves are excluded. Only weeks 2 through 7 of each course are included as prediction opportunities, targeting the window where intervention is both possible and meaningful.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically at the term level: Years 1–2 for training, Year 3 for validation, and Year 4 as the held-out test set. A term-level temporal split is essential here because the hypothesis explicitly concerns prospective deployment performance — random or stratified splits would allow future cohort behavior to leak into training and would not simulate the real deployment scenario. No student-course data from week W+1 onward is used to construct features for week W, enforcing the prediction-time information cutoff throughout.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Two Conditions

Two feature sets are constructed using only information available at or before the end of prediction week W. The **baseline feature set** consists of aggregated weekly event counts per category (views, submissions, logins, forum posts, video completions) for the current and prior two weeks, course-relative z-scores of each count, days since last login, cumulative grade to date, missed deadline flags, and student static attributes. The **experimental feature set** extends the baseline by adding the full event-level token sequence (event type, day-of-week, hour bucket, relative position) for the same three-week window, with the static and cumulative features appended at the sequence level. All scaling, normalization, and encoding transformers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The sequence model is a lightweight Transformer encoder (4 heads, 2 layers, embedding dimension 64) with a CLS token that aggregates sequence context, followed by a two-layer MLP classifier. The primary baseline is LightGBM trained on the aggregated weekly feature set, representing current best practice. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: 60-configuration random search evaluated on the Year 3 validation set using AUCPR. A critical secondary ablation trains LightGBM on a flattened version of the full granular feature set to isolate whether any observed gain is attributable to sequential structure versus simply richer information access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUCPR), evaluated on the Year 4 test set. This is appropriate because dropout is a rare event (estimated 5–15% positive rate), because stakeholders operate under staffing constraints that fix alert volume, and because no deployment threshold is specified in advance. Secondary metrics include Recall at top-5% and top-10% alert rates, average prediction lead time before confirmed withdrawal, expected calibration error, and AUCPR stratified by course domain and student prior GPA band.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter and architecture decisions are made using the Year 3 validation set AUCPR. The test set is evaluated exactly once after model selection is finalized and checkpoints are frozen. No iteration based on test set feedback is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, the information-content confound is handled by the granular-feature LightGBM ablation: only if the Transformer outperforms this ablation can the advantage be attributed to sequential structure. Second, course composition differences are addressed by course-relative z-scoring of all event counts and by reporting metrics stratified by course type and term. Third, cohort shift across terms is assessed via a covariate shift diagnostic on aggregated feature distributions between training and test terms; if substantial drift is detected, results are additionally reported on a propensity-matched subset of stable courses. The experiment establishes whether sequence modeling provides a measurable AUCPR gain over strong aggregated-feature baselines on future terms; it does not establish causal dropout mechanisms or generalize to non-first-year or non-credit-bearing contexts.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it contains multiple interacting flaws—particularly the combination of temporal leakage and preprocessing leakage—which amplify each other. Additionally, the corrupted design mimics a plausible, carefully considered experiment, requiring deep domain and methodological reasoning to detect all issues.

hypothesis: In hospital revenue-cycle operations, a transformer model that encodes the full sequence of claim status updates, denial reason codes, and payer communications will predict whether an initially denied claim is recoverable within 90 days more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on aggregated claim and patient-account features.
domain: Healthcare revenue cycle management for a multi-hospital system handling insurer claim denials and appeals
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Recoverability Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of denied claims data from the hospital system's revenue cycle platform. All claims with a complete 90-day follow-up window are eligible — this excludes any denial occurring in the final 90 days of the observation period. The binary label is defined as: did the denied claim receive any partial or full reimbursement within 90 days of the initial denial date? This window is anchored to the denial date and constructed by joining the claim to the payment ledger. Claims with ambiguous final status at the 90-day mark are excluded (not imputed), and the exclusion rate is reported as a data quality diagnostic. The result is a labeled dataset of hundreds of thousands of denied claims with full event histories.

### 2. Prediction Timestamp and Feature Construction

The critical methodological constraint is that both models see only information available at the moment a denial enters the work queue — operationalized as denial_date + 2 days to account for processing lag. No features derived from post-denial events are permitted for either model.

The shared feature set includes static claim attributes (billed amount, procedure code group, service line, encounter type, discharge diagnosis category, provider department, days from service to submission), payer features (payer ID, contract tier, historical denial rate for payer/procedure combinations computed from training data only), and denial-event features (denial reason code, code family, prior denial count, time-to-denial).

For the transformer, the full ordered sequence of status-update events up to the initial denial is encoded as tuples of (event_type, reason_code, payer_action, days_since_prior_event). For the GBT, the same event history is represented as aggregated scalars: event count, unique reason codes, most recent reason code, and time-to-denial. This ensures information parity — neither model has access to data the other cannot see.

### 3. Split Strategy

The dataset is split 70/15/15 into training, validation, and test sets using a stratified random split on the reimbursement label to preserve class balance across partitions. Group-based splitting by patient account is enforced during sampling to prevent patient-level leakage.

To ensure a stable feature space across all partitions, preprocessing transformers — encoders, scalers, sequence vocabulary — are fit once on the full dataset before model training, and the resulting transformations are then applied uniformly to the training, validation, and test sets.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer is a BERT-style encoder (4–6 heads, 2–3 layers, 64–256 embedding dimensions) with static features concatenated to the [CLS] token before the classification head. The GBT baseline is LightGBM on the aggregated feature set trained with standard library defaults, aside from setting the random seed for reproducibility. Hyperparameter tuning is focused on the transformer, since it is the proposed model and expected to be more sensitive to configuration choices. Class imbalance is addressed in both models via class-weighted loss/sample weights inversely proportional to class frequency.

### 5. Primary Metric

The primary metric is AUROC on the held-out test set, since it is threshold-independent and provides a standard measure of discrimination for binary classification. Dollar-weighted F2, AUPRC, and calibration error are reported as secondary metrics for additional operational context. Stratified evaluation by payer tier and denial code family, plus false-negative rate on top-decile billed-amount claims, guards against aggregate scores masking subgroup failures.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Model development is guided by validation performance initially, but final hyperparameter selection and threshold choice are made by comparing the top candidate configurations on the test set and retaining the best-performing version before reporting final results.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly managed: information asymmetry (controlled by feature parity at a shared prediction timestamp), payer/service-line heterogeneity (controlled by stratified evaluation), and temporal distribution shift (addressed by reporting performance across validation time windows). The experiment establishes whether the transformer outperforms the GBT on this system's data under these conditions. It does not establish cross-system generalizability, and ablations isolating the sequence architecture contribution are recommended as follow-on work.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a forward-in-time temporal split. This leaks future denial resolution patterns into the training data, violating the real-world deployment constraint where models must predict only from historical data. Because payer behaviors and denial policies evolve over time, this artificial mixing of time periods inflates performance estimates and creates a misleading benchmark.

Preprocessing components (encoders, scalers, sequence vocabulary) are fit on the full dataset, including validation and test data. This allows information from unseen future data to influence feature representation, which contaminates the evaluation. For example, rare event codes in the test set could affect vocabulary construction, giving the model an unrealistic advantage not possible in production.

The primary metric is AUROC, which measures pairwise ranking discrimination but does not reflect the actual business goal: maximizing recovered dollars under a fixed collector workload. A model can have high AUROC but poor prioritization of high-value claims under capacity constraints, making this metric misaligned with operational value.

Final hyperparameter and threshold selection are performed using the test set. This repurposes the held-out test set as a development set, eliminating its ability to provide an unbiased estimate of generalization performance. The reported results are therefore optimistically biased.

The GBT baseline receives minimal tuning (library defaults) while the transformer undergoes extensive hyperparameter optimization. This imbalance biases the comparison in favor of the proposed model and confounds whether performance gains come from architecture or optimization effort, invalidating the claim about sequence modeling advantages.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of group-based splitting by patient account is insufficient because random sampling may still allow leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design correctly uses group-based splitting on patient account to prevent leakage, which is appropriate. The core issue is not the absence of grouping but the use of random temporal sampling, which invalidates the forward-prediction setting regardless of grouping.

must_not_claim.claim: Reporting AUROC as a secondary metric is inappropriate or misleading.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUROC is a valid secondary metric for assessing general discriminative capacity and enabling comparison with literature. The flaw is elevating it to primary status, not reporting it at all.

must_not_claim.claim: The feature set lacks important operational variables like collector assignment or appeal type.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The feature set is carefully designed to reflect only data available at triage time (denial_date + 2 days). Including post-triage variables would introduce target leakage. The design correctly excludes such features.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the random split creates forward-looking leakage inappropriate for temporal healthcare operations, and insists on a strict time-based partitioning (training: months 1–24, validation: 25–30, test: 31–36). The defender fails to justify random splitting in this context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that real-world deployment requires models to predict forward in time, and that random splits conflate temporal patterns, violating this assumption.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer detects that preprocessing steps (e.g., encoding, scaling) are fit on the full dataset and recognizes this as evaluation contamination, requiring fit-on-train-only to simulate real deployment conditions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that fitting on full data allows held-out statistics (e.g., rare codes, distribution moments) to influence training, which cannot happen in production systems.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer argues that AUROC is an inadequate primary metric because it does not reflect the hospital’s objective of recovering maximum dollars under limited collector bandwidth, and demands dollar-weighted F2 under capacity constraints as the primary success criterion.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic links the metric to stakeholder incentives: missing high-dollar recoverable claims (false negatives) is costlier than working non-recoverable ones, and AUROC does not prioritize accordingly.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies test set reuse for model selection and insists on single, final evaluation only after all decisions are locked using the validation set.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that using test performance for selection leads to overfitting the test set and upward bias in reported performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes the asymmetric tuning effort and requires equal optimization budgets for both models to isolate architectural advantage from tuning advantage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that unequal tuning effort confounds performance differences, making it impossible to attribute gains to the transformer architecture itself.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Recoverability Prediction

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 3 years of denied claims data from the hospital system's revenue cycle platform. All claims with a complete 90-day follow-up window are eligible — this excludes any denial occurring in the final 90 days of the observation period. The binary label is defined as: did the denied claim receive any partial or full reimbursement within 90 days of the initial denial date? This window is anchored to the denial date and constructed by joining the claim to the payment ledger. Claims with ambiguous final status at the 90-day mark are excluded (not imputed), and the exclusion rate is reported as a data quality diagnostic. The result is a labeled dataset of hundreds of thousands of denied claims with full event histories.

### 2. Prediction Timestamp and Feature Construction

The critical methodological constraint is that both models see only information available at the moment a denial enters the work queue — operationalized as denial_date + 2 days to account for processing lag. No features derived from post-denial events are permitted for either model.

The shared feature set includes static claim attributes (billed amount, procedure code group, service line, encounter type, discharge diagnosis category, provider department, days from service to submission), payer features (payer ID, contract tier, historical denial rate for payer/procedure combinations computed from training data only), and denial-event features (denial reason code, code family, prior denial count, time-to-denial).

For the transformer, the full ordered sequence of status-update events up to the initial denial is encoded as tuples of (event_type, reason_code, payer_action, days_since_prior_event). For the GBT, the same event history is represented as aggregated scalars: event count, unique reason codes, most recent reason code, and time-to-denial. This ensures information parity — neither model has access to data the other cannot see.

### 3. Split Strategy

A strict temporal split is used: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, months 31–36 for the held-out test set. Random splitting is explicitly rejected because the temporal structure of the claim lifecycle means that a random split would allow future knowledge of denial resolution patterns to contaminate training, and because deployment always involves predicting forward in time. Group-based splitting by patient account is additionally enforced within temporal boundaries to prevent patient-level leakage.

All preprocessing transformers — encoders, scalers, sequence vocabulary — are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The transformer is a BERT-style encoder (4–6 heads, 2–3 layers, 64–256 embedding dimensions) with static features concatenated to the [CLS] token before the classification head. The GBT is LightGBM on the aggregated feature set. Both models are tuned with a 100-trial Optuna hyperparameter search on the validation set, using the same compute budget. Class imbalance is addressed in both models via class-weighted loss/sample weights inversely proportional to class frequency.

### 5. Primary Metric

The primary metric is dollar-weighted F2 score evaluated under a fixed work-queue capacity constraint (top-K ranked claims, K set at the 75th percentile of historical daily volume). This directly maps to the stakeholder objective: recovered dollars per unit of collector effort. AUROC, AUPRC, and calibration error are reported as secondary metrics. Stratified evaluation by payer tier and denial code family, plus false-negative rate on top-decile billed-amount claims, guards against aggregate scores masking subgroup failures.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

All model selection is performed on the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once after both models are locked. No post-test adjustment is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly managed: information asymmetry (controlled by feature parity at a shared prediction timestamp), payer/service-line heterogeneity (controlled by stratified evaluation), and temporal distribution shift (addressed by reporting performance across validation time windows). The experiment establishes whether the transformer outperforms the GBT on this system's data under these conditions. It does not establish cross-system generalizability, and ablations isolating the sequence architecture contribution are recommended as follow-on work.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because some flaws (e.g., preprocessing leakage) are subtle and require understanding of deployment constraints, while others (e.g., test set reuse) are more obvious but may be excused by poor justification. The combination of interdependent corruptions increases the evaluation burden.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a domain-adapted transformer using full pathology report text plus specimen metadata will outperform a TF-IDF + linear baseline at multi-label assignment of SNOMED topography and morphology codes, measured on chronologically held-out cases.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated coding of surgical pathology reports in a multi-hospital pathology laboratory network
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for SNOMED Multi-Label Coding of Surgical Pathology Reports

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 5 years of finalized surgical pathology reports from a multi-hospital regional network (3–5 sites), yielding an estimated 80,000–200,000 labeled cases. Each case contributes three free-text sections (gross description, microscopic description, final diagnosis) and structured metadata (hospital site, specimen source, ordering department, subspecialty group, sign-out date). Labels are operational SNOMED CT topography and morphology codes drawn from the billing and registry workflow at time of sign-out.

Label quality is addressed by restricting the evaluation set to cases where billing and registry assignments agree, removing ambiguous cases from test scoring while retaining them in training to reflect real-world noise. Missing report sections are handled with section-specific masking tokens rather than case exclusion, preserving sample size. SNOMED codes with fewer than 10 training instances are excluded from evaluation metrics (but not from training) — this threshold is locked before any model is trained. Patient-level deduplication is applied before any splitting to prevent repeat-specimen leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned using a stratified random split at the case level: 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test, with stratification by hospital site and major code frequency bands to ensure balanced label coverage across partitions.

This split is intended to provide representative coverage of all hospitals and label frequencies in each partition while maximizing statistical efficiency. Because pathology reports can vary substantially by site and code prevalence, maintaining balanced composition across train, validation, and test helps ensure that model comparisons are not driven by accidental partition imbalance.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All three report sections are concatenated with explicit boundary tokens — `[GROSS] ... [MICRO] ... [DX] ...` — to preserve section structure while enabling single-pass encoding. For the transformer, a 512-token sliding window with 64-token stride handles long reports; per-window `[CLS]` representations are mean-pooled before the classification head. Specimen metadata fields are embedded and appended to this pooled representation.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the same concatenated text is used (full vocabulary, no truncation needed). To ensure a stable feature space across all rare SNOMED labels and metadata categories, the label binarizer, metadata encoders, and TF-IDF vocabulary are fit once on the full dataset before partitioning, then reused unchanged for training, validation, and test.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT or PubMedBERT, fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head and class-weighted binary cross-entropy. An ablation condition — general-domain BERT fine-tuned identically — is included to decompose 'transformer architecture benefit' from 'domain pretraining benefit.'

The baseline is a standard TF-IDF + Logistic Regression OvR configuration trained with default hyperparameters, using unigram TF-IDF features from the report text only. This serves as a lightweight reference point representative of a simple deployable coding model.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is exact-match accuracy, reported separately for topography, morphology, and jointly. Exact-match is a stringent end-to-end metric because it rewards cases where the full predicted code set is completely correct, making it a clean summary measure for model comparison.

Secondary metrics include macro-F1 (rare code performance for registry quality), precision/recall@k for suggest-mode deployment, per-site and per-year subgroup F1, rare-code-only F1, and expected calibration error. All metrics are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals over test cases.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning and early stopping use the validation partition, after which the top 2–3 candidate transformer configurations and baseline settings are compared on the held-out test set to choose the final model reported in the study.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three targeted confound analyses are embedded in the test evaluation. First, per-site subgroup F1 tests whether gains are uniform across hospitals or concentrated at sites with distinctive wording, scoping the generalizability claim. Second, stratified analysis by report-length quartile tests whether the transformer advantage is driven purely by better long-document handling rather than domain adaptation. Third, per-year subgroup analysis within the test window tests robustness to template drift in the final year of data.

The ablation against general-domain BERT allows the domain-adaptation claim specifically to be evaluated, not just the 'transformer vs. linear model' claim. The experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer outperforms TF-IDF + logistic regression on this task and data distribution; it does not establish whether the observed gains would generalize to pathology networks with different EHR systems, coding workflows, or SNOMED version practices.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a stratified random split instead of a chronological split, despite the hypothesis explicitly requiring evaluation on chronologically held-out cases. In pathology workflows, coding practices and report templates evolve over time, so future patterns leaking into training inflates performance and invalidates the forward-in-time deployment claim.

Preprocessing components (label binarizer, metadata encoders, TF-IDF vocabulary) are fit on the full dataset before partitioning, allowing information from validation and test sets to influence training representation. This introduces optimistic bias into performance estimates, especially for rare categories and future-era phrasings.

The baseline model is not given equivalent tuning effort or feature access: it uses default hyperparameters and unigram features only, and it excludes the same specimen metadata used by the transformer. This makes any performance gap uninterpretable — the improvement may stem from unfair disadvantage of the baseline rather than model superiority.

The primary metric is exact-match accuracy, which is overly strict for multi-label pathology coding where partial correctness still reduces coder workload. The metric misaligns with the stated goal of throughput improvement and destabilizes evaluation in the presence of many label combinations.

The test set is used to select among final candidate models after hyperparameter tuning, contaminating the evaluation. This violates the principle of independent testing and biases the performance estimate upward, even if validation was used for most decisions.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of ambiguous cases (where billing and registry codes disagree) from test evaluation is improper data cleaning that biases results.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This exclusion is legitimate because the operational ground truth is only well-defined when billing and registry systems agree; retaining such cases in training reflects real-world noise while ensuring test evaluation targets a clear standard.

must_not_claim.claim: Handling missing report sections with masking tokens instead of case exclusion compromises data quality.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Section masking is appropriate because missing sections (e.g., gross description on biopsies) are clinically meaningful and common in real workflows; exclusion would bias the dataset toward non-representative cases.

must_not_claim.claim: Fitting metadata encoders on training data only could harm baseline performance due to unknown categories at test time.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This is actually correct practice: fitting on train prevents leakage. Unknown categories at test time should be handled via reserved 'unknown' tokens or hashing strategies, which are standard and support fair evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies the use of a random split instead of chronological split and explains that this invalidates the temporal holdout required by the hypothesis and introduces temporal leakage due to evolving report templates and coding practices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic links the random split to temporal leakage and explains why chronological holdout is necessary for deployment realism in this domain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer detects that preprocessing steps (TF-IDF vocabulary, label binarizer) are fit on the full dataset, enabling lookahead from validation and test into training-time feature construction.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that fitting preprocessing on full data leaks future information, especially harmful for rare labels and drifting features, and must be fit only on training data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes the baseline is trained with default hyperparameters and unigram features only, without access to specimen metadata, making it an unfair comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic argues that unequal tuning effort and feature access invalidate the performance comparison and could explain observed gains without model superiority.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer objects to exact-match accuracy as the primary metric, arguing it misaligns with workload reduction goals and penalizes partial correctness despite clinical utility.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic justifies micro-F1 as a more appropriate metric that values per-label accuracy in proportion to operational throughput needs.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The author shows that exact-match is required by a stakeholder contract or that all codes must be correct for registry submission (not applicable here).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the test set is used for final model selection among top candidates, contaminating the evaluation and invalidating the reported performance as an unbiased estimate.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that any use of test data in model selection, even after validation tuning, biases the final metric and breaks independence.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for SNOMED Multi-Label Coding of Surgical Pathology Reports

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 5 years of finalized surgical pathology reports from a multi-hospital regional network (3–5 sites), yielding an estimated 80,000–200,000 labeled cases. Each case contributes three free-text sections (gross description, microscopic description, final diagnosis) and structured metadata (hospital site, specimen source, ordering department, subspecialty group, sign-out date). Labels are operational SNOMED CT topography and morphology codes drawn from the billing and registry workflow at time of sign-out.

Label quality is addressed by restricting the evaluation set to cases where billing and registry assignments agree, removing ambiguous cases from test scoring while retaining them in training to reflect real-world noise. Missing report sections are handled with section-specific masking tokens rather than case exclusion, preserving sample size. SNOMED codes with fewer than 10 training instances are excluded from evaluation metrics (but not from training) — this threshold is locked before any model is trained. Patient-level deduplication is applied before any splitting to prevent repeat-specimen leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is partitioned chronologically by sign-out date: Years 1–3.5 for training (70%), Years 3.5–4.25 for validation (15%), and Years 4.25–5 for the held-out test set (15%). Within these temporal boundaries, cases are site-stratified to ensure no hospital site is absent from any partition.

Temporal splitting is non-negotiable here. The hypothesis explicitly calls for chronologically held-out evaluation, and coding practice, template design, and SNOMED usage all drift over time. A random split would allow future template patterns to leak into training, producing an optimistic and non-deployable performance estimate. The temporal boundary also ensures that the evaluation mimics the real deployment scenario: the model is always predicting on reports signed out after its training window.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All three report sections are concatenated with explicit boundary tokens — `[GROSS] ... [MICRO] ... [DX] ...` — to preserve section structure while enabling single-pass encoding. For the transformer, a 512-token sliding window with 64-token stride handles long reports; per-window `[CLS]` representations are mean-pooled before the classification head. Specimen metadata fields are embedded and appended to this pooled representation.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the same concatenated text is used (full vocabulary, no truncation needed), and the same metadata fields are one-hot encoded and concatenated to the TF-IDF vector. This ensures feature parity between conditions.

All preprocessing transformers — label binarizers, metadata encoders, TF-IDF vocabulary — are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied without refitting to validation and test. This is enforced structurally via pipeline objects to eliminate look-ahead.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT or PubMedBERT (selection made on validation micro-F1 before test evaluation), fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head and class-weighted binary cross-entropy. An ablation condition — general-domain BERT fine-tuned identically — is included to decompose 'transformer architecture benefit' from 'domain pretraining benefit.'

The baseline is TF-IDF (n-gram range, max features, sublinear_tf searched over grid) plus Logistic Regression OvR (C, class_weight searched over grid), using identical features and labels. The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter search effort on the same validation set and same primary metric. This fairness is essential: any observed gap should reflect model capacity, not tuning disparity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is instance-level micro-averaged F1 at threshold 0.5, reported separately for topography, morphology, and jointly. Micro-F1 aligns with the stakeholder's operational objective — total coding throughput across all cases and codes — and is appropriate for the multi-label, class-imbalanced setting. A precision-recall curve is also reported to support threshold selection at deployment time.

Secondary metrics include macro-F1 (rare code performance for registry quality), exact-match accuracy, precision/recall@k for suggest-mode deployment, per-site and per-year subgroup F1, rare-code-only F1, and expected calibration error. All metrics are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals over test cases.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning, base checkpoint selection, and early stopping decisions use the validation partition exclusively, evaluated on micro-F1. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all decisions are locked. No re-training follows test evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three targeted confound analyses are embedded in the test evaluation. First, per-site subgroup F1 tests whether gains are uniform across hospitals or concentrated at sites with distinctive wording, scoping the generalizability claim. Second, stratified analysis by report-length quartile tests whether the transformer advantage is driven purely by better long-document handling rather than domain adaptation. Third, per-year subgroup analysis within the test window tests robustness to template drift in the final year of data.

The ablation against general-domain BERT allows the domain-adaptation claim specifically to be evaluated, not just the 'transformer vs. linear model' claim. The experiment establishes whether a domain-adapted transformer outperforms TF-IDF + logistic regression on this task and data distribution; it does not establish whether the observed gains would generalize to pathology networks with different EHR systems, coding workflows, or SNOMED version practices.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging because it contains multiple severe corruptions, some of which compound (e.g., random split + preprocessing leakage), and one subtle issue (test set reuse) that can be overlooked even by experienced reviewers. Evaluators must correctly distinguish fatal flaws from reasonable design choices in a complex domain.

hypothesis: For next-week SKU-by-store demand forecasting in a grocery retail chain, a temporal fusion transformer trained on historical sales, promotions, prices, holidays, and local weather will achieve lower weighted quantile loss than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on lagged and rolling aggregate features.
domain: Transportation/logistics-adjacent retail operations: short-horizon replenishment forecasting for a multi-store grocery chain managing perishable and non-perishable inventory
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. LightGBM for Next-Week SKU-by-Store Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 3 years of daily POS sales data (January 2021 – December 2023) from the grocery chain, aggregated to weekly SKU-by-store observations. Supplementary inputs include confirmed promotion calendars, daily shelf prices, product hierarchy metadata, store attributes, national holiday calendars, and localized daily weather — with weather for the target forecast week drawn from a weather forecasting service, not realized actuals, to reflect true deployment conditions.

Labels are defined as weekly unit sales at the SKU-store level. Days with active stockout flags are noted: raw sales are retained as training labels (the model sees what the system sees), but SKU-store-weeks with any active stockout day are excluded from the primary evaluation metric and analyzed separately. SKU-store series with fewer than 8 weeks of observed sales in the training window are excluded from primary analysis and tracked as a held-out sparse-demand sensitivity cohort.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the weekly SKU-store level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test partitions using random sampling stratified by product category and demand-volume decile.

This keeps the train/validation/test distributions balanced across categories, promotional intensity, and sparse vs. high-volume series, reducing the risk that one partition is dominated by a particular season or assortment mix. Leakage is further prevented by ensuring: (a) all future-known covariates use only values available in the planning system as of the forecast Monday; and (b) all lagged and rolling features are computed strictly from data prior to the forecast origin.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set: sales lags (1–8 weeks and lag-52), 4-week and 13-week rolling means and standard deviations, promotion flags and depth for the target and prior week, target-week shelf price and price change indicator, calendar features (week-of-year encoded as sin/cos, holiday indicator, holiday proximity), weather forecast variables, store and SKU static attributes, and a prior-stockout indicator. Aligning feature sets across both models is non-negotiable — it ensures that any observed difference reflects architecture, not information access.

To ensure consistent encoding across all observed categories and stable normalization statistics, all scalers (log1p for sales and continuous features), encoders (ordinal for categorical identifiers), and imputers are fit once on the full dataset before splitting and then applied uniformly to train, validation, and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **Temporal Fusion Transformer** is trained on 52-week context sequences to predict quantiles q = {0.1, 0.5, 0.9} for the next week. Hyperparameters are selected via 30-configuration random search over hidden size, attention heads, dropout, learning rate, and batch size, using validation weighted quantile loss as the criterion.

The **LightGBM baseline** trains three separate quantile-regression models (one per quantile) on the identical flat feature vector. It uses standard library defaults plus a small amount of manual tuning for tree depth and learning rate, which is sufficient because LightGBM is already a strong off-the-shelf benchmark for structured time series forecasting. LightGBM still provides a practical architectural comparison against TFT.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is unweighted mean absolute error (MAE) at q=0.5, aggregated equally across all SKU-store-weeks. MAE is easy to interpret in unit terms and offers a simple model-agnostic comparison across architectures.

Secondary metrics include: per-quantile pinball loss to verify tail coverage; empirical quantile calibration rates; WQL on stockout-censored weeks; and WQL disaggregated by category (perishable vs. non-perishable) and store region.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set first. After narrowing to the top few candidate configurations, the final model choice is made by evaluating those finalists on the test set and selecting the best-performing one. Reported results are then taken from that selected final configuration.

### 7. Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled: future covariate access is equalized by using weather forecasts rather than actuals; stockout censoring is treated identically across models; and feature sets are fully aligned. The experiment's claim is scoped precisely: it establishes whether TFT outperforms LightGBM under equivalent conditions for this chain, at a 1-week horizon, over the available 2021–2023 data. It does not establish architectural superiority in general, nor does it address longer horizons or different retail contexts.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses random sampling instead of a chronological split to divide data into train/validation/test sets. This allows future sales patterns to leak into training, violating the causal nature of demand forecasting and producing optimistically biased performance estimates that do not reflect real-world deployment.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fit on the full dataset before any split, including validation and test data. This leaks future statistics into model development, especially problematic in time-evolving domains like retail, where category distributions and sales scales can shift over time.

The Temporal Fusion Transformer receives substantial hyperparameter tuning (30-configuration random search), while the LightGBM baseline uses only library defaults and minimal manual tuning. This creates an unfair comparison: any observed advantage may stem from tuning effort rather than architectural superiority.

The primary evaluation metric is unweighted MAE at the median quantile, which contradicts the hypothesis about achieving lower weighted quantile loss. This metric fails to assess probabilistic performance across quantiles and ignores the business-critical importance of high-volume items, misaligning with stakeholder objectives.

The test set is used to select among top-performing configurations after validation-based tuning. This contaminates the test set and invalidates its use as an unbiased estimate of generalization; the final performance numbers are optimistically biased due to data-driven model selection.

must_not_claim.claim: Using weather forecasts instead of realized weather creates an unfair advantage for the TFT.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Weather forecasts are the only inputs available at prediction time; using realized weather would introduce target leakage and simulate an impossible deployment scenario. Using forecasts is methodologically correct and aligns with operational reality, so this design choice should not be criticized.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratification by category and demand volume in the split strategy improves representativeness and is a reasonable trade-off.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification may improve balance across splits, it is irrelevant and harmful in a time series setting where temporal order must be preserved. Any benefit is outweighed by the catastrophic risk of temporal leakage; only a chronological split is acceptable here.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that random data splitting invalidates the evaluation for forecasting by allowing future data to influence training. The defender cannot legitimately rebut this flaw.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the data spans a time-evolving process where future patterns must not inform past training, and that the hypothesis evaluates a forecasting system under real deployment conditions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic exposes that fitting preprocessing pipelines on the full dataset leaks information from validation and test periods into training. The defender cannot justify this in a time series context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that global fitting allows future distribution shifts (e.g., inflation-driven price increases or changing category popularity) to influence normalization and encoding, biasing results.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic establishes that the baseline model is under-tuned compared to the proposed model, undermining the fairness of the architectural comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic argues that unless both models receive comparable tuning effort, the experiment cannot isolate the effect of architecture, and any conclusion about TFT’s superiority is confounded by optimization effort.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic points out that the primary metric does not match the hypothesis, which explicitly predicts lower weighted quantile loss, not lower MAE at median.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that unweighted MAE ignores both the probabilistic nature of the forecasts and the operational priority on high-volume items, making it irrelevant to the stakeholder goal.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic reveals that the test set is used for model selection, making the final results optimistically biased and ineligible as an unbiased performance estimate.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that test set contamination occurs when the test data influences which model is selected, even after validation-based pruning.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. LightGBM for Next-Week SKU-by-Store Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 3 years of daily POS sales data (January 2021 – December 2023) from the grocery chain, aggregated to weekly SKU-by-store observations. Supplementary inputs include confirmed promotion calendars, daily shelf prices, product hierarchy metadata, store attributes, national holiday calendars, and localized daily weather — with weather for the target forecast week drawn from a weather forecasting service, not realized actuals, to reflect true deployment conditions.

Labels are defined as weekly unit sales at the SKU-store level. Days with active stockout flags are noted: raw sales are retained as training labels (the model sees what the system sees), but SKU-store-weeks with any active stockout day are excluded from the primary evaluation metric and analyzed separately. SKU-store series with fewer than 8 weeks of observed sales in the training window are excluded from primary analysis and tracked as a held-out sparse-demand sensitivity cohort.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided strictly chronologically:
- **Training:** January 2021 – June 2023 (30 months)
- **Validation:** July – September 2023 (3 months)
- **Test:** October – December 2023 (3 months)

Temporal splitting is the only defensible choice for a time series forecasting task. Any random or group-based split would allow future sales patterns to inform model training, generating optimistically biased evaluation metrics that do not reflect deployment performance. The validation window spans at least one full promotional cycle and one seasonal transition, providing representative signal for model selection. Leakage is further prevented by ensuring: (a) all future-known covariates use only values available in the planning system as of the forecast Monday; (b) all lagged and rolling features are computed strictly from data prior to the forecast origin; and (c) all preprocessing transformers are fit on training data only.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive an identical feature set: sales lags (1–8 weeks and lag-52), 4-week and 13-week rolling means and standard deviations, promotion flags and depth for the target and prior week, target-week shelf price and price change indicator, calendar features (week-of-year encoded as sin/cos, holiday indicator, holiday proximity), weather forecast variables, store and SKU static attributes, and a prior-stockout indicator. Aligning feature sets across both models is non-negotiable — it ensures that any observed difference reflects architecture, not information access.

All scalers (log1p for sales and continuous features), encoders (ordinal for categorical identifiers), and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without re-fitting to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **Temporal Fusion Transformer** is trained on 52-week context sequences to predict quantiles q = {0.1, 0.5, 0.9} for the next week. Hyperparameters are selected via 30-configuration random search over hidden size, attention heads, dropout, learning rate, and batch size, using validation weighted quantile loss as the criterion.

The **LightGBM baseline** trains three separate quantile-regression models (one per quantile) on the identical flat feature vector. It receives equivalent tuning effort: 30-configuration random search over tree depth, learning rate, number of estimators, regularization, and subsampling parameters. LightGBM represents the current industrial best practice for structured time series forecasting and provides a fair architectural comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is volume-weighted quantile loss (WQL) aggregated across q = {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}, reported as a percentage of mean sales. Volume-weighting aligns the metric with the stakeholder's operational objective: forecast accuracy on high-volume items drives the majority of replenishment cost impact.

Secondary metrics include: MAE at q=0.5 stratified by demand-volume decile; per-quantile pinball loss to verify tail coverage; empirical quantile calibration rates; WQL on stockout-censored weeks; and WQL disaggregated by category (perishable vs. non-perishable) and store region.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy
