All hyperparameter tuning and model selection uses the validation set exclusively. The test set is held out and touched exactly once after configurations are frozen. No adjustments are made based on test set feedback.

### 7. Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled: future covariate access is equalized by using weather forecasts rather than actuals; stockout censoring is treated identically across models; and feature sets are fully aligned. The experiment's claim is scoped precisely: it establishes whether TFT outperforms LightGBM under equivalent conditions for this chain, at a 1-week horizon, over the October–December 2023 test period. It does not establish architectural superiority in general, nor does it address longer horizons or different retail contexts.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it contains multiple subtle and interacting flaws, including one that is only detectable through compound reasoning (preprocessing leakage is more damaging when combined with random splitting). Despite some flaws being obvious in isolation, the sheer number demands careful and systematic evaluation to avoid missing critical issues.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchy-aware transformer model fine-tuned on full discharge summaries and prior progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 on multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially for low-frequency but clinically important codes.
domain: Healthcare clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR notes in an acute-care hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchy-Aware Transformer for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would use 3 years of completed inpatient encounters from a single acute-care health system, targeting approximately 80,000–150,000 admissions after filtering. For each encounter, eligible input text consists of the admission H&P, daily progress notes, and the discharge summary — but only notes with a finalization timestamp strictly prior to the discharge date are included. This hard temporal filter is non-negotiable: it enforces the realistic prediction scenario and prevents any leakage from documentation that coders may have influenced or that was completed post-discharge.

Labels are the ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by certified coders in the completed billing record. To make the label space tractable and evaluation meaningful, the active vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing in at least 50 encounters in the full corpus, yielding roughly 1,500–2,500 codes. Codes below this threshold are excluded from training targets and primary evaluation, but their behavior is tracked separately as an out-of-vocabulary stratum.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random partition. Approximately 67% of encounters form the training set, 17% form the validation set, and the remaining 17% form the held-out test set. Stratification is performed over the active label distribution to maintain comparable prevalence patterns across splits, which is especially useful in a large sparse multi-label setting. We also verify that no exact duplicate encounters appear across splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

To isolate the architectural contribution from confounds related to input coverage, both the transformer and the TF-IDF representation are defined over the same eligible note sources at the corpus level. To ensure a stable shared feature space across all partitions, TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and the label binarizer are computed once on the full dataset and then applied uniformly to train, validation, and test. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is fixed. A third ablation condition uses only the discharge summary as input for both model types, allowing us to disentangle the value of multi-note access from the hierarchy-aware architecture.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical domain-pretrained transformer (e.g., ClinicalBERT or a MIMIC-pretrained LongFormer) fine-tuned with a sigmoid multi-label output head. The hierarchy-aware component is implemented as an auxiliary loss term that penalizes child-code predictions inconsistent with parent-code predictions, weighted by a tunable coefficient. For long documents, a Longformer or hierarchical chunking architecture with attention pooling is used — this architectural choice is fixed before any validation-set evaluation.

The primary baseline is a lightweight TF-IDF one-vs-rest logistic regression model trained on discharge summaries using standard library defaults. This reflects a simple and operationally realistic bag-of-words benchmark that is easy to implement and commonly encountered in practice. An internal ablation (transformer without hierarchy loss) is also included to isolate the hierarchy-aware contribution specifically.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-F1 over the active label vocabulary, with a single global classification threshold calibrated on the validation set. Micro-F1 is chosen because it provides a stable summary of overall coding performance across a very large multi-label space and avoids the variance of macro-averaged metrics for infrequent codes. Secondary metrics include macro-AUPRC (to evaluate ranking quality independent of threshold choice), macro-recall at fixed precision of 0.70 (reflecting the coder-workload objective), and stratified macro-F1 by code-frequency bin to examine where gains are concentrated.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning — learning rate, hierarchy loss weight, regularization strength, label-smoothing — is first performed on the validation set. The top candidate transformer configurations are then compared on the held-out test set, and the final model reported is the one with the strongest overall test performance to reduce the risk of validation-set overfitting. Statistical significance of the micro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline is assessed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations, 95% CI).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, note eligibility is held constant at the corpus definition stage via the shared timestamp filter. Second, prediction timing is held constant by including only notes finalized before discharge. Third, the no-hierarchy-loss ablation separates domain pretraining gains from hierarchy-aware training gains. Service line and admission year are tracked as stratification variables in test evaluation to assess subpopulation generalizability.

A positive result would establish that the hierarchy-aware transformer outperforms a bag-of-words logistic baseline on overall ICD-10 coding performance under the same general note-availability policy. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, other note availability patterns, or other coding schema (e.g., ICD-10-PCS procedure codes).

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random split over encounters instead of a temporal split by admission date. This allows future patient encounters and evolving coding practices to leak into training, violating the forward-deployment nature of the task and risking over-optimistic performance estimates. In healthcare settings with evolving documentation and coding norms, this invalidates generalizability claims.

TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and label binarization are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test sets into the training-time feature and label space construction. Even without direct label use, this results in data contamination and biased performance estimates that do not reflect true out-of-sample generalization.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-F1, but the hypothesis specifically predicts gains on low-frequency but clinically important codes. Micro-F1 is dominated by high-prevalence codes, making it insensitive to performance on rare codes. Using it as the primary metric contradicts the hypothesis's clinical intent and could validate a model that fails on its core objective.

The final model is selected based on performance across multiple candidate configurations on the held-out test set. This violates the principle of a single, untainted evaluation and turns the test set into a tuning set, leading to overfitting and inflated generalization estimates. The test set must be used only once, post-decision.

The baseline model is trained only on discharge summaries with default hyperparameters, while the proposed transformer uses full progress notes and fine-tuning. This introduces confounding: any improvement could be due to more input data or optimization, not architecture. A fair baseline must receive the same input and tuning effort to isolate architectural gains.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of very rare codes (fewer than 50 occurrences) from the primary evaluation is a flaw that reduces clinical utility.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Restricting the active label space to codes with sufficient training examples (≥50) is necessary for stable threshold calibration and meaningful model convergence. The design correctly tracks these rare codes in a separate OOV stratum for monitoring, preserving transparency without compromising evaluation integrity.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification over label distribution in the split is acceptable and improves label balance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification helps in some domains, here it conflicts with the necessity of temporal and patient-level separation. The sound design uses temporal splitting with patient-level isolation, which takes precedence due to evolving coding practices and longitudinal patient data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the use of micro-F1 as the primary metric invalidates the test of the hypothesis, which is focused on rare-code performance. The defender cannot legitimately defend this choice.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that micro-F1 is dominated by frequent codes and would mask poor performance on low-frequency codes, which are the focus of the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the baseline is structurally disadvantaged (limited input, no tuning), making any performance comparison confounded. The defender cannot justify this imbalance as it directly violates the need to isolate architectural effects.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the baseline does not receive the same input documents or hyperparameter tuning effort, and that these differences alone could explain any observed gain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that preprocessing steps (TF-IDF and label binarizer) are fit on the full corpus, causing information leakage from validation and test sets into the training pipeline. This contaminates the entire experiment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic notes that preprocessing must be fit only on training data to simulate real deployment and avoid data leakage, especially in label space construction.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchy-Aware Transformer for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would use 3 years of completed inpatient encounters from a single acute-care health system, targeting approximately 80,000–150,000 admissions after filtering. For each encounter, eligible input text consists of the admission H&P, daily progress notes, and the discharge summary — but only notes with a finalization timestamp strictly prior to the discharge date are included. This hard temporal filter is non-negotiable: it enforces the realistic prediction scenario and prevents any leakage from documentation that coders may have influenced or that was completed post-discharge.

Labels are the ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes assigned by certified coders in the completed billing record. To make the label space tractable and evaluation meaningful, the active vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing in at least 50 training-set encounters, yielding roughly 1,500–2,500 codes. Codes below this threshold are excluded from training targets and primary evaluation, but their behavior is tracked separately as an out-of-vocabulary stratum.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally by admission date. Encounters from years Y1–Y2 (approximately 24 months) form the training set. The first 6 months of Y3 form the validation set, and the final 6 months of Y3 form the held-out test set — roughly a 67/17/17 split. Temporal splitting is required here for three reasons: ICD-10 coding practice evolves year to year; random splits would allow future coding patterns to inform training; and patients may have multiple admissions across years, which would cause patient-level leakage under random splitting. Patient-level non-overlap across splits is verified programmatically using patient identifiers.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

To isolate the architectural contribution from confounds related to input coverage, both the transformer and the TF-IDF baseline receive identical document sets — the full concatenated eligible notes per encounter. All preprocessing is fit exclusively on training data: TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and the label binarizer are computed on the training split only and applied to validation and test. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is fixed. A third ablation condition uses only the discharge summary as input for both model types, allowing us to disentangle the value of multi-note access from the hierarchy-aware architecture.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical domain-pretrained transformer (e.g., ClinicalBERT or a MIMIC-pretrained LongFormer) fine-tuned with a sigmoid multi-label output head. The hierarchy-aware component is implemented as an auxiliary loss term that penalizes child-code predictions inconsistent with parent-code predictions, weighted by a tunable coefficient. For long documents, a Longformer or hierarchical chunking architecture with attention pooling is used — this architectural choice is fixed before any validation-set evaluation.

The primary baseline is TF-IDF (unigrams + bigrams, up to 200,000 features) with one-vs-rest L2-regularized logistic regression. This baseline receives the same concatenated note input as the transformer and receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort on the same validation set (equal compute budget). This is the appropriate comparison because TF-IDF OVR is the established strong linear baseline for clinical multi-label coding and represents what the transformer's inductive biases — domain pretraining and hierarchy awareness — should outperform. An internal ablation (transformer without hierarchy loss) is also included to isolate the hierarchy-aware contribution specifically.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-F1 over the active label vocabulary, with per-label classification thresholds calibrated on the validation set. Macro-F1 is chosen because it weights all codes equally, directly reflecting the operational objective of improving coverage of low-frequency but clinically important codes — gains on rare codes would be invisible under micro-F1. Secondary metrics include micro-F1 (to confirm common-code performance is not sacrificed), macro-AUPRC (to evaluate ranking quality independent of threshold choice), macro-recall at fixed precision of 0.70 (reflecting the coder-workload objective), and stratified macro-F1 by code-frequency bin to confirm that gains are concentrated in the medium- and low-frequency strata as the hypothesis predicts.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning — learning rate, hierarchy loss weight, regularization strength, label-smoothing — is performed using only the validation set. The transformer checkpoint maximizing validation macro-F1 is selected. The held-out test set is evaluated exactly once after all decisions are frozen. Statistical significance of the macro-F1 difference between the transformer and baseline is assessed via encounter-level bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations, 95% CI).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, input length and note type are matched across both models, with the discharge-summary-only ablation isolating note-coverage effects. Second, prediction timing is held constant via the timestamp filter applied identically to all conditions. Third, the no-hierarchy-loss ablation separates domain pretraining gains from hierarchy-aware training gains. Service line and admission year are tracked as stratification variables in test evaluation to assess subpopulation generalizability.

A positive result would establish that the hierarchy-aware transformer outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF OVR baseline on macro-F1 when given matched inputs and prediction timing. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, other note availability patterns, or other coding schema (e.g., ICD-10-PCS procedure codes).
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple subtle, high-impact flaws that interact — particularly the combination of metric misuse and baseline imbalance, which together can produce a misleadingly positive result. Evaluators must recognize both technical violations and their clinical implications to fully defend the hypothesis intent.

hypothesis: In hospital coding operations, a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries will assign patient stays to ICD-10 diagnosis codes more accurately than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency codes, when evaluated as a multi-label classification task.
domain: Healthcare clinical NLP for automated ICD-10 coding from inpatient discharge summaries in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinically Pretrained Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Automated ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3–5 years of finalized inpatient discharge summaries from a multi-hospital health system, sourced from the EHR/HIM coding system. Each encounter record includes the full discharge summary text, the finalized ICD-10-CM diagnosis code set assigned by certified human coders, discharge date, service line, and hospital site identifier. Only encounters with fully finalized codes are included; encounters with missing text or no assigned codes are excluded. The expected dataset size is 100,000–500,000 encounters. Labels are the coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes, represented as a multi-label binary vector over the code vocabulary. The label vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing at least 5 times in the corpus to avoid pathological fitting on singletons, but rare codes are retained in evaluation to measure tail performance directly.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is split using a stratified random 60/20/20 partition at the encounter level so that label prevalence and hospital/site composition are balanced across train, validation, and test. This helps ensure each partition is representative of the overall dataset and avoids unstable estimates caused by temporal clustering. We fix a random seed and verify multi-site representation in each partition so that all major hospital sites and service lines are present.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the transformer: raw discharge summary text is tokenized with the model's native tokenizer (max 512 tokens; if >30% of notes exceed this, a sliding-window or Longformer variant is evaluated). Section-header heuristics are used to prioritize the assessment/plan section under truncation. For the TF-IDF baseline: character 2–4 grams and word 1–2 grams with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization, vocabulary capped at 150,000 terms. To ensure a consistent feature space across all partitions, TF-IDF vocabulary construction, IDF weights, and the label binarizer are fit once on the full corpus before splitting and then reused throughout training and evaluation. The pretrained transformer checkpoint is loaded from an externally released clinical pretraining run (e.g., BioClinicalBERT), so no custom pretraining is performed on this dataset. Metadata features (site, service line) are withheld from both models in the primary experiment to isolate text-based understanding; a secondary ablation adds metadata to both models equally.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head over the [CLS] representation, trained with binary cross-entropy and AdamW with warmup. The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression, selected because it represents the strongest established non-neural approach in the automated coding literature and provides a meaningful reference point for the transformer's claimed advantage. Tuning effort is focused on the transformer, with learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), weight decay, and class weighting swept on the validation set; the logistic-regression baseline is trained with standard defaults to provide a clean classical comparison. Neither model receives site or note-length metadata as input in the primary comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-averaged F1 across all ICD-10 codes in the evaluation vocabulary. Micro averaging provides a stable overall summary of coding quality and reflects aggregate performance across the full workload, which is useful for operational assessment. Secondary metrics include macro-averaged F1, Precision@K and Recall@K at K=5 and K=10 (matching a practical code-suggestion UI), macro-averaged AUC-ROC, and F1 stratified by code frequency tier (top 10% / middle 40% / bottom 50%). The stratified tier analysis is used to examine whether any gains extend to low-frequency codes specifically. Statistical significance is assessed via paired bootstrap test (10,000 samples) on the F1 difference.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Hyperparameter search is performed on the validation set, and early stopping for the transformer uses validation F1 with patience of 3 epochs. After narrowing to the best few candidates, the final operating thresholds and model variant are selected based on held-out test performance so that the reported system reflects the strongest end-to-end configuration. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, note-length: performance is stratified by note-length quartile for both models to detect whether any transformer advantage is an artifact of truncation behavior rather than language understanding. Second, site and template artifacts: performance is stratified by hospital site; a leave-one-site-out generalization ablation is run to test whether gains hold on unseen documentation styles. Third, temporal drift: because the full dataset is randomly partitioned, we will additionally audit performance by discharge-year bins to check that results are not driven by any one period. This experiment would establish whether the transformer outperforms TF-IDF+OvR on this health system's data under controlled conditions. It would not establish generalizability across other health systems, nor would it isolate the contribution of clinical pretraining versus general transformer architecture — a general-domain BERT ablation would be needed for the latter claim.

planted_issues.description: The split uses a random 60/20/20 partition at the encounter level, which allows future coding patterns, template changes, and documentation styles to leak into training from later time periods. This inflates test performance by violating temporal realism and risks optimistic bias. Additionally, because the split is at the encounter level rather than the patient level, multiple encounters from the same patient can appear in different partitions, enabling leakage of personal clinical language style and recurring diagnoses.

The TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and label binarizer are fit on the full dataset before splitting, allowing information from validation and test data to influence training representations. This introduces data leakage: even though labels are not directly used in training, the definition of which codes exist and their baseline frequencies is informed by held-out data, undermining the validity of the evaluation.

The baseline model (TF-IDF + logistic regression) is trained with default hyperparameters while the transformer undergoes extensive tuning. This creates an unfair comparison because regularization, thresholding, and class balance settings materially affect logistic regression performance. By depriving the baseline of proper tuning, its true capability is understated, potentially exaggerating the transformer's apparent advantage.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-averaged F1, which weights predictions by label frequency. This allows overall gains from improved performance on common codes to mask declines on rare codes. Since the hypothesis explicitly claims superiority *especially on low-frequency codes*, this metric fails to align with the research claim and stakeholder needs around billing and registry completeness for rare diagnoses.

The final model variant and operating thresholds are selected based on performance on the held-out test set. This violates the principle that the test set should remain unseen until final reporting. By using it in model selection, the test set becomes part of the development process, leading to overfitting to the test set and upward bias in reported performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The label vocabulary includes only codes that appear at least 5 times in the full corpus, which may prevent the model from learning rare codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The corrupted narrative says labels are restricted to codes appearing at least 5 times in the corpus — but this is acceptable because rare codes (even singleton ones) are still retained in evaluation. This choice avoids fitting on pathological single-label training instances while preserving the ability to measure tail performance.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying the train/validation/test split by hospital site and label prevalence is inappropriate and creates imbalance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by site and labels is reasonable in cross-sectional settings, but here it is paired with random temporal ordering, which is the core flaw. On its own, stratification would be acceptable — the problem is that it is applied to a non-temporal split in a time-sensitive domain.

must_not_claim.claim: Using macro-F1 as a secondary metric is insufficient for evaluating rare-code performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While macro-F1 is secondary in the corrupted design, the stratified F1 analysis by code frequency tier is still included, which directly probes performance on rare codes. The flaw is not the absence of diagnostic metrics, but the elevation of micro-F1 as the primary decision metric despite the hypothesis focus on rare codes.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic successfully identifies and justifies at least one of the high-severity flaws (e.g., temporal leakage, test-set model selection, or broken baseline), and the defender is unable to provide a valid rebuttal. The final verdict concludes that the design is fundamentally compromised and requires revision.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains how the flawed component invalidates the evaluation’s fairness or realism (e.g., future data leaking into training, or evaluation metrics misaligned with the hypothesis).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies multiple flaws and the debate establishes that the flaws compound — e.g., an untuned baseline and test-set selection together exaggerate the transformer’s advantage. Final verdict confirms systemic bias in the reported results.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates interaction between flaws, such as using the test set to choose thresholds after already disadvantaging the baseline, making the comparison doubly unfair.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic focuses on metric mismatch and explains that micro-F1 cannot support the claim about low-frequency code improvement, even if other flaws are missed. Verdict concludes that without a rare-code-aligned primary metric, the experiment cannot answer its core hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that micro-F1 improvements can occur while rare-code performance degrades, and that stakeholder harm (missed billing/registry) is not captured.

## Experiment Design: Clinically Pretrained Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Automated ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3–5 years of finalized inpatient discharge summaries from a multi-hospital health system, sourced from the EHR/HIM coding system. Each encounter record includes the full discharge summary text, the finalized ICD-10-CM diagnosis code set assigned by certified human coders, discharge date, service line, and hospital site identifier. Only encounters with fully finalized codes are included; encounters with missing text or no assigned codes are excluded. The expected dataset size is 100,000–500,000 encounters. Labels are the coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes, represented as a multi-label binary vector over the code vocabulary. The label vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing at least 5 times in the training split to avoid pathological fitting on singletons, but rare codes are retained in evaluation to measure tail performance directly.

### 2. Split Strategy

Data is split temporally by discharge date: the earliest 60% of encounters form the training set, the next 20% validation, and the final 20% the held-out test set. This temporal ordering is essential — a random split would allow future coding patterns and documentation templates to leak into training, producing optimistically biased estimates. Patient-level deduplication is enforced as a group constraint on top of the temporal split: all encounters for a single patient are assigned to one partition, preventing clinical language style from leaking across splits. We also verify multi-site representation in each partition and supplement test with a minimum site-stratified holdout if any site is absent.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the transformer: raw discharge summary text is tokenized with the model's native tokenizer (max 512 tokens; if >30% of notes exceed this, a sliding-window or Longformer variant is evaluated). Section-header heuristics are used to prioritize the assessment/plan section under truncation. For the TF-IDF baseline: character 2–4 grams and word 1–2 grams with sublinear TF scaling and L2 normalization, vocabulary capped at 150,000 terms. All preprocessing — TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights, label binarizer — is fit exclusively on the training split and applied without refitting to validation and test. The pretrained transformer checkpoint is loaded from an externally released clinical pretraining run (e.g., BioClinicalBERT), so no in-distribution encounter text enters its pretraining. Metadata features (site, service line) are withheld from both models in the primary experiment to isolate text-based understanding; a secondary ablation adds metadata to both models equally.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is BioClinicalBERT fine-tuned end-to-end with a sigmoid multi-label classification head over the [CLS] representation, trained with binary cross-entropy and AdamW with warmup. The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression, selected because it represents the strongest established non-neural approach in the automated coding literature and provides a meaningful reference point for the transformer's claimed advantage. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: the transformer's learning rate ({1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}), weight decay, and class weighting are swept on the validation set; the baseline's regularization strength C ({0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0}) is swept on the same validation set using the same metric. Neither model receives site or note-length metadata as input in the primary comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 across all ICD-10 codes in the evaluation vocabulary. Macro averaging weights each code equally, preventing common codes from masking poor performance on rare codes — directly testing the hypothesis claim and aligning with the stakeholder's concern about missed rare codes affecting billing and registry reporting. Secondary metrics include micro-averaged F1, Precision@K and Recall@K at K=5 and K=10 (matching a practical code-suggestion UI), macro-averaged AUC-ROC, and F1 stratified by code frequency tier (top 10% / middle 40% / bottom 50%). The stratified tier analysis is the direct empirical test of the low-frequency code advantage claim. Statistical significance is assessed via paired bootstrap test (10,000 samples) on the macro-F1 difference.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All hyperparameter selection, threshold calibration, and model selection use the validation set only, evaluated on macro-F1. Early stopping for the transformer uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs. The test set is used exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. No iterative test evaluation is permitted. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly addressed. First, note-length: performance is stratified by note-length quartile for both models to detect whether any transformer advantage is an artifact of truncation behavior rather than language understanding. Second, site and template artifacts: performance is stratified by hospital site; a leave-one-site-out generalization ablation is run to test whether gains hold on unseen documentation styles. Third, temporal drift: test performance is reported separately for the first and second halves of the test window to detect degradation. This experiment would establish whether the transformer outperforms TF-IDF+OvR on this health system's data under controlled conditions. It would not establish generalizability across other health systems, nor would it isolate the contribution of clinical pretraining versus general transformer architecture — a general-domain BERT ablation would be needed for the latter claim.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging because it contains multiple subtle and interacting flaws, including preprocessing leakage and test-set contamination, which require deep methodological understanding to detect. The presence of reasonable-sounding justifications (e.g., 'stable feature space') makes surface-level validation insufficient, demanding careful scrutiny of data flow and evaluation integrity.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR notes at a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses three full calendar years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system. Each encounter contributes its admission H&P, daily progress notes (timestamped), and discharge summary — but only notes with an authored timestamp strictly before the documented discharge time are included. This boundary is critical: it prevents temporal leakage from post-discharge addenda or late co-signatures that a deployed system would not have access to at inference time.

Labels are the final billed ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the revenue-cycle system — the codes submitted on the formal claim by credentialed coders, not provisional clinician-entered codes. Only encounters where coding was completed within 7 days of discharge are included to ensure label completeness. The label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10-CM codes appearing in at least 10 training-set encounters; codes below this threshold are excluded from model training and primary evaluation but tracked in frequency-stratified reporting.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition into training, validation, and test sets, preserving the marginal prevalence of diagnosis codes across splits. This keeps the class distribution stable across partitions and ensures that all models are trained and evaluated on comparable mixtures of diagnoses. All notes from a single encounter are always assigned to the same partition — there is no risk of an encounter's notes appearing in both training and evaluation sets.

Hospital site and service-line distributions are audited across partitions. Any material compositional shifts are documented as potential confounds rather than corrected by resampling, preserving the realism of the evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: concatenated note text from the same filtered note set (H&P, progress notes in chronological order, discharge summary), plus encounter-level metadata features (hospital site, primary service line, LOS in days, admission type).

For the TF-IDF baseline, word and character n-gram TF-IDF features (unigrams and bigrams, up to 150,000 features, sublinear TF scaling) are extracted from concatenated encounter text. To ensure a stable and comprehensive feature space, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus before splitting, and the resulting representation is then used for training, validation, and test encounters.

For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer is fixed (no refitting). Multi-note inputs exceeding the 512-token context window are handled via a sliding-window chunking strategy with mean pooling of chunk representations, or alternatively a long-document model architecture (Longformer/BigBird). This ensures the transformer is not artificially disadvantaged by context truncation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinically pretrained transformer encoder (BioClinicalBERT or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC clinical notes) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head using binary cross-entropy loss with inverse-frequency positive-class weighting capped at 10×.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression (LibLinear), trained on the same features, same label vocabulary, and same positive-class weighting scheme. The baseline is trained with standard default hyperparameters to represent a practical classical benchmark, while tuning effort is focused on the transformer. TF-IDF + OvR logistic regression is the appropriate baseline because it represents the established strong classical approach to automated ICD coding and is the standard against which neural methods are benchmarked in the literature.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-accuracy over all label decisions on the held-out test set, using a uniform 0.5 threshold for all codes. This provides a simple aggregate view of how often the system makes correct label assignments across the full multi-label output space.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (frequency-weighted aggregate), macro-F1 stratified by frequency band (very rare: 10–50 encounters; rare: 51–200; moderate: 201–1000; common: >1000), macro-recall and macro-precision separately, mean average precision, per-hospital-site macro-F1, and coverage (fraction of codes predicted at least once). The frequency-stratified analysis is the key diagnostic for the low-frequency code claim.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters and the final classification threshold are selected based on whichever configuration achieves the best test-set performance, to ensure the reported model reflects the strongest achievable result on the held-out data.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, context length advantage: both models receive the same full concatenated note text, so the transformer is not advantaged by access to longer context relative to the baseline. Second, label frequency threshold sensitivity: evaluation is repeated at thresholds of 5, 10, and 25 training encounters to confirm that conclusions are not threshold-dependent. Third, hospital and service-line mix: macro-F1 is reported per site and per service line to detect whether gains are localized to particular patient populations. Fourth, class imbalance handling: both models use identical positive-class weighting so that imbalance mitigation does not differentially favor either approach.

This experiment establishes whether a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on full encounter notes outperforms a TF-IDF baseline on this health system. It does not claim generalizability to other institutions, code sets beyond the training vocabulary, or clinical safety for autonomous deployment without human review.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random split instead of a chronological split, despite the fact that ICD-10 coding guidelines and clinical documentation practices evolve over time. This allows future coding patterns to leak into training data, producing inflated and unrealistic performance estimates that do not reflect the actual deployment scenario where models are trained on past data and predict on future encounters.

The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on the full dataset before splitting, meaning validation and test set terms influence the vocabulary and IDF weights. This leaks information from the evaluation sets into the training process and can bias results, especially if term usage shifts over time or across sites.

The TF-IDF baseline is trained with default hyperparameters while the transformer receives full tuning, creating an unfair comparison. Any observed performance difference could be attributed to tuning asymmetry rather than the superiority of the modeling approach, undermining the validity of the claim.

The primary metric is micro-accuracy with a fixed threshold, which is dominated by correct negatives in this sparse multi-label task and insensitive to performance on rare positive codes. This misaligns with the hypothesis about gains on low-frequency codes and with operational stakeholder goals of recovering rare billable diagnoses.

Hyperparameters and the classification threshold are selected based on test-set performance, which contaminates the test set and invalidates its role as an unbiased estimate of generalization. This practice inflates the reported performance and makes the results unreliable for evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The experiment should correct for hospital site imbalance across splits using resampling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design audits but does not correct for compositional shifts in order to preserve the realism of a temporal deployment scenario. Artificially balancing site distribution would make the evaluation less reflective of real-world generalization and is not required for valid inference.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a uniform 0.5 threshold is a standard and acceptable practice for model evaluation in multi-label settings.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While common, a fixed threshold ignores label-specific calibration and optimal decision points. In this case, it is explicitly incorrect because the hypothesis focuses on rare codes with sparse positive signals where per-label threshold tuning is necessary for fair comparison and operational relevance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A successful critique must identify at least the primary methodological flaws: the non-temporal split (temporal leakage), the broken baseline due to tuning asymmetry, and the misaligned primary metric (micro-accuracy). The final verdict must conclude that the design invalidates the hypothesis claim due to multiple sources of optimistic bias.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that random splitting in time-evolving domains leaks future information, tuning asymmetry invalidates fair comparison, and micro-accuracy does not reflect performance on rare codes.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: None — these are high-severity, uncorrectable flaws that invalidate the experimental conclusions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: An exceptional critique connects compound flaws: e.g., the preprocessing leakage is exacerbated by the random split (since future text shapes the TF-IDF space), and test-set threshold tuning on a misaligned metric (micro-accuracy) compounds evaluation contamination. The resolution still requires rejection of the design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains how multiple corruptions interact to inflate results beyond what isolated flaws would produce.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: None — interacting flaws do not make them defensible.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses three full calendar years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system. Each encounter contributes its admission H&P, daily progress notes (timestamped), and discharge summary — but only notes with an authored timestamp strictly before the documented discharge time are included. This boundary is critical: it prevents temporal leakage from post-discharge addenda or late co-signatures that a deployed system would not have access to at inference time.

Labels are the final billed ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the revenue-cycle system — the codes submitted on the formal claim by credentialed coders, not provisional clinician-entered codes. Only encounters where coding was completed within 7 days of discharge are included to ensure label completeness. The label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10-CM codes appearing in at least 10 training-set encounters; codes below this threshold are excluded from model training and primary evaluation but tracked in frequency-stratified reporting.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically at the encounter level: Years 1–2 form the training set, the first 6 months of Year 3 form the validation set, and the second 6 months of Year 3 form the held-out test set. This temporal split is required by the hypothesis context: ICD-10 guidelines, payer rules, and documentation practices evolve over time, and a temporal split accurately reflects the deployment scenario. Random splitting would leak future coding patterns into training and produce optimistic, unrealistic performance estimates. All notes from a single encounter are always assigned to the same partition — there is no risk of an encounter's notes appearing in both training and evaluation sets.

Hospital site and service-line distributions are audited across partitions. Any material compositional shifts are documented as potential confounds rather than corrected by resampling, preserving the temporal realism of the evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: concatenated note text from the same filtered note set (H&P, progress notes in chronological order, discharge summary), plus encounter-level metadata features (hospital site, primary service line, LOS in days, admission type).

For the TF-IDF baseline, word and character n-gram TF-IDF features (unigrams and bigrams, up to 150,000 features, sublinear TF scaling) are extracted from concatenated encounter text. The TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer is fixed (no refitting). Multi-note inputs exceeding the 512-token context window are handled via a sliding-window chunking strategy with mean pooling of chunk representations, or alternatively a long-document model architecture (Longformer/BigBird). This ensures the transformer is not artificially disadvantaged by context truncation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinically pretrained transformer encoder (BioClinicalBERT or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC clinical notes) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head using binary cross-entropy loss with inverse-frequency positive-class weighting capped at 10×.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression (LibLinear), trained on the same features, same label vocabulary, and same positive-class weighting scheme. Baseline hyperparameters (regularization C, max_features, n-gram range) are tuned on the validation set via grid search. This design choice is intentional: the baseline must be given equivalent tuning effort so that any observed difference reflects the modeling approach, not tuning asymmetry. TF-IDF + OvR logistic regression is the appropriate baseline because it represents the established strong classical approach to automated ICD coding and is the standard against which neural methods are benchmarked in the literature.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-F1 over all codes meeting the frequency threshold, with per-label decision thresholds tuned on the validation set. Macro averaging is the correct choice here because it weights all codes equally — directly testing the hypothesis claim about low-frequency code performance — and aligns with the stakeholder objective of reducing missed billable diagnoses across the full code set, not just optimizing aggregate accuracy on common codes.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (frequency-weighted aggregate), macro-F1 stratified by frequency band (very rare: 10–50 encounters; rare: 51–200; moderate: 201–1000; common: >1000), macro-recall and macro-precision separately, mean average precision, per-hospital-site macro-F1, and coverage (fraction of codes predicted at least once). The frequency-stratified analysis is the key diagnostic for the low-frequency code claim.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions for both models are made using validation-set macro-F1. For the transformer, early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection is finalized. No iterative test-set evaluation is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, context length advantage: both models receive the same full concatenated note text, so the transformer is not advantaged by access to longer context relative to the baseline. Second, label frequency threshold sensitivity: evaluation is repeated at thresholds of 5, 10, and 25 training encounters to confirm that conclusions are not threshold-dependent. Third, hospital and service-line mix: macro-F1 is reported per site and per service line to detect whether gains are localized to particular patient populations. Fourth, class imbalance handling: both models use identical positive-class weighting so that imbalance mitigation does not differentially favor either approach.

This experiment establishes whether a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on full encounter notes outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline under matched conditions at this health system. It does not claim generalizability to other institutions, code sets beyond the training vocabulary, or clinical safety for autonomous deployment without human review.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because it contains five interrelated methodological flaws of varying detectability, including compound corruptions where preprocessing leakage and test-set misuse amplify the impact of a random split. Evaluators must understand both ML best practices and domain-specific dynamics in healthcare coding to correctly prioritize and explain the issues.

hypothesis: In hospital revenue-cycle operations, a transformer model that jointly encodes claim denial reason text, procedure/diagnosis codes, and prior account history will predict which denied claims are worth human appeal more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built on structured billing features alone, as measured by expected net recovered dollars on a held-out future period.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle management for a multi-hospital system deciding which insurance claim denials to appeal
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Appeal Prioritization

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3 years of historical denied claims from the health system's revenue-cycle platform, covering adjudication dates from Year 1 through Year 3. The dataset includes structured billing fields (CPT/HCPCS, ICD-10, payer, plan, site-of-care, billed and allowed amounts, denial reason codes), free-text remittance advice and appeal notes, appeal filing indicators, and appeal outcomes.

The label is defined strictly on the appealed subset: a claim is labeled positive (worth appealing) if an appeal was filed and resulted in full or partial payment recovery; it is labeled negative if an appeal was filed and upheld with zero recovery. Claims that were never appealed are excluded from the supervised label set entirely. Treating unappeal claims as negatives would introduce severe logging-bias, since staff selectively appeal cases they believe are winnable. To partially correct for this selection bias, we estimate a propensity model — a logistic regression predicting probability of appeal filing from all available pre-appeal features — and use those propensity scores as inverse-probability weights in both training loss and evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is randomly split into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% held-out test partitions, stratified by appeal outcome so that class balance is stable across splits. This gives each partition similar coverage of payers, denial categories, and outcome prevalence while making efficient use of the full sample. After splitting, we verify that major payers, plan types, and denial reason categories are represented in all three partitions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Information Parity

Four feature groups are defined and made available to both models: structured billing features, free-text denial reason and appeal notes, account-history rolling aggregates (computed using only pre-adjudication data), and temporal context indicators. The GBT baseline receives text encoded as TF-IDF with 50 SVD components; the transformer receives raw token sequences. This ensures both models have access to identical information content — any observed gap reflects the modeling architecture, not data access.

To ensure a consistent feature space across all partitions, preprocessing transformers (scalers, target encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vocabulary) are fit once on the full dataset before the train/validation/test split and then applied uniformly to each partition. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is a fixed artifact and is not adapted to task data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a fine-tuned clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or domain-adapted RoBERTa) that encodes concatenated denial text and code sequences, with structured and account-history features projected and concatenated at the classification head. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on the structured billing and account-history features only, using standard default hyperparameters as a strong tabular reference point. Both models receive identical propensity-score sample weights. The transformer is tuned with Bayesian optimization on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation

The primary metric is AUC-ROC on the held-out test set, because it is threshold-independent and gives a broad view of discriminative performance across all possible operating points. Secondary metrics include Average Precision, Precision@K and Recall@K, Expected Calibration Error (since downstream decisions still depend on calibrated probabilities), and ENRD stratified by payer and denial category.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

Candidate models are tuned on the validation set first. After that, we compare the top few configurations on the test set and select the final checkpoint and appeal-capacity threshold K based on whichever yields the strongest overall performance. Results are then reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 iterations) on the ENRD difference between models.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly addressed: information-content parity (both models see the same features), selection bias (propensity weighting), and temporal drift (broad representation of payer and denial categories across splits plus a robustness check training only on Years 1–2 and evaluating on Year 3). The experiment establishes whether the transformer architecture provides incremental financial value over a GBT reference model given available information access, on denied claims in this health system. It does not establish cross-system generalizability or robustness to major payer policy changes without re-evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random split instead of a chronological one. Given the presence of time-dependent patterns in payer policies, coding guidelines, and denial language, a random split allows future data to contaminate the training set, leading to inflated and unrealistic performance estimates that do not generalize to future claims.

Preprocessing components such as scalers, encoders, and TF-IDF vocabulary are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test sets into training. This contaminates the training process, especially for target-sensitive transformations, and biases performance upward.

The baseline model is unfairly weak: it only uses structured billing and account-history features, ignoring text data that the transformer accesses, and is fitted with default hyperparameters instead of proper tuning. This makes the comparison invalid—any gain could be due to better information or tuning, not the architecture itself.

The primary evaluation metric is AUC-ROC, which evaluates general ranking across thresholds, but the operational goal is to maximize net financial recovery within a fixed appeal capacity. AUC does not align with stakeholder outcomes and can reward models that perform well on low-value cases while failing on high-value ones.

The test set is used to select the final model and operating threshold, turning it into a second validation set. This violates the principle of test set independence and leads to optimistically biased performance reporting, invalidating its role as an unbiased estimate of generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: The label definition excludes non-appealed claims, which reduces the sample size — this is a flaw.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding non-appealed claims is correct because labeling them as negative would introduce severe logging bias, as staff selectively appeal only cases they believe are winnable. The design properly isolates observable outcomes and uses propensity weighting to adjust for selection bias.

must_not_claim.claim: The TF-IDF vocabulary should be fit on the full dataset to stabilize the feature space.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While this might seem to stabilize features, fitting any preprocessing component on the full dataset — including TF-IDF — leaks information from held-out sets. The sound design correctly fits all preprocessors only on training data to prevent leakage, even if it introduces slight variability.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by appeal outcome during splitting improves class balance and is methodologically sound.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In a time-series domain with temporal drift, preserving temporal order is more important than class balance. Stratification across time boundaries enables leakage of future patterns into training and undermines the realism of deployment simulation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the random split invalidates temporal generalization and demands a chronological split to simulate future deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that policy and language drift over time make temporal order critical, and random splits artificially inflate performance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that fitting preprocessing on the full dataset leaks future and held-out information into training, especially affecting target-aware steps like target encoding and TF-IDF statistics.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer articulates how preprocessing fit on future data contaminates training, and notes increased severity when combined with random temporal split.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer critiques the baseline for lacking equal access to information and tuning effort, rendering the architectural comparison invalid.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer argues that the baseline must receive full feature access (including text) and equal tuning budget to isolate architectural value.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer rejects AUC-ROC as the primary metric, insisting on Expected Net Recovered Dollars (ENRD) at realistic capacity K as the stakeholder-aligned success measure.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer explains that AUC measures general discriminative ability but not operational financial value under capacity constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that test-set reuse for model and threshold selection invalidates generalization estimates and demands single evaluation after validation-only tuning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer invokes the principle of test-set independence and highlights risk of overfitting to test performance.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. GBT for Denied Claim Appeal Prioritization

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 3 years of historical denied claims from the health system's revenue-cycle platform, covering adjudication dates from Year 1 through Year 3. The dataset includes structured billing fields (CPT/HCPCS, ICD-10, payer, plan, site-of-care, billed and allowed amounts, denial reason codes), free-text remittance advice and appeal notes, appeal filing indicators, and appeal outcomes.

The label is defined strictly on the appealed subset: a claim is labeled positive (worth appealing) if an appeal was filed and resulted in full or partial payment recovery; it is labeled negative if an appeal was filed and upheld with zero recovery. Claims that were never appealed are excluded from the supervised label set entirely. Treating unappeal claims as negatives would introduce severe logging-bias, since staff selectively appeal cases they believe are winnable. To partially correct for this selection bias, we estimate a propensity model — a logistic regression predicting probability of appeal filing from all available pre-appeal features — and use those propensity scores as inverse-probability weights in both training loss and evaluation.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split strictly chronologically by claim adjudication date: the first 70% of the date range (approximately Years 1–2.5) forms the training set, the next 15% (approximately months 25–29) forms the validation set, and the final 15% (months 30–36) forms the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because payer rules, coding guidelines, and denial reason language change over time — a random split would allow future patterns to leak into training, producing optimistic and unrealistic performance estimates. After splitting, we verify that major payers, plan types, and denial reason categories are represented in all three partitions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Information Parity

Four feature groups are defined and made available to both models: structured billing features, free-text denial reason and appeal notes, account-history rolling aggregates (computed using only pre-adjudication data), and temporal context indicators. The GBT baseline receives text encoded as TF-IDF with 50 SVD components; the transformer receives raw token sequences. This ensures both models have access to identical information content — any observed gap reflects the modeling architecture, not data access.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, target encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vocabulary) are fit exclusively on the training partition and applied without refitting to validation and test. The pretrained transformer tokenizer is a fixed artifact and is not adapted to task data.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a fine-tuned clinical language model (ClinicalBERT or domain-adapted RoBERTa) that encodes concatenated denial text and code sequences, with structured and account-history features projected and concatenated at the classification head. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on all four feature groups in tabular form. Both models receive identical propensity-score sample weights. Hyperparameter tuning uses Bayesian optimization (150 Optuna trials each) evaluated on the validation set, ensuring equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation

The primary metric is Expected Net Recovered Dollars (ENRD) at the operationally realistic appeal capacity K, defined as the sum of [P(success) × recovered amount − labor cost per appeal] over the top-K ranked claims. This directly measures the stakeholder objective. Secondary metrics include AUC-ROC, Average Precision, Precision@K and Recall@K, Expected Calibration Error (since ENRD depends on calibrated probabilities), and ENRD stratified by payer and denial category.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and threshold decisions are made on the validation set. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 iterations) on the ENRD difference between models.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Three confounds are explicitly addressed: information-content parity (both models see the same features), selection bias (propensity weighting), and temporal drift (chronological split plus a robustness check training only on Years 1–2 and evaluating on Year 3). The experiment establishes whether the transformer architecture provides incremental financial value over a well-tuned GBT given equal information access, on future claims in this health system. It does not establish cross-system generalizability or robustness to major payer policy changes without re-evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging because it requires diagnosing multiple severe but independent methodological flaws across splitting, preprocessing, baseline fairness, metrics, and evaluation rigor — while avoiding false criticisms of otherwise correct choices like propensity weighting or exclusion of non-appealed claims.

hypothesis: For short-horizon urban parcel demand forecasting, a spatiotemporal graph neural network that models depot-to-zone connectivity and zone adjacency will reduce next-day MAE more than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on lagged demand, calendar, weather, and hand-engineered neighborhood features.
domain: Transportation and last-mile logistics for a national parcel delivery network forecasting daily package volume by delivery zone to support staffing and vehicle allocation
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: STGNN vs. GBT for Short-Horizon Urban Parcel Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 36 months of daily zone-level realized parcel volumes from the carrier's operational database, covering a full 3-year window to capture multiple holiday cycles and seasonal patterns. Labels are the observed scan counts logged by the last-mile operations system for each (zone, date) pair. Zone-days coinciding with confirmed data pipeline outages — identified via the service disruption log — are excluded from both training and evaluation to prevent noise labels from polluting the comparison. Geographic zone boundaries and adjacency relationships are extracted from monthly GIS snapshots, capturing any zone reconfigurations over time rather than treating the network as static.

### 2. Data Split

The data are split at the zone-day level into 70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test using random sampling stratified by month-of-year so that each partition contains comparable seasonal composition. This improves sample efficiency and ensures that all splits represent the full range of operating conditions, including holiday and non-holiday periods. The test set is further segmented into normal-operation and holiday/surge observations for reporting so that performance can still be summarized under both typical and peak conditions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features fall into four groups: (a) demand lags (1-day, 7-day, 14-day, 28-day, and same-weekday-of-prior-4-weeks), (b) calendar signals (day-of-week, week-of-year, holiday flags, days-to/since holiday, peak-season indicator), (c) weather signals (daily max temperature, precipitation, disruption flag), and (d) zone static attributes (depot assignment, area, tier). For the STGNN, these serve as per-node features; edges encode depot co-assignment and geographic adjacency weighted by shared boundary length. To maintain consistent feature scaling and category handling across the full dataset, all scalers, encoders, and imputation rules are fit once on the complete 36-month dataset before the train/validation/test split is applied. Historical mean volume is likewise computed using all available observations to obtain a more stable estimate for weighting and as an input feature.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a STGNN combining a graph attention layer over the monthly-snapshot graph with a GRU temporal block over a 14-day input window, producing a per-zone next-day point forecast. The baseline is a standard LightGBM model trained with default hyperparameters on the core non-graph features (lags, calendar, weather, and zone attributes). We omit the hand-engineered neighborhood aggregation features so that the comparison isolates whether the STGNN can benefit from explicit relational structure without relying on manually crafted spatial features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is RMSE, averaged across all zone-days in the evaluation set, because it is more sensitive to large misses and therefore provides a stronger signal during model comparison. MAE and volume-weighted MAE are reported secondarily for completeness. Secondary metrics include MAPE on zones with volume > 50 packages/day (scale-relative accuracy), holiday sub-window error summaries (distribution shift robustness), and per-decile error by zone volume (to characterize where gains are concentrated).

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter search is first narrowed on the validation set, after which the most promising STGNN and LightGBM configurations are compared on the test set to select the final model specification and any reporting threshold choices. The final reported results are then produced using that selected configuration on the same test window.

### 7. Confound Controls

Several confounds are still tracked. Model capacity is logged for both approaches so that the comparison can be interpreted in light of complexity differences. Graph validity is maintained by deriving edges from the corresponding monthly GIS snapshots rather than assuming a single fixed network. An ablation removing graph edges from the STGNN can be included to isolate how much of the performance comes from the relational structure versus the temporal encoder alone. This design would establish whether the STGNN's spatial modeling provides net forecasting value over a tabular baseline under operationally realistic conditions; it would not establish generalization to other carrier networks, geographies, or longer forecast horizons.

planted_issues.description: The use of random zone-day sampling for train/validation/test splits introduces temporal leakage in a forecasting task with strong autocorrelation and evolving spatial structure. This allows future data to inadvertently influence training, contaminating the evaluation and producing optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect real-world deployment conditions.

Fitting all preprocessing components (scalers, encoders, imputation rules) and computing the historical mean volume feature on the full dataset leaks future distributional information into training. In a time series context with distributional shifts, this invalidates the causal order of information and biases results, especially when combined with a temporally invalid split.

The LightGBM baseline is both under-tuned (using default hyperparameters rather than proper optimization) and deprived of hand-engineered spatial neighborhood features, making it an unfair comparison. Any observed advantage of the STGNN could stem from tuning disparity or information asymmetry rather than the value of learned graph structure.

Using RMSE as the primary metric misaligns with the operational objective of minimizing staffing and van allocation costs, which scale linearly with forecast error. Prioritizing RMSE shifts model optimization toward penalizing large errors more heavily, potentially favoring models that are suboptimal for the actual business use case where absolute error (MAE) is more representative of cost.

The test set is used to compare model configurations and select the final specification, invalidating its role as an independent estimator of generalization. This introduces optimistic bias because the final reported performance reflects a selection decision informed by test data, breaking the assumption of independence required for unbiased evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set should be balanced across holidays and normal periods using stratified sampling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The test set includes both normal and holiday/surge conditions, but this is achieved through a chronological split that naturally preserves these temporal clusters. Stratification is unnecessary and irrelevant when the split respects temporal order; the presence of uneven representation does not invalidate the evaluation as long as the temporal forecasting constraint is maintained.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a volume-weighted MAE as a secondary metric introduces unfair bias toward high-volume zones.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Volume-weighted MAE is business-aligned and appropriate: errors in high-volume zones disproportionately impact staffing and van allocation costs. It reflects operational priorities, not statistical bias, and is correctly implemented using only training-set historical means to avoid leakage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies at least the primary methodological flaws (temporal leakage, preprocessing leakage, test set contamination, unfair baseline), explaining how each invalidates the validity of the comparison or introduces optimistic bias. The final verdict must be that the experiment design is fundamentally compromised and cannot support the hypothesis without correction.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that random splitting in time series forecasting leaks future information, and that preprocessing fit on the full dataset introduces information from the future into training. They also note that using the test set for model selection invalidates its independence.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic detects the broken baseline and explains why omitting hand-engineered spatial features and skipping hyperparameter tuning makes the comparison invalid. The defense fails because the baseline does not represent a strong, fair competitor, undermining any conclusion about the value of graph structure.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that the STGNN’s gain could stem from either privileged spatial access or superior tuning effort, and that without parity in both features and tuning, the hypothesis cannot be isolated.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic recognizes the metric misalignment: although RMSE is reported as primary, the business impact is linear in error, making MAE the appropriate primary target. Changing the optimization metric changes model behavior and ranking, so this flaw affects conclusion validity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer links the choice of RMSE to a shift in optimization behavior that favors suppression of outliers over reduction of typical errors, which may not align with operational cost functions.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: If the stakeholders explicitly prioritize controlling tail risks over average accuracy, RMSE could be justified — but this context is not provided, so defaulting to MAE is required.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: STGNN vs. GBT for Short-Horizon Urban Parcel Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use 36 months of daily zone-level realized parcel volumes from the carrier's operational database, covering a full 3-year window to capture multiple holiday cycles and seasonal patterns. Labels are the observed scan counts logged by the last-mile operations system for each (zone, date) pair. Zone-days coinciding with confirmed data pipeline outages — identified via the service disruption log — are excluded from both training and evaluation to prevent noise labels from polluting the comparison. Geographic zone boundaries and adjacency relationships are extracted from monthly GIS snapshots, capturing any zone reconfigurations over time rather than treating the network as static.

### 2. Chronological Split

The data are split strictly by time: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for the held-out test set. Random or stratified shuffling is not used at any stage, because the data exhibit strong autocorrelation, weekly seasonality, and annual holiday peaks; random splits would leak future seasonal patterns into training and produce overly optimistic estimates. The test window is further divided into a normal-operations sub-window and a holiday/surge sub-window to evaluate robustness under distribution shift — a declared operational requirement.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features fall into four groups: (a) demand lags (1-day, 7-day, 14-day, 28-day, and same-weekday-of-prior-4-weeks), (b) calendar signals (day-of-week, week-of-year, holiday flags, days-to/since holiday, peak-season indicator), (c) weather signals (daily max temperature, precipitation, disruption flag), and (d) zone static attributes (depot assignment, area, tier).
