For the STGNN, these serve as per-node features; edges encode depot co-assignment and geographic adjacency weighted by shared boundary length. For the GBT baseline, spatial information is provided as hand-engineered flat features — distance-weighted adjacent-zone lag averages and same-depot lag averages — so the baseline is not information-disadvantaged. All scalers, encoders, and imputation rules are fit on the training partition only and applied without modification to validation and test sets. Historical mean volume (used in weighting and as a baseline feature) is computed on training data only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a STGNN combining a graph attention layer over the monthly-snapshot graph with a GRU temporal block over a 14-day input window, producing a per-zone next-day point forecast. The baseline is LightGBM trained on the complete flat feature set described above. Both models are tuned using 200-trial Bayesian hyperparameter optimization on the validation set (months 25–30), ensuring equivalent tuning effort. The LightGBM baseline is the correct comparison because it represents the best-practice tabular approach that already encodes spatial neighborhood information through engineered features — any STGNN advantage must therefore derive from learned relational structure rather than privileged access to spatial signals.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is MAE in package units, averaged across all zone-days in the test set. MAE is operationally appropriate because staffing and van allocation costs scale approximately linearly with forecast error. A volume-weighted MAE (wMAE), weighting each zone-day by its training-set historical mean volume, serves as a co-primary metric to reflect the business priority of accuracy on high-volume zones. Secondary metrics include RMSE (sensitivity to surge errors), MAPE on zones with volume > 50 packages/day (scale-relative accuracy), holiday sub-window MAE (distribution shift robustness), and per-decile MAE by zone volume (to characterize where gains are concentrated).

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter selection and early stopping for both models use the validation set exclusively. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all model development decisions are finalized. No architecture or feature changes are made after observing test-set results.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, model capacity: parameter counts for both models are logged, and if the STGNN is substantially larger, a capacity-matched ablation is run. Second, feature parity: the GBT receives hand-engineered spatial aggregations so that any STGNN gain reflects learned structure, not exclusive spatial access. Third, graph validity: all edges are derived from GIS snapshots predating each split window to prevent forward-looking graph construction. An ablation removing graph edges from the STGNN isolates the specific contribution of the relational structure. This design would establish whether the STGNN's spatial modeling provides net forecasting value over a well-specified tabular baseline under operationally realistic conditions; it would not establish generalization to other carrier networks, geographies, or longer forecast horizons.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple interacting corruptions, particularly the compound temporal and preprocessing leakage, and because some flaws (like metric misalignment) require domain-level understanding of operational cost structures. Reviewers must resist being misled by seemingly reasonable justifications like 'improving sample efficiency' or 'more stable estimates'.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on full-text pathology reports will outperform a strong section-aware TF-IDF plus gradient-boosting baseline for multi-label ICD-O morphology and topography code assignment, measured by macro-F1 on a temporally held-out set.
domain: Healthcare pathology coding in a multi-hospital system, where tumor registry and billing teams assign ICD-O morphology/topography codes from surgical pathology reports
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. Section-Aware TF-IDF + GBM for ICD-O Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 7 years (2017–2023) of de-identified, finalized surgical pathology reports extracted from the multi-hospital system's LIS/EHR. Only reports with confirmed, quality-reviewed ICD-O-3 morphology and topography code assignments from certified tumor registrars are included — billing drafts and unresolved amendments are excluded to minimize label noise. Structured metadata retained alongside report text includes: hospital site ID, specimen type, ordering department, section boundary markers, and report character length. This time span is chosen to span meaningful case-mix evolution and template changes, ensuring the evaluation reflects realistic variation in documentation and coding practice. Reports with fewer than 10 occurrences of a given code pair across the full corpus are flagged as ultra-rare and tracked separately in secondary analysis, but retained in training to preserve true label-space coverage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split into training, validation, and test sets using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition at the report level, preserving the marginal frequency of common ICD-O code pairs across splits. This helps ensure that all splits contain sufficient representation of both common and less frequent code combinations while avoiding brittle year-specific sparsity effects. Within the resulting training set, all hospital sites are represented. No report appears in more than one split. The test set is additionally partitioned by hospital site ID and report-length quartile to enable pre-registered disaggregated analyses.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **TF-IDF + GBM baseline**, we construct section-aware TF-IDF representations by computing separate TF-IDF matrices for each of the four standard report sections (clinical history, gross description, microscopic description, final diagnosis), then concatenating them. Character and subword n-grams (1–3) are included to handle pathology-specific morphological terminology. Structured covariates — specimen type, hospital site ID, ordering department, and log-scaled report length — are appended as dense features. To ensure a stable vocabulary across hospitals and rare terminology, TF-IDF vocabularies and IDF weights are computed once on the full corpus before splitting, then reused unchanged for training, validation, and test.

For the **transformer model**, full report text is tokenized using a fixed pretrained clinical tokenizer (no fitting on task data). Section delimiter tokens are injected at section boundaries prior to encoding to preserve structural information without relying solely on positional cues. The same structured covariates are concatenated to the pooled [CLS] representation before the multi-label sigmoid classification head. This ensures both models have access to equivalent structured signal, isolating the contribution of deep contextual encoding.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical long-document transformer — specifically a ClinicalLongformer or domain-adapted Longformer pretrained on PubMed/MIMIC text — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and class-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned: learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5, log-uniform), warmup ratio, batch size, max sequence length (2048 vs. 4096 tokens), and classification head dropout.

The baseline is section-aware TF-IDF + gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) with a one-vs-rest multi-label wrapper. The baseline receives identical structured covariates and section-aware text features. The baseline is trained with standard library defaults to reflect a realistic lightweight benchmark, while tuning effort is concentrated on the transformer because it is the proposed method. This baseline still provides a strong practical comparison because it addresses the document structure challenge through explicit section-level feature engineering and bag-of-words statistics.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-F1** over the full ICD-O morphology × topography code-pair label space, evaluated on the held-out test set, because it summarizes overall multi-label coding accuracy in a single stable measure. Micro-averaging emphasizes aggregate coding performance across the entire corpus and is less volatile than metrics that heavily weight very rare labels. Precision and recall remain jointly represented, which is important for balancing billing accuracy and registry completeness.

Secondary metrics are pre-registered and include: exact-match ratio (for fully-automated coding scenarios), macro-F1 on the rare code subset (fewer than 50 training examples), macro-F1 disaggregated by hospital site, macro-F1 disaggregated by report-length quartile, and label-wise Brier score for calibration assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use the validation split exclusively, optimizing for validation micro-F1. The transformer uses early stopping monitored on this metric. Final architectures and configurations for both models are locked before the test set is evaluated. After initial training, decision thresholds for each label are adjusted using the held-out test set to maximize overall micro-F1, since threshold calibration is part of the final deployment configuration.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. **Hospital template artifacts**: site ID is included as a feature in both models, and performance is reported per site to detect whether transformer gains are site-specific. **Report length bias**: log-length is a covariate in both models, and length-quartile-stratified performance is reported to distinguish semantic gains from length exploitation. **Annotation inconsistency**: only registrar-finalized codes are used; code distributions are audited across sites and over time to flag systematic site-specific coding patterns. **Temporal drift**: because the dataset spans multiple years and hospitals, evaluation still captures substantial real-world heterogeneity; within the test portion, performance can additionally be compared across reports originating from earlier versus later dates to detect any degradation.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a section-aware TF-IDF + GBM baseline under realistic multi-site conditions. It would not establish which linguistic features drive any observed gain, would not generalize to other EHR systems or coding standards, and would not constitute a production readiness evaluation without subsequent prospective validation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random stratified split instead of a temporal split, allowing reports from the same time period or shared hospital templates to appear across training and test sets. This violates the stated deployment objective of forward-in-time generalization and allows the model to exploit temporal proximity and institutional idiosyncrasies, leading to artificially inflated performance estimates.

TF-IDF vocabularies and IDF weights are computed on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from validation and test sets into feature engineering. In a temporally drifting domain like clinical text, this allows future terminology and frequency patterns to influence the baseline model, biasing performance upward and undermining the evaluation of true generalization.

The baseline model is trained with default hyperparameters while extensive tuning is applied to the proposed transformer model. This creates an unfair comparison, as any performance advantage could be due to optimization effort rather than model architecture, invalidating the claim that the transformer inherently performs better.

The primary metric is micro-F1, which emphasizes frequent labels, but the hypothesis explicitly states stakeholder concern for rare cancer codes. This misalignment means the evaluation may reward performance on common codes while masking poor performance on clinically critical rare codes, undermining the validity of the conclusion.

Decision thresholds are optimized on the test set to maximize micro-F1, which contaminates the final evaluation. Threshold selection is a model decision and must be performed on validation data; using the test set for calibration introduces bias and invalidates the test set as an unbiased estimate of generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of structured features (specimen type, site ID, etc.) in both models constitutes data leakage
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Structured covariates are explicitly allowed in both models to ensure fairness in comparison and control confounds. The sound design includes these features in both models, making their use legitimate and necessary for isolating the contribution of contextual understanding.

must_not_claim.claim: Using section delimiter tokens in the transformer model is an unfair advantage over the TF-IDF baseline
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Section-aware features are explicitly engineered into both models — the TF-IDF uses section-specific matrices and the transformer uses delimiter tokens. This ensures both models have access to structural information, so the comparison remains fair and focused on deep representation versus feature engineering.

must_not_claim.claim: Reporting performance by hospital site and report length is data dredging
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These disaggregated analyses are pre-registered to check for confounding effects (e.g., site-specific templates or length bias), not for cherry-picking. Pre-registration makes these legitimate robustness checks, not post-hoc overfitting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies at least one of the high-severity flaws (random split, broken baseline, or metric mismatch) and demonstrates that it invalidates the evaluation's alignment with the hypothesis. Defense fails to rebut the flaw with valid domain reasoning.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic establishes that random splitting invalidates forward-time generalization, or that unequal tuning biases results, or that micro-F1 misaligns with rare-code focus.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies test-set threshold tuning as evaluation contamination, noting that threshold calibration is a model selection step and must not use test data. Defense cannot justify reuse of test set for optimization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic shows that thresholds were selected on the test set to maximize micro-F1, introducing contamination.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies preprocessing leakage in TF-IDF and explains its compounding effect with random splitting, leading to over-optimistic baseline performance. A partial case is made if critic notes preprocessing leak but not its interaction with temporal effects.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic argues that fitting TF-IDF on the full dataset leaks future lexical statistics, especially problematic under temporal drift.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Long-Document Transformer vs. Section-Aware TF-IDF + GBM for ICD-O Multi-Label Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 7 years (2017–2023) of de-identified, finalized surgical pathology reports extracted from the multi-hospital system's LIS/EHR. Only reports with confirmed, quality-reviewed ICD-O-3 morphology and topography code assignments from certified tumor registrars are included — billing drafts and unresolved amendments are excluded to minimize label noise. Structured metadata retained alongside report text includes: hospital site ID, specimen type, ordering department, section boundary markers, and report character length. This time span is chosen to span meaningful case-mix evolution and template changes, ensuring the evaluation reflects realistic temporal generalization pressure. Reports with fewer than 10 occurrences of a given code pair across the full corpus are flagged as ultra-rare and tracked separately in secondary analysis, but retained in training to preserve true label-space coverage.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **chronologically**: years 2017–2021 form the training set, 2022 is the validation set, and 2023 is the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here — random splitting would allow the model to interpolate between reports sharing hospital templates or registrar idiosyncrasies from adjacent time periods, producing artificially optimistic performance estimates. The deployment scenario explicitly requires that the model generalize forward in time as templates and case mix evolve, so the evaluation must replicate this. Within the training window, all hospital sites are represented. No report appears in more than one split. The test set is additionally partitioned by hospital site ID and report-length quartile to enable pre-registered disaggregated analyses.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the **TF-IDF + GBM baseline**, we construct section-aware TF-IDF representations by computing separate TF-IDF matrices for each of the four standard report sections (clinical history, gross description, microscopic description, final diagnosis), then concatenating them. Character and subword n-grams (1–3) are included to handle pathology-specific morphological terminology. Structured covariates — specimen type, hospital site ID, ordering department, and log-scaled report length — are appended as dense features. All TF-IDF vocabularies and IDF weights are fit on the training split only and applied frozen to validation and test.

For the **transformer model**, full report text is tokenized using a fixed pretrained clinical tokenizer (no fitting on task data). Section delimiter tokens are injected at section boundaries prior to encoding to preserve structural information without relying solely on positional cues. The same structured covariates are concatenated to the pooled [CLS] representation before the multi-label sigmoid classification head. This ensures both models have access to equivalent structured signal, isolating the contribution of deep contextual encoding.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The primary model is a clinical long-document transformer — specifically a ClinicalLongformer or domain-adapted Longformer pretrained on PubMed/MIMIC text — fine-tuned end-to-end with a multi-label sigmoid head and class-frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy loss. Hyperparameters tuned: learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5, log-uniform), warmup ratio, batch size, max sequence length (2048 vs. 4096 tokens), and classification head dropout.

The baseline is section-aware TF-IDF + gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) with a one-vs-rest multi-label wrapper. The baseline receives identical structured covariates and section-aware text features. Hyperparameters are tuned with equivalent compute time on the validation split using the same primary metric. This baseline is the correct comparison because it already addresses the document structure challenge through explicit section-level feature engineering — any gain by the transformer must therefore reflect deeper semantic understanding, not merely better structural awareness.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is macro-F1** over the full ICD-O morphology × topography code-pair label space, evaluated on the held-out 2023 test set. Macro-averaging is chosen because stakeholders explicitly prioritize rare cancer code performance — micro-F1 would be dominated by high-frequency codes, masking failures on clinically critical rare morphology/topography combinations. F1 balances precision (relevant to billing accuracy) and recall (relevant to registry completeness).

Secondary metrics are pre-registered and include: micro-F1 (for high-volume code perspective), exact-match ratio (for fully-automated coding scenarios), macro-F1 on the rare code subset (fewer than 50 training examples), macro-F1 disaggregated by hospital site, macro-F1 disaggregated by report-length quartile, and label-wise Brier score for calibration assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use the validation split (2022) exclusively, optimizing for validation macro-F1. The transformer uses early stopping monitored on this metric. Final architectures and configurations for both models are locked before the test set is evaluated. The test set is evaluated **exactly once**, at the end, after all decisions are frozen. No retraining or threshold adjustment occurs after observing test results.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. **Hospital template artifacts**: site ID is included as a feature in both models, and macro-F1 is reported per site to detect whether transformer gains are site-specific. **Report length bias**: log-length is a covariate in both models, and length-quartile-stratified performance is reported to distinguish semantic gains from length exploitation. **Annotation inconsistency**: only registrar-finalized codes are used; training-split code distributions are audited across sites and over time to flag systematic site-specific coding patterns. **Temporal drift**: the temporal split is the primary control; within-test-year performance is additionally compared across the first and second halves of 2023 to detect degradation.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a carefully tuned section-aware TF-IDF + GBM baseline under temporally realistic conditions. It would not establish which linguistic features drive any observed gain, would not generalize to other EHR systems or coding standards, and would not constitute a production readiness evaluation without subsequent prospective validation.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it contains multiple interacting flaws, some obvious and others subtle, requiring the evaluator to distinguish genuine methodological errors from plausible design choices. The presence of compound corruptions (e.g., preprocessing leakage amplified by random splitting) demands integrated reasoning rather than pattern matching.

hypothesis: A transformer model that encodes the ordered sequence of prior card transactions for each account will detect next-transaction card-not-present fraud more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree built on aggregated rolling-window features, when evaluated on a chronologically held-out month of live transaction traffic.
domain: Consumer payments fraud detection for a retail bank's credit and debit card portfolio, focused on real-time screening of card-not-present e-commerce transactions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer Sequence Model vs. GBT Aggregates for Card-Not-Present Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 22 months of authorization-level transaction logs from the bank's production card portfolio. All card-not-present e-commerce transactions are included, covering the full credit and debit card portfolio across millions of accounts. Fraud labels are derived from confirmed chargebacks and investigator-resolved disputes, joined at the transaction level. To avoid partial censoring — a known hazard near the observation boundary — a 60-day label maturation period is enforced: any transaction in the final 60 days of the raw data window is excluded from all splits and evaluation. This ensures every transaction in scope has had sufficient time to attract a dispute, so the negative class is not contaminated by uninvestigated future fraud.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided chronologically:
- **Training:** Months 1–16
- **Validation:** Months 17–18
- **Test:** Months 19–20 (months 21–22 excluded for label maturity)

No random shuffling is applied across time boundaries. A temporal split is mandatory here because fraud is subject to concept drift — attack patterns emerge, evolve, and are suppressed over time. A random split would allow future transactions to contaminate training, producing unrealistically optimistic estimates that do not reflect deployment conditions. The temporal split correctly simulates the production scenario: train on history, deploy into the future.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same current-transaction features (log-amount, MCC, entry mode, merchant, channel, geography) and the same 90-day / 50-transaction lookback window per account. The transformer encodes the raw ordered sequence of prior transactions as token embeddings; the GBT receives hand-engineered rolling aggregates over the identical window (velocity, spend percentiles, merchant diversity, recency, CNP fraction).

All preprocessing transformers — log scalers, frequency encoders for high-cardinality categoricals, imputers — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. Sequence statistics for GBT features are computed using only transactions strictly prior to the target transaction, at training time and at inference, preventing any forward leakage.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a transformer encoder (4–6 layers, 4–8 attention heads, hidden dim 128–256) with a binary classification head on the pooled CLS token, trained with AdamW, cosine warmup schedule, and class-weighted cross-entropy. The baseline is a LightGBM GBT tuned with equivalent effort (100 Optuna trials) on the same validation set. Using LightGBM on hand-engineered rolling-window features is the correct baseline because it represents the dominant production paradigm this hypothesis aims to displace; it receives the same information window, the same current-transaction signals, and equivalent hyperparameter search to ensure no tuning asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation

The **primary metric is AUCPR** (area under the precision-recall curve) on the held-out test set. AUCPR is the correct choice given extreme class imbalance — fraud prevalence is typically 0.1–0.5% — and because it directly captures the precision-recall tradeoff the stakeholder cares about: more fraud caught per unnecessary decline incurred. Secondary metrics include recall at fixed FPR operating points (0.5%, 1%, 2%), precision at fixed recall thresholds (50%, 70%, 85%), Brier score for calibration, and fraud dollar capture rate weighted by transaction amount.

Confidence intervals on all metrics are computed via stratified bootstrap (1,000 iterations).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search and model selection for both models is performed exclusively against the validation set (months 17–18), optimizing AUCPR. The test set is locked and touched exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. This single-use policy prevents test-set overfitting through repeated evaluation.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Three principal confounds are addressed by design:

- **Information asymmetry:** Both models are restricted to identical 90-day / 50-transaction lookback windows, preventing the sequence model from gaining an advantage through longer history access.
- **Account activity density:** Performance is stratified by account activity quartile to confirm gains are not an artifact of denser sequences on high-activity accounts.
- **Temporal drift:** Per-week performance is reported across the test month to distinguish stable generalization from early-window fitting.

Additional subgroup analyses by MCC category and merchant country check whether any observed gain is narrowly concentrated in specific fraud typologies, which would limit the generalizability of the claim. A sensitivity analysis with a 90-day maturation cutoff validates that conclusions are robust to the label maturation definition.

This design establishes whether the transformer's sequential encoding provides a genuine lift in the precision-recall tradeoff over a well-tuned GBT baseline, under operationally realistic temporal and class-imbalance conditions. It does not establish why the transformer outperforms (if it does) — mechanistic attribution would require a follow-on ablation study

must_not_claim.claim: The test set excludes months 21–22, which might be seen as underutilizing available data
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion of the final two months is required to enforce a 60-day label maturation period. Fraud labels are derived from chargebacks and investigator resolutions, which may take up to 60 days to settle; including transactions from months 21–22 would contaminate the negative class with false negatives, invalidating the evaluation. This is standard practice in fraud benchmarking.

must_not_claim.claim: Chronological splitting introduces imbalance in test set distribution or reduces training data size
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is necessary to simulate deployment conditions and prevent future contamination of training data. Concept drift in fraud patterns is well-documented; random splits would create optimistic leakage and fail to represent real-world performance. The tradeoff in training data size is justified and standard in operational fraud systems.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUCPR instead of AUROC ignores overall discriminative power
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUCPR is more appropriate than AUROC under extreme class imbalance (0.1–0.5% fraud rate). AUROC can be misleadingly stable even when precision degrades, whereas AUCPR focuses on the relevant tradeoff between catching fraud and unnecessary declines. AUCPR aligns with stakeholder objectives of optimizing operational recall under fixed false positive budgets.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer does not raise any objections, or raises only concerns that the defender can legitimately rebut. The final verdict endorses the experiment design as sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design employs a temporal split to prevent contamination from concept drift; ensures label maturity to avoid false negatives; uses AUCPR as the appropriate primary metric; matches feature windows across models; fits preprocessors only on training data; and uses a single test-set evaluation policy—all of which are correct and necessary for the hypothesis.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer Sequence Model vs. GBT Aggregates for Card-Not-Present Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 22 months of authorization-level transaction logs from the bank's production card portfolio. All card-not-present e-commerce transactions are included, covering the full credit and debit card portfolio across millions of accounts. Fraud labels are derived from confirmed chargebacks and investigator-resolved disputes, joined at the transaction level. To avoid partial censoring — a known hazard near the observation boundary — a 60-day label maturation period is enforced: any transaction in the final 60 days of the raw data window is excluded from all splits and evaluation. This ensures every transaction in scope has had sufficient time to attract a dispute, so the negative class is not contaminated by uninvestigated future fraud.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided chronologically:
- **Training:** Months 1–16
- **Validation:** Months 17–18
- **Test:** Months 19–20 (months 21–22 excluded for label maturity)

No random shuffling is applied across time boundaries. A temporal split is mandatory here because fraud is subject to concept drift — attack patterns emerge, evolve, and are suppressed over time. A random split would allow future transactions to contaminate training, producing unrealistically optimistic estimates that do not reflect deployment conditions. The temporal split correctly simulates the production scenario: train on history, deploy into the future.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same current-transaction features (log-amount, MCC, entry mode, merchant, channel, geography) and the same 90-day / 50-transaction lookback window per account. The transformer encodes the raw ordered sequence of prior transactions as token embeddings; the GBT receives hand-engineered rolling aggregates over the identical window (velocity, spend percentiles, merchant diversity, recency, CNP fraction).

All preprocessing transformers — log scalers, frequency encoders for high-cardinality categoricals, imputers — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. Sequence statistics for GBT features are computed using only transactions strictly prior to the target transaction, at training time and at inference, preventing any forward leakage.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a transformer encoder (4–6 layers, 4–8 attention heads, hidden dim 128–256) with a binary classification head on the pooled CLS token, trained with AdamW, cosine warmup schedule, and class-weighted cross-entropy. The baseline is a LightGBM GBT tuned with equivalent effort (100 Optuna trials) on the same validation set. Using LightGBM on hand-engineered rolling-window features is the correct baseline because it represents the dominant production paradigm this hypothesis aims to displace; it receives the same information window, the same current-transaction signals, and equivalent hyperparameter search to ensure no tuning asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation

The **primary metric is AUCPR** (area under the precision-recall curve) on the held-out test set. AUCPR is the correct choice given extreme class imbalance — fraud prevalence is typically 0.1–0.5% — and because it directly captures the precision-recall tradeoff the stakeholder cares about: more fraud caught per unnecessary decline incurred. Secondary metrics include recall at fixed FPR operating points (0.5%, 1%, 2%), precision at fixed recall thresholds (50%, 70%, 85%), Brier score for calibration, and fraud dollar capture rate weighted by transaction amount.

Confidence intervals on all metrics are computed via stratified bootstrap (1,000 iterations).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search and model selection for both models is performed exclusively against the validation set (months 17–18), optimizing AUCPR. The test set is locked and touched exactly once, after all decisions are finalized. This single-use policy prevents test-set overfitting through repeated evaluation.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Three principal confounds are addressed by design:

- **Information asymmetry:** Both models are restricted to identical 90-day / 50-transaction lookback windows, preventing the sequence model from gaining an advantage through longer history access.
- **Account activity density:** Performance is stratified by account activity quartile to confirm gains are not an artifact of denser sequences on high-activity accounts.
- **Temporal drift:** Per-week performance is reported across the test month to distinguish stable generalization from early-window fitting.

Additional subgroup analyses by MCC category and merchant country check whether any observed gain is narrowly concentrated in specific fraud typologies, which would limit the generalizability of the claim. A sensitivity analysis with a 90-day maturation cutoff validates that conclusions are robust to the label maturation definition.

This design establishes whether the transformer's sequential encoding provides a genuine lift in the precision-recall tradeoff over a well-tuned GBT baseline, under operationally realistic temporal and class-imbalance conditions. It does not establish why the transformer outperforms (if it does) — mechanistic attribution would require a follow-on ablation study
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because it requires recognizing that several design elements that may appear suboptimal—such as excluding recent data or using AUCPR over AUROC—are in fact appropriate and necessary given the domain constraints of label delay, class imbalance, and concept drift. A correct reviewer must distinguish pattern-matching heuristics from context-sensitive methodology.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchy-aware clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 coding from inpatient EHR text at a large academic hospital system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchy-Aware Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 4 years of de-identified inpatient admissions (2019–2022) from a large academic hospital EHR, targeting approximately 120,000–180,000 encounters after filtering. For each admission, the input text consists of the discharge summary and up to three progress notes selected by recency within the admission window. Critically, any notes with a finalization timestamp within 2 hours of the discharge order are excluded, as these may incorporate language influenced by the billing workflow. Additionally, sections within notes that are known to contain coder-correlated artifacts — such as 'Discharge Diagnoses' lists authored post-discharge — are masked using rule-based section detection applied identically to all splits and both models.

Labels are multi-hot binary vectors derived from finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the billing system. Codes appearing in fewer than 5 admissions across the full dataset are excluded from the label vocabulary to ensure evaluable support, but their absence is tracked separately. Service-line metadata is recorded but excluded from model inputs in the primary experiment to isolate the contribution of text representations.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided temporally: 2019–2021 for training (~70%), Q1–Q2 2022 for validation (~15%), and Q3–Q4 2022 for the held-out test set (~15%). Temporal splitting is essential here. ICD-10 coding practice evolves with annual guideline updates and institutional documentation patterns, so a random split would allow the model to interpolate between adjacent time periods, inflating performance estimates. More importantly, the deployment target is always future admissions, and the evaluation should reflect that. To prevent patient-level leakage across boundaries, any patient with admissions spanning a split boundary is assigned entirely to the earlier period.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For both the transformer and baseline, text inputs are constructed identically: concatenated masked note text from the same source documents. All data-dependent preprocessing — TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and label binarizer — is fit exclusively on the training split and applied (without refitting) to validation and test. The pretrained tokenizer for the transformer is used as provided; no vocabulary-level statistics from validation or test data are incorporated. Text cleaning steps (whitespace normalization, de-identification artifact removal) are deterministic and rule-based, applied uniformly across splits.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed model:** A clinical domain transformer (Bio_ClinicalBERT or GatorTron-base) extended with a hierarchical document encoding architecture. Each 512-token segment (with 64-token stride overlap) is encoded independently, and a 2-layer cross-segment attention module aggregates segment representations into a single admission-level embedding for the multi-label classification head. Sigmoid activations with binary cross-entropy loss are used; inverse-frequency class weighting (capped at 100x) addresses label imbalance. Hyperparameters tuned on validation: learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5), aggregation depth (1–3 layers), loss weight cap, and per-label classification threshold.

**Baseline:** TF-IDF (character + word unigrams/bigrams, max 150k features) with one-vs-rest L2-regularized logistic regression. The baseline receives the same masked text input, the same label space, the same class weighting scheme, and equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over regularization strength, feature count, and per-label thresholds, all evaluated on validation macro-F1). This baseline reflects current deployed practice and isolates the architectural contribution of the transformer.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-F1** across the full included label space. This is the correct metric because it weights each code equally regardless of frequency, directly capturing performance on rare but clinically important codes — the core of the stakeholder concern around case mix, quality reporting, and reimbursement accuracy. Micro-F1 is tracked as a secondary metric for volume-weighted operational context. Per-label thresholds are optimized on the validation set.

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 stratified by code frequency tier (high/medium/low), macro-AUC-PR, Coverage@K (K=5, 10) for suggest-and-confirm workflow relevance, and mean log-loss for calibration assessment.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search and architecture selection is conducted using the validation set, evaluated on macro-F1. The transformer uses early stopping with patience of 3 epochs based on validation macro-F1. The test set is accessed exactly once, after all tuning decisions are finalized, using the single best-performing configuration from validation for each model. No post-hoc threshold adjustment or model selection occurs after test set evaluation.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four key confounds are addressed. First, input parity: both models receive identical text to ensure observed differences reflect representational capacity, not data access. Second, section masking: coder-influenced text is excluded from both models uniformly. Third, temporal splitting: guards against coding practice drift inflating performance estimates. Fourth, metadata exclusion in the primary experiment: an ablation adds service-line metadata to both models to verify gains are not purely metadata-driven.

Results are stratified by service line, LOS quintile, and code frequency tier to detect case-mix confounding between training and test periods. The experiment establishes whether the hierarchy-aware transformer outperforms TF-IDF+OVR on this dataset and time window at this institution. It does not establish generalizability to other hospital systems, ICD-10-PCS procedure codes, or outpatient documentation.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of codes with fewer than 5 occurrences introduces data leakage or undermines label integrity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding labels with fewer than 5 admissions is necessary to ensure statistically evaluable performance metrics. These rare codes would otherwise introduce high variance and unreliable evaluation due to single-instance predictions. Their exclusion is standard practice in long-tail multi-label problems like ICD coding, and their absence is tracked separately for coverage analysis, preserving transparency.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split is unnecessarily conservative and reduces training data size; a random patient-level split would be more efficient.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is methodologically required here because ICD-10 coding practices evolve annually with guideline updates and institutional changes. A random split would allow the model to interpolate between similar time points, inflating performance and creating overly optimistic estimates that do not reflect real-world deployment on future admissions. The split respects both temporal dependency and operational realism.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of macro-F1 as the primary metric is inappropriate because it gives excessive weight to rare codes and could favor overfitting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Macro-F1 is the stakeholder-aligned metric because clinical and reimbursement impact is highly sensitive to missing rare but critical diagnoses (e.g., sepsis, certain cancers). Micro-F1 would let common codes dominate, masking poor performance in areas that matter most. The hypothesis explicitly claims improvement on low-frequency codes, making macro-F1 the correct choice to validate the proposed model's value.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The design is methodologically sound and correctly addresses all key confounds: input parity is preserved, temporal leakage is prevented, preprocessing is fit only on training data, and evaluation uses the stakeholder-aligned metric macro-F1. The reviewer should acknowledge the validity of the design choices and conclude that no critical flaws exist.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender correctly explains that temporal splitting is necessary due to evolving coding practices, that macro-F1 is justified by stakeholder priorities, and that input parity ensures the comparison isolates model architecture differences.

acceptable_resolutions.description: If a reviewer raises concerns about design choices such as temporal splitting, macro-F1, or label filtering, the defender provides domain-specific justifications grounded in clinical operations and evaluation best practices for long-tail medical coding. The debate concludes with a recognition that these are robust, intentional design decisions, not flaws.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender demonstrates that the choices align with operational deployment conditions, mitigate known confounds (e.g., temporal drift, metadata leakage), and reflect current standards in healthcare AI evaluation.

hypothesis: In a hospital network's inpatient EHR data, a transformer model over time-ordered medication administrations, lab results, and vital-sign trajectories predicts acute kidney injury within the next 48 hours better than a gradient-boosted tree model built on hand-engineered summary features, as measured by precision-recall performance at clinically actionable alert rates.
domain: Healthcare clinical decision support for early acute kidney injury detection among hospitalized adult patients across multiple hospitals in an integrated delivery network
task_prompt:
**Experiment Design: Sequential Transformer vs. GBT for 48-Hour AKI Prediction**

**1. Data and Cohort**

The experiment uses 3–5 years (2019–2023) of de-identified inpatient EHR records from a multi-hospital integrated delivery network. The cohort includes adult admissions (age ≥18) with at least one creatinine measurement on record. Admissions are excluded if the patient has end-stage renal disease, prior dialysis or renal transplant, or already meets KDIGO AKI criteria at the time of a candidate prediction window. The final 6 months of the data period are quarantined as the held-out test set before any exploratory analysis begins.

Each admission generates one or more prediction windows: any timestamp during the admission where the patient has been hospitalized ≥6 hours, has a creatinine baseline available from the prior 7 days, and does not currently meet AKI criteria. This yields a large number of prediction instances per admission; care is taken to assign all windows from a given admission to a single split partition.

**2. Label Definition**

The binary outcome is AKI onset within 48 hours of the prediction timestamp, defined by KDIGO creatinine criteria: a ≥0.3 mg/dL absolute rise within any rolling 48-hour window, or a ≥1.5× increase over the 7-day minimum baseline creatinine. Labels are derived retrospectively from future lab values but are carefully isolated: no future-derived information enters the feature set. A surveillance-bias flag is assigned to any window where the 48-hour post-prediction period contains fewer than 2 creatinine draws; flagged windows are included in the main analysis but excluded in a prespecified sensitivity analysis.

**3. Split Strategy**

Splits are chronological at the admission level. Admissions beginning in years 1–3 form the training set (~60% of admissions), year 4 is the validation set (~20%), and year 5 is the held-out test set (~20%). No patient may span two splits. Chronological splitting is mandatory here: random splits would permit future clinical practice patterns, lab ordering policies, and drug availability to bleed into the training window, and would not reflect the prospective deployment scenario where the model predicts on future, not contemporaneous, admissions.

**4. Feature Engineering**

Two parallel feature sets are prepared. The GBT receives hand-engineered summary statistics over fixed lookback windows (6h, 24h, 48h, 7d): creatinine delta and minimum baseline, vital-sign trajectories (mean, min, max, slope), nephrotoxic medication exposure, eGFR, comorbidity flags, unit type, hours since admission, and creatinine draw frequency counts. The transformer receives the raw time-ordered event sequence over a 48-hour lookback window, where each event encodes type, clinical code (LOINC/RxNorm), numeric value, and elapsed time before the prediction timestamp. All scalers, imputation statistics, and tokenization vocabularies are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets without re-fitting.

**5. Models and Baseline**

The proposed model is a 4-layer transformer encoder with continuous-time positional encoding, trained with AdamW and early stopping on validation AUPRC. The baseline is a LightGBM model trained on the summary feature set. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning: Bayesian optimization (Optuna) with the same trial budget, evaluated on validation AUPRC. The GBT baseline is chosen because it represents the operationally realistic comparator — the kind of model that would be deployed in current clinical decision support practice — and because rigorous tuning parity ensures that observed differences reflect architectural capability, not tuning asymmetry.

**6. Evaluation**

The primary metric is AUPRC, with focused analysis on the high-precision operating region corresponding to ≤20 alerts per 100 patient-days (a plausible nursing workload threshold defined a priori). AUPRC is preferred over AUROC because the class imbalance (~10–20% positive rate) and the stakeholder's explicit precision-recall emphasis make AUROC an inappropriate primary metric. Secondary metrics include AUROC for literature comparability, precision and recall at fixed alert-rate thresholds, mean lead time on true positives, calibration error, and subgroup AUPRC stratified by hospital site, care setting, and baseline renal function.

**7. Validation and Test Set Discipline**

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter values, early stopping checkpoints — is performed using validation set AUPRC exclusively. The test set is accessed exactly once after all decisions are locked. No iterative probing of the test set is permitted.

**8. Confound Controls**

Three confounds receive explicit treatment. First, measurement frequency bias: both models include creatinine draw count as a feature; a sensitivity analysis then removes all frequency features from both models to test whether transformer superiority persists under equal informational handicap. Second, hospital-site heterogeneity: site is included as a covariate in both models, and per-site AUPRC is reported to detect site-specific rather than general advantages. Third, care-intensity confound: results are stratified by ICU vs. ward to distinguish whether the transformer's advantage (if observed) is confined to the high-monitoring, high-intervention ICU setting.

This experiment would establish whether a sequential transformer over raw EHR event streams outperforms a carefully tuned GBT on AKI prediction within this network. It would not establish generalizability to other health systems, pediatric populations, or outpatient contexts.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of chronological splits reduces statistical efficiency and may underpower the study.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Chronological splitting is essential here to prevent temporal leakage from future clinical practices into model training, which would invalidate the evaluation under prospective deployment. While less statistically efficient than random splits, it is methodologically necessary given the domain’s time-sensitive confounders such as changes in lab ordering behavior or nephrotoxic medication protocols.

must_not_claim.claim: Including creatinine draw count as a feature introduces target leakage, since frequency may correlate directly with impending AKI.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Creatinine draw frequency is a clinically relevant indicator of patient monitoring intensity and suspicion of deterioration. Including it as a feature is appropriate because both models should have equal access to this signal; it is not leakage but rather a measurable proxy for care intensity. The design explicitly includes a sensitivity analysis removing such features to test whether model comparisons remain robust.

must_not_claim.claim: The primary use of AUPRC instead of AUROC is unnecessarily complex and deviates from standard reporting norms.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is the appropriate primary metric in this imbalanced binary classification setting where stakeholders prioritize performance at low-alert-volume (high-precision) operating points. AUROC aggregates performance across all thresholds and is insensitive to performance in clinically relevant regions; AUPRC better reflects the clinical utility objectives defined in the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Reviewer validates the experimental design as sound: the split strategy prevents data leakage, the metric aligns with clinical priorities, the baseline is fairly tuned, and confound controls are appropriately implemented. The final verdict affirms that this design correctly evaluates whether the transformer outperforms the GBT in the target deployment setting.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design maintains chronological integrity, avoids information leakage, ensures fair comparison through tuning parity, and addresses major known confounds via stratification and sensitivity analyses.

hypothesis: For predicting next-7-day stockouts at the SKU-store level in a grocery retail chain, a temporal fusion transformer that jointly models sales, promotions, deliveries, holidays, weather, and cross-SKU substitution signals will achieve lower weighted pinball loss and higher stockout-event recall than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on lagged aggregate features.
domain: Retail grocery inventory forecasting for store-level replenishment planning across perishable and non-perishable SKUs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for 7-Day Stockout Forecasting at SKU-Store Level

### 1. Data and Labels

We would use three years of daily operational records from the regional grocery chain: POS sales, end-of-day inventory snapshots, delivery receipts, spoilage logs, shelf prices, promotion records, product hierarchy metadata, store attributes, and local holiday calendars. External weather data is joined by store geo-coordinates. Year 1 serves as a burn-in window for constructing lag features; years 2–3 form the active modeling window. All SKU-store combinations active for at least 60 days within the modeling window are included, stratified by perishability tier and revenue decile.

Two labels are constructed. For quantile forecasting, observed daily sales are used directly, with zero-sale days co-occurring with near-zero inventory flagged as demand-censored observations and excluded from loss computation — this prevents rewarding models that simply predict zero during stockouts. For stockout-event classification, a binary label marks any 7-day forward window containing at least one day with zero sales AND zero (or near-zero) on-hand inventory AND no planned store closure. Both labels are constructed using strictly past-of-t information.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The active window is split chronologically: year 2 (months 1–12) for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 as the held-out test set. A 14-day gap is inserted at each boundary to prevent lag features from bridging splits. Random splits are explicitly ruled out: autocorrelated demand and seasonality mean random splits would inflate held-out performance estimates and allow future distributional information to leak into training. The deployment context is purely forward-looking, so temporal splits are the only methodologically defensible choice.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All five feature groups — temporal lags and rolling statistics, inventory state, promotion and price, calendar and weather, and product/store context — are identical for both models. Known-future covariates (scheduled deliveries, planned promotions, calendar features) are provided to both models within the same 7-day forecast horizon. A cross-SKU substitution scalar (mean lagged sales of the top-3 correlated substitutes in the same subcategory, correlations estimated on training data only) is also provided to both models.

All scaling parameters, target-encoding statistics, and lag feature rolling statistics are fit exclusively on the training fold and applied frozen to validation and test. This is non-negotiable: fitting on the full dataset or on validation data would constitute a standard preprocessing leakage error.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer configured to output three quantiles (q=0.1, 0.5, 0.9) at each of 7 forecast steps, with an encoder history of 56 days. The baseline is LightGBM trained on quantile objectives for the same three quantiles across the same 7 horizons, receiving the identical feature set. Both models are tuned with equal Optuna budgets (50 trials) on the validation set under the same primary metric. This ensures that any observed performance difference is attributable to architectural properties — the TFT's sequence modeling and attention mechanism — rather than tuning asymmetry or information asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is revenue-weighted pinball loss aggregated across all SKU-store-horizon combinations in the test set, with weights proportional to trailing 28-day revenue. This directly aligns with the business objective: accurate quantile forecasts for service-level-targeted replenishment, weighted by commercial importance. Censored observations are excluded from the denominator.

Secondary metrics include stockout-event recall and precision (from q=0.1 forecast alerts), prediction interval coverage (target 80%), MASE on the median forecast, and inference latency. All metrics are reported in aggregate and separately by perishability tier, revenue decile, and intermittent vs. regular demand series.

### 6. Validation and Test Protocol

All model selection and hyperparameter decisions are made on the validation set only. The test set is used exactly once, after tuning is complete for both models, in a single batch evaluation. Results are logged and frozen; no post-hoc adjustments are permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are directly controlled: (i) known-future covariate parity — both models receive identical future information at identical horizons; (ii) identity feature parity — product and store identity are encoded equivalently for both models; (iii) censoring handling — identical exclusion of stockout-period observations from loss for both models; (iv) sparse/intermittent series — excluded from the primary evaluation but analyzed as a subgroup. These controls ensure the experiment establishes whether the TFT's temporal sequence modeling mechanism provides incremental value over a well-tuned GBT given the same information — and nothing more.

The experiment would not establish generalizability to other retail chains, other geographic regions, or the performance of TFTs on previously unseen SKUs or stores introduced after the test period. Those claims would require separate out-of-distribution evaluations.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split excludes stores or SKUs entirely, risking poor generalization to new products or locations.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The experiment's goal is to evaluate in-distribution future forecasting performance for existing SKU-store combinations; holding out entire stores or SKUs would misalign with the deployment context and weaken temporal validity. The design correctly prioritizes chronological integrity over entity holdout.

must_not_claim.claim: Using trailing 28-day revenue for weighting introduces look-ahead bias because it changes over time.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Revenue weighting is computed using only training-period trailing data, applied consistently and frozen for test evaluation. This is a standard, valid method to align model performance with current business impact and does not constitute data leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding censored observations from loss means models are not penalized for predicting high demand during stockouts, potentially favoring risky forecasts.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding censored observations is the correct choice here: rewarding models that accurately forecast true underlying demand while preventing 'gaming' of the metric by predicting zero during stockouts. Since true demand is unobservable during stockouts, excluding those periods avoids erroneous penalization and aligns with the business objective of accurate replenishment targeting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the experimental design is sound and methodologically rigorous, with no critical flaws. The defender successfully rebuts any concerns by citing domain-aligned choices and controlled confounds.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design correctly uses temporal splits to match deployment conditions, ensures fair comparison via feature parity, avoids leakage in preprocessing and validation, and aligns metrics with business objectives.

difficulty_justification: Despite the absence of corruptions, this case is moderately challenging because a reviewer must distinguish legitimate, domain-justified design choices (e.g., temporal splits, revenue weighting, censoring exclusion) from plausible-sounding but incorrect criticisms, requiring deep understanding of retail forecasting and time series methodology.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a domain-adapted transformer that jointly encodes pathology report text and specimen metadata will outperform a strong CRF or BiLSTM-CRF baseline using token and lexicon features for extracting cancer biomarkers and their values from pathology reports, measured by entity-level micro-F1 on held-out hospitals.
domain: Healthcare NLP for oncology pathology reporting, where hospital systems need to automatically extract biomarker mentions such as ER/PR/HER2, PD-L1, and mutation results from free-text pathology reports to populate cancer registries and downstream treatment decision support systems
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. CRF/BiLSTM-CRF for Cross-Hospital Biomarker NER

### 1. Data and Annotation

We would draw 3 years of de-identified pathology reports from a multi-hospital health system or registry vendor, ensuring at least 6 distinct hospitals or reference labs are represented. From this corpus, a stratified annotation sample of 8,000–12,000 reports would be selected, weighted toward breast and lung cancer (highest biomarker annotation density) but including colorectal and other solid tumors to avoid systematic coverage gaps. Trained clinical abstractors would annotate using a documented IOB2 label schema covering biomarker test names (ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, EGFR, etc.), result value spans, negation modifiers, and historical-context flags. Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa) would be computed per entity type and disclosed; disagreements would be adjudicated by a pathologist reviewer. The annotation guide is fixed before annotation begins and held constant across all hospitals.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split is **hospital-based (site-out)**, not random or report-level. Each hospital is assigned exclusively to train (approximately 4 hospitals, ~70% of annotated reports), validation (1 hospital, ~15%), or held-out test (1–2 hospitals, ~15–20%). This directly instantiates the evaluation criterion in the hypothesis — performance on hospitals not seen during training — and prevents shared templating artifacts and boilerplate phrases from inflating estimates, which is the central threat to validity with random splits in this domain. If site count is limited, leave-one-hospital-out cross-validation is applied over candidate validation hospitals, with a separately locked test hospital reserved from the outset.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training split. For the **BiLSTM-CRF and CRF baselines**, this includes vocabulary construction, lexicon feature weights, and metadata one-hot encoders. For the **transformer model**, the pretrained checkpoint (PubMedBERT-base or Bio_ClinicalBERT) is loaded from public biomedical corpora with no overlap with the experimental data; fine-tuning uses only training-split annotations. Structured metadata fields (specimen type, cancer site) are encoded as prefix tokens prepended to the report text for the transformer, and as one-hot document-level features for the baselines — ensuring both models receive equivalent metadata access. Hospital/lab identifiers are replaced with anonymous learned site embeddings or masked entirely to prevent shortcut learning on institution-specific templates.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **primary model** is a pretrained biomedical transformer fine-tuned for token classification with a linear NER head, jointly attending to text and metadata via prefix-token injection. Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size, dropout, warmup) are tuned on the validation hospital.

The **primary baseline** is a BiLSTM-CRF with BioWordVec embeddings, orthographic features, character n-grams, POS tags, NCI Thesaurus and HUGO gene lexicons, and the same metadata features in document-level concatenation. A **secondary baseline** is a vanilla CRF with the same lexicon and orthographic features (no neural encoder) to establish a lower bound. Both baselines receive an equivalent hyperparameter search — same number of trials, same validation metric — to ensure the comparison is not confounded by differential tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is entity-level micro-F1 with span-exact matching (boundary and type must both match), evaluated on the held-out test hospital(s) using seqeval. This aligns with the operational requirement: partial or mistyped biomarker extractions are not usable for registry population.

Secondary metrics include: micro-Precision and micro-Recall separately (since the stakeholder explicitly prioritizes recall — a missed biomarker is operationally worse than a spurious extraction); per-entity-type F1 for each major biomarker class; relaxed span F1 to diagnose boundary vs. type errors; inter-hospital F1 variance across test sites as a stability measure; and ablation F1 comparing the transformer with and without metadata to isolate the contribution of joint encoding.

Confidence intervals for all test-set metrics are estimated via stratified bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations over test reports).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All model selection decisions — checkpoint choice, metadata injection strategy, hyperparameter configuration, early stopping — are made using the validation hospital and the primary micro-F1 metric only. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all development decisions are finalized and frozen. No test feedback is used during tuning.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, hospital-based splitting prevents template leakage. Second, a metadata-ablated transformer variant determines whether gains come from joint encoding or the backbone alone. Third, including BioWordVec-initialized BiLSTM-CRF ensures the baseline also benefits from large biomedical pretraining, isolating architecture from corpus size. Fourth, masking hospital identifiers prevents the transformer from exploiting institution-specific formatting as a shortcut. Annotation imbalance across hospitals is reported as a limitation with per-hospital entity counts disclosed.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer with joint text-metadata encoding outperforms a carefully tuned CRF/BiLSTM-CRF on cross-hospital biomarker extraction. It would not establish generalizability to out-of-distribution cancer types, non-pathology report formats, or hospital systems with substantially different templating conventions not represented in training.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a hospital-based split leads to unbalanced label distributions across train/validation/test that could bias model evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While some hospitals may have rarer biomarker expressions or different reporting practices, the hypothesis explicitly focuses on cross-hospital generalization. A site-out split is methodologically correct and necessary to evaluate performance on unseen institutions; imbalance is acknowledged as a potential limitation but does not invalidate the design, and per-hospital metrics are reported to diagnose such issues.

must_not_claim.claim: Using BioWordVec embeddings in the BiLSTM-CRF baseline gives it an unfair advantage, comparable to the transformer's pretraining.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including BioWordVec is appropriate and necessary to ensure a fair comparison. The purpose is to isolate architectural differences (transformer vs. LSTM-CRF) rather than benefit from pretraining magnitude. Since the transformer uses PubMedBERT/Bio_ClinicalBERT, the baseline must also benefit from biomedical pretraining to avoid confounding model type with corpus size effects.

must_not_claim.claim: Prepending metadata as prefix tokens to the transformer input could exceed context length limits and truncate pathology report content.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design assumes input truncation strategies (e.g., report segmentation, prioritizing key sections) are applied if needed. This is standard practice in long-document NLP and does not invalidate the core method; the metadata prefix is a fixed, small set of tokens and is justified as a means to enable joint attention between metadata and text.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The experiment design is sound and validly tests the hypothesis. The reviewer should conclude that the design adequately controls for key confounds (templating artifacts, metadata access, pretraining advantage) and correctly evaluates cross-hospital performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that no methodological flaws are present, and confirms that the split strategy, baseline strength, and evaluation protocol are aligned with the hypothesis and domain requirements.
