hypothesis: For next-day SKU-by-fulfillment-center demand forecasting in a grocery e-commerce platform, a temporal fusion transformer trained on historical sales, promotions, pricing, and weather signals will reduce weighted quantile loss more than a gradient-boosted tree model built on engineered lag features, especially for fast-moving perishable items.
domain: E-commerce grocery operations demand forecasting at the SKU-by-warehouse level for inventory replenishment and spoilage control
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: TFT vs. GBT for Next-Day SKU-by-Fulfillment-Center Demand Forecasting

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 30 months of daily production data from the grocery e-commerce platform, covering all active SKU × fulfillment-center combinations. The dataset includes daily order volumes, price and promotion calendars, inventory availability flags, regional holiday calendars, and D+1 weather forecasts matched to each facility's service region. A 30-month window ensures the training set spans at least two full seasonal cycles while leaving a meaningful held-out test period.

The central data quality challenge is stockout censoring: observed sales understate true demand whenever inventory was unavailable. We address this in two steps. First, all SKU-day observations where the inventory availability flag indicates a stockout or active delisting are marked as censored. Second, a demand reconstruction model — trained only on non-censored training observations — imputes unconstrained demand for censored periods. This corrected series is used as the training target and evaluation ground truth, with both models receiving the same corrected signal. Censored days are excluded from loss computation in all evaluations.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split is strictly chronological: months 1–18 for training, months 19–22 for validation, and months 23–30 for the held-out test set. No shuffling or stratification that crosses temporal boundaries is applied.

This choice is non-negotiable given the deployment context. The model will be used for live planning under future promotional regimes and seasonal patterns it has not seen. A random or stratified-by-SKU split would allow future seasonal structure to leak into training via correlated time-series patterns, producing misleadingly optimistic validation estimates. Chronological splitting is the only evaluation strategy that faithfully simulates the production use case.

The 8-week test window is deliberately chosen to span a major promotional period, ensuring both models are stress-tested on the regime-shift scenarios where the hypothesis predicts the largest TFT advantage.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive the same feature set: lagged sales (D-1, D-2, D-3, D-7, D-14, D-28), rolling 7- and 28-day means and standard deviations, day-of-week, week-of-year, and month indicators, current-day promotion flag and discount depth, days since last promotion, D+1 weather forecast, most recent stockout duration, and catalog metadata (category, perishable flag). The TFT consumes these as structured time-varying inputs and static entity embeddings per its architecture; the GBT receives them as a flat tabular row.

All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, the stockout correction model — are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test data. The 'days until next promotion' feature is explicitly excluded from both models, as it encodes future information not available at forecast time in a production setting.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Fusion Transformer with quantile output heads at q10, q50, and q90. The baseline is a LightGBM ensemble with separate quantile regression models for each quantile target. LightGBM is the correct baseline because it represents the current best practice for tabular demand forecasting and directly isolates whether the sequence architecture adds value over well-engineered lag features.

Both models receive identical features and identical tuning effort: Bayesian hyperparameter optimization over the same number of trials, evaluated on the same validation metric. No covariate advantages are granted to either model.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is volume-weighted Quantile Loss (WQL) averaged across q10/q50/q90, with fast-moving perishables weighted 3×. This directly reflects operational impact: high-quantile forecast calibration drives replenishment decisions that prevent stockouts, and median accuracy drives waste reduction on perishables. RMSE or MAPE alone would obscure the asymmetric cost structure.

Secondary metrics include per-quantile pinball loss, empirical coverage of the [q10, q90] interval (target ~80%), segment-stratified WQL by perishable status and promotion activity, and week-by-week WQL over the test window to confirm temporal stability of any observed gains.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter tuning and model selection decisions use the validation set (months 19–22) only. The test set is evaluated exactly once after all configurations are finalized, executed as a single scripted batch run. An ablation study — removing TFT entity embeddings — is pre-registered before any test set results are examined, to isolate architectural contributions from representational capacity.

### 7. Controls and Scope

Four confounds are actively controlled: covariate asymmetry (eliminated by matching feature sets), stockout bias (eliminated by consistent censored-observation handling), tuning asymmetry (eliminated by matched optimization budgets), and calendar leakage (eliminated by temporal splitting and promotion-stratified analysis). The experiment establishes whether the TFT architecture improves next-day quantile forecast accuracy on this platform and covariate set. It does not generalize to other platforms, longer horizons, or explain the mechanism of any observed gain.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (8 months) is almost twice as long as the training set (18 months); this imbalance risks underfitting and invalidates the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In time series forecasting, especially in operational settings with regime shifts, a longer test period is appropriate to assess generalization under future conditions. The 30-month window is chosen to ensure sufficient coverage of seasonal and promotional cycles, and chronological splitting prioritizes temporal realism over data size balance. The training period still contains significantly more observations than typical random splits would allocate, and model capacity is tuned appropriately.

must_not_claim.claim: The 'days until next promotion' feature should be included as a known future input for the TFT, since it's available in production.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While future promotion calendars may be known in advance, including this feature would grant the TFT a structural covariate advantage not available to the GBT in fair comparison. To preserve covariate symmetry and isolate architectural differences, both models must receive the same input information. 'Days until next promotion' is excluded from both to prevent any future leakage and ensure fairness.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a demand reconstruction model trained on non-censored data introduces bias, because the model may not generalize well to imputing demand during stockout periods.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stockout bias is a known, severe confound in demand forecasting. The use of a reconstruction model trained only on non-censored training data is a documented, standard technique to estimate true demand. It is applied identically to both models and excluded from loss computation on censored days, making it a valid and necessary correction. Alternative approaches (e.g., zero-censoring) would create stronger downward bias.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the experiment design is methodologically sound and raises no valid criticisms that cannot be rebutted with domain or statistical reasoning. The defender successfully defends all challenged decisions using principles of causal inference, temporal validation, and fairness in model comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critique targets a design choice that is justifiable under forecasting best practices (e.g., chronological split, shared features, stockout correction), and the defender provides correct technical and operational rationale aligned with the hypothesis and data structure.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate ends with the reviewer identifying a perceived flaw, but the defender demonstrates through logical argument or reference to standard practice that the choice is correct, leading the meta-evaluator to conclude no material flaw exists.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer questions a legitimate but non-problematic aspect (e.g., test set length, exclusion of future features), and the defender explains why the choice preserves validity, fairness, and operational relevance.

hypothesis: For predicting 7-day mechanical failure of CNC milling machines, a temporal transformer trained on multivariate sensor sequences and machine operating context will achieve higher precision-recall AUC than a gradient-boosted tree model built on hand-engineered aggregate features from the same telemetry.
domain: Manufacturing predictive maintenance for a multi-factory CNC machining operation where unplanned spindle or toolhead downtime disrupts production schedules
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Transformer vs. GBT for 7-Day CNC Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 36 months of continuous industrial historian and MES data from a multi-factory CNC milling operation. The 36-month horizon captures sufficient rare failure events for stable PR-AUC estimation while spanning seasonal and operational cycles. Continuous telemetry (spindle vibration RMS, motor current per axis, temperature, feed rate, spindle load) is retained at 1-minute resolution. Discrete signals — alarm codes, tool changes, job changeovers, maintenance work orders — are retained at event granularity.

Failure labels are derived from maintenance work orders. A machine-day observation receives a positive label if a fault-driven stoppage or component replacement occurs within a 7-day forward window from the prediction timestamp. A 24-hour exclusion zone immediately before each failure event is dropped from the dataset, preventing pre-failure alarm cascades from leaking into training examples that are nominally 'pre-failure.' A 48-hour exclusion zone after each maintenance event is similarly dropped. Work orders with ambiguous cause codes are excluded. Machines retired or relocated during the window are censored at their last active timestamp.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for the held-out test set. Splits are applied per-machine by timestamp, ensuring no machine has post-split observations in the training set. A random or stratified split would be incorrect here — telemetry is strongly autocorrelated, and pre-failure alarm sequences would contaminate earlier training windows under random sampling. The temporal split forces both models to generalize forward in time, matching the deployment setting.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature representations are constructed from identical raw inputs over a 72-hour lookback window:

- **Transformer inputs**: the raw normalized multivariate time series (1-minute or 5-minute resolution), alarm code embeddings, and static context (machine type, plant ID, age in service, runtime since last maintenance) appended as a context token.
- **GBT inputs**: hand-engineered aggregates over four temporal scales (1h, 6h, 24h, 72h) — rolling mean, std, min, max, 95th percentile per sensor; alarm code frequency and recency; time-since-maintenance; machine type and plant ID.

All normalization statistics, imputation medians, and categorical encodings are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Rolling features are computed strictly from past observations at each prediction timestamp.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is an encoder-only Temporal Transformer (4–6 heads, 2–3 layers, learned positional encoding) with a classification head on the CLS token, trained with AdamW, cosine LR schedule, and class-weighted cross-entropy. The primary baseline is LightGB safeguarded by a speed-limited load-breaker mechanism (simulated in feature set only). no

The GBT baseline is the correct comparison because it represents current best practice for tabular predictive maintenance and isolates the specific question: does sequence modeling of raw multivariate telemetry outperform aggregate feature engineering when both have access to identical information?

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **PR-AUC (Average Precision)**. With a 1–3% positive rate, ROC-AUC overstates classifier quality by weighting true negatives heavily; PR-AUC directly measures ranking quality over the rare failure class. Statistical significance is assessed via 1,000-sample bootstrap on the test set.

Secondary metrics include ROC-AUC, precision and recall at a capacity-compatible operating threshold (set on validation, applied to test), F1 at that threshold, Expected Calibration Error (for downstream scheduling tools that consume raw probabilities), and the lead-time distribution of true positive alerts.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search uses validation set PR-AUC. The test set is held out entirely until a single final evaluation after all design decisions are frozen. No iterative test-set feedback is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, information parity: both models use the same 72-hour window and the same signals, including alarm codes, eliminating the concern that transformer gains reflect finer-grained alarm access. Second, plant effects: per-plant PR-AUC breakdowns on the test set are reported; if aggregate advantage is plant-localized, the claim is scoped accordingly. Third, machine age and runtime are included as features in both models to absorb lifecycle effects.

This design establishes whether temporal sequence modeling outperforms hand-engineered aggregates under controlled information parity in a forward-looking chronological evaluation. It does not generalize beyond the participating factories or establish which specific failure modes drive any observed advantage.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological split might reduce training data size and lead to underfitting, so a random split would be better.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In this domain, telemetry is highly autocorrelated and regime-dependent. A random split would allow future data to leak into the training set via sequences from the same machine, contaminating the evaluation and invalidating the forward-prediction premise. Chronological splitting per machine is necessary to simulate real deployment conditions and maintain temporal integrity.

must_not_claim.claim: PR-AUC is an unusual metric; ROC-AUC should be the primary metric for comparability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: With a 1–3% positive rate, ROC-AUC overstates performance by rewarding large numbers of true negatives, which are abundant but operationally uninformative. PR-AUC focuses on ranking quality within the rare positive class and is better aligned with operational goals of catching true failures under realistic alert budgets.

must_not_claim.claim: The exclusion of a 48-hour window after maintenance events is overly aggressive and wastes usable data.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Post-maintenance periods represent artificially healthy states that do not reflect normal degradation dynamics. Including them would bias the model away from detecting early signs of failure by treating recent-repair intervals as 'safe' periods, weakening generalization to sustained operational risk.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer does not identify any critical flaws in the design. The defender successfully justifies the chronological split, the choice of PR-AUC, and the use of exclusion zones as appropriate for the hypothesis and domain.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer acknowledges that the design correctly avoids temporal leakage, evaluates under realistic operating constraints, and ensures information parity between models. The critique, if any, focuses only on non-fatal concerns that do not undermine the validity of the comparison.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns about potential model leakage or evaluation bias, but the defender demonstrates through reference to the sound design that all preprocessing steps are fit only on training data, splits are strictly temporal and machine-separated, and test usage is one-off.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender shows that normalization, encoding, and feature computation respect temporal holdout boundaries and that no future information contaminates training.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Transformer vs. GBT for 7-Day CNC Failure Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use 36 months of continuous industrial historian and MES data from a multi-factory CNC milling operation. The 36-month horizon captures sufficient rare failure events for stable PR-AUC estimation while spanning seasonal and operational cycles. Continuous telemetry (spindle vibration RMS, motor current per axis, temperature, feed rate, spindle load) is retained at 1-minute resolution. Discrete signals — alarm codes, tool changes, job changeovers, maintenance work orders — are retained at event granularity.

Failure labels are derived from maintenance work orders. A machine-day observation receives a positive label if a fault-driven stoppage or component replacement occurs within a 7-day forward window from the prediction timestamp. A 24-hour exclusion zone immediately before each failure event is dropped from the dataset, preventing pre-failure alarm cascades from leaking into training examples that are nominally 'pre-failure.' A 48-hour exclusion zone after each maintenance event is similarly dropped. Work orders with ambiguous cause codes are excluded. Machines retired or relocated during the window are censored at their last active timestamp.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–30 for validation, and months 31–36 for the held-out test set. Splits are applied per-machine by timestamp, ensuring no machine has post-split observations in the training set. A random or stratified split would be incorrect here — telemetry is strongly autocorrelated, and pre-failure alarm sequences would contaminate earlier training windows under random sampling. The temporal split forces both models to generalize forward in time, matching the deployment setting.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature representations are constructed from identical raw inputs over a 72-hour lookback window:

- **Transformer inputs**: the raw normalized multivariate time series (1-minute or 5-minute resolution), alarm code embeddings, and static context (machine type, plant ID, age in service, runtime since last maintenance) appended as a context token.
- **GBT inputs**: hand-engineered aggregates over four temporal scales (1h, 6h, 24h, 72h) — rolling mean, std, min, max, 95th percentile per sensor; alarm code frequency and recency; time-since-maintenance; machine type and plant ID.

All normalization statistics, imputation medians, and categorical encodings are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Rolling features are computed strictly from past observations at each prediction timestamp.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is an encoder-only Temporal Transformer (4–6 heads, 2–3 layers, learned positional encoding) with a classification head on the CLS token, trained with AdamW, cosine LR schedule, and class-weighted cross-entropy. The primary baseline is LightGBM trained on the hand-engineered feature set, with scale-pos-weight set for imbalance. Both models receive Bayesian hyperparameter optimization (Optuna) over the validation set with equal trial budgets. A majority-class classifier and logistic regression serve as sanity-check lower bounds.

The GBT baseline is the correct comparison because it represents current best practice for tabular predictive maintenance and isolates the specific question: does sequence modeling of raw multivariate telemetry outperform aggregate feature engineering when both have access to identical information?

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **PR-AUC (Average Precision)**. With a 1–3% positive rate, ROC-AUC overstates classifier quality by weighting true negatives heavily; PR-AUC directly measures ranking quality over the rare failure class. Statistical significance is assessed via 1,000-sample bootstrap on the test set.

Secondary metrics include ROC-AUC, precision and recall at a capacity-compatible operating threshold (set on validation, applied to test), F1 at that threshold, Expected Calibration Error (for downstream scheduling tools that consume raw probabilities), and the lead-time distribution of true positive alerts.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter search uses validation set PR-AUC. The test set is held out entirely until a single final evaluation after all design decisions are frozen. No iterative test-set feedback is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, information parity: both models use the same 72-hour window and the same signals, including alarm codes, eliminating the concern that transformer gains reflect finer-grained alarm access. Second, plant effects: per-plant PR-AUC breakdowns on the test set are reported; if aggregate advantage is plant-localized, the claim is scoped accordingly. Third, machine age and runtime are included as features in both models to absorb lifecycle effects.

This design establishes whether temporal sequence modeling outperforms hand-engineered aggregates under controlled information parity in a forward-looking chronological evaluation. It does not generalize beyond the participating factories or establish which specific failure modes drive any observed advantage.
difficulty_justification: The case requires nuanced judgment: a reviewer must distinguish between legitimate methodological defenses (temporal split, PR-AUC, exclusion zones) and actual flaws. The absence of corruptions tests whether the reviewer can resist over-critiquing reasonable design choices in a high-stakes industrial setting.

hypothesis: In a hospital-network clinical coding workflow, a domain-adapted long-document transformer that jointly encodes discharge summaries and key note sections will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus linear one-vs-rest baseline.
domain: Healthcare clinical informatics for inpatient hospital coding, where professional coders assign ICD-10 diagnosis codes from discharge summaries and associated physician documentation for billing and quality reporting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would use three years of finalized inpatient encounters (January 2021 – December 2023) from a multi-hospital health system, targeting approximately 80,000–200,000 admissions. Each encounter must include a discharge summary and at least one finalized ICD-10-CM diagnosis code on the billing claim, assigned by a certified coder before any audit appeal. We retain service line, hospital site, and timestamps as metadata.

The label vocabulary is restricted to codes appearing at least 50 times in the training partition — a threshold determined after splitting to prevent leakage. This typically yields 1,500–3,000 active codes. Encounters with amended billing records or missing discharge summaries are excluded. This restriction scope-limits the evaluation to codes with evaluable support while preserving the long-tail challenge the hypothesis targets.

### 2. Temporal and Patient-Level Split

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 (years 2021–2022) for training (~67%), months 25–30 (first half 2023) for validation (~17%), and months 31–36 (second half 2023) for the held-out test set (~17%). Within each partition, no patient (MRN) appears in more than one partition, preventing patient-level leakage for patients with multiple admissions.

A temporal split is mandatory here. ICD-10 coding guidelines update annually, coding practice drifts, and the operational deployment target is always future admissions. A random split would allow the model to learn from coding patterns that postdate a test admission, inflating estimated performance on the task that actually matters.

### 3. Feature Construction and Preprocessing

For both the transformer and the baseline, the input text is the concatenation of the discharge summary, the attending admission note, and the final progress note or attestation, delimited by section markers. This ensures feature parity: neither model has privileged access to document sections.

All preprocessing artifacts — TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, label binarizer, code frequency thresholds — are fit exclusively on the training split and applied to validation and test. For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer is fixed; only the classification head and fine-tuning weights are estimated from training data.

A controlled ablation evaluates the transformer on discharge summary only (a narrower but common baseline input condition), isolating the contribution of multi-section input from architectural advantage.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a domain-adapted long-document transformer (Longformer or BigBird pretrained on clinical text), fine-tuned end-to-end with multi-label binary cross-entropy and inverse-square-root frequency weighting to address label imbalance. Long inputs are handled via 512-token overlapping chunks with learned attention pooling over chunk representations.

The baseline is TF-IDF (word 1–2-grams and character 1–3-grams) with logistic regression one-vs-rest, operating on the identical input text. Both models undergo 50-iteration Bayesian hyperparameter search on the validation set, evaluated by macro-F1, ensuring tuning effort is matched. This baseline is the correct comparison: it is well-established in the clinical coding literature, is feature-equivalent, and is specifically what the hypothesis claims to surpass.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-averaged F1** across the active code vocabulary. Macro-F1 weights each code equally, directly measuring performance breadth across the code set — including clinically important but infrequent codes — and aligns with the stakeholder's concern about coding completeness, not just high-volume billing accuracy.

Secondary metrics include micro-F1 (volume-weighted billing accuracy), macro-recall (completeness), and macro-precision (suggestion quality). Critically, macro-F1 is also reported stratified by code frequency tier (high/medium/low), by hospital site, by service line, and across early vs. late test months, to evaluate generalization and detect temporal degradation.

### 6. Validation and Test Policy

All hyperparameter tuning, architecture selection, and ablation decisions are made using the validation set only. Prediction thresholds (per-code sigmoid cutoffs) are tuned on validation data. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all such decisions are finalized. No model adjustments are made after observing test results.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, input text parity: both systems receive the same concatenated sections, so any performance difference must arise from representational capacity. Second, document length: F1 is stratified by document length quartile; a transformer advantage that appears only for very long documents would implicate input length rather than architecture. Third, site and service-line composition: per-site and per-service-line F1 are reported on the test set to detect whether gains are driven by a specific subpopulation over-represented in training.

This design establishes whether the transformer architecture outperforms TF-IDF+OvR on macro-F1 under matched input and tuning conditions on future admissions from this health system. It does not claim universal superiority across all deployment contexts or performance on sub-threshold rare codes.

must_not_claim.claim: The label vocabulary threshold of 50 occurrences is too high and excludes rare but clinically important codes, rendering the evaluation incomplete.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion of codes with fewer than 50 training occurrences is methodologically sound: codes must have sufficient training examples to support reliable model learning and statistically stable evaluation. Evaluating on extremely rare codes is not feasible without specialized few-shot methods, which are outside the scope of this hypothesis. The design explicitly preserves the long-tail challenge through codes in the 50–99 frequency range and monitors rarer codes via a meta-label.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a temporal split rather than a random patient split risks introducing temporal distribution shift that unfairly disadvantages the models.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is not a flaw but a necessity in this domain: the operational goal is to deploy on future admissions, and coding practices evolve yearly due to guideline updates and training cycles. A random split would allow models to learn from future data, creating a time-travel leakage and inflating performance. The temporal split reflects real-world deployment conditions and is therefore the correct choice.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The design is sound; the reviewer acknowledges that no fundamental errors exist in the evaluation setup and that all key methodological decisions are justified by the domain and hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that temporal splitting prevents future leakage, that preprocessing is correctly isolated to the training set, that the baseline is appropriately strong and feature-matched, and that macro-F1 is the correct primary metric for evaluating performance across rare and common codes evenly.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises concerns but is satisfied by the defender's domain-appropriate justifications for design choices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender explains why seemingly conservative choices (e.g., strict label threshold, temporal split, identical input text) are required by the real-world constraints of clinical coding workflows and the specific claim being tested — namely, architectural advantage under fair and deployable conditions.

difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because all design choices appear potentially questionable to a novice reviewer (e.g., temporal split, minimum frequency threshold, macro-F1), but each is correctly justified by domain-specific constraints. The challenge lies in distinguishing legitimate concerns from valid methodological decisions that reflect real-world deployment needs.
